reading.title: The Psychology of Habit
reading.passage:
Much of what we do each day is not the result of careful decisions but of habit. From the route we take to work to the way we hold a cup, a large proportion of our behaviour is automatic, carried out with little conscious thought. Psychologists have long been interested in how such habits form and why they are so difficult to change.

Research suggests that habits develop through a simple loop. First there is a cue, a signal that triggers the behaviour; then the routine, the behaviour itself; and finally a reward, which the brain comes to associate with the cue. Over time, this association becomes so strong that the cue alone is enough to set the routine in motion, often before we are even aware of it.

This automatic quality is, in many ways, useful. If we had to think consciously about every small action, daily life would be exhausting. By handing routine tasks over to habit, the brain frees up attention for more demanding problems. The difficulty arises only when the habits in question are ones we would rather be without.

Breaking an unwanted habit, researchers argue, is rarely a matter of willpower alone. A more effective strategy is to keep the same cue and reward but to replace the routine with a healthier alternative. Simply trying to stop, without putting anything in the habit's place, tends to fail. Understanding the structure of habits, then, does not make change effortless, but it does help to explain why good intentions so often come to nothing.

questions.q: Most of our daily behaviour is the result of careful decisions.
A habit loop begins with a ________, a signal that triggers the behaviour.
Over time, the brain comes to associate the reward with the cue.
Why does the writer say the automatic quality of habits is useful?
options: it frees the brain's attention for more demanding problems
it removes the need for any rewards

questions.q: Researchers believe willpower alone is usually enough to break a habit.
A better strategy is to keep the cue and reward but replace the ________.
Understanding the structure of habits makes changing them effortless.

listening.title: Lecture: The Water Cycle in Cities
listening.script:
Today I want to look at how water moves through a city, which is rather different from the natural water cycle you may have studied at school. In the countryside, when rain falls, much of it soaks into the soil and slowly makes its way into rivers. In a city, however, most surfaces — roads, roofs, car parks — are hard and waterproof. Rain cannot soak in, so it runs off quickly into drains.

This rapid run-off creates two problems. First, because the water arrives all at once, drainage systems can be overwhelmed, leading to sudden flooding after heavy rain. Second, as the water flows across the city, it picks up pollutants — oil, chemicals, litter — and carries them into rivers.

Urban planners have developed several responses. So-called green infrastructure, such as rain gardens and permeable pavements, allows some water to soak into the ground as it would naturally. Some cities have even created artificial wetlands to slow the flow and filter out pollution. The key idea in all these approaches is to make the city behave a little more like the countryside.

questions.q: How is a city different from the countryside when rain falls?
options: most city surfaces are hard, so water runs off quickly
rain soaks into city soil very easily

questions.q: Rapid run-off can overwhelm drainage systems, causing sudden ________.
As it flows, the water picks up ________ such as oil and chemicals.
Rain gardens and permeable pavements are examples of green ________.
Some cities create artificial ________ to slow the flow and filter pollution.
The aim of these approaches is to make the city behave more like the countryside.

speaking.q: Let's discuss habits and routines. Why do you think people find it so hard to change their habits, and do you believe schools should teach children how to form good habits? Give reasons and examples.

writing.q: Some people believe the government should be responsible for helping citizens lead healthier lives, while others think this should be left to individuals. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: A large proportion of behaviour is automatic.
We act with little conscious thought.
A cue triggers the behaviour.
The brain forms an association.
Replace the routine with a healthier one.
Thinking about every action would be exhausting.
It frees attention for demanding problems.
Willpower alone rarely works.
Replace it with a healthier alternative.
Good intentions often come to nothing.

reading.title: The Economics of Happiness
reading.passage:
For a long time, economists measured the success of a country largely by its wealth, usually expressed as the total value of goods and services it produced. In recent decades, however, a growing number of researchers have begun to ask a more difficult question: does greater wealth actually make people happier?

The evidence is surprisingly mixed. Within a single country, richer people do tend to report being more satisfied than poorer ones. Yet when whole nations are compared over time, the picture changes. Beyond a certain level of income — enough to meet basic needs comfortably — further increases in average wealth seem to bring only small gains in reported happiness.

Several explanations have been offered. One is that people quickly grow used to improvements in their circumstances, so that a higher income soon feels normal. Another is that much of our sense of well-being is relative: what matters is not how much we have in absolute terms, but how we compare with those around us. If everyone becomes richer together, few people feel any better off.

These findings have led some governments to take an interest in measuring well-being directly, rather than relying on economic figures alone. Critics argue that happiness is too vague and personal to measure reliably. Supporters reply that, however imperfect such measures may be, ignoring well-being entirely is a greater mistake. The debate is far from settled, but it has already changed the way many people think about progress.

questions.q: Economists have traditionally measured a country's success mainly by its wealth.
Within one country, richer people tend to report greater satisfaction than poorer ones.
What happens to happiness when a nation grows richer beyond a certain income level?
One explanation is that people quickly grow ________ to improvements in their circumstances.
Much of our well-being is ________: we compare ourselves with those around us.
Critics think happiness can be measured easily and reliably.
The debate about measuring well-being has now been completely settled.

listening.title: Tutorial: Giving a Good Presentation
listening.script:
Right, let's talk about how to give a presentation that actually holds people's attention. The first thing to remember is that your audience can only take in so much at once. A common error is to fill every slide with text and then read it out. People can either read or listen, but not both well, so your slides should support you, not replace you.

My second piece of advice is to tell a story. Even a technical talk works better if it has a clear beginning, middle and end, rather than being a list of disconnected facts. Give your audience a reason to care in the first minute.

Third, practise out loud, and time yourself. Almost everyone underestimates how long they will take, and nothing loses an audience faster than running over time. Finally, expect questions, and welcome them. If you don't know an answer, it's perfectly fine to say you'll find out and follow up later. Pretending to know something you don't is far more damaging than simply being honest.

questions.q: A common error is to fill every slide with ________ and read it out.
The speaker says people can read and listen equally well at the same time.
What is the speaker's second piece of advice?
options: tell a story with a clear structure

questions.q: You should practise out loud and ________ yourself.
Nothing loses an audience faster than running over ________.
The speaker says it is acceptable to admit you do not know an answer.

speaking.q: Describe a time you had to speak in front of other people. You should say: what the occasion was, what you talked about, and how you felt; and explain whether it went well. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q:
The table below shows the percentage of people in three age groups who reported being 'satisfied with life' in a survey in 2000 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (% reporting life satisfaction):
- Aged 18–34 — 2000: 72%, 2020: 61%
- Aged 35–54 — 2000: 68%, 2020: 64%
- Aged 55+ — 2000: 70%, 2020: 75%

vocab.example: Success was measured by wealth.
Richer people report being more satisfied.
The evidence is surprisingly mixed.
People grow used to better circumstances.
It is not about wealth in absolute terms.
Well-being is partly relative.
Governments now measure well-being.
Critics say happiness is too vague.
It is hard to measure reliably.
The debate is far from settled.

reading.title: The Quiet Comeback of the Tram
reading.passage:
A century ago, electric trams rattled through the streets of hundreds of cities, carrying millions of passengers every day. By the middle of the twentieth century, however, most of these systems had vanished. As private cars became more affordable and bus networks expanded, city authorities came to see tram lines as old-fashioned and inflexible. Tracks were torn up, and the tram seemed destined to become a museum piece.

In recent decades, that judgement has been quietly reversed. Faced with congested roads and worsening air quality, a growing number of cities have chosen to build new tram networks, often described by planners as 'light rail'. Modern trams run on electricity, produce no exhaust fumes at street level, and can move large numbers of people along a fixed route far more efficiently than cars. Where a busy road might carry a few thousand drivers an hour, a single tram line can move many times that number.

Supporters argue that the benefits go beyond transport. Property values often rise along new tram routes, and shops report increased trade once a line opens, partly because passengers tend to walk past storefronts rather than speed past in cars. Some studies suggest that well-designed tram systems persuade people to give up their cars altogether, although the evidence on this point is far from settled.

Critics, however, point to the enormous cost of construction. Laying track, rerouting underground pipes and buying vehicles can run into billions, and the work often disrupts city centres for years. They argue that flexible electric buses can deliver similar environmental benefits at a fraction of the price. Defenders of the tram respond that passengers are simply more willing to use rail, which they perceive as faster, smoother and more permanent than a bus route that could be cancelled at any time.

questions.q: A century ago, trams operated in hundreds of cities.
Modern trams release exhaust fumes at street level.
Riding a modern tram is more expensive than riding a bus.
According to the passage, why might shops benefit from a new tram line?
options: passengers walk past storefronts on foot
trams deliver goods directly to shops
shops pay lower taxes near tram lines

questions.q: Planners often describe modern tram systems as ________ rail.
There is now conclusive evidence that trams make people abandon their cars.
Critics argue that flexible electric ________ can provide similar environmental benefits more cheaply.
Defenders of trams claim that, compared with buses, passengers see rail as:

listening.title: Booking a Guided Cycling Tour
listening.script:
OPERATOR: Good morning, Riverside Cycle Tours, how can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a guided cycling tour for next weekend.
OPERATOR: Lovely. We run two tours. The city heritage tour lasts two hours, and the coastal tour lasts a full day. Which would you prefer?
CALLER: The coastal one sounds great.
OPERATOR: Good choice. It costs thirty-five pounds per person, and that includes bike hire and a helmet.
CALLER: Perfect. There'll be four of us.
OPERATOR: That's fine. Can I take a name for the booking?
CALLER: Yes, it's under Foster, F-O-S-T-E-R.
OPERATOR: And which day would you like, Saturday or Sunday?
CALLER: Sunday, please.
OPERATOR: Right. We meet at nine o'clock outside the old lighthouse. Please bring a water bottle and wear comfortable shoes.
CALLER: Should we bring our own lunch?
OPERATOR: No need, a picnic is provided halfway through. But do let me know if anyone has any allergies.
CALLER: One of us can't eat nuts.
OPERATOR: Noted, thank you. I'll send a confirmation by email this afternoon.

questions.q: Which tour does the caller choose?
The tour costs £________ per person.
The price includes bike hire and a ________.
The booking is made under the name ________.
The group will meet outside the old ________.
A ________ is provided halfway through the tour.

speaking.q: Let's talk about transport. How do you usually travel around your town or city? Do you prefer public transport or travelling by car, and why? Has the way you travel changed in the last few years? Answer each question in a few sentences.

writing.q: In many cities, governments are investing heavily in public transport such as trams and trains rather than building more roads for cars. Do the advantages of this approach outweigh the disadvantages? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Trams rattled through the streets.
Most tram systems had vanished.
Tram lines were seen as inflexible.
Cities face congested roads.
Trams move people efficiently.
Passengers perceive rail as faster.
Construction disrupts city centres.
The cost can be enormous.
That judgement has been reversed.
The tram seemed destined to disappear.

reading.title: Why Cities Are Going Dark
reading.passage:
For most of human history, the night sky was a familiar sight: a dark dome scattered with thousands of stars. Today, more than three-quarters of the world's population lives under skies brightened by artificial light, and in the largest cities only a handful of the brightest stars remain visible. This glow, produced by street lamps, buildings and advertising, is known as light pollution.

The causes are easy to identify. Much outdoor lighting is poorly designed, sending light upwards or sideways instead of down onto the ground where it is needed. A single badly aimed lamp wastes energy and adds to the sky glow that hangs over urban areas. As cities have grown and lighting has become cheaper, the total amount of light escaping into the night has risen steadily.

The consequences reach far beyond astronomy. Many animals depend on natural cycles of light and dark. Newly hatched sea turtles, which normally crawl towards the faint light of the horizon over the sea, can be drawn inland by the brighter glow of coastal towns. Migrating birds, which navigate by the stars, become confused near brightly lit buildings. Even human health may be affected, since exposure to light at night can disturb the body's natural sleep rhythms.

Encouragingly, light pollution is one of the easier environmental problems to address. Unlike carbon dioxide, light does not linger; switch off a lamp, and the pollution vanishes instantly. A number of towns have begun fitting shielded lamps that direct light downwards, dimming street lighting late at night, and creating 'dark-sky reserves' where artificial light is strictly limited. Supporters say these measures save money and energy while restoring a view of the stars that earlier generations took for granted.

questions.q: Most people in the world now live under skies affected by artificial light.
Light pollution is partly caused by lamps that send light in the wrong direction.
Sea turtles are completely unaffected by the lights of coastal towns.
Light pollution costs governments more than any other form of pollution.
Why does the passage say light pollution is relatively easy to fix?
options: because lamps are cheap to remove
because the pollution disappears as soon as lamps are switched off
because few people care about seeing stars
because animals quickly adapt to bright light

questions.q: Areas where artificial light is strictly limited are called dark-sky ________.
Exposure to light at night can disturb the body's natural ________ rhythms.

listening.title: Community Garden Volunteer Induction
listening.script:
Welcome, everyone, and thank you for offering to volunteer at the Bridge Street Community Garden. Let me run through a few things before we start. The garden is open to volunteers every day except Monday, when it closes for maintenance. Most people come on Saturday mornings, which is also when we hold our weekly planning meeting at ten o'clock.

When you arrive, please sign in at the wooden shed near the entrance and collect a pair of gloves; we ask everyone to wear them for safety. Tools are kept in the green container, and we'd be grateful if you returned them clean at the end of your shift.

At the moment our main project is the new vegetable beds along the eastern wall. We're growing tomatoes, beans and herbs, and in the autumn we hope to plant fruit trees. Anything we harvest is shared among the volunteers, and any surplus is donated to the local food bank.

Finally, a quick word on safety. Please don't lift anything too heavy on your own, and let me know straight away if you spot any broken equipment. If you have any questions today, I'll be by the greenhouse. Enjoy your first session!

questions.q: The garden is closed for maintenance every ________.
The weekly planning meeting starts at ________ o'clock.
Volunteers must sign in at the wooden ________ near the entrance.
Where are the tools kept?
Any surplus produce is donated to the local food ________.
The speaker will be by the ________ if anyone has questions.

speaking.q: Describe a place in nature that you enjoy visiting. You should say: where it is, how often you go there, and what you do there; and explain how it makes you feel. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q:
The table below shows the average number of stars visible to the naked eye at night from four locations of differing size in 2010 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (average number of visible stars):
- City centre — 2010: 60, 2023: 35
- Suburb — 2010: 250, 2023: 180
- Small town — 2010: 700, 2023: 600
- Rural area — 2010: 2,500, 2023: 2,300

vocab.example: Stars were scattered across the sky.
Artificial light brightens the sky.
A glow hangs over the city.
Lamps should be aimed downwards.
Light does not linger in the air.
Birds migrate across continents.
Birds navigate by the stars.
Light can disturb sleep rhythms.
Shielded lamps point downwards.
These measures restore the view of stars.

reading.title: The Hidden Life of Soil
reading.passage:
To most people, soil is simply dirt, something to be brushed off shoes and washed from vegetables. Yet a single handful of healthy soil may contain billions of living organisms, more than the number of human beings who have ever lived. Far from being lifeless, soil is among the most crowded and active environments on the planet.

The majority of these organisms are invisible to the naked eye. Bacteria and fungi break down dead leaves and roots, releasing the nutrients that living plants need to grow. Threadlike fungi, in particular, form vast underground networks that connect the roots of different plants, allowing them to exchange water and chemical signals. Some scientists have playfully nicknamed this network the 'wood wide web', comparing it to the way computers share information.

Larger creatures play their part too. Earthworms, perhaps the most familiar soil dwellers, swallow soil as they tunnel, mixing its layers and creating channels through which air and water can pass. Their activity helps rainwater soak into the ground rather than running off the surface, reducing the risk of flooding.

This hidden world is under threat. Intensive farming, heavy machinery and certain chemicals can damage soil structure and kill the organisms that keep it fertile. Once destroyed, healthy soil can take centuries to form again, since it builds up at a rate of only a few centimetres every hundred years. Researchers warn that in some regions soil is being lost far faster than it is being created.

For this reason, a growing number of farmers are adopting methods designed to protect the soil rather than exhaust it. These include leaving crop remains on fields, planting cover crops between harvests, and reducing how often the land is ploughed. Supporters argue that healthier soil not only produces better crops but also stores large amounts of carbon, making it a quiet but valuable ally in the fight against climate change.

questions.q: A single handful of healthy soil can contain billions of organisms.
Most soil organisms can be seen with the naked eye.
What does the 'wood wide web' refer to?
options: an underground network of fungi linking plant roots
a type of computer used by scientists

questions.q: Earthworms create ________ through which air and water can pass.
Soil that has been destroyed can be fully restored within a few years.
Soil is being lost at the same rate in every region of the world.
Healthy soil can store large amounts of ________, helping to fight climate change.

listening.title: Tutorial: A River Field-Trip Report
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, Maya, James, how is the report on your river field trip coming along?
MAYA: Quite well, I think. We've finished measuring the width and depth at three points along the river.
TUTOR: Good. And did you record the speed of the water?
JAMES: We did, but we're not sure our method was accurate. We timed how long a floating orange took to travel ten metres.
TUTOR: That's a perfectly standard technique, actually. The orange floats just below the surface, so it isn't affected too much by the wind.
MAYA: Oh, that's reassuring. Our main problem now is the graphs. We've got so much data that the report feels cluttered.
TUTOR: My advice would be to choose two or three key graphs and put the rest in an appendix at the back.
JAMES: That makes sense. Should we include photographs as well?
TUTOR: Yes, but label them clearly and refer to them in the text. A photo with no explanation isn't very useful.
MAYA: And when is the deadline again?
TUTOR: You have until the fifteenth. But I'd suggest aiming to finish a few days early, so you have time to check it over.
JAMES: Thanks, that's really helpful.

questions.q: The students measured the river's width and ________ at three points.
To measure the water's speed, they timed a floating ________ over ten metres.
What does the tutor say about their method for measuring speed?
options: it is inaccurate and should be repeated
it is a perfectly standard technique
it is affected too much by wind
it is too difficult for students

questions.q: The tutor suggests putting extra graphs in an ________ at the back.
The tutor advises labelling photographs clearly and referring to them in the ________.
The report deadline is the ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss the natural environment. Why do you think some people feel disconnected from nature today? What can schools do to help young people appreciate the natural world? And do you think individuals or governments are more responsible for protecting the environment? Discuss each question in detail.

writing.q: Modern farming methods can increase the amount of food that is produced but may damage the environment in the long term. Do you think the benefits of intensive farming outweigh its drawbacks? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Soil is full of tiny organisms.
Most are invisible to the eye.
Fungi release nutrients into the soil.
Plants exchange water and signals.
Earthworms are familiar soil dwellers.
Intensive farming can harm soil.
Organisms keep the soil fertile.
Some methods exhaust the soil.
Farmers adopt new methods.
Soil is an ally against climate change.

reading.title: The Second-Hand Economy
reading.passage:
Not long ago, buying second-hand clothes carried a certain stigma. For many shoppers, used garments suggested a lack of money rather than a deliberate choice. Charity shops were places to donate unwanted items, not destinations for fashionable buyers. In the space of a single generation, that attitude has changed dramatically.

Today the resale of clothing is one of the fastest-growing parts of the fashion industry. Online platforms allow people to sell items directly from their wardrobes to buyers anywhere in the country, and some of these companies are now worth billions. Vintage shops, once found only in a few corners of large cities, have spread to towns of every size. What was once seen as a sign of hardship is increasingly regarded as a mark of style and good sense.

Several forces lie behind this shift. Concern about the environmental cost of fashion has grown sharply, as shoppers learn how much water, energy and waste are involved in producing new clothes. Buying second-hand extends the life of a garment and keeps it out of landfill. At the same time, rising prices have made careful spending more attractive, and social media has helped to make unique, pre-owned items desirable rather than embarrassing.

The trend is not without its critics. Some argue that the sheer convenience of online resale simply encourages people to buy and discard more than ever, with second-hand purchases sitting alongside, rather than replacing, new ones. Others worry that as second-hand fashion becomes profitable, prices in charity shops will rise, leaving fewer affordable options for those who genuinely rely on them.

Even so, few expect the resale market to shrink. Younger shoppers in particular appear comfortable mixing new and used items without a second thought. For a generation that grew up sharing and streaming rather than owning, the idea that clothes should have only one owner may already seem strangely old-fashioned.

questions.q: In the past, some shoppers felt that buying used clothes suggested they were short of money.
Vintage shops are now found only in large cities.
According to the passage, one environmental benefit of buying second-hand is that it:
options: uses more water than making new clothes
makes clothes last only one season
lowers the price of new clothes

questions.q: Online resale platforms have made some companies extremely valuable.
Most second-hand clothes are now more expensive than new clothes.
Critics worry that easy online resale encourages people to buy and ________ more than before.
Some fear that rising charity-shop prices will leave fewer ________ options for people who rely on them.

listening.title: Lecture: The Science of Sleep
listening.script:
In today's lecture I want to look at why sleep matters far more than many people assume. We often treat sleep as wasted time, but research over the past few decades suggests the opposite. While we sleep, the brain is remarkably busy, sorting through the day's experiences and deciding which memories to keep.

A typical night's sleep is divided into several cycles, each lasting roughly ninety minutes. Within each cycle we pass through light sleep, deep sleep and a stage known as REM, during which most dreaming occurs. Deep sleep appears to be especially important for the body's physical recovery, while REM sleep seems to support learning and emotional balance.

What happens when we don't get enough? Studies consistently show that people who are short of sleep perform worse on tasks requiring concentration, and they tend to make poorer decisions. Perhaps more surprisingly, lack of sleep has been linked to weight gain, because it disturbs the hormones that control appetite.

The good news is that most of these effects can be reversed by returning to a regular sleep pattern. Researchers recommend keeping consistent bedtimes, avoiding bright screens late at night, and getting daylight in the morning. In our next session, we'll examine how these patterns change as people grow older.

questions.q: Each sleep cycle lasts roughly ________ minutes.
Most dreaming occurs during the stage known as ________.
According to the lecturer, deep sleep is especially important for:
The lecturer says a lack of sleep has been linked to weight gain.
Lack of sleep disturbs the hormones that control ________.
Researchers advise getting ________ in the morning to improve sleep.

speaking.q: Let's talk about clothes and shopping. How important are clothes and fashion to you? Do you prefer shopping in stores or online, and why? Have your shopping habits changed in recent years? Answer each question in a few sentences.

writing.q:
The table below shows where shoppers in one country bought their clothes in 2015 and 2024, as a percentage of all clothing purchases. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

DATA (% of clothing purchases by channel):
- High-street shops — 2015: 55%, 2024: 38%
- Online (new clothes) — 2015: 30%, 2024: 40%
- Second-hand / resale — 2015: 8%, 2024: 18%
- Other — 2015: 7%, 2024: 4%

vocab.example: Used clothes once carried a stigma.
Buying second-hand extends a garment's life.
It was a deliberate choice.
Attitudes have changed dramatically.
The resale of clothing is growing.
Pre-owned items have become desirable.
The convenience of resale is appealing.
People buy and discard clothes quickly.
Second-hand fashion is now profitable.
Few expect the market to shrink.

reading.title: How Animals Find Their Way
reading.passage:
Every year, millions of animals undertake journeys that would defeat the most experienced human traveller. Arctic terns fly from one end of the Earth to the other and back again; salmon return from the open ocean to the very stream in which they were born; and monarch butterflies travel thousands of kilometres to a forest none of them has ever seen. How they manage such feats without maps or instruments has puzzled scientists for generations.

Part of the answer lies in the senses. Many birds appear to navigate using the position of the sun by day and the patterns of stars by night. Others, including some fish and turtles, seem able to detect the Earth's magnetic field, using it rather like an internal compass to sense direction and even location. Salmon, meanwhile, are thought to rely heavily on smell, recognising the unique chemical signature of their home river as they approach the coast.

What makes these abilities so remarkable is that they often require no learning at all. A young cuckoo, raised by foster parents of another species, will migrate alone to a wintering ground it has never visited, guided entirely by instinct. This suggests that the information needed for the journey is somehow built into the animal from birth.

Yet instinct is not the whole story. Some long-lived animals, such as certain whales and elephants, clearly improve their navigation through experience, learning the locations of feeding grounds and water sources over many years. In these species, older individuals often lead the group, and their knowledge can be vital to the survival of the others.

Understanding how animals navigate is more than a scientific curiosity. As human activity changes the planet, animals increasingly encounter obstacles their ancestors never faced, from artificial light to shifting climates. The better we understand the delicate systems that guide them, the better our chance of helping these travellers complete journeys that have continued, unbroken, for thousands of years.

questions.q: Arctic terns travel between the two ends of the Earth.
Salmon are thought to find their home river mainly by sight.
Some fish and turtles are believed to navigate by detecting:
A young cuckoo learns its migration route by following its parents.
Whales and elephants navigate purely by instinct, with no role for experience.
Scientists now fully understand how every species navigates.
A young cuckoo is guided on its journey entirely by ________.
In some species, ________ individuals lead the group because of their knowledge.

listening.title: Museum Late-Night Opening
listening.script:
Good evening, and welcome to the Natural History Museum's first Late Night opening of the season. Let me give you a quick guide to tonight's events. The main exhibition, on the history of flight, is open until ten o'clock on the first floor, and entry is included with your ticket.

In the central hall, there will be a short talk by one of our curators at half past seven, lasting about twenty minutes. If you'd like a seat, I'd recommend arriving a little early, as it does get busy.

For families, there's a hands-on activity in the discovery room, where children can handle real fossils under the guidance of our staff. That runs continuously until nine.

The café on the ground floor is open all evening and is serving hot drinks and light meals. Please note that the upstairs restaurant is closed for a private event tonight.

Finally, the gift shop is offering twenty per cent off all books this evening only. If you have any questions, our staff are wearing bright blue badges and will be happy to help. Do enjoy your visit, and please remember that photography without flash is welcome throughout the museum.

questions.q: The main exhibition is about the history of ________.
The curator's talk begins at ________.
In the discovery room, children can handle real ________.
Why is the upstairs restaurant closed?
options: it is hosting a private event
it has run out of food

questions.q: The gift shop is offering ________ per cent off all books.
Staff can be recognised by their bright blue ________.

speaking.q: Describe an animal you find fascinating. You should say: what it is, where it is usually found, and what it looks like; and explain why you find it fascinating. You have one minute to prepare, then speak for two minutes.

writing.q: Large amounts of money are spent on researching and protecting wild animals and their migration routes. Some people think this money would be better used to address human problems. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Animals undertake long journeys.
Such feats puzzle scientists.
They detect the magnetic field.
Salmon recognise their river's chemical signature.
The cuckoo is guided by instinct.
Their abilities are remarkable.
Their knowledge can be vital.
Animals face new obstacles.
These are delicate systems.
Their ancestors never faced such problems.

reading.title: The Return of the Heirloom Tomato
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the tomatoes sold in supermarkets were bred for one main quality: the ability to survive long journeys. Growers favoured thick-skinned, uniformly red fruit that could be picked while still firm and ripened later in trucks. Flavour was rarely a priority. As a result, many shoppers came to think of the tomato as a watery, rather tasteless vegetable.

In recent decades, however, so-called heirloom tomatoes have made a striking comeback. These are older varieties whose seeds have been saved and passed down by gardeners for generations, sometimes for more than a hundred years. They come in unusual shapes and colours – deep purple, pale yellow, even green when fully ripe – and they are prized for their rich, complex taste. Because they bruise easily and do not all ripen at the same time, they are difficult to grow on a large scale.

Small farms and farmers' markets have been central to the revival. Selling directly to customers, growers do not need fruit that can withstand weeks of transport, so they can choose flavour over toughness. Many chefs have also championed heirloom varieties, putting them on restaurant menus and persuading diners that a tomato can be a luxury rather than a filler.

Scientists point out that heirloom tomatoes are valuable for another reason. Modern commercial crops rely on a narrow range of genetic material, which makes them vulnerable to new diseases. The older varieties preserve genetic diversity that breeders may one day need. For this reason, several seed banks now collect and store heirloom seeds, treating them as a resource for the future rather than a curiosity from the past.

questions.q: Supermarket tomatoes in the twentieth century were mainly chosen for their taste.
Heirloom tomato seeds have sometimes been kept by families for over a century.
Heirloom tomatoes are easy to produce in large quantities.
Heirloom tomatoes are cheaper than ordinary supermarket tomatoes.
According to the passage, why are farmers' markets important to the heirloom revival?
options: They sell tomatoes at the lowest prices
Growers there do not need fruit that survives long transport

questions.q: Scientists value heirloom varieties mainly because they
options: look more attractive than modern crops

questions.q: Heirloom tomatoes are difficult to transport partly because they ________ easily.

listening.title: Joining a Community Garden
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, Riverside Community Garden, how can I help?
VISITOR: Hi, I'd like to rent a plot for the season. How does it work?
STAFF: Of course. Each plot is four metres by three metres, and the rental fee is forty-five pounds for the whole year.
VISITOR: That sounds reasonable. Does that include tools?
STAFF: It includes the use of our shared shed, which has spades, forks and watering cans. You'd only need to bring your own gloves and seeds.
VISITOR: Great. When can I start?
STAFF: Plots become available on the first of March. We ask members to attend one training session before they begin – it lasts about two hours and covers watering and composting.
VISITOR: And is there a waiting list at the moment?
STAFF: There is, but it's short. You'd probably be offered a plot within three weeks. If you'd like to join, just fill in the form on our website and pay the deposit of ten pounds.
VISITOR: Perfect, I'll do that today. Thank you!

reading.title: Bees and the Business of Pollination
reading.passage:
When people think of bees, they usually picture honey. Yet for many farmers, the most valuable thing bees provide is not honey at all but pollination. As bees move from flower to flower in search of nectar, they carry pollen with them, allowing plants to produce fruit and seeds. Without this service, crops such as almonds, apples and blueberries would yield far less.

In some parts of the world, pollination has become a large industry in its own right. Each spring in California, for example, the vast almond orchards burst into flower at almost the same time. The trees need enormous numbers of bees, far more than live in the area naturally. To solve this, beekeepers transport hives by truck from across the country, sometimes travelling thousands of kilometres. For a few weeks, the orchards hum with rented bees before the hives are loaded up and driven to the next crop.

This system is efficient, but it carries risks. Moving hives over long distances can stress the insects and help spread diseases between colonies. Beekeepers have also reported sudden, unexplained losses, in which most of the worker bees in a hive disappear. The causes are still debated, but pesticides, parasites and poor nutrition are all thought to play a part.

Researchers are therefore looking at ways to reduce the pressure on honeybees. Some farms are planting strips of wildflowers along the edges of fields to support wild pollinators such as bumblebees and solitary bees. Others are experimenting with growing several crops together so that something is always in flower. The hope is that a more varied landscape will be healthier for bees – and, in the long run, more secure for the farmers who depend on them.

questions.q: For many farmers, pollination is more valuable than the honey bees produce.
California's almond orchards flower at very different times across the season.
Beekeepers in California earn more money than beekeepers in other states.
Transporting hives long distances can help diseases spread between colonies.
Why are bees trucked into the almond orchards?
options: There are not enough local bees for the crop
The orchards have no wild flowers

questions.q: What is one solution farms are trying to support pollinators?
options: Planting strips of wildflowers near fields

questions.q: Bees carry ________ from flower to flower, allowing plants to produce fruit.

listening.title: A Tour of a Honey Farm
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to Golden Valley Honey Farm. I'm Megan, and I'll be showing you around today. Before we start, a few safety points. Please don't stand directly in front of a hive, because that's the bees' flight path. If a bee lands on you, stay calm and let it fly off – they very rarely sting.
We keep around eighty hives here, spread across two paddocks. Each hive can hold up to fifty thousand bees in summer. The bees fly out to collect nectar, and a strong hive can produce about thirty kilograms of honey in a good year.
We harvest the honey twice a season, in December and again in late February. After harvesting, we let the honey settle for three days before bottling it. Today, you'll get to taste two kinds: a light honey from clover, and a darker one from eucalyptus.
At the end of the tour, the farm shop is open, and members of our visitor club get a ten per cent discount. Right, let's head to the first paddock.

reading.title: Growing Food in the City
reading.passage:
As cities grow larger, the distance between farms and dinner tables grows with them. Much of the food eaten in big cities travels hundreds, even thousands, of kilometres before it is sold. This long journey adds to costs and pollution, and the freshest produce often loses quality on the way. In response, a movement known as urban farming has been spreading through towns and cities around the world.

Urban farming takes many forms. In some places, residents turn unused plots of land into shared vegetable gardens. In others, restaurants grow herbs on their rooftops, or schools plant fruit trees in their grounds. A more high-tech approach is vertical farming, in which crops are grown indoors on stacked shelves under electric lights. Because the conditions are tightly controlled, these farms can produce vegetables all year round, regardless of the weather outside.

Supporters argue that urban farming brings several benefits. Food can be grown close to where it is eaten, cutting transport and keeping produce fresh. Empty land is put to good use, and neighbours often come together to tend shared gardens, strengthening their community. Children, too, can learn where their food comes from.

There are limits, however. City land is expensive, and the amount of food that rooftops and small plots can produce is modest compared with the needs of millions of residents. Vertical farms, meanwhile, use a great deal of electricity for lighting, which can make their vegetables costly. For these reasons, few experts believe that cities will ever feed themselves entirely. Most see urban farming as a useful addition to the food supply rather than a replacement for the countryside – one that reconnects people with the food they eat.

questions.q: Food eaten in large cities often travels long distances before being sold.
Vertical farms depend heavily on good outdoor weather.
Urban farming is more popular in cold countries than in warm ones.
Vertical farming can produce vegetables throughout the year.
What is one social benefit of shared city gardens mentioned in the passage?
Why can vegetables from vertical farms be expensive?
options: They use a lot of electricity for lighting

questions.q: What is the general view of experts about urban farming?
options: It will soon feed entire cities
It is a waste of money
It is a helpful addition rather than a full replacement
It only works in the countryside

questions.q: In vertical farming, crops are grown indoors on stacked ________ under electric lights.

listening.title: A Lecture on Vertical Farming
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to give you an overview of how a modern vertical farm actually works. Imagine a warehouse with no windows. Inside, plants are grown not in soil but in water enriched with nutrients – a method called hydroponics.
The plants sit on shelves stacked up to twelve layers high, which means a single building can grow as much as a field many times its size. Lighting comes entirely from LED lamps, which the farmers can adjust to suit each crop. Leafy greens such as lettuce and spinach grow especially well this way and can be ready to harvest in about twenty-five days.
Now, the big advantage is control. There are no pests from outside, so almost no pesticides are needed, and water use is around ninety per cent lower than in a normal field, because the water is recycled. The main drawback, as you might guess, is the cost of energy for the lights.
For next week, please read chapter four and prepare two questions on energy use. Thank you.

According to the lecturer, what is the main drawback of vertical farms?
options: The cost of energy for lighting

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Food Waste
reading.passage:
Around the world, a surprising amount of food never reaches anyone's plate. Some studies suggest that roughly a third of all food produced is lost or thrown away. This waste happens at every stage, from the farm to the kitchen, and it carries costs that go far beyond the price of the food itself.

In poorer countries, much of the loss occurs early. Crops may rot in the fields because there are not enough workers to harvest them in time, or they spoil during storage because farms lack refrigeration. Poor roads can mean that fruit and vegetables are damaged or delayed on the way to market. In these cases, food is lost before it ever reaches consumers.

In wealthier countries, the pattern is different. Here, much of the waste happens in homes, shops and restaurants. Shoppers often buy more than they need, attracted by special offers, and then throw away what they cannot eat. Supermarkets may reject perfectly good vegetables simply because they are the wrong shape or size. Confusion over date labels also plays a part: many people throw food away on the 'best before' date, even though it is often still safe to eat.

Reducing waste matters for more than just money. Growing food uses land, water and energy, so wasted food means wasted resources. When that food rots in landfill, it also releases gases that contribute to climate change. Encouragingly, there are signs of progress. Some shops now sell 'imperfect' fruit at lower prices, charities collect surplus food for those in need, and clearer labelling is helping shoppers decide what is genuinely past its best.

questions.q: Some studies estimate that about a third of all food produced is lost or wasted.
In poorer countries, most food waste happens in people's homes.
Supermarkets sometimes reject vegetables because of their shape or size.
Governments in wealthy countries have passed laws banning food waste.
Why is food often lost early in poorer countries?
options: Farms may lack refrigeration and good roads

options: Labels are too small to read
People throw away food that is still safe

listening.title: A Supermarket Food-Rescue Scheme
listening.script:
MANAGER: Thanks for coming in. So, you're interested in volunteering with our food-rescue scheme?
VOLUNTEER: Yes, I read about it online. How does it work exactly?
MANAGER: Each evening, we set aside food that's close to its sell-by date but still perfectly good. Volunteers collect it and deliver it to two local charities.
VOLUNTEER: How much food are we talking about?
MANAGER: On an average day, about sixty kilograms. Most of it is bread, fruit and vegetables.
VOLUNTEER: When would I need to be here?
MANAGER: The collection slot is from six to seven in the evening, four days a week. We ask volunteers to commit to at least one shift weekly.
VOLUNTEER: That works for me. Do I need anything special?
MANAGER: Just sensible shoes and, ideally, a driving licence, though it's not essential. We provide insulated bags and a short training session, which takes about forty minutes.
VOLUNTEER: Great. Where do I sign up?
MANAGER: I'll give you a form now, and you can start on Monday.

reading.title: Ancient Grains, Modern Plates
reading.passage:
For thousands of years, farmers grew a wide range of grains suited to their local soil and climate. As global agriculture became more industrial, however, a small number of crops – mainly wheat, rice and maize – came to dominate the world's fields and diets. Many older grains were pushed to the margins, surviving only in remote regions where traditional farming continued. Today, several of these so-called ancient grains are enjoying renewed attention.

One example is quinoa, a crop grown high in the South American Andes for centuries. Unlike wheat, quinoa contains all the protein building blocks the human body needs, and it grows well in poor, dry soils where other crops struggle. As its reputation as a 'superfood' spread, demand rose sharply in wealthier countries, and prices climbed. This brought income to many Andean farmers, although some worried that local families could no longer afford a food that had long been part of their diet.

Other ancient grains are also returning to the table. Millet, once a staple across much of Africa and Asia, needs little water and can ripen quickly, making it valuable in regions facing drought. Spelt and einkorn, early forms of wheat, are reappearing in bakeries that market them as more natural alternatives to modern flour.

Supporters of ancient grains point to two main advantages. First, many are hardy and can grow in difficult conditions, which may help farmers adapt as the climate changes. Second, growing a wider variety of crops reduces the risk that a single disease could threaten the food supply. Critics caution, however, that fashion can be fickle, and that careful planning is needed to ensure that demand benefits the farmers who grow these grains rather than leaving them exposed when tastes change again.

questions.q: As agriculture became more industrial, a few crops came to dominate the world's diets.
Quinoa contains all the protein building blocks the human body needs.
Rising quinoa prices made the crop more affordable for Andean families.
Millet is now the most widely grown grain in the world.
Why is millet considered valuable in dry regions?
options: It needs little water and ripens quickly
It is the cheapest grain to buy
It can only grow in wet soil

options: It reduces the risk that one disease threatens the food supply
It removes the need for farmers

questions.q: What concern do critics raise about ancient grains?
options: Demand driven by fashion may not last
They cannot be sold in bakeries

questions.q: Spelt and einkorn are early forms of ________.

listening.title: A Radio Feature on Quinoa
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Welcome back. In this part of the programme, we're looking at quinoa, the grain that took the world by storm. With me is food researcher Dr Alan Pierce. Alan, where does quinoa come from?
DR PIERCE: It's been grown in the Andes for around five thousand years, mostly at high altitude – sometimes above three thousand metres.
PRESENTER: And why did it suddenly become so popular?
DR PIERCE: Two reasons, really. It's high in protein, about fourteen per cent by weight, and it suits people who can't eat wheat. Between 2006 and 2013, the world price roughly tripled.
PRESENTER: That must have helped the farmers.
DR PIERCE: It did, at first. Many earned far more than before. But the higher price also meant some local families ate less of it themselves. The good news is that production has now spread to over seventy countries, which has helped stabilise prices.
PRESENTER: Fascinating. Dr Pierce, thank you for joining us. After the break, we'll be cooking with quinoa in the studio.

reading.title: The Slow Return of the Night Train
reading.passage:
For decades, the overnight sleeper train seemed destined to disappear from Europe. As budget airlines spread across the continent in the early 2000s, travellers chose cheap, fast flights over long rail journeys. Many national rail operators cut their night services, arguing that the carriages were old, the routes unprofitable, and the demand too small to justify the cost of running them.

In recent years, however, the night train has begun an unexpected comeback. A growing number of passengers, particularly younger ones, have become uneasy about the environmental impact of flying. A single short flight can produce far more carbon than the same trip by rail. For these travellers, climbing into a bunk in one city and waking up in another feels both responsible and pleasantly old-fashioned.

Austria has led this revival. Its state railway took over several abandoned routes and rebranded them under a single network, adding modern sleeping compartments and reliable timetables. The service connects cities such as Vienna, Zurich and Hamburg, allowing passengers to avoid a hotel night while travelling. Tickets are not always cheap, but many customers say they value the comfort and the extra time the journey saves.

Obstacles remain. Building new sleeper carriages is expensive, and trains must cross several national borders, each with its own rules and ticketing systems. Critics also point out that night trains still carry only a tiny share of long-distance travellers. Even so, supporters believe that with steady investment and political backing, the romance of the sleeper could once again become a practical choice for ordinary journeys.

questions.q: Budget airlines became popular in Europe during the early 2000s.
National rail operators claimed that night services were too profitable to cancel.
Younger travellers are mentioned as a group concerned about the environmental cost of flying.
The Austrian night-train network is the busiest rail service in Europe.

reach any city faster than by plane

RECEPTIONIST: Good afternoon, Riverside Hostel, how can I help?
GUEST: Hi, I'd like to book a bed for three nights, arriving on the fourteenth of August.
RECEPTIONIST: Certainly. Would you prefer a shared dormitory or a private room?
GUEST: A shared dorm, please. What's the price per night?
RECEPTIONIST: A dorm bed is eighteen pounds a night, and that includes breakfast.
GUEST: Great. Does the breakfast have a vegetarian option?
RECEPTIONIST: Yes, we always have fruit, bread and eggs. Breakfast is served from seven until ten.
GUEST: Perfect. And is there a locker for my bag?
RECEPTIONIST: Every bed has its own locker, but you'll need to bring your own padlock, or buy one at reception for four pounds.
GUEST: I'll buy one there. Can I check in early, around midday?
RECEPTIONIST: Check-in is officially from two o'clock, but you can leave your luggage with us before then.
GUEST: That works. Could I have your address for my map?

GUEST: Thank you. I'll see you on the fourteenth.

reading.title: Why People Leave the Countryside
reading.passage:
Across much of the world, people are moving from rural areas into cities. This shift, known as urbanisation, has reshaped societies within a single generation. In some countries, villages that once held hundreds of families now have only a handful of elderly residents, while nearby cities grow larger every year.

The reasons behind this movement are mostly economic. Farming often provides an uncertain income, and modern machinery means fewer workers are needed on the land. Young people in particular are drawn to cities by the promise of steady jobs, higher wages and a wider choice of careers. Education plays a part too: most universities and training colleges are located in urban centres, so students leave home to study and frequently stay on after graduating.

Life in the city is not always easier, however. Newcomers may struggle with high rents, crowded housing and the cost of daily living. Some find that the jobs they hoped for are scarce, and competition is fierce. Even so, surveys suggest that few people who move to the city wish to return permanently to the countryside, partly because of the services and entertainment that urban life offers.

Governments have tried various policies to slow the flow. Some offer grants to encourage businesses to open in rural towns, while others improve roads and internet access so that people can work remotely without leaving their villages. These measures have had mixed results. Although better connections help, they rarely outweigh the powerful pull of the city, where opportunities, services and social life remain concentrated.

questions.q: Urbanisation has changed some societies very quickly.

Most universities mentioned in the passage are located in rural areas.

options: They dislike farming as an activity
They are attracted by jobs and better wages
They are forced to leave by the government

questions.q: What does the passage say about people who move to the city?
options: Most want to return to the countryside soon
Few wish to move back permanently

questions.q: The movement of people from rural areas to cities is known as ________.
Some governments offer ________ to encourage businesses to open in rural towns.

listening.title: A Talk on a Local Museum
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the Harbour History Museum. Let me give you a quick overview before you explore. The museum is open every day except Monday, from nine in the morning until five in the afternoon. Entry for adults is twelve dollars, but students get in for just six dollars with a valid card. On the ground floor you'll find the main exhibition about early settlers who arrived here by sea in the eighteen hundreds. There are real letters, tools and photographs on display. Upstairs, the second floor is dedicated to the history of the railway that connected our town to the capital in nineteen oh six. If you're hungry, the cafe is at the back of the building and serves light meals and coffee until four thirty. Please note that photography is allowed, but flash is not, as it can damage the older documents. Finally, the gift shop near the exit sells postcards, books and souvenirs. Guided tours leave from this desk every hour on the hour, and they last about forty minutes. Enjoy your visit.

speaking.q:
Describe a place you would like to move to in the future. You should say:
- where this place is

- what you would do there
and explain how your life might change if you lived there.

writing.q: In many countries, large numbers of people are moving from the countryside to live in cities. Do the advantages of this trend outweigh the disadvantages? Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Hidden Rivers Beneath Cities
reading.passage:
Many large cities were built beside rivers, which provided water, transport and protection. As these cities grew, however, some of their smaller streams became a problem. They flooded streets, carried waste and spread disease. The solution chosen by engineers in the nineteenth century was often dramatic: they buried the rivers underground, sending them through brick tunnels hidden beneath the expanding city.

London is a famous example. Several of its minor rivers, such as the Fleet and the Tyburn, once flowed openly through the landscape. Today they run unseen below busy streets, channelled into the sewer system. Most residents pass over them every day without ever realising that water is moving beneath their feet. Similar hidden waterways exist under cities from New York to Tokyo.

Burying rivers solved immediate problems of flooding and sanitation, but it created new ones. Without natural channels, heavy rain has nowhere to go, and modern cities increasingly suffer from sudden floods when drains overflow. The loss of open water also removed habitats for fish, birds and insects, reducing the variety of urban wildlife.

For these reasons, some cities are now reversing the old decisions. In a process known as 'daylighting', engineers reopen buried streams, allowing them to flow above ground once more. The South Korean capital, Seoul, carried out one of the best-known projects, removing a motorway to uncover a stream that had been hidden for decades. The restored waterway has lowered local temperatures, attracted wildlife and become a popular place for residents to relax, showing that yesterday's solutions can sometimes be undone.

questions.q: Many large cities were originally built next to rivers.
Nineteenth-century engineers usually left small city streams flowing on the surface.
Most London residents are aware of the rivers flowing beneath the streets.
The daylighting project in Seoul cost more than similar projects in other cities.
According to the passage, why did engineers bury small rivers?
options: To stop flooding, waste and disease

questions.q: What is one negative result of burying rivers mentioned in the passage?
options: Heavy rain has nowhere to go

questions.q: The process of reopening buried streams is called ________.
In Seoul, engineers removed a ________ to uncover a hidden stream.

listening.title: Asking for Directions to a Festival
listening.script:
VISITOR: Excuse me, could you tell me how to get to the river festival?
LOCAL: Of course. Are you walking or taking the bus?
VISITOR: Walking, I think. Is it far?
LOCAL: Not at all, about fifteen minutes. Go straight along this road until you reach the library, then turn left.
VISITOR: Past the library, turn left. Got it.
LOCAL: After that, keep going until you see a small bridge. Cross the bridge and the festival is in the park on your right.
VISITOR: And what time does it start?
LOCAL: The stalls open at eleven, but the main concert begins at three in the afternoon.
VISITOR: Is there an entrance fee?
LOCAL: It's free to enter, though you'll pay for food and drinks. There's also a craft market near the entrance.
VISITOR: Lovely. Is there anywhere to leave a bicycle?
LOCAL: Yes, there's a bike rack just before the bridge. Oh, and bring an umbrella, the forecast says rain after four.
VISITOR: Thanks so much for your help.
LOCAL: No problem. Enjoy the festival.

speaking.q: Some people think governments should spend money on restoring natural features like rivers and parks in cities, even when it is expensive. To what extent do you agree, and what benefits might such projects bring to people living in urban areas?

Public space | Men | Women

reading.title: The Language Map of a Single Street
reading.passage:
In many large cities, a single street can contain dozens of languages. Shop signs in one alphabet sit beside menus in another, and conversations overlap in a mixture of tongues. Researchers who study this everyday display of written language call it the 'linguistic landscape'. By photographing and mapping signs, they can learn a great deal about who lives in a neighbourhood and how communities change over time.

The languages on display are rarely random. When migrants settle in a new area, they often open shops, restaurants and services aimed at others from their home country. Signs in their language appear, creating a visible marker of the community's presence. As earlier groups move away and newer arrivals take their place, the balance of languages on the street gradually shifts. A careful observer can sometimes read decades of migration in a row of shopfronts.

Official languages also play a role. In some cities, the law requires that public signs appear in the national language, even where most residents speak something else at home. This can create a contrast between formal signs above the door and handwritten notices in the window, which reflect the language people actually use day to day.

Studying linguistic landscapes is more than an academic curiosity. City planners use the information to decide where to provide translation services or multilingual health advice. Businesses use it to understand their customers. For the residents themselves, the mixture of scripts and sounds is simply part of daily life, a quiet record of journeys made from many corners of the world to one shared corner of a city.

questions.q: The term 'linguistic landscape' refers to the written language displayed in public spaces.
The languages seen on a street usually appear at random.
In some cities, the law requires public signs to include the national language.
Researchers find studying linguistic landscapes more difficult than studying spoken language.
Why do signs in a migrant group's language often appear in a new area?
options: Migrants open shops and services for their community
They are required by international law

questions.q: How can city planners use information about linguistic landscapes?
options: To decide where to offer translation services

questions.q: Researchers learn about neighbourhoods by photographing and ________ signs.
Handwritten notices in shop windows often reflect the language people use ________ to day.

listening.title: A Lecture on Place Names
listening.script: LECTURER: Good morning. Today we'll look at how place names can reveal the history of human movement. A town's name is rarely an accident. Very often it records who lived there long ago, sometimes a thousand years before. Take names ending in certain sounds. In parts of England, towns ending in 'by' often mark places once settled by Scandinavian peoples, while endings like 'ton' point to earlier farming villages. So just by reading a map, a historian can trace which groups arrived and when. Names also travel with migrants. When settlers crossed the ocean to North America, they frequently named new towns after the places they had left behind, which is why you find a Boston, a Cambridge and a Plymouth on both sides of the Atlantic. Sometimes the original meaning is forgotten entirely. A river name might come from an ancient word simply meaning 'water', repeated by each new group who did not understand the older language. For your essay this week, choose one town near where you grew up, research the origin of its name, and explain what it tells us about the people who lived there. Please keep it under one thousand words and submit it by Friday.

questions.q: What can place names reveal, according to the lecturer?
In England, town names ending in 'by' often mark places settled by ________ peoples.
Why do some North American towns share names with European ones?
options: Settlers named them after places they had left
They were founded at the same time

questions.q: Some river names come from an ancient word simply meaning ________.
The essay must be kept under ________ words.
When must the essay be submitted?

speaking.q: Let's talk about languages and places. Are there many different languages spoken in the area where you live? Do you enjoy hearing or seeing other languages around you? Why or why not?

writing.q: When people move to a new country, some believe they should adopt the local language and customs as quickly as possible, while others think they should keep their own traditions. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Mapping the World Before Satellites
reading.passage:
Long before satellites circled the planet, people found ingenious ways to map the world around them. Early travellers relied on the position of the sun and stars, the direction of winds and the shape of coastlines to find their way. Their knowledge was often passed on by word of mouth or recorded in simple sketches, but it allowed traders and explorers to cross vast distances with surprising accuracy.

One remarkable example comes from the islands of the Pacific. Navigators there developed a deep understanding of ocean swells, the patterns of waves that move across open water. By feeling the motion of their canoes, experienced sailors could detect islands far beyond the horizon, long before any land became visible. Some communities even made 'stick charts' from thin pieces of wood, which showed the way swells bent around islands. These charts were not carried on voyages but were studied beforehand and memorised.

In Europe and the Middle East, mapmakers took a different approach, gradually combining the reports of many travellers into single drawings of known lands. Early maps were frequently inaccurate, mixing real geography with legend, yet each new voyage added detail and corrected old mistakes. The invention of reliable instruments for measuring latitude slowly made charts more trustworthy.

What all these traditions shared was a willingness to observe the natural world closely and to learn from those who had travelled before. Modern technology can now pinpoint any location in seconds, but it rests on centuries of patient observation. The achievements of early navigators remind us that human curiosity, more than any single device, has always been the true engine of exploration.

questions.q: Early travellers used natural signs such as the sun and winds to navigate.
Pacific navigators carried their stick charts with them during voyages.
Early European maps sometimes combined real geography with legend.
Pacific stick charts were more accurate than early European maps.
How could experienced Pacific sailors detect distant islands?
options: By feeling ocean swells through their canoes
By reading printed charts at sea

questions.q: What made European charts gradually more trustworthy?
Pacific navigators studied the patterns of ocean ________ to find their way.
The passage says human ________ has always been the true engine of exploration.

listening.title: Planning a Geography Field Trip
listening.script: TEACHER: Right, let's go over the details for next week's geography field trip. We'll be studying the coastline at Sandy Bay. The coach leaves school at eight fifteen sharp, so please don't be late. The journey takes around an hour and a half, so we should arrive by quarter to ten. First, we'll walk along the beach to look at how the waves shape the cliffs. Then, after lunch, we'll measure the height of the dunes using simple equipment, which I'll hand out on the day. You'll need to bring a notebook, a pencil and, importantly, waterproof boots, because the sand is often wet. Don't bring expensive cameras; your phones will be fine for photos. Lunch is not provided, so pack your own, and bring at least one litre of water each. The cost of the trip is nine dollars, which covers the coach. Please return the signed permission form to me by Thursday. We'll be back at school by four o'clock, in time for the usual buses. Any questions can wait until the end. Thank you.

and explain why this journey is memorable for you.

reading.title: The Return of the Local Newspaper
reading.passage:
For many years, experts predicted that printed local newspapers would soon disappear. As readers moved online and advertisers followed them, hundreds of small-town papers in Britain and the United States closed their doors. Many towns were left without any dedicated source of local news, a situation that journalists began to call a "news desert".

Recently, however, a quiet revival has begun. Some communities, frustrated by the lack of reliable local reporting, have started their own papers funded by readers rather than by advertising. These titles are often run by a small team or even by volunteers, and they focus on stories that larger national outlets ignore, such as council decisions, school events and local planning disputes.

Researchers at a media institute studied twenty of these new papers over two years. They found that towns with an active local paper had higher rates of voting in local elections, and residents reported feeling more connected to their neighbours. The study suggested that a healthy local press does more than report facts; it helps hold local officials to account and gives a community a shared sense of identity.

Not everything is positive. Most reader-funded papers remain financially fragile, and many depend on grants that may not be renewed. Some critics also warn that small teams cannot always check facts as carefully as larger newsrooms once did. Even so, supporters argue that an imperfect local paper is far better than no paper at all, and that these experiments may point the way towards a more sustainable future for community journalism.

questions.q: Many local newspapers closed because advertisers moved online.
The new community papers earn most of their money from advertising.
The media institute studied papers in more than one country.
According to the study, towns with an active local paper had:
What does the term "news desert" describe?

A region with very hot weather
A place with no dedicated local news source

listening.title: Joining the Community Newsletter
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, Riverside Community Centre, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I saw a poster about a volunteer newsletter and I'd like to get involved.

STAFF: Wonderful. Could I take your name first?

CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.

STAFF: Thank you, Daniel. We meet every Tuesday evening at seven o'clock in the main hall. Would that suit you?

CALLER: Tuesday's perfect. What kind of help do you need?

STAFF: At the moment we're short of people to write short articles about local events, and we also need someone to take photographs.

CALLER: I can definitely write. I used to study journalism.

STAFF: That's ideal. The first issue you'd work on comes out on the fifteenth of March, so the deadline for articles is the eighth.

CALLER: Right, the eighth. Is there a fee to join?

STAFF: No, it's completely free, but we do ask volunteers to commit to at least three months.

CALLER: That's fine. Should I bring anything on Tuesday?

STAFF: Just a notebook and a pen. We'll provide everything else and give you a short training session.

reading.title: How Photographs Shape Our Memories
reading.passage:
Most people now carry a camera everywhere in the form of a smartphone. We take photographs of meals, holidays and ordinary moments, often without thinking. Psychologists have become interested in how this habit affects the way we remember the events we capture.

In one experiment, visitors to a museum were asked to look at certain objects and to photograph others. The next day, they remembered fewer details about the items they had photographed than about the items they had simply observed. The researchers called this the "photo-taking impairment effect". They suggested that when we rely on a camera to record something, our brain may put in less effort to remember it.

However, the picture is not so simple. A later study found that the effect depended on how people took the photo. When participants chose carefully what to include in the frame, zooming in on a detail, their memory of the object actually improved. The act of deciding what mattered seemed to focus attention rather than reduce it.

Photographs also shape memory long after an event. When we look back at old images, we tend to remember the photographed moments and forget the ones that were never captured. In this way, a photo collection slowly becomes a kind of edited version of our past. Some researchers warn that this can distort memory, making holidays seem happier or trips seem busier than they really were.

The lesson, experts say, is not to stop taking photographs but to take them thoughtfully. Putting the phone away for part of an experience, and choosing images with care, may help us both enjoy the moment and remember it more accurately.

questions.q: In the museum study, people remembered photographed objects better than observed ones.
Taking a photo can improve memory if the person frames the shot carefully.
The museum study was carried out in more than one country.
The "photo-taking impairment effect" suggests that relying on a camera:
options: reduces the brain's effort to remember

questions.q: According to the passage, an old photo collection can:
options: become an edited version of the past

questions.q: When participants ________ in on a detail, their memory improved.
Experts advise taking photographs more ________.
Experts recommend that people stop taking photographs altogether.

listening.title: A Talk on Smartphone Photo Habits
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Thanks for coming along today. I want to share a few findings from our survey on how students use phone cameras. We asked four hundred students about their photo habits over one week.

The first result surprised us. On average, each student took around sixty photos a week, but kept fewer than half of them. Most pictures were deleted within a day.

Second, when we asked why people photograph things, the most common answer was not to share online but to remember later. About seventy per cent said memory was their main reason.

Third, and perhaps most interesting, students who reviewed their photos within a week remembered events far better than those who never looked back at them. So the problem may not be taking photos but failing to revisit them.

Finally, a small group said they deliberately leave their phones in a bag during important moments, such as concerts. They told us this helped them feel more present. We'd like to test that idea properly in our next study, which begins in September. If you'd like to join, please add your email to the list at the back of the room.

On average each student took about ________ photos a week.

speaking.q:
Describe a photograph that is special to you. You should say:
- when and where it was taken
- who or what is in the photograph
- why you took it
and explain why this photograph is important to you.

Write at least 250 words.
