reading.title: The Spread of Online Rumours
reading.passage:
False information has always existed, but the internet allows it to travel faster than ever before. A single rumour can reach millions of people within hours, often before anyone has had the chance to check whether it is true. Understanding how rumours spread has therefore become an important area of research.

A large study of messages shared on a social platform found that false stories spread more quickly and more widely than accurate ones. Surprisingly, the difference was not caused by automated accounts but by ordinary users. People were more likely to pass on stories that were new and surprising, and false stories were often designed to be exactly that. Truthful reports, by contrast, tended to be more ordinary and so attracted less attention.

Emotion also plays a powerful role. Stories that make people feel angry or afraid are shared far more often than calm, neutral ones. Those who create false information understand this well and deliberately use strong emotional language to encourage sharing. As a result, a frightening but untrue claim may outperform a reassuring and accurate one.

Researchers have tested several ways to slow the spread. One simple method is to ask users to pause and consider whether a story is accurate before they share it. In experiments, this small delay reduced the sharing of false stories without greatly affecting true ones. Another approach, sometimes called "prebunking", teaches people in advance to recognise common tricks used in misleading content.

No single solution is likely to be enough. Yet the research offers a hopeful message: because most rumours are spread by ordinary people rather than machines, small changes in human behaviour could make a real difference.

questions.q: False stories in the study spread more widely than accurate ones.
The rapid spread of false stories was mainly caused by automated accounts.
The study measured how long users spent reading each story.
Why do people tend to share false stories, according to the passage?
"Prebunking" refers to:
options: deleting false stories after they spread
teaching people in advance to spot tricks
paying users to share true stories

questions.q: Stories that make people feel ________ or afraid are shared more often.
Asking users to ________ before sharing reduced the spread of false stories.
The researchers believe a single solution will be enough to stop rumours.

listening.title: A Workshop on Checking Facts Online
listening.script:
TUTOR: Welcome to today's workshop on checking facts online. By the end, you'll have three simple tools you can use whenever you see a surprising story.

The first tool is the source. Before you believe a claim, ask who is behind it. Click on the account or website name and see whether it is a recognised news organisation or something you've never heard of.

The second tool is the date. A lot of misleading posts use real photos, but from years ago. A picture of a flood might be genuine, yet taken in a completely different country in two thousand fifteen. Always look for when something actually happened.

The third tool is comparison. If a story is true and important, other reliable outlets will usually be reporting it too. If only one unknown site has the story, be cautious.

Now, a quick note about images. There's a free tool that lets you search where a photo first appeared online. We'll practise using it after the break, which starts at eleven and lasts fifteen minutes. Please bring a laptop if you have one, and we'll work through some real examples together.

questions.q: The first tool the tutor mentions is checking the ________.

If only one unknown site reports a story, you should:
The break starts at ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss information in society. Why do you think false stories spread so easily on the internet? What responsibility, if any, do ordinary people have when sharing news online?

The table below shows the percentage of adults in one country who said they had shared a news story online that they later found to be false, by age group, in 2023.

Shared false news online (% of age group)
------------------------------------------
Age group | Yes | No
18-29     |  34 |  66
30-44     |  27 |  73
45-59     |  19 |  81
60+       |  41 |  59

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

reading.title: Podcasts and the New Age of Audio
reading.passage:
A little over a decade ago, few people had heard the word "podcast". Today, hundreds of millions of listeners around the world tune in to audio programmes on every imaginable subject, from history and science to comedy and crime. The rapid growth of podcasting has changed not only how we consume information but also how stories are told.

One reason for this success is convenience. Unlike television, a podcast does not demand a person's eyes. Listeners can follow a programme while commuting, exercising or doing housework. This means audio fits into parts of the day that other media cannot reach. For many people, a podcast has replaced the radio as a companion during routine tasks.

Podcasting has also lowered the barrier to creating media. Producing a television show requires expensive equipment and a large team, but a podcast can be recorded with a simple microphone and a laptop. As a result, voices that were once excluded from traditional broadcasting now have a platform. Listeners can find shows hosted by experts, hobbyists and ordinary people sharing personal experiences.

The industry faces challenges, however. With so many programmes competing for attention, it has become harder for new shows to be noticed. Many podcasts struggle to make money, since listeners are reluctant to pay and advertising income is shared among an enormous number of titles. Some popular hosts have signed exclusive deals with large companies, a trend that worries those who value the open and independent character of early podcasting.

Despite these concerns, audio shows no sign of decline. As smart speakers and in-car systems make listening even easier, the podcast seems likely to remain a central part of how people learn, relax and stay informed for years to come.

questions.q: Podcasting was already a familiar word more than fifty years ago.
A podcast can be produced with much simpler equipment than a television show.
Most podcast listeners say they prefer crime shows to comedy.
According to the passage, one key advantage of podcasts over television is that they:
options: do not require the listener's eyes

questions.q: Why are some people worried about exclusive deals with large companies?
options: They make podcasts too expensive to record
They threaten the independent character of podcasting
They reduce the number of listeners

questions.q: Podcasting has lowered the ________ to creating media.
Many podcasts struggle to make ________.
The passage suggests that audio is likely to decline soon.

listening.title: Planning a Student Podcast
listening.script:
MAYA: So, we're really doing this? Our own podcast?

LIAM: Definitely. I've booked the recording room in the library for Thursday afternoon.

MAYA: Great. What should the show be about?

LIAM: I was thinking student life, but with a focus on advice. You know, study tips, budgeting, that kind of thing.

MAYA: I like that. Practical but friendly. How long should each episode be?

LIAM: Maybe twenty minutes. Long enough to be useful, short enough to finish on a bus ride.

MAYA: Perfect. And how often will we release them?

LIAM: Let's aim for one every two weeks to start. We can always do more later.

MAYA: Sounds manageable. Do we need any special equipment?

LIAM: The library lends out microphones, so we just need to bring headphones and a memory stick to save the files.

MAYA: Got it. What about a name?

LIAM: I quite like "Campus Corner". It's simple and easy to remember.

MAYA: Campus Corner. Yeah, that works. Let's plan the first episode topics before Thursday, then.

questions.q: The recording room is booked for ________ afternoon.
The podcast will mainly focus on:
Each episode will be about ________ minutes long.
How often do they plan to release episodes at first?

reading.title: The Hidden Work of Online Moderation
reading.passage:
Every minute, users upload an enormous amount of material to social platforms: photographs, videos, comments and messages. Much of this content is harmless, but a small proportion breaks the rules, containing violence, abuse or dangerous misinformation. Deciding what to remove and what to allow is the task of content moderation, a job that most users never see.

In the early days of the internet, platforms relied mainly on human moderators. These workers reviewed reported posts one by one, often making difficult decisions in just a few seconds. The work could be deeply unpleasant, as moderators were repeatedly exposed to disturbing images. Many companies have since been criticised for failing to protect the mental health of these employees.

As the volume of content grew, human review alone became impossible. Platforms began to use automated systems that scan posts and flag those that appear to break the rules. These tools can work at enormous scale and never tire, but they are far from perfect. A system may wrongly remove a harmless joke while failing to detect a cleverly disguised threat. Language, humour and cultural context are difficult for machines to understand.

For this reason, most large platforms now use a combination of the two. Automated tools handle the easiest and most obvious cases, while human moderators deal with the difficult ones that require judgement. Even so, mistakes are common, and decisions can vary from one reviewer to another.

Moderation also raises wider questions. Who should decide what is acceptable, and according to whose values? Rules that seem reasonable in one country may appear unfair in another. As platforms continue to grow, the challenge of moderating them fairly, and at a vast scale, is only likely to become harder.

questions.q: Most of the material uploaded to platforms breaks the rules.
Early platforms depended mainly on human moderators.
Automated systems are more accurate than human moderators in every case.
Human moderators are now paid more than they were in the early days.
Why have some companies been criticised?
options: for failing to protect moderators' mental health

questions.q: According to the passage, automated tools struggle most with:
Most large platforms now use a ________ of automated tools and human reviewers.
Rules that seem reasonable in one ________ may seem unfair in another.

listening.title: A Lecture on Content Moderation
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I'll outline how online platforms try to keep their spaces safe, and why it's so difficult.

Let's start with scale. One major platform reports that users upload roughly five hundred hours of video every single minute. No team of humans could possibly watch all of that, so automation is essential.

But automation has limits. Consider sarcasm. A comment that looks aggressive on the surface might actually be a joke between friends. Machines frequently get this wrong, which is why human reviewers are still needed for the tricky cases.

There's also a human cost. Studies suggest that moderators who view distressing material for long periods are at higher risk of stress and burnout. Some companies now limit how long staff spend on the worst content and offer counselling, though critics say this isn't enough.

Finally, think about fairness. The same post might be acceptable in one culture and offensive in another. Platforms operating in dozens of countries must somehow apply consistent rules across very different societies.

For your essay this week, I'd like you to choose one of these three problems, scale, accuracy, or fairness, and suggest one realistic improvement. It's due next Friday.

reading.title: The Origins of the Jury
reading.passage:
The idea that ordinary people, rather than judges or officials, should decide whether someone is guilty of a crime is one of the oldest features of many legal systems. Today we call this group a jury, and in some countries it is regarded as a basic protection against unfair punishment. Yet the jury did not appear suddenly. It developed slowly over many centuries, and its early form looked very different from the one we know now.

In medieval England, a jury was not a group of strangers who listened to evidence. Instead, jurors were local people who were expected to know something about the case or the accused already. They were chosen precisely because they lived nearby and could speak about a person's character and reputation. In a sense, they acted more like witnesses than the neutral judges of fact we expect today.

Over time, this changed. As towns grew larger and communities became less close-knit, it became unrealistic to assume that jurors would personally know everyone involved. Courts began to expect jurors to base their decisions on evidence presented in the courtroom, not on private knowledge. The modern principle that a juror should arrive without firm opinions, and decide only on what is heard, slowly took hold.

The jury system has often been praised for placing power in the hands of citizens, but it has also faced criticism. Some argue that ordinary people lack the training to weigh complex evidence, especially in cases involving science or finance. Others reply that this very lack of expertise is a strength, because it forces lawyers to explain their arguments in clear, everyday language. Whatever its weaknesses, the jury remains a powerful symbol of the belief that justice should not be left to officials alone.

questions.q: Early English jurors were usually strangers to the accused.
Medieval jurors were expected to comment on a person's reputation.
The growth of towns made it harder for jurors to know everyone involved in a case.
Most modern countries have now abolished the jury system.
According to the passage, why did jurors change from witnesses into judges of fact?
options: Judges ordered the change by law

questions.q: One argument in favour of using ordinary people as jurors is that
options: they force lawyers to use clear language

questions.q: Today a juror is expected to arrive without firm ________ and decide only on what is heard.

CALLER: Yes, it's J-7-4-2-9.

CALLER: That's very clear. Thank you for your help.

reading.title: When Is a Promise a Contract?
reading.passage:
People make promises every day. We promise to meet a friend, to lend a book, or to help a neighbour move house. Most of these promises are simply part of social life, and if they are broken, the law takes no interest. Yet some promises are different. When certain conditions are met, a promise becomes a contract, and the law may step in to enforce it.

What turns an ordinary promise into a binding agreement? Lawyers usually point to a few key elements. There must be an offer made by one person and an acceptance by another. There must also be something called consideration, which means that each side gives or promises something of value. If I agree to sell you my bicycle for a sum of money, both of us are giving something, and a contract can form.

Another important condition is that both parties intend the agreement to have legal force. A casual remark between friends is usually not meant to be legally binding, even if money is mentioned. Business agreements, by contrast, are generally assumed to be serious. This difference in intention helps courts decide which promises deserve protection.

Not every agreement, however, will be upheld. If one person was forced or seriously misled into agreeing, a court may decide that no real contract existed. Likewise, agreements to do something illegal cannot be enforced, no matter how clearly they were made. In this way, contract law tries to balance two aims: respecting the freedom of people to make their own bargains, while refusing to support agreements that are unfair or harmful.

questions.q: The law treats every broken promise as a legal matter.
Consideration means that each side gives or promises something of value.
Business agreements are usually assumed to be legally serious.
Contract law was first developed by merchants in Italy.
Which of these is NOT listed as a key element of a contract?
A court may decide no real contract existed if a person was
Agreements to do something ________ cannot be enforced by the courts.
Contract law tries to respect people's freedom to make their own ________.

listening.title: A Gym Membership Agreement
listening.script:
STAFF: Welcome to FitZone. Are you here to sign up for a membership?
VISITOR: Yes, but I'd like to check the terms first. I've been caught by tricky contracts before.
STAFF: That's sensible. Let me explain. Our standard plan costs 45 dollars a month, and there's a one-off joining fee of 20 dollars.
VISITOR: And how long am I tied in for?
STAFF: The minimum term is 3 months. After that, you can cancel any time with 14 days' notice.
VISITOR: What if I move to another city?
STAFF: If you move more than 30 kilometres away, you can end the contract early at no extra cost. We just need proof of your new address.
VISITOR: That's fair. Is there a discount for paying yearly?
STAFF: Yes, if you pay for a full year up front, you get two months free, so you effectively pay for 10.
VISITOR: Good. And can I freeze my membership while on holiday?
STAFF: You can freeze it for up to 4 weeks each year. Just let us know in advance.
VISITOR: Perfect. I'll take the monthly plan for now.

reading.title: The Right to Be Forgotten
reading.passage:
In the past, an embarrassing mistake might be reported in a local newspaper and then quietly forgotten. The paper would yellow, and few people would ever read it again. Today, the situation is very different. Information posted online can remain available for years, easily found through a simple search. This permanence has led to a new debate in law and ethics: should people have a right to be forgotten?

The idea is that, under certain conditions, individuals can ask search engines or websites to remove links to old information about them. Supporters argue that people should not be defined forever by a single event, especially if it happened long ago or was never very serious. A person who made a minor mistake as a teenager, they say, should not have it appear at the top of every search decades later.

Critics, however, raise an important concern. Removing information, they argue, can come close to rewriting history. If powerful or wealthy individuals are able to erase awkward facts about their past, the public may lose access to information that is genuinely in its interest. Journalists in particular worry that the right could be used to hide wrongdoing rather than to protect ordinary privacy.

Most legal systems that recognise this right try to strike a balance. They allow removal in some cases, such as outdated or irrelevant personal details, while refusing it in others, such as matters of public importance. Deciding which is which is rarely simple, and reasonable people often disagree. As more of our lives are recorded online, courts are likely to face these difficult questions more and more often.

questions.q: Online information is generally harder to find than old newspaper reports.
Supporters believe people should not be defined forever by one past event.
Journalists worry the right could be used to hide wrongdoing.
The right to be forgotten was first introduced in Asia.
What is the main concern raised by critics?
options: Removing information can resemble rewriting history

questions.q: In which case are legal systems most likely to refuse removal?
Information posted online can remain available for years, easily found through a simple ________.
Most legal systems try to strike a ________ between removal and public interest.

listening.title: A Talk on Managing Your Online Records
listening.script: SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to today's session on managing your online records. Many of you have asked how to deal with old posts and search results, so let's cover the basics. First, remember that almost everything you share leaves a trace. Even a deleted post may survive in copies for some time. My first tip is to do a search of your own name at least once every 3 months. This way, you'll know what others can see. Second, check your privacy settings. On most platforms, these are buried under a menu, and the default is often set to public rather than private. Switching to private takes only about 5 minutes but makes a real difference. Third, if you find something harmful, you can submit a removal request. Be patient, though: companies may take up to 30 days to respond, and they will not always agree. Finally, think before you post. The strongest protection is simply not to share information you would later regret. If you remember just one thing from today, let it be this: prevention is far easier than removal.

reading.title: The Ethics of Self-Driving Cars
reading.passage:
Self-driving cars were once a dream of science fiction, but they are now being tested on real roads in several countries. Supporters promise a future with fewer accidents, since most crashes today are caused by human error such as tiredness or distraction. A machine, they argue, never falls asleep and never checks its phone. Yet handing control to a computer also raises difficult ethical questions that engineers cannot solve alone.

One famous problem involves unavoidable accidents. Imagine a car that suddenly faces a choice: it can swerve and risk the life of its passenger, or stay on course and risk the lives of several pedestrians. A human driver would react by instinct, with no time to think. A self-driving car, however, must be programmed in advance, which means that someone has to decide, calmly and beforehand, whose safety matters most.

These decisions are not only technical but deeply moral. Should a car always protect its passenger, since that is who bought it? Or should it aim to save the greatest number of people, even at the owner's expense? Different cultures may answer such questions differently, which makes a single global rule hard to imagine. A design considered fair in one country might seem unacceptable in another.

There is also the question of responsibility. If a self-driving car causes harm, who is to blame: the owner, the manufacturer, or the programmer who wrote the software? Traditional laws assume a human driver behind the wheel, and they do not fit neatly into this new situation. Until these legal and ethical issues are settled, many believe that fully driverless cars will remain limited, no matter how advanced the technology becomes.

questions.q: Most car crashes today are caused by mechanical faults.
A self-driving car's response to an accident must be decided in advance.
Different cultures may answer these moral questions differently.
Self-driving cars are already cheaper than ordinary cars.
Why is the 'unavoidable accident' problem harder for a machine than a human?
options: The choice must be programmed beforehand

questions.q: According to the passage, why is a single global rule hard to imagine?
options: Different cultures answer the questions differently

questions.q: Most crashes today are caused by human error such as tiredness or ________.
Traditional laws assume a human ________ behind the wheel.

TUTOR: And what was the biggest single worry?

TUTOR: Yes, responsibility again. That keeps coming up. For next week, I'd like a short essay of about 800 words on whether current laws are ready for these vehicles. Please submit it by Friday.

reading.title: Whistle-blowers and the Public Good
reading.passage:
Sometimes an employee discovers that the organisation they work for is doing something wrong, perhaps hiding dangerous faults in a product or wasting public money. They face a hard choice: stay silent and protect their job, or speak out and risk losing it. A person who chooses to reveal such wrongdoing is often called a whistle-blower, and society's attitude towards them has changed greatly over time.

In the past, whistle-blowers were frequently treated as traitors. Loyalty to one's employer was seen as a basic duty, and those who broke it could expect to be dismissed and to struggle to find work elsewhere. Today, by contrast, many countries have passed laws to protect such people, recognising that they often act in the public interest rather than for personal gain.

These laws usually try to set limits. A whistle-blower is generally protected only if they report a genuine concern, such as a danger to health or a serious crime, and if they follow proper channels where possible. Someone who leaks information simply out of anger, or who reveals secrets for money, may not receive the same protection. The aim is to encourage honest reporting while discouraging careless or selfish behaviour.

Even with such protection, speaking out remains difficult. Whistle-blowers may face pressure from colleagues, damage to their careers, and years of stress before any wrongdoing is confirmed. Supporters argue that this is exactly why strong legal protection matters: without it, few people would take the risk, and serious problems might never come to light. The treatment of whistle-blowers, they suggest, is a useful measure of how seriously a society takes honesty and accountability.

questions.q: In the past, whistle-blowers were often regarded as traitors.
Modern laws protect whistle-blowers in every situation without limits.
Someone who reveals secrets for money may not receive full protection.

options: Because they often act in the public interest
To reduce the number of employees

options: report a genuine concern through proper channels

questions.q: The aim of the laws is to encourage honest reporting while discouraging careless or ________ behaviour.

listening.title: An Advice Line for Workplace Concerns
listening.script:
ADVISER: Good afternoon, you've reached the Workplace Concerns advice line. How can I help you today?
CALLER: Hi. I think my company is hiding a safety problem, and I don't know what to do.
ADVISER: I understand, that's a serious matter. First, let me reassure you: anything you tell me stays confidential. Can I ask how long you've worked there?
CALLER: About 5 years.
ADVISER: Thank you. My advice is to keep a written record of what you've seen, with dates if you can. That will protect you later.
CALLER: Should I report it inside the company first?
ADVISER: Usually, yes. Most firms have an internal reporting procedure, and the law expects you to try it first in most cases. If nothing is done within 30 days, you can take the matter to an outside body.
CALLER: And could I lose my job for this?
ADVISER: You're protected by law, provided your concern is genuine. If you're dismissed for reporting, you can claim compensation. We also run a free workshop on this every Tuesday at 6 p.m.
CALLER: That sounds helpful. Thank you so much.

reading.title: The Rise of the School Library
reading.passage:
For much of the nineteenth century, schools in many countries owned very few books. A typical classroom might contain a single reading primer that pupils shared, copying out passages by hand. Reading materials were expensive, and printing was slow, so the idea of a dedicated room full of books for children seemed almost impossible. Education at the time focused on memorising rules rather than encouraging independent exploration of ideas.

The situation began to change as printing costs fell and reformers argued that pupils learned best when they could choose what to read. Early school libraries were often small and tightly controlled. Teachers selected every title, and borrowing was treated as a privilege rather than a right. Even so, these modest collections introduced many children to stories, history and science beyond the limits of their lessons. By the early twentieth century, a growing number of educators believed that a good library was as important as a good teacher.

Today the school library has expanded far beyond rows of printed books. Many now provide computers, audio recordings and quiet spaces for group work. Librarians help pupils judge whether the information they find online is reliable, a skill that did not exist a hundred years ago. Some schools have replaced physical books almost entirely with digital collections, although researchers warn that screen reading can reduce concentration for certain learners. Whatever form it takes, the library continues to do what its early supporters hoped: it gives young people the freedom to follow their own curiosity.

questions.q: Nineteenth-century classrooms usually contained large collections of books.
Falling printing costs contributed to the spread of school libraries.
In early school libraries, pupils were free to borrow any book they wished.
Most school libraries in the early twentieth century were larger than public libraries.
According to the passage, what new skill do modern librarians help pupils develop?
options: Judging the reliability of online information

questions.q: What concern do researchers raise about digital collections?
options: They take up too much space
Screen reading can reduce concentration for some learners

questions.q: Education in the nineteenth century focused on memorising ________ rather than independent exploration.
By the early twentieth century, many educators thought a good library was as important as a good ________.

listening.title: Joining the University Study Group
listening.script:
TUTOR: Hello, you must be the new student who wanted to join one of our study groups.

STUDENT: Yes, that's right. I heard they meet every week.

TUTOR: Most do. The history group meets on Tuesdays at four o'clock in Room 12. The science group meets on Thursdays.

STUDENT: I think the history one suits me better. How many people usually come?

TUTOR: Normally about eight, though it can rise to twelve before exams. Each session lasts ninety minutes.

STUDENT: Do I need to bring anything?

TUTOR: Just a notebook and the reading list, which you can download from the student portal. There's no fee, but you should sign up online first so we know the numbers.

STUDENT: And who leads the sessions?

TUTOR: A senior student called Maria leads the history group. She's very patient and explains things clearly. If you miss a week, just send her a short message.

STUDENT: That sounds perfect. I'll register tonight.

questions.q: The history group meets on ________ at four o'clock.

reading.title: Learning a Second Language as an Adult
reading.passage:
It is often said that children learn languages effortlessly while adults struggle. There is some truth in this, but the picture is more complicated than the popular belief suggests. Young children do seem to pick up pronunciation more naturally, partly because their brains are still highly flexible. However, adults bring advantages of their own. They already understand how language works, they can study grammar consciously, and they are better at organising their time.

Researchers have found that motivation plays an enormous role in adult learning. An adult who needs a language for work or who feels a strong personal connection to a culture will often progress faster than a bored teenager in a classroom. Regular, meaningful practice matters more than raw talent. Many successful adult learners set small daily goals, such as learning ten new words or holding a short conversation, instead of trying to master everything at once.

Technology has also changed how adults study. Mobile applications allow learners to practise during short breaks, and video calls make it possible to speak with native speakers thousands of kilometres away. Yet experts warn that apps alone are rarely enough. Real progress usually requires speaking with other people, making mistakes, and being corrected. The most effective approach combines convenient tools with genuine human interaction. In the end, age is far less important than patience, regular effort and a willingness to feel uncomfortable while learning.

questions.q: Children tend to acquire pronunciation more naturally than adults.
Adults have no advantages when learning a new language.
Motivation has little effect on how quickly adults learn.
Most adult learners give up within the first year.
According to the passage, what matters more than natural talent?
What do experts say about language-learning apps?
options: They are usually enough on their own
They are rarely enough by themselves

questions.q: Many adults set small daily goals, such as learning ten new ________.
The most effective approach combines convenient tools with genuine human ________.

listening.title: Booking a Language Class
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, City Language Centre. How can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to enrol in a Spanish course for beginners.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. We run beginner classes on Monday and Wednesday evenings, starting at six thirty.

CALLER: How long is each lesson?

RECEPTIONIST: Two hours, so you finish at half past eight. The full course runs for ten weeks.

CALLER: And what's the cost?

RECEPTIONIST: It's two hundred and forty dollars, which includes the textbook.

CALLER: That's reasonable. Is there a test before I start?

RECEPTIONIST: Not for beginners, but you'll do a short placement chat if you've studied before. For complete beginners we just sign you straight up.

CALLER: Great. What do I need to bring on the first day?

RECEPTIONIST: Just a pen and a notebook. We'll give you the textbook in class. Oh, and please arrive ten minutes early to collect your student card.

CALLER: Perfect, I'll be there.

reading.title: Why Homework Divides Opinion
reading.passage:
Few topics in education create as much disagreement as homework. Supporters argue that practising at home helps pupils remember what they learned in class. They claim that homework builds discipline, teaches children to manage their time, and gives parents a window into what their children are studying. For older students preparing for important examinations, regular practice at home is widely seen as essential.

Critics, however, point to a growing body of research suggesting that the benefits of homework are smaller than many people assume, especially for younger children. They argue that hours of repetitive tasks can cause stress, reduce time for sleep and play, and even make pupils dislike learning. Some studies have found almost no link between homework and better results in primary school, although the effect appears stronger as children grow older.

A number of schools have responded by changing their approach rather than abolishing homework completely. Instead of long worksheets, teachers may ask pupils to read for pleasure, interview a family member, or complete a small creative project. The aim is to make tasks meaningful and manageable rather than simply long. Many educators now agree that the quality of homework matters far more than the quantity. The debate is unlikely to end soon, but it has pushed schools to think more carefully about why they set tasks in the first place.

questions.q: Supporters believe homework can help children manage their time.
Research shows homework is equally beneficial for all age groups.
Some schools have completely abolished all forms of homework.
The effect of homework on results appears stronger for older children.
What problem do critics associate with too much homework?
options: Stress and less time for rest

options: By doubling the number of worksheets

listening.title: A Talk on Managing Study Time
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today I want to share a few practical tips for managing your study time. The first tip is simple: plan your week in advance. On Sunday evening, write down everything you need to finish and decide which day you'll do it.

The second tip is to study in short blocks. Research suggests that working for about twenty-five minutes and then taking a five-minute break keeps your mind fresh. People call this the Pomodoro method.

Third, find a quiet place with few distractions. Turn off your phone notifications, because checking messages can break your concentration for several minutes each time.

Fourth, start with the hardest task while your energy is high. Most students leave difficult work until late at night, which is the worst time to attempt it.

Finally, review your notes within twenty-four hours of a lesson. This simple habit can almost double how much you remember. If you follow even two of these tips, you should notice a real difference within a month.

reading.title: The Quiet Value of Mistakes
reading.passage:
In many classrooms, making a mistake feels like something to be ashamed of. Pupils fall silent rather than risk a wrong answer, and a single low mark can leave a lasting impression. Yet a growing number of teachers argue that mistakes are not the enemy of learning but one of its most powerful tools. When handled well, an error can reveal exactly what a student has misunderstood.

Research in psychology supports this idea. When learners try to answer a difficult question and get it wrong, their brains appear to pay closer attention to the correct answer when it finally arrives. In other words, struggling a little before being corrected can lead to stronger, longer-lasting memories. Simply being told the right answer, without any effort, tends to be forgotten more quickly.

Of course, this only works if students feel safe enough to take risks. In a harsh environment where errors are mocked, fear takes over and learning slows. Skilful teachers therefore create a culture in which mistakes are treated as ordinary steps on the road to understanding. They praise effort rather than only celebrating correct answers, and they encourage pupils to explain how they arrived at a wrong response. The message is clear: progress rarely follows a straight line, and the path to mastery is usually paved with errors that, far from being failures, are valuable lessons in disguise.

questions.q: Some teachers regard mistakes as a useful part of learning.
Being simply told the correct answer is remembered better than struggling first.
Fear of being mocked can slow down learning.
The author believes teachers should never give marks at all.
What does research in psychology suggest about getting an answer wrong?
options: It helps the brain attend to the correct answer

questions.q: What do skilful teachers praise, according to the passage?

listening.title: Library Orientation Tour
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome to the campus library. Let me give you a quick tour so you know where everything is. As you come in, the help desk is on your right, and that's where you can ask any question or report a problem.

NEW STUDENT: Where do I find the textbooks for my course?

GUIDE: Course textbooks are kept on the second floor in the reserve section. You can borrow them for three hours at a time so that everyone gets a turn.

NEW STUDENT: Only three hours? That's short.

GUIDE: It is, but ordinary books on the upper floors can be borrowed for two weeks. The first floor is the silent study area, so please keep your phone on silent there.

NEW STUDENT: Is there anywhere I can work with a group?

GUIDE: Yes, the group study rooms are in the basement. You can book one online for up to four hours. And printing is available on every floor; it costs ten pence per page.

NEW STUDENT: Thanks, that's really helpful.

reading.title: Learning Through Play
reading.passage:
For a long time, play and serious learning were seen as opposites. Play was something children did during breaks, while real education meant sitting quietly and listening. In recent decades, however, researchers studying young children have challenged this view. They argue that play is not a distraction from learning but one of the most natural ways in which children come to understand the world.

When children build with blocks, they are quietly exploring ideas about balance, shape and number. When they invent stories together, they practise language, cooperation and the ability to see a situation from another person's point of view. Because play feels enjoyable rather than forced, children are often willing to repeat an activity many times, and this repetition helps important skills become firmly established. The lessons learned this way can be surprisingly deep.

This does not mean that all play is equally useful, or that children never need direct teaching. Many experts recommend a balance, sometimes called guided play, in which an adult gently shapes an activity towards a learning goal while still allowing the child plenty of freedom. A teacher might join a game and ask thoughtful questions rather than simply giving instructions. The growing interest in play has encouraged some schools to redesign their early classrooms, replacing rows of desks with open spaces where curiosity can flourish. Far from being a waste of time, well-designed play may be among the most efficient forms of learning we have.

The passage states that children never need any direct teaching.

options: Language, cooperation and seeing other viewpoints

questions.q: What is 'guided play', according to the passage?
options: An adult gently shaping play towards a learning goal

reading.title: The Lost Wells of the Desert
reading.passage:
For thousands of years, people living in dry regions have depended on hidden sources of water. In a remote part of North Africa, archaeologists recently studied a network of ancient wells that once allowed a small community to survive far from any river. The wells were dug by hand into soft rock, sometimes reaching depths of more than twenty metres. Reaching water at such depths required both careful planning and a great deal of physical effort.

The team found that the wells were not placed at random. Instead, they followed the line of an underground channel where water naturally collected after rare storms. Local builders seem to have understood the shape of the land remarkably well, even though they had no maps or instruments. By cutting steps into the sides of some wells, they made it possible for people and animals to walk down to the water during dry months.

What surprised the researchers most was the system used to keep the water clean. Layers of small stones at the bottom of each well acted as a simple filter, trapping sand and dirt. Some wells were also covered with flat slabs to reduce evaporation and to stop animals from falling in. These features suggest that the community had a sophisticated understanding of how to manage a scarce and precious resource.

Although the settlement was eventually abandoned, possibly because the climate grew even drier, the wells remained partly usable for centuries. Travellers crossing the desert continued to rely on them long after the original builders had gone. Today the site offers valuable clues about how early people adapted to harsh environments without modern technology.

questions.q: Some of the wells were more than twenty metres deep.
The builders used maps to decide where to dig the wells.
The community traded water with neighbouring settlements.
What did the layers of small stones do?
options: Marked the depth of the water

questions.q: Why were some wells covered with flat slabs?
options: To reduce evaporation and protect animals

questions.q: Steps were cut into some wells so that people and ________ could reach the water.

reading.title: Reading the Past in Pottery
reading.passage:
When archaeologists dig at an ancient site, the most common objects they find are not gold or jewels but broken pieces of pottery. These fragments, known as sherds, may look unimportant, yet they can tell us a great deal about the people who once used them. Because clay is cheap and easily shaped, almost every household in the ancient world owned pots for cooking, storage and serving food.

The shape of a pot often reveals its purpose. A wide, shallow bowl was probably used for mixing or serving, while a tall jar with a narrow neck was ideal for storing oil or wine. The decoration can be just as informative. Patterns painted on the surface sometimes match styles found in distant regions, suggesting that goods, and perhaps ideas, travelled along trade routes.

Pottery is also useful for dating. Styles changed gradually over time, so experts can compare a new find with pieces whose age is already known. If a particular type of cup appears only in one short period, finding it in a layer of soil helps to fix the date of everything around it. This method is not perfect, but it provides a reliable starting point.

Modern science has added new tools to this old craft. By examining the chemical make-up of the clay, researchers can often identify exactly where it was dug from the ground. This means a single broken cup found far from home can reveal a long-distance connection that no written record mentions. In this way, the humble sherd continues to surprise the people who study it.

questions.q: Pottery is the most common type of object found at ancient sites.
Most ancient households could not afford pottery.
What does a tall jar with a narrow neck suggest about its use?
options: It was used for storing oil or wine

options: Styles can be compared with finds of known age

Just call security on extension three-three-two, and someone will come straight away. Any questions? No? Great, let's get started.

reading.title: The Builders of the Stone Circle
reading.passage:
On a windy plain in northern Europe stands a circle of large standing stones, raised by people who lived more than four thousand years ago. For a long time, visitors assumed that such monuments must have been built by a powerful king who forced others to work. Recent research, however, paints a different picture, one in which ordinary farming communities came together by choice.

The stones themselves are enormous, with the largest weighing as much as a small truck. Moving them was clearly a huge task, yet there is no sign of the kind of central authority that would normally organise such a project. Instead, archaeologists believe that several villages cooperated, each sending workers and food during the building season. The effort may have lasted many years, with families returning each summer.

Why the circle was built remains a matter of debate. Some experts argue that it was used to mark the changing seasons, since certain stones line up with the rising sun at particular times of year. Others suggest it was a meeting place where scattered communities gathered to trade, celebrate and settle disputes. These two ideas are not necessarily in conflict.

What seems certain is that the monument was important enough for people to invest enormous effort over generations. Building it would have required cooperation, shared knowledge and a strong sense of common purpose. In this sense, the stone circle is less a symbol of one ruler's power than a record of what a community could achieve when it worked together.

options: Certain stones line up with the rising sun

In the afternoon, there's a small visitor centre with a short film. Entry costs three pounds, but students get in for two. We should be back at college by about five o'clock. If you have any questions before the day, just email me. Don't forget your notebooks.

reading.title: Writing Before Paper
reading.passage:
Long before paper was invented, ancient peoples needed ways to keep records. In one early civilisation, scribes pressed marks into soft clay tablets using a cut reed. Once the marks were made, the tablet could be dried in the sun or baked in a fire, turning it into a hard and lasting object. Thousands of these tablets have survived, partly because clay does not rot in the way that paper does.

Most of the tablets are surprisingly ordinary. Rather than recording great battles or the words of kings, they list quantities of grain, numbers of animals and amounts owed by one person to another. In other words, writing seems to have been invented mainly to keep track of business, not to tell stories. Only later did people begin to use it for poetry, law and history.

Learning to write was difficult and took many years. The system used hundreds of different signs, so only a small number of people, mostly trained scribes, could read and write. This gave scribes an important position in society. They were employed by temples, rulers and wealthy traders, and their skills were highly valued.

Because the tablets are so detailed, they offer a remarkably clear view of daily life. We can read about the price of barley, the wages of workers and even complaints about poor-quality goods. These small everyday records, never meant to last, have become one of our richest sources of information about a world that vanished long ago.

questions.q: Clay tablets have survived partly because clay does not rot like paper.
Most surviving tablets record the words of kings and great battles.
According to the passage, why was writing first invented?
Why could only a small number of people read and write?
options: The system used hundreds of signs and took years to learn

questions.q: Scribes were valued and employed by temples and rulers.
Female scribes were as common as male scribes.
Scribes pressed marks into soft clay tablets using a cut ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Ancient Records
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to talk about how ancient societies kept written records, and why those records matter to us now. The earliest writing wasn't used for literature at all. Surprisingly, it was used for accounting, things like counting sheep or recording how much grain a family had stored. Scribes wrote on clay because it was cheap and easy to find. Once a tablet dried, it became very durable, which is why so many survive today, often in better condition than documents written on paper centuries later. Now, learning this writing system was no small task. A student might train for ten years before becoming a fully qualified scribe. As a result, literacy was rare, and scribes enjoyed real status. For your assignment, I'd like you to choose one tablet from the museum's online collection and write five hundred words on what it tells us about daily life. The deadline is the fifteenth of March. Please email me if you have trouble accessing the collection.

Why did scribes write on clay?
options: It was cheap and easy to find

questions.q: A student might train for ________ years to become a fully qualified scribe.
For the assignment, students must write ________ words.

reading.title: The Sunken Trading Ship
reading.passage:
Off the coast of a small Mediterranean island, divers discovered the remains of a wooden ship that sank more than two thousand years ago. The ship had been carrying a heavy cargo when it went down, probably during a sudden storm. Although the wooden hull had largely rotted away, the cargo lay scattered on the seabed, preserved by the cold, dark water.

Most of the cargo consisted of large clay jars once filled with wine and olive oil. By studying the shape of these jars, archaeologists could work out roughly where the ship had come from and where it might have been heading. The presence of goods from several different regions suggested that the vessel had stopped at more than one port before its final voyage.

Excavating a wreck under water is slow and expensive. Divers can only work for short periods, and every object must be carefully recorded before it is moved. To help with this, the team created a detailed map of the seabed, marking the exact position of each item. This information allows researchers to understand how the cargo had been arranged inside the ship.

The discovery is valuable because shipwrecks act like time capsules. Unlike a settlement, which may be used and changed over centuries, a wreck captures a single moment when the ship sank. Everything on board belongs to the same short period, giving an unusually clear picture of trade, taste and technology at one point in the ancient world.

questions.q: The ship probably sank during a sudden storm.
The wooden hull of the ship was found completely intact.
What did most of the cargo consist of?
options: Large clay jars of wine and oil

questions.q: What did the presence of goods from several regions suggest?
options: The ship had stopped at more than one port

questions.q: A shipwreck captures a single moment in time, unlike a settlement.
The crew of the ship survived the sinking.
The cargo was preserved by the cold, dark ________.
The team created a detailed ________ of the seabed to record each item's position.

listening.title: An Underwater Archaeology Talk
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thanks for coming to today's talk on underwater archaeology. People often imagine treasure chests full of gold, but the reality is rather different. Most of what we find is everyday cargo, like jars, pottery and tools. The real treasure, if you like, is the information these objects give us. Working under water is challenging. Divers can usually only stay down for about forty minutes at a time before they have to come back up. Visibility can be poor, and currents can move small objects, so everything has to be mapped before it's touched. A typical excavation might run over several summers, because you simply can't rush it. One of our most exciting recent finds was a cargo of wine jars, which helped us trace an ancient trade route between two islands. If you'd like to get involved, we run a beginners' course every June. The fee is eighty dollars, and no diving experience is needed to attend the first session.

options: Everyday cargo like jars and tools

questions.q: Divers can usually stay down for about ________ minutes at a time.

reading.title: The Comeback of Vinyl Records
reading.passage:
For decades, the vinyl record seemed destined to disappear. When the compact disc arrived in the 1980s, and later when music moved online, many people assumed that the old format would vanish completely. Factories that once pressed millions of records closed down, and shops cleared their shelves to make room for newer products. Yet in recent years, something surprising has happened: vinyl has returned, and sales now grow steadily each year.

Several reasons explain this revival. Some listeners say that records produce a warmer, richer sound than digital files, although audio engineers continue to debate whether this difference is real or simply imagined. Others enjoy the physical experience of owning music. A vinyl album comes with large artwork, printed lyrics, and a sense of value that a streamed track cannot offer. Younger buyers, in particular, seem attracted to the ritual of placing a record on a turntable and listening to an entire album from start to finish.

The industry has responded quickly to this demand. New pressing plants have opened in Europe and North America, and popular artists now release special vinyl editions alongside their digital albums. However, manufacturers face a problem: the machines used to press records are old, and few companies still build new ones. Because of this shortage, waiting times for production can stretch to many months.

Despite these difficulties, the future of vinyl looks bright. The format may never again dominate the market as it once did, but it has clearly found a loyal audience willing to pay for a slower, more deliberate way of enjoying music.

questions.q: People predicted that vinyl records would die out after the introduction of the compact disc.
Audio engineers all agree that vinyl sounds better than digital files.
Vinyl records are now cheaper to produce than digital downloads.
According to the passage, why do younger buyers enjoy vinyl?
options: They like the ritual of playing a whole album
They collect records to sell later

questions.q: What problem do vinyl manufacturers currently face?
A vinyl album includes large artwork, printed ________, and a sense of value.
Because of the machine shortage, production waiting times can last many ________.
The author believes vinyl will once again dominate the music market.

listening.title: Joining a Community Choir
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good evening, you've reached the Riverside Community Choir. How can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to join. Could you tell me when you meet?

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We rehearse every Thursday from seven until nine in the evening, at the Maple Street Hall.

CALLER: Lovely. Do I need any singing experience?

RECEPTIONIST: Not at all. We welcome complete beginners, and there's no audition to join.

CALLER: That's a relief. How much does it cost?

RECEPTIONIST: The membership fee is forty pounds for the whole term, and that includes your sheet music.

CALLER: Great. And is there a performance at the end?

RECEPTIONIST: Yes, we hold a concert in December at the town theatre. Tickets sell quickly, so do invite your family early.

CALLER: Wonderful. Should I bring anything to the first rehearsal?

RECEPTIONIST: Just a water bottle and a notebook. Oh, and please arrive about ten minutes early so we can register you.

CALLER: Perfect. I'll see you on Thursday, then.

reading.title: How the Brain Responds to Rhythm
reading.passage:
Almost everyone has felt the urge to tap a foot or nod along to a catchy beat. This response is so natural that scientists have long been curious about why rhythm affects us so powerfully. Recent research suggests that the human brain is unusually sensitive to regular patterns of sound, and that this sensitivity may be linked to movement.

When we hear a steady beat, areas of the brain that control motion become active, even if we sit perfectly still. In other words, simply listening to music prepares the body to move. Researchers believe this connection explains why rhythm can feel almost impossible to resist. Some studies have also found that babies only a few months old will move more energetically when they hear music than when they hear ordinary speech.

Rhythm does more than encourage movement, however. Doctors have discovered that a steady beat can help people who have difficulty walking. Patients recovering from certain illnesses often find that stepping in time to music allows them to walk more smoothly and confidently. This technique, sometimes used in hospitals, shows that rhythm has practical as well as emotional value.

Scientists do not yet fully understand why music developed in human societies. Some argue that it strengthened social bonds, helping early groups work and celebrate together. Others suggest it began simply as a pleasurable side effect of the brain's love of patterns. Whatever the explanation, it is clear that our relationship with rhythm runs deep, shaping not only how we feel but also how we move.

questions.q: Scientists have recently become interested in why rhythm affects people.
Brain areas linked to movement can become active even when a person is sitting still.
Babies move more when they hear music than when they hear speech.
The rhythm technique used in hospitals is more effective than ordinary physiotherapy.
According to the passage, why might rhythm help some patients?
options: It helps them walk more smoothly
It reduces their need for medicine

questions.q: What is one suggested reason that music developed?
Listening to a steady beat prepares the body to ________.
Some scientists think music began as a side effect of the brain's love of ________.

listening.title: Booking a Practice Room
listening.script:
STAFF: Hello, music centre front desk, how can I help you?

STUDENT: Hi there, I'd like to book a practice room for the weekend.

STAFF: Sure. Which day would suit you?

STUDENT: Saturday morning, if possible.

STAFF: Let me check. We have a room free from ten o'clock. Would an hour be enough?

STUDENT: Actually, could I have two hours? I'm preparing for an exam.

STAFF: No problem, so that's ten until twelve. Which instrument will you be using?

STUDENT: The piano, please. Does the room have one?

STAFF: Yes, Room Four has a grand piano. The charge is fifteen dollars an hour for students.

STUDENT: That's fine. Do I need to bring my own music stand?

STAFF: No, every room has one, but please bring your own headphones if you want to record.

STUDENT: Got it. And how do I get in on the day?

STAFF: Just give your name at reception, and we'll hand you the key. Don't forget your student card.

reading.title: The Rise of Street Performers
reading.passage:
In busy city centres around the world, it is common to see musicians, dancers, and other entertainers performing in public spaces. Known as buskers, these street performers have a long history, but their role in modern cities is changing in interesting ways.

In the past, busking was often viewed with suspicion. Some authorities treated street performers as little more than beggars, and in certain cities the practice was banned altogether. Today, however, many local governments take a different attitude. They recognise that lively streets attract visitors and create a pleasant atmosphere, so they now encourage performers rather than driving them away.

Several cities have introduced official schemes to manage busking. Performers must apply for a permit, audition before a panel, and follow rules about noise and location. In return, they gain the right to perform in popular spots without fear of being moved on. Supporters argue that such schemes raise standards and protect both performers and the public. Critics, on the other hand, worry that too many rules remove the spontaneous spirit that makes street performance special.

For the performers themselves, busking can be a difficult way to earn a living. Income depends heavily on the weather, the time of day, and the generosity of passers-by. Even so, many enjoy the freedom it offers. Some treat busking as a stepping stone towards a professional career, while others simply love the direct connection with an audience that a concert hall cannot provide. Whatever their reasons, street performers continue to bring colour and surprise to the cities they call home.

questions.q: In the past, some cities completely banned street performing.
Most modern city governments still treat buskers as beggars.
Official busking schemes usually require performers to audition.
Cities with busking schemes earn more tourist income than cities without them.
Why do critics dislike strict busking rules?
According to the passage, a performer's income depends on all of the following EXCEPT:
Under official schemes, performers must apply for a ________.
Some performers see busking as a ________ towards a professional career.

listening.title: Volunteering at a Music Festival
listening.script:
ORGANISER: Thanks for offering to volunteer at the festival. Let me run through the details.

VOLUNTEER: Great, I'm really looking forward to it.

ORGANISER: The festival runs over the first weekend of August. Volunteers work one shift of six hours each day.

VOLUNTEER: That sounds manageable. What kind of jobs will I be doing?

ORGANISER: Most volunteers help at the entrance, checking tickets and giving out programmes. Some work at the information tent instead.

VOLUNTEER: I'd prefer the information tent, if that's all right.

ORGANISER: Noted. Now, all volunteers receive a free meal voucher and a festival T-shirt.

VOLUNTEER: Lovely. Do I need to arrive early?

ORGANISER: Yes, please report to the staff gate by eight thirty in the morning. Bring your confirmation email and some form of identification.

VOLUNTEER: Will there be training beforehand?

ORGANISER: There's a short briefing online the week before. It lasts about thirty minutes and covers safety procedures.

VOLUNTEER: Perfect. I'll watch it and see you in August.

reading.title: Music Education in Schools
reading.passage:
Music has been part of school life for centuries, yet its place in the modern curriculum is far from secure. In many countries, lessons in singing and playing instruments were once considered essential. Today, however, as schools focus increasingly on subjects such as mathematics and science, music is sometimes pushed to the edge of the timetable, or removed altogether.

Those who defend music education point to a growing body of evidence. Studies suggest that learning an instrument can improve a child's concentration, patience, and ability to work in a team. Playing in an orchestra or a band, for example, teaches young people to listen carefully to others and to coordinate their actions. Some researchers also claim that musical training supports skills used in other subjects, such as recognising patterns and managing time, although the strength of this link is still debated.

Critics do not deny that music has value, but they raise practical concerns. Instruments and qualified teachers are expensive, and schools with limited budgets must make difficult choices. In poorer areas, children may have little access to music lessons at all, which means that the benefits are not shared equally. This inequality troubles many educators.

Several solutions have been suggested. Some schools lend instruments to pupils who cannot afford their own, while others partner with local orchestras to bring professional musicians into the classroom. A number of governments now fund national programmes designed to give every child at least some experience of making music. Whether such efforts can survive future budget cuts, however, remains uncertain.

questions.q: Music has only recently become part of school education.
Music is sometimes removed from the school timetable.
Researchers fully agree that musical training improves performance in other subjects.
Children in poorer areas perform worse in music exams than wealthier children.
According to the passage, what does playing in an orchestra teach young people?
What practical concern do critics raise about music education?
Some schools lend ________ to pupils who cannot afford their own.
Certain schools partner with local ________ to bring musicians into class.

listening.title: A Lecture on the History of the Guitar
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning, everyone. Today I'll give a short introduction to the history of the guitar, one of the most popular instruments in the world.

The guitar's ancestors date back thousands of years, but the instrument we recognize today took shape in Spain during the nineteenth century. A craftsman named Antonio Torres developed a larger body that produced a fuller, louder sound. His design became the standard for what we now call the classical guitar.

The next great change came in the twentieth century, with the arrival of the electric guitar. By attaching small devices called pickups, makers could send the sound through an amplifier. This allowed guitarists to play far more loudly, which proved essential as bands performed in larger venues.

Electric guitars quickly became central to popular music. By the nineteen sixties, they defined the sound of rock and roll, and they remain hugely influential today. Yet the acoustic guitar has never disappeared. Many musicians still prefer its warm, natural tone for quieter songs.

In your reading this week, please focus on chapter three, which explains how the materials used to build guitars affect their sound. We'll discuss it in our next session.

questions.q: Where did the modern classical guitar take shape?
Antonio Torres developed a larger ________ that produced a fuller sound.
Electric guitars use small devices called ________ to send sound to an amplifier.
Why was the louder electric guitar important?
By the nineteen ________, electric guitars defined the sound of rock and roll.
For next time, students should focus on chapter ________ of the reading.

speaking.q: Let's talk about learning. Did you ever learn to play a musical instrument when you were younger? If so, what was it like; if not, would you like to learn one now, and why?

writing.q:
Some people think that governments should spend money supporting traditional music, while others believe public money should be spent on more important things, such as healthcare and education. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Business of Music Streaming
reading.passage:
Two decades ago, most people bought music as physical objects: a CD in a shop or, earlier still, a record or cassette. Today, the majority simply press play on a streaming service. For a small monthly fee, listeners gain instant access to millions of songs. This shift has transformed not only how we listen but also how the entire industry earns its money.

For record companies, streaming has brought welcome relief. After years of falling sales caused by illegal downloading, revenue has begun to climb again. Streaming services pay the companies each time a song is played, and although each play earns only a tiny sum, the huge number of plays adds up to significant income. As a result, many large labels now report healthy profits.

For individual artists, however, the picture is more complicated. The amount earned per play is extremely small, and the money is divided among record companies, songwriters, and performers before any reaches the artist. Established stars with millions of fans can still earn a great deal, but lesser-known musicians often struggle to make a living from streaming alone. Many depend instead on concerts and merchandise to survive.

The rise of streaming has also changed the kind of music that gets made. Because services often reward songs that are played frequently, some writers now create shorter tracks with catchy openings, designed to grab attention within seconds. Critics fear that this pressure encourages artists to follow formulas rather than take creative risks. Supporters reply that listeners, not algorithms, ultimately decide what becomes popular, and that good music will always find an audience.

questions.q: Twenty years ago, most people bought music as physical objects.
Record company revenue fell because of illegal downloading before streaming arrived.
Each individual play of a song earns artists a large amount of money.
Streaming services pay songwriters more than they pay performers.
Why do many lesser-known musicians rely on concerts and merchandise?
options: Streaming alone pays them too little

questions.q: How has streaming affected the kind of music made?
Streaming services pay companies each time a song is ________.
Supporters argue that listeners, not ________, decide what becomes popular.

listening.title: A Talk on Planning a School Concert
listening.script:
TEACHER: Right, let's go over the plan for our end-of-year concert. I want everyone to know their tasks.

First, the concert will be held in the main hall on the twenty-second of June, starting at seven in the evening. Please make sure your families save the date.

Now, we need several teams. The first team will look after the stage, setting up chairs, music stands, and microphones. The second team will manage the lighting, and the third will sell refreshments during the interval.

Tickets go on sale next Monday. They cost five pounds for adults and are free for children under twelve. All the money we raise will go towards new instruments for the school.

Rehearsals will take place every Wednesday after class, in the music room. Please do not miss them, as we have a lot of pieces to prepare. If you cannot attend, let me know in advance.

Finally, on the night itself, everyone should arrive by six o'clock to get ready. Wear black trousers and a white shirt. Any questions can wait until the end.

Source           | 2015 | 2022
Physical sales   | 40%  | 14%

reading.title: The Return of the Tram
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, the electric tram was seen as a relic of the past. In many European and North American cities, tram lines were torn up during the 1950s and 1960s to make room for cars and buses, which were then regarded as cleaner and more flexible. Yet in recent decades the tram has made a surprising comeback, and dozens of cities have reopened or extended their networks.

The main reason for this revival is concern about traffic and air quality. As city centres became choked with cars, planners began searching for ways to move large numbers of people without adding to pollution. Modern trams run on electricity and produce no exhaust fumes at street level. A single tram can carry as many passengers as several buses, which helps to reduce the total number of vehicles on the road.

Trams also bring economic benefits. Studies in France suggest that property values often rise along new tram routes, and shops near stops report higher numbers of customers. Because the rails are fixed and visible, investors tend to trust that the service will remain in place for many years, unlike a bus route that can be cancelled at short notice.

There are drawbacks, however. Building a tram line is expensive, and the construction work can disrupt traffic for months. Some critics argue that buses powered by electricity could deliver similar benefits at a lower cost. Despite these objections, many city governments continue to invest in trams, viewing them as a symbol of a modern, environmentally friendly city.

questions.q: Trams were removed from many cities in the mid-twentieth century.
Modern trams release exhaust fumes at street level.
One tram can carry roughly the same number of people as several buses.
Tram drivers in France earn higher wages than bus drivers.
According to the passage, why do investors trust tram routes more than bus routes?
options: The rails are fixed and unlikely to be removed

questions.q: What is one drawback of trams mentioned in the passage?

Critics argue that electric ________ could deliver similar benefits more cheaply.

listening.title: Joining a City Bike Scheme
listening.script:
ASSISTANT: Good morning, you've reached the City Bike membership desk. How can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to sign up for the public bike scheme, please.
ASSISTANT: Of course. Can I take your name first?
CALLER: It's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.
ASSISTANT: Thank you. And which type of membership would you like? We have a daily pass for three pounds, a monthly pass for eighteen pounds, or an annual pass for ninety pounds.
CALLER: I think the monthly one suits me best.
ASSISTANT: Good choice. The first thirty minutes of every trip are free, and after that it's one pound per half hour. The nearest station to the city centre is on Market Street.
CALLER: Perfect. How do I unlock a bike?
ASSISTANT: You'll receive a code by text message. Just enter it on the keypad at the docking station. Remember to return the bike before midnight, or there's a small late fee.
CALLER: Got it. Thank you very much.

reading.title: Designing Streets for People
reading.passage:
Traditionally, city streets were designed mainly for vehicles. Wide roads, fast traffic and large car parks shaped the way towns grew. In recent years, however, a new idea has gained popularity among planners: the street should belong to people first and cars second. This approach is sometimes called 'people-friendly' design.

One common feature of such design is the widening of pavements. When footpaths are broad, pedestrians feel safer and are more likely to walk rather than drive. Cafes can place tables outside, and street performers can attract crowds. Narrowing the space for cars also tends to slow traffic, which reduces the risk of accidents.

Another feature is the planting of trees and the creation of small green spaces. Trees provide shade in summer and make a street more pleasant to spend time in. Research suggests that greener streets encourage shoppers to stay longer, which can benefit local businesses. Some cities have even removed parking spaces to plant gardens, a decision that often angers drivers at first but is later welcomed by residents.

Not everyone agrees with these changes. Shop owners sometimes worry that removing parking will drive away customers who arrive by car. Delivery companies complain that narrow streets make their work harder. Planners respond that the long-term gains in health and community life outweigh these difficulties. As more cities experiment with people-friendly streets, the debate over how to balance the needs of cars and people is likely to continue for many years.

questions.q: In the past, streets were planned mainly with vehicles in mind.
Wider pavements tend to discourage people from walking.
Some cities have replaced parking spaces with gardens.
Most shop owners now support the removal of all car parks.
According to the passage, what effect do trees have on shoppers?
options: They encourage shoppers to stay longer
They reduce the number of shops
They block the view of stores

questions.q: What is one complaint made by delivery companies?
options: Narrow streets make their work harder

questions.q: Narrowing the space for cars tends to ________ traffic.
Planners say the long-term gains in health and ________ life outweigh the difficulties.

listening.title: A Walk Through the Old Quarter
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to our walking tour of the Old Quarter. The whole route takes about ninety minutes, and we'll stop several times along the way. Please keep to the footpath, as some streets still have light traffic. Our first stop is the Market Square, which was built in eighteen forty. Notice the fountain in the centre; it was a gift from a local merchant. From there we'll walk down Baker Lane, one of the narrowest streets in the city, only two metres wide in places. Halfway along you'll see a small bakery that has been open for over a hundred years. Next we'll reach the river bridge, a popular spot for photographs. If you look to the left, you can see the clock tower, which chimes every hour. Finally, we'll finish at the Garden Park, where you can rest and buy refreshments. The cafe there closes at five, so please allow time. Are there any questions before we set off?

It has been open for over a hundred years
It only sells bread on weekends

Describe a place in your town that you enjoy spending time in. You should say:
- where it is

and explain why you enjoy spending time in this place.
