reading.title: The Problem of Urban Noise
reading.passage:
In busy cities, noise is everywhere. Traffic, construction work, sirens and crowds all add to a constant background hum that many people barely notice. Yet researchers warn that this noise is more than a simple annoyance. Long exposure to high levels of sound can affect sleep, raise stress and even harm the heart over many years.

Road traffic is usually the largest single source of noise in a city. Cars, lorries and motorcycles produce sound from their engines and from the contact between their tyres and the road. At higher speeds, tyre noise becomes the main problem. This is one reason why some cities have lowered speed limits in residential areas, hoping to make streets quieter as well as safer.

Various solutions have been tried. Barriers along motorways can block some of the sound from reaching nearby homes. Special road surfaces absorb noise rather than reflecting it. Quieter electric vehicles may also help, although they are not silent, since much of their sound comes from the tyres rather than the engine.

City planners increasingly treat quiet as a resource worth protecting. Some cities have created 'quiet zones', often in parks or gardens, where people can escape the noise for a while. Measuring noise across a whole city is difficult, but new sensors now allow planners to map the loudest areas and target them for improvement. As populations grow, managing urban noise is likely to become an ever more important task.

questions.q: Many people stop noticing the constant background noise of a city.
Electric vehicles make absolutely no sound.
At higher speeds, tyre noise becomes the main source of traffic sound.
Quiet zones are more popular with older residents than with young people.
Why have some cities lowered speed limits in residential areas?
options: To make streets quieter and safer
To raise more money from fines

questions.q: What do special road surfaces do, according to the passage?
options: Absorb noise rather than reflecting it

questions.q: Long exposure to high levels of sound can affect ________ and raise stress.
New ________ allow planners to map the loudest areas of a city.

listening.title: A Lecture on City Noise
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we're looking at noise pollution in cities and why it matters more than people often think. First, let's define the problem. Sound is measured in units called decibels. A quiet library is around forty decibels, while a busy road can reach eighty or more. Above eighty-five decibels, long exposure can begin to damage hearing. Now, the biggest source of city noise is road traffic, accounting for about seventy percent of the total in most surveys. The second source is construction, followed by aircraft near airports. Researchers have found that people living on noisy streets report poorer sleep and higher stress levels. One famous study followed residents for ten years and found a clear link between traffic noise and heart problems. So what can be done? Lower speed limits help, as does better road surfacing. Planting trees has a small effect, mostly psychological. In the next session, we'll examine how three cities have tried to reduce noise, and whether their methods actually worked.

questions.q: Sound is measured in units called ________.
A quiet library is around ________ decibels.
Above how many decibels can long exposure damage hearing?
Road traffic accounts for about ________ percent of city noise.
What was found by the study that followed residents for ten years?
options: A link between noise and better sleep
A link between traffic noise and heart problems
No effect from noise at all
A link between noise and longer life

questions.q: Planting trees has a mostly ________ effect.

speaking.q: Let's discuss noise in modern life. Why do you think some people are more sensitive to noise than others? Do you think city governments should do more to control noise pollution, and how might they do it?

writing.q:
The table below shows the average daytime noise levels (in decibels) recorded in four areas of a city in 2000 and 2020.

Area | 2000 | 2020
City centre | 72 | 78
Residential | 58 | 62
Park | 45 | 44
Industrial zone | 80 | 75

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Long exposure to loud noise can damage hearing.
Constant noise is more than a simple annoyance.
Traffic is the main source of city noise.
Barriers along the motorway block some of the sound.
Special surfaces absorb noise rather than reflecting it.
Hard walls reflect sound back into the street.
Speed limits are lower in residential areas.
Planners treat quiet as a valuable resource.

reading.title: Cities Built Around Water
reading.passage:
Throughout history, many great cities have grown up beside rivers, lakes or the sea. Water offered early settlers fresh drinking supplies, fish to eat and an easy way to move goods. Before roads and railways were built, a boat could carry far heavier loads than any cart pulled by horses. For this reason, ports such as London, Venice and Shanghai became centres of trade and wealth.

Water also shaped the appearance of these cities. Venice, built on a group of small islands, replaced ordinary streets with canals, and its citizens travelled by boat rather than on foot or by carriage. Even today, goods in the old centre are delivered by water. In many river cities, the riverside became the most valuable land, lined with warehouses, markets and grand buildings.

Yet living close to water brings dangers as well as benefits. Floods have destroyed homes and businesses in countless cities, and the risk is growing as sea levels rise. Some cities have built high walls and barriers to hold back the water, while others are exploring softer methods, such as creating wetlands that can soak up floodwater naturally.

In modern times, old industrial waterfronts have often been transformed. Disused docks and factories have been turned into parks, museums and apartments, attracting visitors and new residents. Planners now see the waterfront not only as a route for trade but also as a place for leisure and culture, a change that reflects the shifting role of water in city life.

questions.q: Before roads and railways, boats could carry heavier loads than horse-drawn carts.
In Venice, goods in the old centre are still delivered by water today.
The risk of flooding in coastal cities is decreasing.
Venice attracts more tourists than any other city in Europe.
What is one 'softer' method some cities use against floods?
options: Creating wetlands to soak up water

questions.q: How have many old industrial waterfronts been changed?
options: Turned into parks, museums and apartments

questions.q: In many river cities, the ________ became the most valuable land.
Planners now see the waterfront as a place for leisure and ________.

listening.title: Planning a Harbour Festival
listening.script: ORGANISER: Right, let's go through the plan for the Harbour Festival. The event runs over the weekend, from Saturday the fifteenth to Sunday the sixteenth of June. We're expecting around twelve thousand visitors across the two days. The main stage will be set up near the old lighthouse, and music starts at eleven each morning. Now, parking is limited, so we're strongly encouraging people to arrive by ferry. The ferry from the north pier runs every twenty minutes and costs four pounds return. Food stalls will line the eastern quay, and there'll be a craft market in the warehouse building. One thing to remember: the firework display takes place on Saturday only, at nine in the evening, so we need extra safety staff that night. Volunteers should collect their badges from the information tent, which opens at eight in the morning. Any questions about timing should go to me directly. Let's make this the best festival yet.

questions.q: The festival runs from Saturday the ________ to Sunday the sixteenth of June.
Organisers expect around ________ visitors over the two days.
Music starts at ________ each morning.
The ferry from the north pier costs ________ pounds return.
When does the firework display take place?
options: Saturday at nine in the evening

questions.q: Volunteers collect their badges from the ________ tent.

speaking.q: Let's talk about rivers and water. Is there a river, lake or sea near where you live? Do you enjoy spending time near water, and what do you usually do there?

writing.q:
Many old industrial areas near rivers and harbours are now being turned into housing, parks and cultural spaces. Some people think this is a positive development, while others worry that it pushes out local communities and traditional industries.

Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

Boats carried goods between distant ports.

reading.title: The Rise of the Suburb
reading.passage:
In the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, millions of people moved out of crowded city centres and into the suburbs, the residential areas that surround a city. The growth of the suburb was made possible above all by transport. First the railway, and later the private car, allowed people to live far from where they worked and still travel into the centre each day.

For many families, the suburb offered an attractive way of life. Houses there were larger and cheaper than in the city, and most came with a garden. The air was cleaner, the streets were quieter, and children had more space to play. Advertisements of the time often presented the suburb as a healthy escape from the dirt and noise of the industrial city.

However, the spread of suburbs has brought problems of its own. Because homes are spread over a wide area, people often have little choice but to drive, which increases traffic and pollution. Shops and services may be far apart, and those without a car can feel isolated. Some critics argue that suburbs lack the lively street life found in older neighbourhoods.

In response, planners have begun to rethink suburban design. New developments often place homes, shops and workplaces closer together, so that residents can walk or cycle for daily needs. Better bus and train links are being added to reduce car use. Whether these changes can overcome the deep car dependence of older suburbs remains an open question, but the suburb itself seems certain to remain a central feature of modern life.

questions.q: The growth of suburbs was made possible mainly by improvements in transport.
Houses in the suburbs were generally more expensive than those in the city.
Advertisements often presented the suburb as a healthy escape from the city.
More women than men chose to move to the suburbs.
Why do suburban residents often have little choice but to drive?
options: Cars are free in the suburbs
Homes are spread over a wide area

questions.q: How are planners rethinking suburban design?
options: By placing homes, shops and workplaces closer together

questions.q: Those without a car in the suburbs can feel ________.
Better bus and train ________ are being added to reduce car use.

listening.title: A Talk on Suburban Growth
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thanks for coming to today's talk on how suburbs have changed. Let me start with some numbers. In nineteen hundred, only about fifteen percent of our city's population lived in the suburbs. By two thousand, that figure had risen to nearly sixty percent. The single most important factor was transport. The first suburban railway opened in eighteen eighty, cutting the journey to the city centre from two hours on foot to just twenty-five minutes by train. Later, as cars became affordable, suburbs spread even further out. But this growth had costs. Today, the average suburban household owns two cars and makes around ten car trips a day. Air quality has suffered, and traffic congestion is now a daily complaint. The good news is that newer suburbs are being designed differently, with shops within walking distance and frequent bus services. In our next meeting, we'll look at one such suburb in detail and ask whether it truly reduces car use.

questions.q: In 1900, about ________ percent of the population lived in the suburbs.
By 2000, the figure had risen to nearly ________ percent.
The first suburban railway opened in ________.
How long did the train journey to the city centre take?

Newer suburbs are designed with shops within ________ distance.

speaking.q: Let's discuss where people choose to live. What are the advantages and disadvantages of living in the suburbs compared with the city centre? Do you think more people will choose to live in city centres in the future, and why?

The table below shows the percentage of a city's population living in different areas in 1950, 1985 and 2020.

Area | 1950 | 1985 | 2020
City centre | 45% | 30% | 35%
Inner suburbs | 35% | 38% | 36%
Outer suburbs | 15% | 27% | 24%
Rural fringe | 5% | 5% | 5%

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: They moved from the city to a quiet suburb.
The suburb is a purely residential area.

reading.title: Reading the Clouds
reading.passage:
Long before satellites and computer models, people learned to predict the weather by watching the sky. Clouds, in particular, offered useful clues about what the coming hours might bring. Even today, knowing how to read clouds remains a practical skill for sailors, farmers and hikers who cannot always rely on a phone signal.

Clouds are usually grouped according to their height and shape. High, thin clouds made of ice crystals, called cirrus, often appear as delicate streaks across a blue sky. On their own they are harmless, but when they thicken and spread, they can signal that rain is approaching within a day or so. Lower, puffy clouds known as cumulus generally indicate fair weather, though if they grow tall and dark they may develop into storm clouds.

The most dramatic of all are cumulonimbus clouds, which can rise several kilometres into the atmosphere. These towering formations are linked to heavy rain, thunder and sometimes hail. Experienced observers watch their shape carefully: a flat, anvil-like top is a strong warning that a thunderstorm is near.

Of course, cloud-watching has limits. It tells us little about conditions far away, and modern forecasts combine many sources of data to achieve accuracy. Yet learning the language of clouds still connects us to the sky in a direct and rewarding way.

questions.q: People predicted weather from the sky before modern technology existed.
Cirrus clouds always bring rain within a few hours.
Cumulonimbus clouds are the most common type seen over oceans.
What does a flat, anvil-shaped cloud top warn of?
Which clouds usually indicate fair weather?
Cirrus clouds are made of ice ________.
Cumulonimbus clouds can rise several ________ into the atmosphere.
Modern forecasts rely on more than one source of information.

listening.title: Joining a Weather Club
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, you've reached the Riverside Weather Club. How can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to sign up as a new member, please.
RECEPTIONIST: Wonderful. Can I take your name?
CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Hart. That's H-A-R-T.
RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. And which membership would you like? We have standard at fifteen pounds a year, or family at twenty-five.
CALLER: I'll take the standard one, just for myself.
RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. Members receive a monthly newsletter and access to our weather station data. We also run a session on the first Saturday of every month.
CALLER: What time does that start?
RECEPTIONIST: It begins at ten o'clock and usually lasts two hours. We meet at the community hall on Mill Lane.
CALLER: Great. Do I need to bring anything?
RECEPTIONIST: Just a notebook. We provide instruments for measuring rainfall and temperature.
CALLER: Perfect. And how do I pay?
RECEPTIONIST: You can pay online once I email you a link. What's your address?
CALLER: It's danhart@email.com.
RECEPTIONIST: Got it. I'll send that over today.

Average rainy days per month

vocab.example: Scientists can now predict storms several days in advance.

Modern models improve the accuracy of forecasts.
Learning about clouds is a rewarding hobby.

reading.title: The Power of Wind
reading.passage:
Wind is simply air in motion, yet it has shaped landscapes, powered ships and, more recently, generated electricity. The basic cause of wind is the uneven heating of the Earth's surface by the sun. Warm air rises and cooler air rushes in to replace it, creating the movement we feel as a breeze or a gale.

Different regions experience characteristic winds. Coastal areas, for example, often feel a sea breeze during the day, when the land heats faster than the water. At night the pattern reverses, and a land breeze blows out towards the sea. These local winds are usually gentle, but on a global scale wind systems can be powerful and remarkably steady.

For centuries, people have tried to capture the energy of the wind. Traditional windmills ground grain and pumped water, while sailing ships crossed oceans using nothing but moving air. Today, wind turbines convert this same energy into electricity. A single large turbine can supply enough power for hundreds of homes, and farms of turbines now stand on hills and out at sea.

Wind energy is clean and renewable, but it has drawbacks. The wind does not always blow, so supply can be unpredictable, and some people object to the appearance of turbines in the countryside. Even so, as nations seek alternatives to fossil fuels, the ancient power of the wind is being put to new and ambitious use.

questions.q: Wind is caused by the sun heating the Earth's surface unevenly.
A sea breeze blows from the sea towards the land during the day.
Land breezes are stronger than sea breezes.
What did traditional windmills do?
According to the passage, one drawback of wind energy is that
options: the wind is not always blowing

questions.q: A single large turbine can power hundreds of ________.
Wind energy is described as clean and ________.
Some people dislike how turbines look in the countryside.

listening.title: Booking a Wind Farm Tour
listening.script:
GUIDE: Hello, Coastal Energy Tours, this is Megan speaking.
VISITOR: Hi, I'd like to book a tour of the wind farm for this weekend.
GUIDE: No worries. We run tours on Saturday and Sunday. Which day suits you?
VISITOR: Saturday, if possible.
GUIDE: Saturday's fine. We have two times, ten in the morning or two in the afternoon.
VISITOR: The afternoon one, please. How long does it last?
GUIDE: About ninety minutes. It includes a short walk to the base of a turbine and a visit to the control room.
VISITOR: Sounds great. How much is it?
GUIDE: Tickets are eighteen dollars for adults and twelve for children.
VISITOR: Two adults, then.
GUIDE: That's thirty-six dollars. Could I get a name for the booking?
VISITOR: Sure, it's Karen Foster.
GUIDE: Thanks, Karen. One last thing, please wear sturdy shoes, as the path can be muddy.
VISITOR: Will do. Should we arrive early?
GUIDE: Yes, please come fifteen minutes before the start so we can sign you in.

questions.q: Which day does the visitor choose?
Which tour time does the visitor pick?
The tour lasts about ________ minutes.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when the weather affected your plans. You should say:
- when and where it happened

- how the weather changed your plans
and explain how you felt about the situation.

vocab.example: The uneven heating of the Earth creates wind.
A cool breeze blew in from the sea.

reading.title: Understanding Droughts
reading.passage:
A drought is a long period during which an area receives much less rain than usual. Unlike a sudden flood or storm, a drought develops slowly and may last for months or even years. Because it creeps up gradually, people sometimes fail to recognise its seriousness until water supplies are already low.

Droughts have many causes. Natural shifts in wind and ocean temperatures can reduce the amount of rain that reaches a region. Human activity also plays a part: when too much water is taken from rivers and underground stores, the land becomes more vulnerable to dry spells. In some areas, the clearing of forests has reduced rainfall still further.

The effects of a drought can be severe. Crops fail, livestock suffer, and the price of food may rise sharply. Rivers and reservoirs shrink, threatening both drinking water and the generation of hydroelectric power. In the worst cases, communities are forced to leave their homes in search of water elsewhere.

Managing droughts requires careful planning. Farmers can choose crops that need less water, while cities can repair leaking pipes and encourage people to use water wisely. Some regions store rainwater during wet periods to prepare for dry ones. Although droughts cannot be prevented entirely, their impact can be greatly reduced when communities act early and work together.

questions.q: A drought usually develops quickly, like a storm.
Taking too much water from rivers can make droughts worse.
Droughts are more common now than a hundred years ago.
According to the passage, one effect of drought is that
What can farmers do to manage droughts?
When rivers and reservoirs shrink, the generation of ________ power is threatened.
Some regions store ________ during wet periods to prepare for dry ones.
Droughts can be completely prevented with good planning.

listening.title: A Talk on Saving Water
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Good evening, everyone, and thank you for coming to tonight's talk on saving water at home. With drier summers becoming more common, small changes can make a real difference. Let me share a few simple ideas.
First, the bathroom is where most household water is used. A running tap can waste up to six litres a minute, so turn it off while brushing your teeth. Taking a shorter shower instead of a bath also saves a great deal.
Second, think about the garden. Watering early in the morning means less water is lost to evaporation. Collecting rainwater in a barrel is another easy step, and the plants actually prefer it.
Third, check for leaks. A dripping tap may seem harmless, but it can waste thousands of litres a year. Fixing it is usually quick and cheap.
Finally, when buying new appliances, look for water-efficient models. A modern washing machine uses far less than an older one. If we all take these steps, our towns will be far better prepared for dry weather. Thank you, and I'm happy to take questions.

questions.q: Where is most household water used?
A running tap can waste up to ________ litres a minute.
Watering the garden early in the morning reduces loss from ________.
The speaker suggests collecting rainwater in a ________.
What does the speaker say about a dripping tap?
options: It can waste thousands of litres a year

reading.title: The Mystery of Lightning
reading.passage:
Few natural events are as striking as a flash of lightning. In an instant, a bolt of light splits the sky, followed moments later by a crash of thunder. For thousands of years people feared lightning as a sign of anger from the gods. Today, scientists understand a great deal about how it forms, though some questions remain.

Lightning begins inside storm clouds, where strong currents of air cause tiny ice particles and water droplets to collide. These collisions separate electrical charges within the cloud, with lighter, positive charges gathering near the top and heavier, negative charges sinking to the bottom. When the difference in charge becomes great enough, electricity leaps across the gap, either within the cloud or down to the ground.

A lightning bolt is astonishingly powerful. It can heat the surrounding air to temperatures far hotter than the surface of the sun. This sudden heating makes the air expand so quickly that it produces the sound we know as thunder. Because light travels faster than sound, we see the flash before we hear the crash, and the delay can be used to estimate how far away the storm is.

Despite its dangers, lightning also brings benefits. The intense energy helps convert nitrogen in the air into a form that plants can absorb, acting as a natural fertiliser. Researchers continue to study lightning, partly to improve safety and partly because much about its behaviour still puzzles them.

questions.q: In the past, some people believed lightning showed the anger of gods.
Positive charges gather at the bottom of storm clouds.
Lightning strikes are more frequent over land than over the sea.
What causes the sound of thunder?
options: Air expanding rapidly from sudden heat

questions.q: Why do we see lightning before we hear thunder?
Lightning can heat the air to temperatures hotter than the surface of the ________.

Scientists now fully understand every aspect of lightning.

listening.title: A Lecture on Thunderstorm Safety
listening.script:
LECTURER: Welcome, class. Today I want to cover thunderstorm safety, because knowing what to do can save your life. Let's start with a simple rule.
When you see lightning, count the seconds until you hear thunder. If the gap is less than thirty seconds, the storm is close enough to be dangerous, and you should seek shelter immediately. A solid building is best.
Now, what should you avoid? First, stay away from tall, isolated objects such as a single tree in a field. Lightning tends to strike the highest point. Second, keep out of open water; swimmers and boaters are especially at risk.
If you are caught outdoors with no shelter nearby, crouch low with your feet together, but never lie flat on the ground. Lying down increases your contact with the surface.
Indoors, you are usually safe, but avoid using taps or showers during a storm, since pipes can carry a charge. It is also wise to unplug sensitive electronics.
Finally, remember the thirty-thirty rule: wait at least thirty minutes after the last thunder before going back outside. Storms can return quickly. Any questions?

What kind of shelter is best?

reading.title: Cities and the Heat
reading.passage:
Anyone who has walked through a city on a hot summer day knows that it can feel noticeably warmer than the surrounding countryside. This effect, known as the urban heat island, occurs because cities absorb and store more heat than natural landscapes. As more people move into towns and cities, understanding and reducing this problem has become increasingly important.

Several features of cities contribute to the effect. Roads and buildings are often made of dark materials such as asphalt and brick, which soak up sunlight during the day and release it slowly at night. Tall buildings also trap heat between them, while the lack of trees means there is little natural shade or cooling. On top of this, vehicles, factories and air conditioners all release additional warmth into the air.

The consequences are more than mere discomfort. Higher temperatures increase the demand for energy as people switch on cooling systems, which in turn raises costs and pollution. During heatwaves, the elderly and the sick are especially at risk, and hospitals may see a rise in admissions.

Fortunately, there are ways to cool our cities. Planting more trees provides shade and releases moisture into the air. Painting roofs in light colours reflects sunlight rather than absorbing it, and creating parks and ponds offers cooler spaces for residents. With careful design, even the busiest city can become a more comfortable place to live.

TUTOR: Good. What have you found so far, Leo?
LEO: Green roofs are gardens planted on top of buildings. They can lower the roof temperature by as much as twenty degrees in summer.
TUTOR: That's impressive. Any drawbacks?

TUTOR: Fair point. Maya, what about trees?
MAYA: A single mature tree can cool the air around it like several air conditioners. The main challenge is finding space in crowded streets.
TUTOR: Right. When is the presentation due?
LEO: On the fourteenth of March.
TUTOR: Good. And how long should it be?
MAYA: Twelve minutes, plus time for questions.
TUTOR: Perfect. Remember to include a clear diagram. Visuals really help the audience follow along.
LEO: Will do. Thanks for the advice.

reading.title: The Science of the Warm-Up
reading.passage:
For many years, athletes were told to stretch before exercise to prevent injury. Coaches believed that holding a muscle in a stretched position for thirty seconds or more would prepare the body for sudden movement. Recent research, however, suggests that this kind of static stretching may actually reduce power for a short time afterwards, making it less suitable just before a sprint or a jump.

A modern warm-up usually begins with light activity such as jogging or cycling. The aim is to raise the temperature of the muscles and increase the flow of blood. Warmer muscles contract more quickly and are less likely to tear. After this, athletes often perform dynamic stretches, which involve moving the limbs through their full range of motion. Leg swings and arm circles are common examples.

The length of a warm-up depends on the sport. A weightlifter may need only ten minutes, while a swimmer preparing for a race might spend half an hour in the pool. Temperature also matters: on a cold morning, the body takes longer to reach a state of readiness, so athletes are advised to extend their preparation.

Despite these findings, static stretching has not disappeared entirely. Many trainers now recommend it after exercise rather than before, when it may help the muscles return to their normal length and support recovery. The key message from researchers is that timing, not the stretch itself, makes the difference.

questions.q: In the past, coaches thought static stretching could help avoid injury.
Static stretching may temporarily lower power output before explosive movements.
A weightlifter requires a longer warm-up than a swimmer before competing.

options: To raise muscle temperature and blood flow

reading.title: How the Body Cools Itself
reading.passage:
During hard exercise, the muscles produce a great deal of heat. If the body could not get rid of this heat, the core temperature would rise to a dangerous level within minutes. Fortunately, humans have an effective cooling system that allows them to keep moving even in warm conditions.

The main method of cooling is sweating. Glands in the skin release water onto the surface of the body. As this water evaporates, it draws heat away from the skin and lowers the temperature. This is why a breeze feels so pleasant on a hot day: moving air speeds up evaporation. In very humid weather, however, the air already holds a lot of water, so sweat evaporates slowly and cooling becomes far less efficient.

Blood also plays an important role. When the body becomes hot, the small vessels near the skin widen, allowing more blood to flow close to the surface. Heat then passes from the blood into the surrounding air. This is why some people turn red during intense exercise.

Athletes who train in hot climates gradually adapt to the heat. After about two weeks, they begin to sweat sooner and in greater amounts, and their sweat contains less salt. These changes help them perform better and reduce the risk of overheating. Coaches often plan a period of gentle training, sometimes called acclimatisation, before an important competition in a hot country.

questions.q: Without a cooling system, body temperature could reach a dangerous level quickly.
Sweat cools the body most effectively in humid conditions.

reading.title: The Marathon and the Wall
reading.passage:
Many runners who attempt a marathon describe a moment of sudden exhaustion that strikes after about thirty kilometres. They call this experience 'hitting the wall'. The legs feel heavy, the pace slows, and even finishing the race can seem impossible. Scientists have studied this phenomenon for decades and now understand much of what causes it.

The body stores energy in two main forms: fat and a substance called glycogen, which is held in the muscles and the liver. During a long run, the body uses both, but glycogen is the quicker and easier fuel. The problem is that the supply of glycogen is limited. Most runners carry enough for around two hours of hard effort. When it runs low, the body must rely more heavily on fat, which releases energy far more slowly. This sudden change is what produces the feeling of hitting the wall.

Experienced runners use several methods to delay this moment. In the days before a race, they eat large amounts of carbohydrate to build up their glycogen stores, a practice often called carbohydrate loading. During the race itself, they take small amounts of sugar through drinks or gels to keep their fuel levels higher for longer.

Pacing is equally important. A runner who starts too fast burns through glycogen quickly and is more likely to suffer later. By holding a steady, controlled pace from the beginning, an athlete can spread the use of fuel across the whole distance. Training the body to burn fat more efficiently, through long and gentle runs, can also push the wall further away.

options: Fat is stored in the brain

options: They are more likely to struggle later

listening.script: COACH: Good evening, everyone, and welcome to the Greenfield Running Club. I'll quickly explain how things work. We meet three times a week: Tuesday and Thursday evenings, and Saturday mornings. The Tuesday session is for beginners, so if you're new, that's the best place to start. We gather at the entrance of Greenfield Park at half past six. Please don't be late, because the group sets off together. Bring a water bottle and wear bright clothing so that drivers can see you in the dark. The annual membership fee is sixty pounds, but for your first month it's free, so you can decide whether the club suits you. Members also get a discount at the sports shop on the high street. After each session, many of us go to the café next to the park for a hot drink and a chat. It's a friendly group, and you don't need to be fast to join. If you have any questions, come and find me afterwards. Now, let's get our shoes on and warm up.

reading.title: The Rise of Wearable Trackers
reading.passage:
A decade ago, only professional athletes had access to detailed information about their bodies during exercise. Today, millions of ordinary people wear small devices on their wrists that count their steps, measure their heart rate, and estimate the number of calories they burn. These wearable trackers have changed the way many people think about fitness.

The appeal of such devices is easy to understand. By setting a daily goal, such as ten thousand steps, a user receives constant feedback and a sense of progress. Many trackers send a gentle reminder to move after a long period of sitting. Some studies suggest that people who use these devices do become slightly more active, at least in the first few months.

However, the technology is not without problems. The measurements are not always accurate, especially for activities such as cycling or swimming. A tracker may also create unnecessary anxiety, with some users feeling guilty if they fail to reach their target on a particular day. There is also a concern about privacy, since the data collected can reveal a great deal about a person's daily routine.

Researchers warn that a tracker is only a tool. Wearing one does not automatically lead to better health; the user must still make the effort to change their habits. For some, the device provides useful motivation. For others, the novelty fades, and the tracker ends up forgotten in a drawer. The challenge for designers is to create products that keep people engaged for the long term rather than just a few exciting weeks.

reading.title: Sleep and Athletic Performance
reading.passage:
Athletes spend long hours training their bodies, paying close attention to diet, technique and recovery. Yet one of the most powerful tools for improvement is also one of the most overlooked: sleep. Growing evidence shows that the amount and quality of sleep an athlete gets can have a direct effect on how well they perform.

During deep sleep, the body releases hormones that repair muscle tissue damaged by hard training. The brain, too, uses this time to organise the skills practised during the day. A tennis player who learns a new serve, for example, may find that the movement feels more natural after a good night's rest. This process, in which the brain strengthens new memories during sleep, is one reason why coaches now treat rest as part of training rather than as separate from it.

Lack of sleep has the opposite effect. Studies of basketball players have found that those who slept less reacted more slowly, made more errors, and tired sooner. Their accuracy when shooting also fell. Perhaps more worrying, tired athletes are more likely to suffer injuries, partly because their judgement and balance are affected.

Many professional teams now employ specialists to advise players on their sleep. The advice is often simple: keep a regular bedtime, avoid bright screens late at night, and make the bedroom cool and dark. Some teams even adjust their travel schedules to reduce the effects of crossing time zones. While these measures may seem minor, the gains in performance can be significant, especially when the difference between winning and losing is measured in fractions of a second.

questions.q: Sleep is often given less attention than diet and technique by athletes.
During deep sleep the body releases hormones that repair muscle tissue.
Basketball players who slept less performed more accurately when shooting.

options: The brain strengthens new memories during sleep
Sleep removes the need to practise

listening.title: A Lecture on Recovery for Athletes
listening.script: LECTURER: Today I want to focus on recovery, which is just as important as training itself. Many young athletes believe that more training always means better results, but the body actually improves during the rest that follows hard effort. Let me outline three key areas. First, sleep. Aim for at least eight hours a night; this is when most muscle repair takes place. Second, nutrition. After exercise, the body needs both protein, to rebuild muscle, and carbohydrate, to refill its energy stores. A simple meal within an hour of finishing is ideal. Third, gentle movement. Complete rest is not always best. A light walk or an easy swim the day after a hard session can actually speed up recovery by improving blood flow. I'd also remind you to drink plenty of water, as even slight dehydration slows the process. Finally, listen to your body. Pain that does not fade after a couple of days is a warning sign, and you should see a physiotherapist rather than push through it. Recovery is not laziness; it is part of becoming stronger.

questions.q: What is the main message of the lecture?
options: Recovery is as important as training

reading.title: The Rise of the Four-Day Week
reading.passage:
Over the past decade, a growing number of companies have experimented with a shorter working week. Instead of the traditional five days, employees work only four, yet they continue to receive the same monthly salary. Supporters argue that this arrangement rewards productivity rather than the simple act of being present at a desk.

The largest trial to date took place in the United Kingdom, where around sixty firms allowed staff to take an extra day off each week for six months. At the end of the experiment, most of the businesses reported that output had remained stable, and a few even noticed a modest increase. Employees, meanwhile, described feeling less tired and more satisfied with their jobs. Rates of sickness fell, and fewer workers chose to resign.

Not everyone is convinced, however. Critics point out that the trial mainly involved office-based businesses, where tasks can often be compressed into fewer hours. In sectors such as healthcare or manufacturing, where a physical presence is required throughout the day, the model may prove far harder to apply. Some managers also worry that meetings and deadlines simply pile up, leaving the four working days more stressful than before.

Economists remain divided about the long-term effects. A few predict that shorter weeks will eventually become standard as automation handles routine tasks. Others believe the change will spread slowly, limited to industries that can afford the experiment. What seems clear is that the question of how much time we should spend at work is no longer taken for granted.

questions.q: Workers in the four-day week trial received a reduced salary.
Most firms in the UK trial reported that output stayed roughly the same.
The UK trial included a large number of hospitals.
The four-day week trial was repeated in several other countries at the same time.
According to the passage, what did employees report during the trial?
Why might the model be difficult to apply in healthcare?
options: A physical presence is needed all day

questions.q: During the trial, rates of ________ fell among staff.
Some economists predict shorter weeks will spread as ________ handles routine tasks.

listening.title: Booking a Career Workshop
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, City Careers Centre, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to sign up for one of your job-skills workshops.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We run two this month. The first is on writing a strong CV, held on the fourteenth of March. The second covers interview techniques, on the twenty-first.

CALLER: I think the interview one would suit me best. How much does it cost?

RECEPTIONIST: It's thirty pounds for the day, but students pay only eighteen. Are you a student?

CALLER: Yes, I am. Does the price include lunch?

RECEPTIONIST: It includes coffee and a light snack, but not a full lunch. The session runs from ten in the morning until four in the afternoon.

CALLER: That's fine. Where exactly is it held?

RECEPTIONIST: In Room B on the second floor of our Bridge Street building. Please bring a copy of your CV.

CALLER: Great. Can I pay on the day?

RECEPTIONIST: You can, but a deposit of five pounds secures your place. Shall I take your name?

speaking.q: Let's talk about work and study. Do you prefer to work or study in the morning or in the evening? Why? Is there a part of the day when you feel most productive?

Sector | 2015 | 2023
Finance | 12% | 48%
Manufacturing | 3% | 9%
Education | 8% | 31%
Retail | 2% | 6%

Write at least 150 words.

reading.title: Robots on the Factory Floor
reading.passage:
Industrial robots have been a feature of large factories since the 1970s, but until recently they were expensive, heavy and difficult to program. As a result, only major manufacturers, especially car makers, could justify the cost. Smaller businesses simply continued to rely on human labour for most of their tasks.

This situation is now changing. A new generation of machines, often called collaborative robots or 'cobots', is far cheaper and easier to set up. Rather than being locked behind safety cages, cobots are designed to work alongside people, handing over parts or lifting heavy items while a human carries out the more delicate steps. A factory can teach a cobot a new task simply by guiding its arm through the movement, with no specialist programming required.

Supporters say cobots remove the most repetitive and physically demanding jobs, which are often the cause of injury. Workers can then concentrate on tasks that require judgement, creativity or careful inspection. In several small workshops, the introduction of cobots has reportedly allowed firms to take on more orders without hiring extra staff, helping them compete with larger rivals.

There are concerns, of course. Some employees fear that the machines will eventually replace them entirely rather than simply assist. Others note that the savings often benefit owners more than the workforce. Researchers who study the issue suggest that the outcome depends heavily on how companies choose to use the technology. Where firms retrain staff to manage and maintain the robots, jobs tend to be preserved. Where they do not, the workforce may shrink. The technology itself, they argue, decides very little; the choices made by people decide a great deal.

questions.q: Industrial robots only appeared for the first time in the last few years.
Cobots are designed to work close to human staff.
Teaching a cobot a new task requires specialist programming.
Cobots are now more popular in Asia than in Europe.
Why could only large manufacturers use early robots?
options: They were costly and hard to program

questions.q: According to researchers, what mainly determines the effect on jobs?
options: How companies choose to use the technology

questions.q: Cobots remove the most repetitive and physically ________ jobs.
Where firms ________ staff to manage the robots, jobs tend to be preserved.

listening.title: Asking About a Warehouse Job
listening.script:
APPLICANT: Hello, I'm calling about the warehouse assistant job advertised online.

MANAGER: Great, thanks for ringing. Have you done warehouse work before?

APPLICANT: A little. I worked for a delivery company for about a year.

MANAGER: That's useful. The role is full-time, forty hours a week, mostly day shifts but occasionally weekends.

APPLICANT: That's fine for me. What does the job actually involve?

MANAGER: Picking and packing orders, checking stock, and sometimes operating a forklift. Do you have a forklift licence?

APPLICANT: Not yet, but I'm happy to train.

MANAGER: No problem, we provide training in the first month. The starting pay is twenty-four dollars an hour, rising to twenty-six after six months.

APPLICANT: That sounds good. When would I start?

MANAGER: We're hoping someone can begin on the second of April. Could you send your CV to jobs@swiftstore.com today?

APPLICANT: Absolutely. Should I bring anything to the interview?

MANAGER: Just some photo identification and two references. Thanks very much.

options: Train for it on the job

speaking.q:
Describe a machine or piece of technology that has changed the way people work. You should say:

and explain how it has changed the work for the better or worse.

Some people believe that robots and machines will eventually do most jobs currently done by humans, which is a positive development. Others think this will cause serious problems. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

reading.title: The Growth of the Gig Economy
reading.passage:
The term 'gig economy' describes a labour market in which people earn money through short, separate pieces of work rather than holding a single permanent job. A driver who delivers food, a designer who completes one project at a time, and a tutor who teaches a handful of online students are all examples. Digital platforms, usually accessed through a smartphone app, connect these workers directly with customers.

The appeal for workers is flexibility. They can, in theory, decide when and how much to work, fitting their tasks around family duties or studies. For companies, the attraction is cost: they pay only for the work that is done and avoid the expense of permanent staff, such as paid holidays or sick leave. Some economists welcome this efficiency, arguing that it allows the economy to respond quickly to changing demand.

Critics, however, highlight the insecurity that often accompanies such work. Because gig workers are usually classed as self-employed, they may receive no minimum wage, no pension and no protection if they fall ill. Income can vary sharply from week to week, making it hard to plan ahead or obtain a loan. In some cities, drivers have protested that the apps quietly reduce their pay while taking a growing share of each transaction.

Governments are slowly responding. A handful of countries have passed laws granting gig workers certain basic rights, such as a guaranteed minimum payment per hour. Companies argue that strict rules will remove the very flexibility that makes the model attractive. The debate is likely to continue, because the gig economy reflects a deeper question: in a world of constant change, who should bear the risk when work itself becomes uncertain?

questions.q: Gig workers typically hold one permanent job.
Digital platforms connect gig workers with customers.
Gig workers are usually classed as self-employed.
Most gig workers in the study earned more than office workers.
What is the main appeal of gig work for workers?
Why do some drivers protest against the apps?
options: The apps quietly reduce their pay
The apps require a fee to download

questions.q: For companies, the main attraction of gig work is lower ________.
Some countries have passed laws granting gig workers a guaranteed minimum ________ per hour.

listening.title: A Talk on Freelancing
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to this short session on starting out as a freelancer. Let me share three practical tips that helped me when I began.

First, set your rates carefully. Many beginners charge far too little because they fear losing the work. My advice is to research what others in your field ask, and then set a price you can actually live on.

Second, keep clear records. From day one, save every invoice and note every payment. When tax season arrives, you'll be glad you did. I lost almost two hundred dollars in my first year simply because I forgot to track expenses.

Third, never depend on a single client. If that one client disappears, so does your income. Aim to have at least three regular clients so that your earnings stay steady. It took me about eight months to reach that point, but it made an enormous difference to my peace of mind.

Finally, remember to take breaks. Freelancing can blur the line between work and rest, and burnout is a real danger. Thank you, and I'm happy to take questions.

questions.q: What mistake do many beginners make with their rates?
From day one, the speaker advises saving every ________.
The speaker lost almost ________ dollars by not tracking expenses.
The speaker suggests having at least ________ regular clients.
It took the speaker about ________ months to build a steady client base.
What danger does the speaker mention at the end?

speaking.q: Let's discuss flexible work more broadly. Some people say flexible jobs give workers more freedom, while others say they create insecurity. What do you think are the main advantages and disadvantages of flexible work for society as a whole?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of registered gig workers (in thousands) in a city across three years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Type of work | 2019 | 2021 | 2023
Food delivery | 14 | 38 | 52
Ride-hailing | 22 | 30 | 41
Freelance design | 9 | 13 | 20
Online tutoring | 5 | 18 | 27

Write at least 150 words.

Flexibility is the main appeal of gig work.

reading.title: Lifelong Learning in a Changing Job Market
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, a typical career followed a simple pattern. A person learned a trade or earned a qualification in their youth, then practised that same skill until retirement. Today, this pattern is breaking down. New technologies appear so rapidly that the knowledge gained at the start of a career may be outdated long before it ends. As a result, the idea of 'lifelong learning' has moved from a pleasant slogan to a practical necessity.

Lifelong learning means continuing to study and acquire new skills throughout one's working life, not just in school or university. It can take many forms: a weekend course, an online certificate, on-the-job training, or even informal learning from colleagues. Governments and employers increasingly encourage it, recognising that a skilled and adaptable workforce is essential for a competitive economy.

The benefits extend beyond the individual. Workers who keep learning are better able to move between roles when their original jobs disappear, reducing the pain of sudden unemployment. Companies, meanwhile, can fill skill shortages from within rather than searching endlessly for new recruits. Some studies even suggest that older employees who continue to learn remain mentally sharper and more engaged at work.

Yet barriers remain. Courses cost money, and many workers struggle to find the time alongside their jobs and families. Those in low-paid or insecure positions, who arguably need new skills the most, are often the least able to afford them. Critics warn that unless training is made genuinely accessible, lifelong learning could widen rather than narrow the gap between the well-prepared and the left-behind. The challenge for policymakers is to ensure that the chance to learn is shared as widely as the need for it.

questions.q: In the twentieth century, many people practised one skill for most of their career.
Lifelong learning only happens in schools and universities.
Workers who keep learning find it easier to move between roles.
Most companies now offer free childcare to support training.
Why has lifelong learning become a necessity?
What concern do critics raise about lifelong learning?
options: It could widen the gap between workers

questions.q: A skilled and ________ workforce is seen as essential for a competitive economy.
Those in low-paid or insecure jobs are often the least able to ________ training.

listening.title: A Lecture on Skills of the Future
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to outline which skills researchers expect to matter most over the coming decade. Surveys of employers point to three broad groups.

The first group is digital skills. This does not mean everyone must become a computer programmer. Rather, it means being comfortable with everyday digital tools and understanding how data is collected and used. Roughly seventy per cent of new jobs are expected to require at least basic digital ability.

The second group is what we might call human skills: communication, teamwork and the ability to solve problems creatively. These are precisely the abilities that machines find hardest to copy, which is why their value is rising rather than falling.

The third group is adaptability. Because roles change so quickly, the willingness to learn something new may matter more than any single qualification. One large survey found that employees who described themselves as eager to learn were promoted twice as often as their peers.

My final point is this: these three groups overlap. The most successful workers, the evidence suggests, combine all three rather than relying on just one.

questions.q: What does the lecturer say digital skills mean for most people?
options: Being comfortable with everyday digital tools

questions.q: About ________ per cent of new jobs will need basic digital ability.
Why is the value of human skills rising?
options: Machines find them hardest to copy

questions.q: The third group of skills the lecturer mentions is ________.
Eager learners were promoted ________ as often as their peers.
What is the lecturer's final point?

speaking.q: Let's talk about learning new skills. Have you learned any new skill recently? How do you usually prefer to learn something new - from a teacher, from a book, or online? Do you think it is harder to learn new things as people get older?

writing.q:
In many countries, people are expected to keep learning new skills throughout their working lives. Some think governments should pay for this training, while others believe it is the responsibility of individuals. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: He earned a qualification in his youth.
Early knowledge may become outdated quickly.
Lifelong learning is now a necessity.
Workers acquire new skills over time.
Informal learning from colleagues is common.
An adaptable workforce is highly valued.
Firms search endlessly for new recruits.
Learners stay more engaged at work.
Cost is a major barrier to training.

Good policy should narrow the gap.
Policymakers must share the chance to learn.

reading.title: The Quiet Spread of Remote Work
reading.passage:
Before 2020, working from home was viewed by many managers with suspicion. They feared that without supervision, employees would be distracted and less productive. A small number of companies allowed remote work, but it was usually treated as a special favour rather than a normal arrangement. Then, almost overnight, a global health crisis forced millions of office workers to do their jobs from home, and long-held assumptions were tested on an enormous scale.

The results surprised many sceptics. In a large number of cases, productivity did not collapse; in fact, some teams reported completing more work than before. Employees saved hours previously lost to commuting and often felt they had gained control over their daily schedules. Companies, for their part, realised they could reduce spending on expensive office space.

Yet remote work is not without drawbacks. New employees, in particular, can struggle to learn from more experienced colleagues when everyone is scattered across different homes. The casual conversations that once sparked fresh ideas become rarer. Some workers report feeling isolated, and the boundary between work and personal life can blur until rest becomes difficult.

For these reasons, many organisations have settled on a middle path known as 'hybrid' working, in which staff spend part of the week in the office and the rest at home. Supporters argue that this approach captures the benefits of both worlds: the focus of quiet home days and the energy of face-to-face collaboration. Critics counter that hybrid schedules can be messy to coordinate, and that employees who come into the office more often may unfairly receive more attention and promotion. What is certain is that the office, as a fixed daily destination, is unlikely ever to return to exactly what it was.

questions.q: Before 2020, most managers strongly encouraged working from home.
Some teams reported doing more work after switching to remote work.
New employees can find it harder to learn from experienced colleagues remotely.
Hybrid workers are paid more than fully remote workers.
Why did many managers distrust remote work before 2020?
options: They feared workers would be distracted

questions.q: What is a drawback of remote work mentioned in the passage?
Employees saved hours previously lost to ________.
Many organisations have settled on a middle path known as ________ working.

listening.title: Two Colleagues Discuss Hybrid Work
listening.script:
MAYA: Hey Tom, did you see the email about the new hybrid policy?

TOM: I did. So from next month we have to be in the office three days a week, right?

MAYA: That's it. Tuesday, Wednesday and Thursday for everyone. The other two days we can work from home.

TOM: Honestly, I don't mind. I quite like having the team together midweek. The Mondays at home were getting a bit lonely.

MAYA: Same here. Though my commute takes nearly ninety minutes each way, so three days is plenty.

TOM: Ouch, that's long. Mine's only about twenty-five minutes.

MAYA: Lucky you. Oh, and they've booked the big meeting room every Wednesday at two for a team catch-up.

TOM: Good idea. Are they changing anything about the desks?

MAYA: Yes, we won't have fixed desks anymore. You book one through the app the night before.

TOM: Hmm, I'll have to remember that. Right, I'd better get back to it. See you Wednesday.

speaking.q: Let's consider the future of the office. Do you think most people will still travel to a workplace in twenty years' time? What might be the effects on cities and communities if large numbers of people work mainly from home?

writing.q:
The table below shows how employees at one company divided their working time between the office and home in three different years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Location | 2019 | 2021 | 2023
Fully in office | 78% | 14% | 22%
Hybrid | 9% | 31% | 58%
Fully remote | 13% | 55% | 20%

Write at least 150 words.

They feared workers would slack without supervision.

reading.title: How Sea Ice Shapes the Polar Climate
reading.passage:
Sea ice is frozen seawater that floats on the surface of the polar oceans. Unlike icebergs, which break off from glaciers on land, sea ice forms directly in the ocean when temperatures fall low enough for the salty water to freeze. Each winter, vast sheets of ice spread across the Arctic and around Antarctica, and each summer much of this ice melts again. This seasonal rhythm has continued for thousands of years.

Sea ice plays a surprising role in regulating the planet's temperature. Its bright white surface reflects most of the sunlight that strikes it back into space, a property scientists call albedo. Open ocean water, by contrast, is dark and absorbs heat. When sea ice shrinks, more dark water is exposed, and the ocean warms faster. This warming then melts even more ice, creating a cycle that speeds up change in the polar regions.

The ice is also vital for wildlife. Polar bears travel across Arctic sea ice to hunt seals, and they struggle to find food when the ice forms late or breaks up early. Tiny algae grow on the underside of the ice and feed small creatures, which in turn support fish, birds and whales. A healthy ice cover therefore underpins an entire food web.

In recent decades, satellite measurements have shown that Arctic summer sea ice has been declining. Researchers estimate that the area covered in September has fallen by roughly forty per cent since the late 1970s. The situation in Antarctica is more complicated, with ice expanding in some years before retreating sharply in others. Scientists continue to debate exactly how these patterns will develop, but most agree that the long-term trend in the Arctic is downward.

questions.q: Sea ice and icebergs are formed in the same way.
The amount of sea ice changes with the seasons.
Antarctic sea ice has behaved in a consistent way each year.
Scientists have agreed on the exact future size of Antarctic sea ice.
The term 'albedo' refers to a surface's ability to:
Why does losing sea ice tend to speed up warming?
options: Dark water absorbs more heat than ice

questions.q: Tiny ________ grow on the underside of the ice and feed small creatures.
Arctic September sea ice has fallen by about ________ per cent since the late 1970s.

listening.title: Booking a Coastal Kayak Tour
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, Harbour Kayak Centre, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a sea kayak tour for next weekend.

STAFF: Lovely. We run two trips: the morning tour leaves at nine, and the afternoon one at half past two. Which would suit you?

CALLER: The morning one, please. How long does it last?

STAFF: It's about three hours, including a short break on a quiet beach.

CALLER: Great. And the price?

STAFF: It's forty pounds per adult, and that includes all the equipment and a waterproof jacket.

CALLER: Perfect. There'll be two of us.

STAFF: No problem. Could I take your name?

CALLER: Yes, it's Diana Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.

STAFF: Thank you. One last thing: please bring shoes that can get wet, and meet us at the boathouse fifteen minutes early.

CALLER: Will do. See you Saturday.

speaking.q: Do you enjoy spending time near the sea or rivers? Why or why not? Talk about what you usually do there.

The table below shows the area of Arctic summer sea ice (in million square kilometres) in selected years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Year | Sea ice area
1980 | 7.8
1995 | 6.5
2010 | 4.9
2023 | 4.2

Snow has a high albedo because it reflects most sunlight.
Dark water absorbs heat instead of reflecting it.
Clean water is vital for human health.
A healthy ice cover underpins the whole food web.
Arctic summer sea ice has continued to decline.

The situation in Antarctica is more complicated.
The long-term trend in the Arctic is downward.

reading.title: The Hidden Power of Ocean Currents
reading.passage:
Beneath the surface of the world's oceans lies a vast system of moving water known as ocean currents. These currents act like enormous rivers within the sea, carrying warm and cold water across thousands of kilometres. They are driven mainly by two forces: winds blowing across the surface, and differences in the temperature and saltiness of the water itself.

One of the best-known currents is the Gulf Stream, which transports warm water from the tropics towards north-western Europe. Without it, countries such as the United Kingdom would experience far colder winters, despite their northern position. In this way, ocean currents help to spread heat around the globe and keep regional climates more stable than they would otherwise be.

Currents also influence the lives of marine creatures. In certain places, cold water rises from the deep ocean towards the surface, a process called upwelling. This rising water brings nutrients that feed tiny plant-like organisms, which in turn attract huge numbers of fish. Some of the world's richest fishing grounds, such as those off the coast of Peru, depend on this process.

Scientists study currents using floating instruments that drift with the water and send back data by satellite. Such measurements have revealed that some currents may be slowing down as the climate changes and as melting ice adds fresh water to the sea. Researchers are concerned because even small changes in these flows could affect weather patterns far inland. Understanding the deep machinery of the oceans, they argue, is essential if we are to predict the climate of the coming century.

questions.q: Ocean currents are powered only by the wind.
The Gulf Stream makes winters in the United Kingdom milder.
Upwelling brings nutrients to the surface of the ocean.
The fishing grounds off Peru are the largest in the world.
What does the passage compare ocean currents to?
Why are scientists concerned about currents slowing down?
options: It could affect weather far inland

questions.q: Cold water rising from the deep ocean is called ________.
Currents are studied using floating instruments that send back data by ________.

listening.title: Library Tour for New Students
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the university library. I'll give you a quick tour so you know where everything is.

GUIDE: We're standing now in the main entrance on the ground floor. Just to your left is the help desk, where you can ask staff anything and borrow a laptop for the day.

STUDENT: How many books can we take out at once?

GUIDE: Undergraduates can borrow up to twelve books for three weeks. If nobody else has requested them, you can renew online.

GUIDE: Now, the first floor is the quiet study area, so please keep noise to a minimum there. The second floor has group rooms you can book for discussions.

STUDENT: Is there anywhere to get a coffee?

GUIDE: Yes, there's a small cafe on the third floor, open until eight in the evening on weekdays.

GUIDE: Finally, remember the library closes at ten at night during term, but only at five on Saturdays. Any questions? No? Then let's head upstairs.

speaking.q:
Describe a place you have visited near water that you found memorable. You should say:
- where it was and when you went
- who you went with
- what you did there
and explain why this place was memorable for you.

writing.q: Some people believe that governments should spend more money on studying the oceans, while others think this money would be better used solving problems on land. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

vocab.example: Ocean currents carry warm water across the globe.
The Gulf Stream transports warm water northwards.
Warm water flows from the tropics to colder regions.
Currents keep regional climates more stable.
Upwelling brings nutrients to the surface.
The rising water carries nutrients that feed plankton.
The nutrients attract huge numbers of fish.
The instruments drift with the water and send back data.
Measurements have revealed that some currents are slowing.
Researchers are concerned about changes in the currents.
Changes in currents could affect weather far inland.
Scientists try to predict the climate of the coming century.

reading.title: Coral Reefs: Cities Beneath the Waves
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are often described as the rainforests of the sea, and for good reason. Although they cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor, they are home to roughly a quarter of all marine species. Fish, crabs, sponges and countless other creatures shelter, feed and breed among their colourful structures. For millions of people, reefs also provide food, income from tourism, and protection for coastlines against storms.

A coral reef is built by tiny animals called polyps. Each polyp produces a hard outer skeleton of limestone, and over thousands of years the skeletons of generations of polyps build up into the large structures we see today. Living inside the polyps are microscopic algae that use sunlight to make food, sharing some of it with their hosts. This partnership gives corals both their nourishment and their bright colours.

The relationship is delicate, however. When the water becomes too warm, the corals expel the algae living within them. Without the algae, the corals turn white and may starve, an event known as bleaching. If cooler conditions return quickly, the reef can recover, but repeated or prolonged bleaching can kill large areas of coral.

Conservationists are testing several ways to protect reefs. Some grow young corals in underwater nurseries and later transplant them onto damaged reefs. Others are searching for naturally heat-resistant corals that might survive warmer seas. While such efforts cannot replace action to limit ocean warming, scientists hope they can buy precious time for these remarkable underwater cities.

questions.q: Coral reefs cover most of the ocean floor.
About a quarter of marine species live among coral reefs.
Bleaching always kills the entire reef permanently.
Coral nurseries are more expensive than other protection methods.
What do the algae inside polyps provide to the coral?
What causes coral bleaching according to the passage?
Reefs are built by tiny animals called ________.
Some conservationists grow young corals in underwater ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Reducing Plastic Waste
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Thanks for coming to today's session on cutting plastic waste at home. I want to share a few simple changes that really add up.

SPEAKER: First, think about water. Buying bottled water creates a surprising amount of plastic. A reusable bottle costs only a few pounds and pays for itself within a month.

SPEAKER: Second, shopping bags. Keeping two or three cloth bags in your coat or car means you never need a plastic one at the till.

SPEAKER: Third, look at the bathroom. Switching to a bar of soap instead of a plastic bottle of liquid soap can save several containers a year.

SPEAKER: Now, recycling is helpful, but remember it's the last step, not the first. Reducing what we buy matters far more. Studies suggest that only about nine per cent of all plastic ever made has actually been recycled.

SPEAKER: Finally, I'll email everyone a short checklist tomorrow. If you'd like a printed copy, please add your name to the sheet by the door before you leave.

reading.title: Mangroves: Forests Between Land and Sea
reading.passage:
Mangroves are unusual trees that grow along tropical coastlines, in the muddy zone where rivers meet the sea. They thrive in salty water that would kill most other plants, partly because their roots filter out much of the salt and partly because special pores in their leaves release any salt that gets through. With their tangled roots rising out of the mud, mangrove forests form a strange landscape that is neither fully land nor fully sea.

These forests provide a remarkable range of benefits. Their dense roots act as a natural barrier, weakening waves and reducing the damage caused by storms and even tsunamis. The same roots trap mud and sand, slowly building up the coastline and protecting it from erosion. For the people who live nearby, mangroves can mean the difference between a flooded village and a safe one.

Mangroves are also nurseries for sea life. Young fish, shrimp and crabs hide among the roots, safe from larger predators, before moving out to open water as adults. Many commercial fisheries therefore depend indirectly on healthy mangrove forests. In addition, scientists have found that mangroves store large amounts of carbon in their muddy soils, helping to slow climate change.

Despite these advantages, mangroves are disappearing in many regions. They are often cleared to make space for fish farms, hotels and farmland. Once a mangrove forest is removed, the coast it protected becomes far more vulnerable. Recognising this, several countries have begun large planting programmes, and local communities are increasingly involved in caring for the new trees.

questions.q: Most ordinary plants would die in the salty water where mangroves grow.
Mangrove roots can reduce the damage caused by storms.
Mangroves store carbon in their muddy soils.
Planting programmes have already restored most of the world's lost mangroves.
How do mangroves deal with salt?
options: Their roots filter it and leaves release it
They store it in their fruit
They grow only in fresh rivers

questions.q: Why are mangroves important for fisheries?
options: They are nurseries for young sea life

questions.q: Mangrove roots trap mud and sand, protecting the coast from ________.
Mangroves are often cleared to make space for fish ________, hotels and farmland.

listening.title: A Lecture on the Water Cycle
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today we'll look at the water cycle, the continuous movement of water around our planet. It's powered almost entirely by energy from the sun.

LECTURER: The cycle begins with evaporation. The sun heats the surface of oceans, lakes and rivers, turning liquid water into invisible vapour that rises into the air.

LECTURER: As this vapour climbs higher, the air cools, and the vapour turns back into tiny droplets. This stage is called condensation, and it's what forms the clouds you see.

LECTURER: When those droplets grow heavy enough, they fall as precipitation, that is, rain, snow or hail. Most of this water lands back in the ocean, but a good deal falls on land.

LECTURER: On land, some water soaks into the ground, where it's stored as groundwater. The rest flows over the surface in streams and rivers, eventually returning to the sea, and the whole cycle starts again.

LECTURER: One key point for your essays: the total amount of water on Earth stays almost the same. It simply changes form and location.

questions.q: The water cycle is powered almost entirely by energy from the ________.
The stage where liquid water turns into vapour is called ________.
What forms when water vapour cools and turns into droplets?
Rain, snow and hail are all forms of ________.
Water stored underground is called ________.
What does the lecturer say about the total amount of water on Earth?

reading.title: Turning Seawater into Drinking Water
reading.passage:
More than ninety-seven per cent of the water on Earth is salty seawater, which humans cannot drink. As populations grow and fresh water becomes scarce in many regions, the idea of removing salt from seawater, a process called desalination, has become increasingly attractive. Today, thousands of desalination plants around the world supply drinking water to cities, farms and industries, particularly in dry countries near the coast.

The most common modern method is known as reverse osmosis. Seawater is pushed at high pressure against a membrane, a thin barrier with holes so small that water molecules can pass through but salt cannot. The result is fresh water on one side and very salty water, called brine, on the other. Older plants instead boil seawater and collect the steam, but this generally uses far more energy.

Desalination offers an obvious benefit: a reliable supply of fresh water that does not depend on rainfall. For desert cities, this can be a lifeline. Yet the technology has drawbacks. It demands large amounts of energy, which is expensive and, if it comes from fossil fuels, harmful to the climate. Disposing of the leftover brine is also difficult, since returning it to the sea can damage local marine life if it is not handled carefully.

Engineers are working hard to improve the process. Some plants now run partly on solar or wind power, cutting both costs and pollution. Others are designed to dilute brine before releasing it, or to recover useful minerals from it. Although desalination will never solve every water shortage, many experts believe it will play a growing part in supplying the cities of the future.

questions.q: Most of the water on Earth is fresh and drinkable.
Reverse osmosis pushes seawater against a membrane at high pressure.
Boiling seawater usually uses more energy than reverse osmosis.
Solar-powered desalination plants are cheaper to build than wind-powered ones.
What is 'brine' as described in the passage?
What is a major drawback of desalination mentioned in the passage?
options: It demands large amounts of energy
It only works in cold climates

questions.q: The process of removing salt from seawater is called ________.
In reverse osmosis, water passes through a thin barrier called a ________.

listening.title: Volunteering for a Beach Clean-up
listening.script:
ORGANISER: Hi there, are you here to sign up for Saturday's beach clean-up?

VOLUNTEER: Yes, I am. What do I need to do?

ORGANISER: Great to have you. We meet at the car park next to the lighthouse at half past eight in the morning.

VOLUNTEER: Okay. Do I need to bring anything?

ORGANISER: We provide gloves and bags, but please bring a hat and plenty of water, since it can get hot. Wear closed shoes too, not sandals.

VOLUNTEER: Got it. How long does it usually last?

ORGANISER: We work for about two hours, then everyone shares a free barbecue lunch as a thank-you.

VOLUNTEER: That sounds lovely. Is there a limit on numbers?

ORGANISER: We can take up to fifty volunteers each session. We've got about thirty so far, so there's plenty of room.

ORGANISER: Last thing, could you put your phone number on this form so we can text you if the weather changes?

VOLUNTEER: Of course. Here you go.
