reading.title: The Promise of Self-Healing Concrete
reading.passage:
Concrete is the most widely used construction material on the planet, yet it has a stubborn weakness: it cracks. Even tiny cracks allow water and air to reach the steel reinforcement inside, causing it to rust and weaken the structure over time. For decades, repairing these cracks has been a costly and never-ending task. In recent years, however, materials scientists have developed a remarkable alternative known as self-healing concrete, which can mend its own damage without human intervention.

The most studied form of this material relies on bacteria. Specific strains, such as those from the genus Bacillus, are mixed into the concrete along with a food source, usually a calcium-based compound. These bacteria can remain dormant for many years, surviving inside the dry, alkaline material. When a crack forms and rainwater seeps in, the moisture activates the bacteria. They begin to feed and, as a by-product, produce limestone. This limestone gradually fills the crack, sealing it before serious damage can occur.

Laboratory trials have shown that cracks up to roughly half a millimetre wide can be closed within three weeks. The technology is not yet cheap; adding the bacterial agents increases the cost of concrete by around a quarter. Because of this, engineers currently reserve it for structures where repairs are difficult or dangerous, such as underground tunnels, marine barriers and bridge supports. Supporters argue that the higher initial price is justified by the savings on future maintenance.

Researchers are also exploring non-biological methods. One approach embeds tiny capsules filled with a liquid healing agent throughout the concrete. When a crack tears through a capsule, the liquid escapes, flows into the gap and hardens. Although still experimental, such systems could one day allow buildings to last far longer than they do today, reducing both costs and the enormous carbon emissions associated with producing new cement.

questions.q: Small cracks in concrete can lead to the corrosion of the steel inside it.
Bacillus bacteria die quickly once placed inside dry concrete.
Self-healing concrete is now cheaper to produce than ordinary concrete.
The bacterial method has already been used in a famous national monument.
What substance do the bacteria produce that seals the cracks?
Why is self-healing concrete currently used mainly in tunnels and bridges?
options: It is the only material strong enough
Repairs in such places are hard or hazardous

questions.q: Laboratory tests show cracks up to about ________ millimetre wide can heal within three weeks.
In the capsule method, tiny capsules are filled with a liquid ________ agent.

listening.title: Joining a University Materials Lab Tour
listening.script: TOUR GUIDE: Good morning, everyone, and welcome to the Department of Materials Engineering. My name is Helen, and I'll be showing you around the labs today. Before we start, let me give you a few practical details. The tour lasts about ninety minutes, and we'll finish back here in the main foyer at around eleven thirty. Please keep your visitor badge visible at all times. Now, our first stop is the metals workshop on the second floor. There you'll see how we test the strength of steel samples. After that, we move to the ceramics lab, which is on the ground floor, just past the cafe. The third stop is the polymer lab, where students study plastics and rubbers. One important rule: safety goggles must be worn in every lab, and you'll find them in a blue box by each door. If you have any questions during the tour, please save them until we reach each room, so everyone can hear the answer. Finally, photography is allowed, but please don't touch any of the equipment. Right, if everyone's ready, let's head to the lift and begin.

Type of concrete | 2010 | 2020

reading.title: Shape Memory Alloys
reading.passage:
Most metals, once bent out of shape, stay bent. A paper clip that has been twisted will not straighten itself, and a dented car panel must be hammered back into form. A special family of materials, however, behaves very differently. Known as shape memory alloys, these metals can return to a previously fixed shape when they are heated. The effect seems almost magical, but it is the result of well-understood changes in their internal structure.

The most common shape memory alloy is a mixture of nickel and titanium, often called Nitinol. At low temperatures the atoms inside the metal are arranged in a flexible pattern, so the material can be bent quite easily. When it is warmed above a certain temperature, the atoms shift into a more rigid arrangement, and the metal springs back to the shape it was given during manufacture. This switch between two atomic structures can be repeated many thousands of times.

The practical uses are wide-ranging. In medicine, thin wires made of Nitinol are used in dental braces; warmed by the mouth, they apply a gentle, steady pressure to move teeth. Surgeons also use tiny tubes called stents, which are inserted in a compressed form and then expand to hold blood vessels open once they reach body temperature. Engineers have built temperature-controlled valves and even eyeglass frames that recover their shape after being sat on.

Despite these advantages, shape memory alloys are not suitable for every situation. They are relatively expensive to produce, and they can become tired after very heavy use, eventually losing some of their ability to recover. They also work only within a limited temperature range. Even so, ongoing research continues to find fresh applications, and many engineers believe these clever metals will play an increasing role in robotics and aerospace in the years ahead.

questions.q: An ordinary paper clip returns to its original shape on its own after being twisted.
Nitinol is made mainly from nickel and titanium.
The change between the two atomic structures can only happen a few times.
Nitinol stents are more expensive than stents made from other materials.
What causes a shape memory alloy to return to its fixed shape?
options: Heating it above a certain temperature

questions.q: Which is mentioned as a drawback of shape memory alloys?
options: They are toxic to the body

reading.title: Graphene: A Material One Atom Thick
reading.passage:
When scientists in Manchester first isolated graphene in 2004, they did so using a surprisingly simple method: sticky tape and a block of graphite, the same soft carbon found in ordinary pencils. By repeatedly peeling layers apart with the tape, they eventually obtained a sheet just one atom thick. This achievement, which later won a Nobel Prize, opened the door to what many call a wonder material.

Graphene is made of carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb pattern. Despite being incredibly thin, it is extraordinarily strong, around two hundred times stronger than steel of the same weight. At the same time it is light, almost transparent, and an excellent conductor of both heat and electricity. Few materials combine so many useful properties in a single substance, which explains the intense interest it has attracted from researchers worldwide.

The potential applications are enormous. Because graphene conducts electricity so well, it could lead to faster, smaller electronic devices and flexible touchscreens that fold like paper. Engineers are testing it in stronger, lighter aircraft parts and in filters able to remove salt from seawater. Some battery makers hope it will allow phones to charge in minutes rather than hours. There are even experimental medical sensors that use graphene to detect tiny changes in the body.

Yet progress has been slower than the early excitement suggested. The main obstacle is manufacturing: producing large, flawless sheets of graphene cheaply and reliably remains difficult. Small flakes are easy to make, but joining them without faults is another matter. Until these production challenges are overcome, many of graphene's most ambitious uses will stay confined to the laboratory. Even so, few doubt that this remarkable form of carbon will eventually leave its mark on everyday technology.

questions.q: Graphene was first isolated using highly advanced and expensive equipment.
Graphene is heavier than steel of the same thickness.
Graphene can conduct both heat and electricity well.
Making large, fault-free sheets of graphene is still a major difficulty.
What shape describes the arrangement of carbon atoms in graphene?
According to the passage, why has progress been slower than expected?
options: It is hard to manufacture large flawless sheets

questions.q: Graphene is roughly ________ times stronger than steel of the same weight.
Some battery makers hope graphene will let phones charge in ________ rather than hours.

listening.title: A Short Talk on Recycling Plastics
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thanks for coming to today's lunchtime talk on plastic recycling. I want to clear up some common confusion about what actually happens to the plastics we throw away. First, not all plastics are equal. Each item carries a small number, from one to seven, inside a triangle. Numbers one and two, used for drink bottles and milk containers, are the easiest to recycle and the most valuable. Number three, often found in pipes, is much harder to process. Now, before plastics can be recycled, they have to be sorted, usually by machines that detect different types. After sorting, the plastic is washed to remove food and labels, then shredded into small flakes. These flakes are melted and formed into pellets, which factories buy as raw material. One surprising fact: a single plastic bottle can be recycled into fibers for clothing. However, plastic cannot be recycled forever; after a few cycles the quality drops too much. That's why reducing what we use in the first place still matters most. Any questions before we finish?

questions.q: Which plastics are described as easiest to recycle?
Each plastic item carries a number from one to ________ inside a triangle.
After sorting, the plastic is ________ to remove food and labels.

reading.title: The Return of Bamboo as a Building Material
reading.passage:
For thousands of years, people across Asia and South America have built homes, bridges and scaffolding from bamboo. In the modern era, however, it was often dismissed as a poor person's timber, suitable only for temporary structures. Today that view is changing rapidly. As architects and engineers search for materials that are both strong and environmentally friendly, bamboo is enjoying a striking revival.

Bamboo is technically a grass, not a tree, yet certain species are astonishingly tough. Weight for weight, some have a tensile strength comparable to steel, meaning they resist being pulled apart very effectively. What makes bamboo especially attractive, though, is the speed at which it grows. While a hardwood tree may take fifty years to mature, many bamboo species can be harvested after only three to five years. During its growth it also absorbs large amounts of carbon dioxide, helping to offset emissions elsewhere.

There are challenges, of course. Untreated bamboo can be attacked by insects and may rot in damp conditions. To overcome these problems, engineers treat the stems with natural preservatives and design joints that keep water away. Modern processing has also produced engineered bamboo, in which thin strips are glued together under pressure to form strong, uniform beams and panels. These products behave far more predictably than raw stems and can be used in larger buildings.

In several countries, bamboo is now being used for schools, sports halls and even multi-storey housing. Supporters point out that it grows in regions where people most need affordable, low-carbon construction. Critics warn that careful management is needed to prevent over-harvesting, and that building codes in many countries have yet to fully recognise the material. Nevertheless, with the construction industry under growing pressure to cut its environmental impact, this ancient plant may well become a key material of the future.

questions.q: In modern times bamboo was sometimes seen as suitable only for temporary buildings.
Bamboo is a type of tree.
Some bamboo species can be harvested faster than hardwood trees mature.
Engineered bamboo is more expensive than steel of the same size.
What property makes some bamboo comparable to steel?
What problem can affect untreated bamboo?
options: It can rot or be attacked by insects

questions.q: Many bamboo species can be harvested after only ________ to five years.
In engineered bamboo, thin strips are ________ together under pressure.

listening.title: Students Discuss a Construction Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, Mia and Tom, how's the design project on sustainable housing coming along? 
MIA: Quite well. We've decided to focus our model on bamboo as the main material. 
TUTOR: Interesting choice. What's drawn you to it? 
TOM: Mainly the speed of growth. We read that some species mature in about four years, which is far quicker than timber. 
MIA: And it absorbs a lot of carbon dioxide while it grows. 
TUTOR: Good points. Have you thought about the weaknesses? 
TOM: Yes. It can rot in wet conditions, so we're designing a roof with a wide overhang to keep the walls dry. 
MIA: We're also treating the bamboo with a natural preservative rather than chemicals. 
TUTOR: Sensible. When is the model due? 
MIA: We submit it on the twentieth of May, and the presentation is the following week. 
TUTOR: Right. My one piece of advice is to include a cost comparison with concrete. That will strengthen your argument. 
TOM: Good idea. We'll add a table. 
TUTOR: Excellent. Send me a draft by Friday and I'll give you feedback.

speaking.q: Let's talk about nature and the environment. Do you think it is important to protect the environment? Why? What do you and your family do to reduce waste at home? Do you prefer products made from natural materials or man-made ones? Why?

writing.q:
In many countries, traditional building materials such as bamboo and timber are being replaced by concrete and steel. Some people think this is a positive development, while others believe traditional materials should be preserved. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: They dismissed the idea as impractical.
There has been a revival of interest in the craft.
Steel cables have high tensile strength.
Its strength is comparable to steel.
Hardwood trees take decades to mature.
The bamboo is harvested after a few years.
Planting trees can offset carbon emissions.

They need affordable housing.

reading.title: Why Some Plastics Take Centuries to Break Down
reading.passage:
Plastic has transformed modern life. It is cheap, light, waterproof and can be moulded into almost any shape. Yet the very qualities that make plastic so useful are also the source of one of the world's most serious environmental problems. Ordinary plastics do not decay in the way that natural materials such as wood or cotton do. A plastic bottle dropped on a beach may still be recognisable hundreds of years from now.

The reason lies in the chemistry of plastics. They are made of long chains of repeating units called polymers, joined by strong bonds. The microbes that normally break down natural waste have not evolved the tools to digest these artificial chains. Instead of rotting, most plastic simply breaks into ever smaller pieces. These tiny fragments, known as microplastics, can be carried by wind and water into soil, rivers and the bodies of animals, where their full effects are still being studied.

Scientists are responding in several ways. One promising avenue is the development of biodegradable plastics, often made from plant starch, which microbes can break down within months under the right conditions. However, many of these materials only decompose properly in industrial composting plants, not in an ordinary garden or the open sea. If thrown away carelessly, they may behave almost like conventional plastic.

Another line of research is even more surprising. Certain bacteria and fungi have been found that can slowly consume particular plastics, and researchers are studying the enzymes these organisms use. In the laboratory, engineered versions of these enzymes have broken down plastic bottles in a matter of days. Whether such methods can be scaled up to deal with the millions of tonnes of plastic produced each year remains uncertain. For now, experts agree that using less plastic and reusing what we have are still the most reliable solutions.

questions.q: Plastic decays at roughly the same rate as wood and cotton.
Natural microbes generally cannot digest the chains found in ordinary plastics.
Many biodegradable plastics break down fully only under certain conditions.
Microplastics have been proven to cause specific diseases in humans.
What are the long chains of repeating units in plastic called?
What does the passage say happens to most plastic instead of rotting?
options: It breaks into ever smaller pieces

questions.q: Many biodegradable plastics are made from plant ________.
Experts say using less plastic and ________ what we have are the most reliable solutions.

listening.title: A Lecture on Materials and the Environment
listening.script: LECTURER: Good morning. Today we'll look at how the choice of materials affects a product's environmental footprint across its whole life. We call this life-cycle thinking. It has four main stages. The first is raw material extraction, such as mining metal or drilling for oil. The second stage is manufacturing, where the material is turned into a product, often using a great deal of energy. The third stage is use, which can last anywhere from days to decades. And the fourth, which is too often ignored, is disposal. Now, here's a key insight. A material that seems clean at one stage may be harmful at another. Aluminium, for instance, is wonderfully recyclable, yet producing it from raw ore consumes enormous amounts of electricity. By contrast, recycling aluminium uses only about five percent of that energy. This is why designers are urged to think about the entire life cycle, not just one stage. In your assignment, due next Thursday, I'd like you to compare two everyday products using this framework. Choose products you genuinely use, and remember to cite at least three sources.

questions.q: The approach described is called ________ thinking.
How many main stages does the life cycle have?
The stage that is too often ignored is ________.
Recycling aluminium uses only about ________ percent of the original energy.
The assignment is due next ________.
What must students do in the assignment?

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you tried to reduce waste or recycle something. You should say:
- what you did
- when and where it happened
- how easy or difficult it was
- and explain how you felt about doing it.

You will have one minute to prepare and should speak for one to two minutes.

writing.q:
The table below shows the estimated number of years that four materials take to break down in the natural environment. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Material | Years to break down
Paper | 1
Cotton fabric | 5
Aluminium can | 200
Plastic bottle | 450

Write at least 150 words.

The plastic splits into tiny fragments.

reading.title: The Quiet Decline of Pollinators
reading.passage:
Bees, butterflies and other pollinating insects perform a service that is easy to overlook. By carrying pollen from flower to flower, they enable many plants to produce the fruits and seeds on which both wild ecosystems and human agriculture depend. Scientists estimate that roughly three quarters of the crop species grown for food benefit in some way from animal pollination. Without these creatures, supermarket shelves would look noticeably emptier and far less colourful.

In recent decades, however, researchers across Europe and North America have recorded a steady fall in pollinator numbers. The causes are varied and often work together. The spread of intensive farming has replaced flower-rich meadows with vast fields of a single crop, leaving insects with little to eat once that crop has finished blooming. Certain pesticides, meanwhile, can weaken bees even at doses too small to kill them outright, impairing their memory and their ability to navigate back to the hive.

Climate change adds a further complication. Many plants now flower earlier in the year than they once did, and if the insects that feed on them do not adjust their own timing, the two can fall out of step. A bee that emerges after its favourite flowers have already withered may struggle to find enough food. This mismatch is difficult to measure precisely, but several long-term studies suggest it is becoming more common.

The good news is that small actions can help. Planting wildflowers along field edges, reducing pesticide use and leaving patches of land uncultivated all give pollinators a better chance. Some cities have even created 'bee corridors', strips of flowering plants that link parks and gardens across the urban landscape. Such measures will not reverse the decline on their own, but they show that recovery is possible where people are willing to make room for nature.

questions.q: Around 75 per cent of food crop species gain some benefit from animal pollination.
Pesticides only harm bees when used in large quantities.
Bee corridors have been shown to increase honey production in cities.
According to the passage, intensive farming harms pollinators mainly because it
options: leaves insects without food after a crop stops flowering
increases the temperature of the soil
removes water sources from the land

questions.q: The 'mismatch' described in paragraph three refers to
options: a gap between pesticide use and farming laws
a difference between wild and farmed bees
a timing difference between flowering and insect activity
a conflict between cities and rural areas

questions.q: Strips of flowering plants linking green spaces in cities are called bee ________.
Some pesticides can damage a bee's memory and its ability to ________ back to the hive.
The author believes individual actions alone can completely stop the decline of pollinators.

listening.title: Joining a Community Garden
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, Greenfield Community Garden, how can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to sign up for a plot, please.
STAFF: Lovely. Can I take your name?
CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.
STAFF: Thank you. And we have two plot sizes. The standard plot is six pounds a month, and the large one is ten pounds.
CALLER: I'll take the standard, thanks.
STAFF: Great. Now, members are asked to volunteer for two hours each week, usually on a Saturday morning.
CALLER: That's fine. Do I need to bring my own tools?
STAFF: We provide spades and forks, but please bring your own gloves. One more thing, we don't allow chemical pesticides on site, only natural methods.
CALLER: Good to know. When can I start?
STAFF: Your plot will be ready on the fourteenth of March. Just come to the main gate on Hill Road and ask for me, Sarah.
CALLER: Perfect. Thank you, Sarah.

reading.title: Cleaning Water with Reeds
reading.passage:
When most people picture a sewage treatment plant, they imagine concrete tanks, pumps and the steady hum of machinery. Yet in a growing number of towns, dirty water is being cleaned by something far gentler: a bed of reeds. These constructed wetlands copy the way natural marshes filter water, and they are proving to be a cheap and reliable option for smaller communities.

The principle is simple. Waste water is allowed to flow slowly through gravel in which tall reeds are planted. As the water moves, solid particles settle out and sink. More importantly, the roots of the reeds carry oxygen down into the gravel, and this oxygen feeds colonies of bacteria. It is these bacteria, rather than the plants themselves, that do most of the work, breaking down harmful substances into simpler, safer compounds.

Constructed wetlands have several advantages over conventional plants. They use very little energy because they rely on gravity and natural processes rather than powerful pumps. They are pleasant to look at and can provide a home for frogs, dragonflies and water birds. Maintenance costs are also low, since there are few moving parts to break down. For a village far from a city's sewer network, such a system can be an attractive solution.

There are limits, however. A reed bed needs a fairly large area of flat land, which is not always available, and it works less efficiently in very cold weather when bacteria become sluggish. Engineers also warn that the systems must be designed carefully; a poorly built wetland can become blocked and start to smell. Even so, where the conditions are right, letting plants and bacteria share the labour offers a quiet alternative to the noisy machinery of the past.

questions.q: Constructed wetlands imitate the way natural marshes clean water.
The reeds themselves do most of the work of breaking down harmful substances.
Reed bed systems are more expensive to build than concrete plants.
What is the main role of the reeds' roots in the system?
options: To absorb all the dirty water
To carry oxygen down to the bacteria

questions.q: One disadvantage of constructed wetlands mentioned is that they
In a reed bed, waste water flows slowly through ________ in which the plants grow.
The systems use little energy because they rely on ________ rather than powerful pumps.

listening.title: A Tour of the Wetland Centre
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the Riverside Wetland Centre. I'm Tom, and I'll be your guide this morning. Before we set off, a few quick points. The full walk takes about ninety minutes and covers two kilometres, so do wear comfortable shoes. We'll stop at three viewing platforms along the way. The first looks out over the reed beds, where you might spot a heron if we're lucky. Please keep your voices low near the water so we don't frighten the birds. Photography is welcome, but flash is not allowed in the bird hide. Halfway round, there's a small cafe where you can buy hot drinks and snacks. The toilets are next to the cafe, not at the entrance, so plan accordingly. Finally, the gift shop closes at four o'clock, so if you'd like a souvenir, please visit before then. Right, if everyone's ready, let's head down to the first platform.

questions.q: The full walk lasts about ________ minutes.
The walk covers a distance of ________ kilometres.

speaking.q:
Describe a natural place you have visited that you found peaceful. You should say:

and explain why you found it peaceful.

Some people believe that protecting the environment is the responsibility of governments, while others think individuals should also take action. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Return of the Beaver
reading.passage:
For centuries the beaver was a familiar sight across the rivers of Europe, hunted for its fur and for a substance from its body once valued in medicine. By the early twentieth century, however, relentless trapping had driven the animal close to extinction across much of the continent. In many countries it vanished entirely, and the rivers fell silent of the steady gnawing and splashing that had once been so ordinary.

In recent years the beaver has begun to return, partly through deliberate reintroduction programmes and partly by spreading naturally from surviving populations. This comeback has been welcomed by many ecologists, who describe the beaver as an 'ecosystem engineer'. By felling small trees and building dams, beavers create ponds and wetlands that benefit a wide range of other species, from fish and frogs to insects and birds.

The dams bring practical benefits too. They slow the flow of water during heavy rain, which can reduce the risk of flooding further downstream. The ponds behind them also trap sediment and filter out pollutants, improving water quality. During dry spells, the stored water is released gradually, helping streams to keep flowing when they might otherwise run dry.

Not everyone is delighted, however. Farmers sometimes complain that beaver dams flood fields or block drainage channels, and that the animals chew through valuable trees. Managing these conflicts is now an important part of conservation work. Some authorities install pipes through dams to control water levels, while others pay landowners for any damage. Supporters argue that, on balance, the beaver does far more good than harm, and that learning to live alongside it is a small price for the richer, wetter landscapes it creates.

questions.q: Beavers were once hunted both for their fur and for a medicinal substance.
The beaver became extinct across the whole of Europe.
Reintroduction programmes have proved more successful than natural spread.
Why do ecologists call the beaver an 'ecosystem engineer'?
options: It builds nests in tall trees
It creates habitats that help many other species
It travels long distances along rivers
It feeds on a wide variety of fish

questions.q: According to the passage, beaver dams can help reduce flooding by
options: slowing the flow of water during heavy rain

questions.q: The ponds behind beaver dams trap ________ and filter out pollutants.
Some authorities install ________ through dams to control water levels.
Farmers have raised concerns that beavers can damage trees and flood fields.

listening.title: Lecture: Rewilding Projects
listening.script: LECTURER: Today I want to introduce the idea of rewilding, which means returning land to a wilder, more natural state. There are three main approaches you should know. The first is passive rewilding, where we simply stop farming an area and let nature take over without further help. It's the cheapest method, but it can be slow. The second approach involves reintroducing key species, such as the beaver or, in some larger projects, wild horses. These animals shape the landscape in ways that benefit others. The third approach focuses on removing barriers, for example taking out old dams so that fish can migrate freely again. Now, a common question is how long results take. Honestly, you should expect to wait at least ten years before major changes appear. Funding is another challenge, as most projects rely on grants rather than profit. Next week we'll examine a case study from Scotland, so please read chapter four beforehand.

questions.q: Rewilding means returning land to a wilder, more ________ state.
Which approach is described as the cheapest?
One example of a reintroduced large animal is the wild ________.
Removing old dams allows fish to ________ freely again.
Major changes may take at least ________ years to appear.
Most rewilding projects make a profit.

speaking.q: Let's discuss wildlife conservation more broadly. Why do you think some people are more concerned about protecting animals than others? Do you believe humans have a duty to protect species that are not useful to us? How might attitudes towards nature change in the future?

The table below shows the percentage of land covered by forest in three countries in 1990, 2005 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Country | 1990 | 2005 | 2020
Country A | 45% | 41% | 38%
Country B | 22% | 25% | 30%
Country C | 60% | 58% | 57%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Relentless hunting reduced the population sharply.
The species was driven close to extinction.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Light
reading.passage:
For most of human history, the night was genuinely dark. Today, in many parts of the world, true darkness has become a rarity. Streetlights, illuminated signs, floodlit stadiums and the glow of windows combine to brighten the sky, a phenomenon scientists call light pollution. From space, the wealthy regions of the planet shine like scattered embers, and the band of the Milky Way, once visible to almost everyone, is now hidden from the majority of city dwellers.

This flood of artificial light has consequences that reach far beyond a spoiled view of the stars. Many animals depend on the natural rhythm of light and dark to guide their behaviour. Newly hatched sea turtles, for instance, instinctively head towards the brightest horizon, which over millions of years has reliably meant the open sea. Where brightly lit hotels line the coast, the hatchlings may crawl inland instead, with fatal results. Migrating birds, too, can be disoriented by illuminated buildings and collide with them in large numbers.

Insects are particularly vulnerable. Drawn helplessly towards lamps, they exhaust themselves circling the light or fall prey to waiting predators. Because insects sit near the base of countless food chains, their losses ripple outwards, affecting the birds and bats that feed on them. Some researchers suspect that light pollution is one of several overlooked pressures contributing to the worldwide decline of insect populations.

Unlike many environmental problems, this one has a refreshingly simple remedy. Light pollution disappears the instant a light is switched off, and much of it stems from poorly aimed fixtures that scatter their beams uselessly upwards. Shielding lamps so they point downwards, dimming them late at night, and using warmer colours that disturb wildlife less can all make a substantial difference. A handful of communities have even established 'dark sky reserves', where lighting is strictly controlled so that residents and visitors can once again experience a properly starry night.

questions.q: The Milky Way is now hidden from most people who live in cities.
Sea turtle hatchlings naturally move away from the brightest horizon.
Light pollution is the single biggest cause of the global decline in insects.
Why are insects described as 'particularly vulnerable'?
options: They are attracted to lamps and become easy prey or exhausted
They migrate over very long distances
They depend entirely on artificial light

questions.q: The writer suggests that light pollution differs from many environmental problems because it
options: can be reversed instantly by switching off lights

questions.q: Much light pollution comes from poorly aimed ________ that scatter beams upwards.
Areas where lighting is strictly controlled are known as dark sky ________.
Using warmer light colours can reduce the disturbance to wildlife.

listening.title: Radio Interview: Stargazing in the City
listening.script:
HOST: Welcome back. I'm here with astronomer Dr Lena Park to talk about stargazing for beginners. Lena, where should city dwellers start?
DR PARK: Thanks for having me. My first tip is to escape the worst of the city glow. Even driving twenty minutes out can make a huge difference. Second, let your eyes adjust. It takes about thirty minutes in the dark before you can see faint stars, so be patient.
HOST: And what about equipment?
DR PARK: Honestly, beginners don't need a telescope. A simple pair of binoculars reveals far more than people expect, and they cost much less. I'd also recommend a free app to help you identify constellations.
HOST: Any common mistake to avoid?
DR PARK: Yes. Don't look at your phone screen, because the bright light ruins your night vision instantly. If you must check the time, use a red light. Oh, and the best viewing is around the new moon, when the sky is darkest.
HOST: Wonderful advice. Thank you, Lena.

questions.q: Driving about ________ minutes out of the city helps reduce the glow.
Eyes take roughly ________ minutes to adjust to the dark.
What does Dr Park recommend for beginners instead of a telescope?
Dr Park says looking at a phone screen harms your night vision.
If you need to check the time, you should use a ________ light.
The best time for viewing is around the ________ moon.

speaking.q: Let's talk about the night sky. Can you see many stars where you live? Did you ever look at the stars when you were a child? Would you like to visit a place with very dark skies? Why or why not?

writing.q:
In many cities, artificial light at night is increasing rapidly. Some people think this brings important benefits, while others argue the harm it causes to people and wildlife is too great. To what extent do you agree or disagree that cities should reduce their use of artificial light at night?

Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: Coral Reefs Under Stress
reading.passage:
Coral reefs are often called the rainforests of the sea, and the comparison is apt. Although they cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor, they shelter roughly a quarter of all marine species at some point in their lives. They also protect coastlines from storms and support the livelihoods of millions of people through fishing and tourism. Yet these vibrant structures are surprisingly fragile, and across the world they are showing signs of severe stress.

A living coral is in fact a partnership. The coral animal builds a hard skeleton of limestone, while tiny algae live within its tissues. These algae use sunlight to produce food, much of which they share with their host, and they give the coral its brilliant colours. The relationship is finely tuned, and it breaks down when conditions change too quickly. When the surrounding water grows too warm, the coral expels the algae, turning ghostly white in a process known as bleaching. A bleached coral is not yet dead, but it is starving, and if the heat persists it will die.

Rising sea temperatures, driven by a warming climate, are the principal threat, but they are not the only one. As the ocean absorbs more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, the water becomes slightly more acidic, making it harder for corals to build their skeletons. Pollution from the land, overfishing and physical damage from anchors and careless divers add still further pressure.

Researchers are racing to respond. Some are breeding corals that can tolerate warmer water, then transplanting them onto damaged reefs. Others are establishing protected zones where fishing is banned, allowing reefs to recover undisturbed. Such efforts are valuable, but most scientists agree they can only buy time. The long-term survival of the world's reefs, they warn, depends above all on reducing the greenhouse gas emissions that are heating the seas.

questions.q: Coral reefs cover less than one per cent of the ocean floor.
A coral that has bleached is already dead.
Tourism causes more damage to reefs than fishing does.
What gives a healthy coral its bright colours?
options: The algae living in its tissues

questions.q: Why does more carbon dioxide in the ocean harm corals?
options: It makes the water more acidic, hindering skeleton growth

questions.q: When water becomes too warm, coral expels its algae in a process called ________.
Some researchers are breeding corals that can ________ warmer water.
Most scientists believe reef survival depends mainly on cutting greenhouse gas emissions.

listening.title: Student Discussion: A Marine Biology Project
listening.script:
MAYA: Hi Josh, shall we plan our marine biology project? It's due in three weeks.
JOSH: Sure. I think we should focus on coral bleaching, it's such a hot topic.
MAYA: Agreed. So, what's the structure? I suggest we split it into three parts: causes, effects, and possible solutions.
JOSH: Sounds good. I'm happy to take the causes section. Could you handle the solutions part?
MAYA: Of course. And maybe we do the effects together. For data, the university library has some great journals, but we'll also need recent figures.
JOSH: I found a government database that updates its sea temperature records every month. That should be useful.
MAYA: Excellent. One more thing, the lecturer wants a presentation as well as the written report, right?
JOSH: Yes, ten minutes each. Let's practise the week before so we're not nervous.
MAYA: Good plan. Let's meet again next Tuesday at the library, say two o'clock.
JOSH: Perfect. I'll bring my laptop.

reading.title: The Quiet Spread of the Smart Speaker
reading.passage:
A decade ago, the idea of talking to a small cylinder on the kitchen counter and expecting a sensible reply would have struck most people as faintly absurd. Today, voice-controlled smart speakers sit in millions of homes, used to set timers, check the weather, play music and switch off the lights. Their rapid adoption is one of the clearest signs of how quickly an unfamiliar technology can become part of ordinary domestic life.

Manufacturers initially marketed the devices as convenient assistants, but their real appeal has proved harder to pin down. Surveys suggest that many owners value the speakers less for any single task than for the sense that the home has become responsive and effortless. Households with young children, in particular, report that voice commands suit users who cannot yet type or read a screen.

The technology is not without its critics. Because the devices listen for a wake word, a microphone is always active, and recordings are sometimes sent to company servers to be analysed. Privacy campaigners argue that consumers underestimate how much information they are handing over in exchange for convenience. Manufacturers respond that audio is only stored after the wake word is detected, though investigations have occasionally found that devices misfire and record private conversations by mistake.

Researchers studying the long-term effects are cautious. Some worry that constant access to instant answers may weaken the habit of working problems out independently, especially among children. Others point out that earlier predictions of harm from new household technologies, from television to the internet, were frequently exaggerated. What seems certain is that the smart speaker, once a novelty, has settled into the background of daily life with remarkably little resistance.

questions.q: Smart speakers were widely accepted by households within a short period.
Manufacturers were confident from the start about exactly why people would value the devices.
Smart speakers are more popular in cities than in rural areas.
According to the passage, why do families with young children find the speakers useful?
options: The devices are cheaper for large households
Voice commands suit users who cannot type or read screens
They reduce the need for parental supervision

questions.q: The microphone stays active because the device listens for a ________ word.
What is the researchers' overall attitude to long-term effects?
options: They are certain the devices cause harm
They refuse to study the question

questions.q: The writer suggests past fears about new household technologies were often overstated.

listening.title: Setting Up a New Internet Account
listening.script:
AGENT: Good morning, you've reached Brightline Broadband. How can I help?
CUSTOMER: Hi, I've just moved house and I'd like to set up a new internet account.
AGENT: Of course. Can I take your full name?
CUSTOMER: It's Daniel Fisher. That's F-I-S-H-E-R.
AGENT: Thank you. And the new address?
CUSTOMER: Fourteen Maple Road, in Bristol.
AGENT: Lovely. We have two packages. The standard one is twenty-five pounds a month, and the faster fibre package is thirty-eight pounds.
CUSTOMER: I work from home, so I'll take the fibre one.
AGENT: Good choice. An engineer will need to visit. The earliest date is the fifteenth of March, between nine and twelve.
CUSTOMER: The morning's fine.
AGENT: Great. And a contact number?
CUSTOMER: It's oh seven, double six, four, three, one, nine, eight, two.
AGENT: Perfect. You'll receive a confirmation email within one hour.

speaking.q: Let's talk about technology at home. Do you use any voice-controlled devices, such as a smart speaker? How often do you use the internet at home, and what for? Has technology changed the way your family spends time together?

Device | 2018 | 2024

reading.title: Working From Anywhere
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the idea of work was tied to a particular place: an office, a factory or a shop. The arrival of fast home internet and cheap laptops loosened that link, and a global health crisis in 2020 broke it almost overnight. Millions of employees who had never worked outside an office discovered that, for many tasks, the office was not strictly necessary.

The benefits were quickly obvious. Workers saved hours each week that had previously been spent commuting, and many reported sleeping better and exercising more. Employers, for their part, found they could reduce spending on expensive city-centre buildings. Some companies announced that staff could work from anywhere in the country, or even abroad, provided the work was completed.

Yet the picture was not uniformly positive. Younger employees, in particular, complained that working from home left them isolated and made it harder to learn from more experienced colleagues. Casual conversations in corridors and shared kitchens, it turned out, had quietly served an important purpose: they spread knowledge and built trust in ways that scheduled video calls struggled to replace.

A further concern was that the boundary between work and home life became blurred. Without the journey home to mark the end of the day, some workers found themselves answering messages late into the evening. Managers began to talk about a 'right to disconnect', and a few governments considered laws to protect it. By the middle of the decade, most organisations had settled on a compromise known as hybrid working, with staff spending part of the week at home and part in a shared office. Whether this arrangement proves durable, or merely a stage on the way to something else, remains to be seen.

questions.q: Before 2020, most office employees had already worked remotely at some point.
Some employers were able to spend less on office buildings.
Remote work led to higher salaries for most employees.
What problem did younger workers especially report?
options: Feeling isolated and learning less from colleagues
Too many video calls each day

questions.q: Informal chats in corridors helped spread knowledge and build ________.
What is the 'right to disconnect' concerned with?
options: Protecting workers from working late into the evening

questions.q: The writer is certain that hybrid working will last permanently.

listening.title: Campus Co-working Space Induction
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the new student co-working space on level two. I'll run through a few things before you start using it. The space is open from seven in the morning until eleven at night, seven days a week. Outside those hours you'll need a staff pass, which most of you won't have. There are sixty desks in total, and around half of them have monitors you can plug your laptop into. If you need one of those, get here early, because they go fast. Printing is available at the back, and it costs eight cents per page in black and white. To connect to the wifi, the network name is StudyZone, and the password is on the card by the door. One last thing: please don't eat hot food at the desks. There's a kitchen down the hall with a microwave you're welcome to use. Any questions, just ask at the help desk near the lift.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Streaming
reading.passage:
Streaming a film or a song feels weightless. There is no disc to manufacture, no packaging to throw away and nothing to gather dust on a shelf. For this reason, many people assume that digital entertainment is environmentally harmless. The reality is more complicated, and the difference lies in the vast, unseen infrastructure that makes streaming possible.

Every time a video is played, data travels from a distant data centre, across networks, to a screen. The data centres themselves are enormous buildings packed with servers that must run continuously and be kept cool, both of which consume large amounts of electricity. As the global appetite for high-definition and ultra-high-definition video has grown, so too has the energy required to deliver it.

Estimating the precise footprint of streaming is surprisingly difficult, and early figures circulated in the press turned out to be wildly inaccurate. Some widely repeated claims compared an hour of streaming to driving a car for several kilometres; later, more careful studies found the true figure to be far smaller. The honest answer is that the impact depends heavily on the device, the screen size and the source of the local electricity.

What is clear is that small choices add up across billions of users. Watching on a phone rather than a large television, lowering the video quality when high definition is not needed, and downloading a favourite programme once instead of streaming it repeatedly can all reduce demand. Some companies have responded by powering their data centres with renewable energy, which removes much of the concern. Ultimately, streaming is not the environmental villain it was once portrayed to be, but neither is it the cost-free pleasure it appears to be on the surface.

questions.q: Many people wrongly assume streaming has no environmental impact.
Data centres need a lot of electricity for running and cooling servers.
Data centres employ more workers than traditional film studios.
What does the passage say about early media claims on streaming's footprint?
options: They turned out to be very inaccurate

questions.q: The environmental impact depends on the device, the screen size and the source of the local ________.
Which action does the writer suggest can reduce demand?
options: Watching on a phone instead of a large TV

questions.q: The writer concludes that streaming is completely free of environmental cost.

listening.title: A Talk on Data Centres
listening.script: LECTURER: Today I want to give you a quick overview of how a modern data centre works, because most of us rely on them every day without ever seeing one. A data centre is essentially a large building full of computers, called servers, that store and process information. A medium-sized facility might contain around ten thousand servers running at once. The biggest challenge is not the computing itself but heat. Servers generate a great deal of warmth, and if they overheat they fail, so roughly forty per cent of a centre's electricity can go on cooling alone. For years the standard solution was air conditioning, but newer designs use outside air, or even cold seawater, to cut energy use. One company in Norway built its centre inside a former mine to take advantage of the cool, stable temperature underground. The lesson here is that location matters enormously. A well-placed facility can use a fraction of the power of a poorly placed one. We'll look at three case studies next week.

What does the lecturer say is the biggest challenge?
Around ________ per cent of electricity can be used for cooling.

In what ways might people be encouraged to use digital services more responsibly? Do you believe new technologies generally help or harm the environment in the long run?

Function | Share of electricity

reading.title: When Machines Recognise Faces
reading.passage:
Facial recognition technology, once confined to science fiction, now unlocks phones, speeds passengers through airports and helps police search crowds. The underlying idea is simple: a computer measures the distances between features on a face and compares the result with images in a database. The accuracy of these systems has improved dramatically over the past decade, but their growing presence has provoked an equally sharp debate about how, and whether, they should be used.

Supporters point to clear practical gains. At busy airports, automated gates can process travellers in seconds, and missing children have been located in crowds where human observers would have failed. In each case, the appeal is the same: a machine can scan thousands of faces far faster than any person.

Critics, however, raise two distinct objections. The first concerns accuracy. Studies have repeatedly shown that some systems perform less reliably on women and on people with darker skin, partly because the images used to train them were not sufficiently varied. A wrongful match in a policing context can have serious consequences for an innocent person. The second objection is broader and concerns the very idea of being identified automatically in public. Even a perfectly accurate system, critics argue, would allow authorities to track an individual's movements across an entire city, eroding the ordinary expectation of anonymity in public life.

Governments have responded in strikingly different ways. Some cities have banned the use of the technology by public bodies altogether, while others have embraced it with few restrictions. A middle path, favoured by several regulators, would permit its use only for specific, serious purposes and under independent oversight. What is not in dispute is that the technology is advancing faster than the rules meant to govern it, leaving societies to decide, often after the fact, where the limits should lie.

questions.q: Facial recognition works by measuring distances between features on a face.
The accuracy of these systems has remained unchanged over the last ten years.
Most travellers prefer automated gates to human staff at airports.
Why do some systems perform less reliably on certain groups?
options: The cameras are of poor quality
The training images were not varied enough

questions.q: Critics worry the technology could erode the expectation of ________ in public life.
What 'middle path' do several regulators favour?
options: A complete ban on the technology
Use only for serious purposes under independent oversight

questions.q: The writer states that the technology is developing faster than the rules controlling it.

listening.title: Seminar Discussion on Surveillance
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, you've both read the chapter on public surveillance. Maya, what stood out for you?
MAYA: I was surprised by the scale, honestly. The chapter says one large city has over half a million cameras now.
TUTOR: That's right, and the number is rising. Tom, did anything trouble you?
TOM: The accuracy gap, definitely. The author cites a study where error rates were almost five times higher for some groups than for others.
TUTOR: A crucial point. And what did the author suggest as a remedy?
MAYA: Independent oversight, mainly. She argued that no single agency should both run the system and judge whether it's fair.
TOM: I'd add transparency. People should at least know when and where they're being recorded.
TUTOR: Good. For next week, I'd like a two-page summary comparing two cities. Pick one that restricts the technology and one that doesn't. Email it to me by Friday at the latest.
MAYA: Will do.

reading.title: The Promise and Peril of Online Reviews
reading.passage:
Before the internet, a traveller choosing a hotel relied on a guidebook, a travel agent or the recommendation of a friend. Today, that same traveller can read the opinions of thousands of previous guests in a matter of seconds. Online reviews have become one of the most influential forces in modern commerce, shaping decisions about everything from restaurants and films to surgeons and schools. Their rise reflects a deeper shift: trust has moved away from official experts and towards the collective judgement of ordinary consumers.

The appeal is easy to understand. A single review may be biased or mistaken, but the average of many tends, in theory, to reveal something close to the truth. Businesses that consistently disappoint their customers can no longer hide behind clever advertising, and even small enterprises can build a reputation purely on the quality of what they offer.

In practice, however, the system is vulnerable to manipulation. Some companies pay for glowing reviews of their own products, while others post negative ones about competitors. Detecting these fakes is a constant battle, and the platforms that host reviews invest heavily in software designed to spot suspicious patterns. Even genuine reviews can mislead, because the people most motivated to write are often those who were either delighted or furious, leaving the quieter majority unheard.

There is also a subtler cost. When every purchase is preceded by an anxious search through ratings, the simple act of choosing can become stressful, and consumers may defer endlessly to the crowd rather than trusting their own taste. Reviews, in short, have democratised information in a way that would have astonished an earlier generation, yet they have also created new forms of doubt. The challenge for the reader is no longer finding opinions, but judging which of them deserve to be believed.

questions.q: Online reviews influence decisions in many different fields, not just travel.
The writer says a single review is always reliable.
Most online reviews are written by professional critics.
According to the passage, why might genuine reviews still mislead?
options: They are always written by competitors
Writers tend to be those who were delighted or furious
They are too short to be useful

questions.q: Platforms invest heavily in ________ designed to spot suspicious patterns.
What 'subtler cost' does the writer identify?
options: Reviews are too expensive to access
Choosing can become stressful and people defer to the crowd
Reviews take too long to load

questions.q: The writer concludes that the main difficulty now is judging which opinions to believe.

listening.title: Lecture on Trust in the Digital Economy
listening.script: LECTURER: In this final session we turn to trust, which I'd argue is the hidden currency of the digital economy. Think about it: when you buy from a stranger online, you're trusting a system you can't see. Reviews are one mechanism that makes this possible. But how much can we trust the reviews themselves? Researchers in one study examined around twelve thousand product reviews and estimated that nearly one in five showed signs of being fake. That's a striking figure. Interestingly, the fakes weren't always positive; about a third were negative reviews aimed at rivals. So what can be done? The study identified three useful signals. First, the timing — genuine reviews trickle in, whereas fakes often arrive in bursts. Second, the language — fakes tend to be vaguer and more emotional. Third, the reviewer's history — a brand-new account with a single five-star review deserves suspicion. None of these is decisive alone, but together they help. We'll explore the ethics of all this in your seminar on Thursday.

questions.q: The lecturer calls trust the hidden ________ of the digital economy.
The study examined around ________ product reviews.

What was true of about a third of the fake reviews?

they were negative and aimed at rivals

What does the lecturer say about the three signals?
options: none is decisive alone but together they help

Source | 2014 | 2024

Advertising | 13% | 11%

Write at least 150 words.

reading.title: Why We Remember Faces
reading.passage:
Most people can recall the face of someone they met only once, even years later, yet they struggle to remember that person's name. Psychologists believe this gap exists because the human brain evolved to treat faces as uniquely important social signals. A specialised region near the base of the brain, often called the fusiform face area, becomes highly active whenever we look at a face. This region does not respond in the same powerful way to ordinary objects such as chairs or cars.

Researchers who study recognition argue that faces are processed as whole patterns rather than as separate parts. When a photograph of a familiar face is turned upside down, people find it surprisingly difficult to identify, because the usual arrangement of eyes, nose and mouth is disrupted. This effect is far weaker for objects, which suggests that the brain handles faces using a special holistic strategy.

Names, by contrast, are arbitrary labels with no natural link to a person's appearance. There is nothing about the sound of a name that connects it to a particular face, so the brain has no helpful pattern to rely on. Studies show that people remember a stranger's job or hobby more easily than their name, simply because such details can be linked to images or stories.

Some scientists suggest that this difference has practical value. In early human groups, instantly recognising a face as friend or stranger could affect survival, while remembering an abstract label was far less urgent. Whatever the explanation, the experience of knowing a face yet forgetting its owner's name remains one of the most common and frustrating features of everyday memory.

questions.q: People generally find it easier to remember names than faces.
The fusiform face area reacts strongly to everyday objects like cars.
Turning a photograph of a face upside down makes it harder to recognise.
The fusiform face area is larger in women than in men.
According to the passage, faces are processed as
Why are names hard to remember, according to the text?
options: They have no natural link to appearance

questions.q: In early human groups, quickly recognising a face as friend or stranger could affect ________.
People recall a stranger's job or ________ more easily than their name.

listening.title: Booking a Memory Study Session
listening.script:

RECEPTIONIST: Just bring your student card and a pair of glasses if you wear them. You'll be paid fifteen pounds for taking part.

CALLER: That's helpful, thank you. Should I arrive early?

RECEPTIONIST: Yes, please come ten minutes before to fill in a consent form.

CALLER: Will do. Thanks very much.

reading.title: The Comfort of Routine
reading.passage:
Many people feel calmer when their days follow a familiar pattern. Eating breakfast at the same time, taking the same route to work, or reading before bed can all provide a quiet sense of security. Psychologists explain that routines reduce the number of small decisions we must make, freeing mental energy for more demanding tasks. When everyday choices become automatic, the mind is less likely to feel overloaded.

Research into stress suggests that predictability itself can be soothing. In one study, volunteers who knew exactly when a mildly unpleasant noise would occur reported less anxiety than those who heard the same noise at random moments. Knowing what is coming, even when it is not pleasant, appears to give people a feeling of control. This sense of control is closely linked to lower levels of stress hormones in the body.

However, the same researchers warn that routines can become limiting. People who depend too heavily on fixed habits may feel deeply uncomfortable when circumstances change, such as during travel or a new job. A degree of flexibility, they argue, helps individuals adapt without losing the benefits of structure. The healthiest approach seems to combine reliable daily anchors with a willingness to try new things.

Interestingly, routines also shape how we remember time. When every day is nearly identical, weeks can seem to blur together, and months pass with little to distinguish them. Adding occasional novelty, such as visiting an unfamiliar place, creates stronger memories and can make life feel richer and longer. In this way, the comfort of routine and the value of variety are not opposites but partners in a balanced mind.

questions.q: Routines can reduce the number of small decisions a person makes each day.
Volunteers who knew when the noise would come felt more anxious.
A sense of control is connected to lower stress hormone levels.
The study on noise was carried out in several different countries.
According to the passage, too much reliance on routine can
What makes weeks seem to blur together?
Routines free mental energy for more ________ tasks.
Adding occasional ________ creates stronger memories.

listening.title: A Talk on Daily Habits
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today I want to share a few simple ideas about building better daily habits. Many of us try to change everything at once, and that's usually why we fail. Instead, start with one small action. For example, if you want to read more, begin with just five pages a day. It sounds tiny, but consistency matters far more than size.

The second tip is to link a new habit to something you already do. After you make your morning coffee, do ten minutes of stretching. The coffee acts as a reminder, so you don't have to rely on willpower alone. Researchers call this habit stacking.

Third, track your progress on a simple calendar. Put a cross on each day you succeed. After about three weeks, most people start to feel the habit becoming natural. Don't worry if you miss a day; just avoid missing two in a row.

Finally, be patient. Lasting change takes time, often around two months before a habit feels automatic. Small steps, repeated daily, add up to remarkable results.

questions.q: Why do many people fail to change habits, according to the speaker?
options: they try to change everything at once

questions.q: To read more, the speaker suggests starting with ________ pages a day.
Linking a new habit to an existing one is called habit ________.
You should track progress on a simple ________.
After about how long does a habit start to feel natural?
A habit may take around ________ months to feel automatic.

speaking.q:
Describe a daily routine that you enjoy. You should say:
- what the routine is
- when and where you do it
- how long you have followed it
and explain why this routine is important to you.

writing.q:
Some people believe that following a fixed daily routine is the best way to live a productive life. Others think that being flexible and spontaneous leads to greater happiness. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: A morning routine can reduce stress.
Predictability can be soothing.

A degree of flexibility helps people adapt.

reading.title: The Bystander Effect
reading.passage:
When an emergency happens in a public place, we might assume that the more people who are present, the more likely someone will help. Surprisingly, psychologists have found the opposite to be true. The presence of many onlookers often makes any single person less likely to act. This puzzling pattern is known as the bystander effect.

One explanation is the diffusion of responsibility. When a crowd witnesses an emergency, each individual assumes that someone else will step forward. Because the duty to help feels shared among many people, no one feels personally obliged to take action. In contrast, a lone witness knows that if they do nothing, nobody else will, and so they tend to respond more quickly.

A second factor is social influence. People often look to others to decide how to behave in uncertain situations. If everyone around appears calm, an observer may conclude that the situation is not really an emergency at all. This collective hesitation can delay help, sometimes with serious consequences. The fear of embarrassment, of overreacting in front of strangers, can also hold people back.

Researchers stress that the bystander effect is not a sign of cruelty. Most people genuinely want to help others. The problem lies in the way crowds shape individual judgement. Encouragingly, studies show that simply being aware of the effect can reduce it. When people understand why they might hesitate, they are more willing to overcome that hesitation. Some training programmes now teach individuals to act decisively, for example by pointing directly at one person and asking them specifically for assistance, which removes the sense of shared but unclaimed responsibility.

questions.q: A larger crowd usually makes a single person more likely to help.
A lone witness tends to respond more quickly than someone in a crowd.
The bystander effect shows that most people are cruel.
The bystander effect was first identified in the 1960s.
What is the diffusion of responsibility?
options: each person assuming someone else will act

questions.q: According to the passage, awareness of the effect
In uncertain situations, people often look to ________ to decide how to behave.
Training programmes teach people to point at one person and ask them ________ for help.

listening.title: Volunteer First-Aid Training
listening.script:
TRAINER: Right, thanks for coming to the first-aid volunteer session. Let me run through the key points before we practise. First, always check that the area is safe before you approach. You can't help anyone if you become a casualty yourself.

VOLUNTEER: What if there's a crowd around?

TRAINER: Good question. In a crowd, take charge clearly. Point at one person and say, you, in the blue jacket, call an ambulance. Being specific is vital, because if you just shout for help, everyone assumes somebody else will do it.

VOLUNTEER: And what number do we call?

TRAINER: In the UK it's nine nine nine. Stay calm and give your exact location.

VOLUNTEER: Should we move the injured person?

TRAINER: Only if they're in immediate danger, for example near a fire. Otherwise keep them still. Finally, remember to reassure them; a calm voice makes a huge difference. The full course lasts six weeks, with one session each Wednesday evening.

questions.q: What should you do first at an emergency?
In a crowd, you should point at one person and be ________.
In the UK, the emergency number is ________.
When should you move an injured person?

reading.title: The Science of First Impressions
reading.passage:
Within a fraction of a second of meeting someone, we form an opinion about them. Long before we have heard a single word, our minds have already judged whether the stranger seems trustworthy, confident or friendly. Psychologists who study these rapid judgements call them first impressions, and research suggests they are formed astonishingly quickly, sometimes in under a tenth of a second.

These instant assessments are based largely on appearance, particularly the face. Features such as a relaxed expression or steady eye contact tend to create an impression of warmth, while a tense face may signal the opposite. Although such snap judgements are often inaccurate, they can be remarkably difficult to change. Once an initial impression has formed, people tend to interpret later information in ways that confirm what they already believe.

This tendency, known as confirmation bias, helps explain why first impressions are so powerful. If we decide that someone is unfriendly, we may notice every cool remark while overlooking their kinder actions. In this way, an early misjudgement can quietly shape an entire relationship. Job interviews, where decisions are sometimes influenced within minutes, show how this can carry real consequences.

Yet first impressions are not fixed forever. Studies indicate that repeated, positive contact can gradually reshape an opinion, especially when the new behaviour clearly contradicts the original belief. Being aware of how quickly and unfairly we judge others can also make us more cautious. Rather than trusting that first flash of feeling, we can choose to stay open, gathering further evidence before reaching firm conclusions about the people we meet.

questions.q: First impressions can form in less than a tenth of a second.
Snap judgements about people are always accurate.
Confirmation bias makes people notice information that fits their existing belief.
Most job interviews last less than ten minutes.
According to the passage, first impressions are based largely on
What can gradually reshape an opinion of someone?
Steady ________ contact tends to create an impression of warmth.
Being aware of how we judge others can make us more ________.

listening.title: Preparing for a Job Interview
listening.script:
ADVISOR: Hi there, come on in. You said you've got an interview coming up?

STUDENT: Yeah, on Monday, for an internship at a marketing firm.

ADVISOR: Great. Let's go over a few tips. First impressions matter a lot, so arrive about fifteen minutes early. It shows you're reliable.

STUDENT: Got it. What about clothes?

ADVISOR: Dress smartly but comfortably. For marketing, a neat shirt is fine; you don't need a full suit. The key is to look tidy and feel confident.

STUDENT: Should I bring anything?

ADVISOR: Bring two copies of your resume and a notebook. And prepare one or two questions to ask them at the end. It shows genuine interest.

STUDENT: I always get nervous, though.

ADVISOR: That's normal. Take a slow breath before you answer, and remember to smile. A warm smile creates a positive impression instantly. Finally, send a short thank-you email within twenty-four hours. Most candidates forget that, so it really makes you stand out.

reading.title: Why Music Moves Us
reading.passage:
Almost every known human culture makes music, and almost everyone responds to it emotionally. A slow melody in a minor key can bring a listener close to tears, while a lively rhythm can lift the spirits within seconds. Psychologists have long been fascinated by this power, asking why patterns of sound should have such a strong hold on human feeling.

Part of the answer lies in expectation. As we listen, our brains constantly predict what note or beat will come next, based on patterns we have heard throughout our lives. When a piece of music fulfils these predictions, we feel a pleasant sense of resolution. When it surprises us, perhaps with an unexpected chord, the small jolt can be thrilling. Skilled composers play with these expectations, delaying or twisting them to create tension and release.

Music also connects strongly to memory. A song heard during an important moment in our youth can, decades later, instantly summon the emotions of that time. This is why a single familiar tune can transport an older listener back to a particular summer or friendship. Researchers believe music and personal memory are stored in overlapping regions of the brain, which may explain their tight bond.

There is a social side as well. Singing or playing together encourages people to move and breathe in time, which appears to strengthen feelings of trust and belonging. From ancient ceremonies to modern concerts, shared music has helped bind communities together. Some scientists even suggest that this group-bonding function, rather than mere entertainment, is the deeper reason music became such a universal part of human life.

questions.q: Almost every known human culture creates music.
Our brains stop predicting notes once a song becomes familiar.
Music and personal memory may be stored in overlapping brain regions.
Minor keys are more popular than major keys worldwide.
According to the passage, a pleasant sense of resolution comes when music
What does singing together appear to strengthen?
An unexpected chord can produce a small ________ that feels thrilling.
Some scientists suggest music's deeper purpose is group-________.

listening.title: A Lecture on Music and the Brain
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we'll explore why music has such a strong effect on the human brain. Let's begin with a simple fact: listening to music we enjoy releases a chemical called dopamine, the same substance linked to pleasure from food or rewards.

Now, interestingly, the peak of pleasure often comes just before the most exciting moment in a song, not during it. Your brain is anticipating the climax, and that anticipation itself feels good.

Music also affects movement. It's almost impossible to keep completely still when a strong rhythm plays. This is because areas controlling movement activate automatically, even if you're sitting down.

There are practical uses too. Hospitals increasingly use music to help patients relax before surgery, and it can reduce the amount of pain medication needed by roughly twenty percent. Music therapy is also used to support people recovering from stroke.

For next week, please read chapter four and prepare a short summary. We'll discuss how rhythm helps memory in our seminar on Friday.

reading.title: The Silk Roads and the Spread of Ideas
reading.passage:
For more than a thousand years, a loose network of trade routes linked the markets of China with those of the Mediterranean. Historians have come to call this network the Silk Roads, though silk was only one of many goods that travelled along it. Spices, glass, paper and precious metals all passed from hand to hand across deserts and mountain ranges, often changing owners many times before reaching their final destination.

The distances involved were enormous, and few merchants made the entire journey themselves. Instead, goods moved in stages, with traders specialising in particular sections of the route. A bolt of cloth might be carried by a Chinese caravan to a Central Asian city, sold there to a local dealer, and then sold again to a Persian merchant who took it further west. This system of relay trade meant that profits were shared among many participants.

Yet the Silk Roads carried far more than merchandise. Religions, scientific knowledge and artistic styles also spread along the same paths. Buddhism, for instance, moved from India into China largely through the activity of travelling monks who accompanied trading caravans. Techniques for making paper, first developed in China, eventually reached the Islamic world and later Europe, transforming the way information was recorded and shared.

Disease, unfortunately, travelled just as easily as ideas. The same connections that allowed goods and beliefs to circulate also enabled epidemics to move between distant populations. Some historians argue that the great outbreaks of plague in the fourteenth century were carried, at least in part, along these very routes. The Silk Roads, in this sense, were a powerful reminder that connection always brings risks as well as rewards.

questions.q: Silk was the only important product traded along the Silk Roads.
Most merchants travelled the whole length of the route personally.
The relay system meant that profit was divided between numerous traders.
Buddhist monks often travelled together with trading caravans.
Chinese paper-making techniques reached Europe before they reached the Islamic world.
The plague outbreaks of the fourteenth century killed more people in China than in Europe.
What does the passage suggest about the Silk Roads overall?
options: They were used only by Chinese merchants
They brought both benefits and dangers
They were mainly a military route

questions.q: Goods often changed owners many times before reaching their final ________.

CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Okafor. That's O-K-A-F-O-R.
RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. The workshop runs on Saturday the fourteenth, starting at half past ten.
CALLER: And how long does it last?
RECEPTIONIST: About two hours, so it finishes at half past twelve. The cost is fifteen pounds for adults.
CALLER: That's fine. Do I need to bring anything?

CALLER: Great. Is there parking nearby?
RECEPTIONIST: There's a car park on Market Street, about five minutes' walk away.
CALLER: Perfect. Can I pay on the day?
RECEPTIONIST: Yes, by card only, I'm afraid. We don't accept cash for workshops.
CALLER: No problem. Thank you very much.
