reading.title: The Printing Revolution in Europe
reading.passage:
Before the middle of the fifteenth century, books in Europe were produced by hand. Copying a single volume could take a skilled scribe many months, and the cost of such labour meant that books remained rare and expensive. Most were owned by churches, universities or wealthy individuals, and ordinary people had little direct contact with the written word.

This situation changed dramatically with the development of printing using movable metal type. The new technique, usually associated with the German craftsman Johannes Gutenberg, allowed identical pages to be produced in large numbers at a fraction of the previous cost. A press that might once have produced a handful of pages a day could now turn out hundreds, and the same letters could be rearranged endlessly to set new texts.

The consequences were far reaching. As printed books became cheaper, they reached a far wider audience than ever before. Literacy rates began to climb, and reading gradually ceased to be the privilege of a narrow elite. Ideas that might once have circulated slowly among a few scholars could now be distributed across a continent within a single generation.

Not everyone welcomed these changes. Some authorities feared that the rapid spread of printed material would allow dangerous or heretical opinions to reach the public, and various attempts were made to control what could be published. Despite such efforts, the flow of printed information proved almost impossible to stop. By the end of the sixteenth century, the printed book had become a permanent feature of European life, reshaping religion, science and politics alike.

questions.q: Before printing, copying one book could take a scribe several months.
Handwritten books were mostly owned by ordinary working people.
Movable type allowed the same letters to be reused for different texts.
Gutenberg earned a large fortune from his printing press.

Why did some authorities try to control printing?
options: They feared the spread of dangerous opinions

questions.q: What is the main idea of the final paragraph?
options: Printing became a lasting and influential force

GUIDE: Undergraduates can borrow up to twelve items, and you can keep most of them for three weeks. If you go straight ahead, you'll find the silent study area on the ground floor. Please remember, no talking and no phones there.
STUDENT: Where are the group study rooms?
GUIDE: Those are on the first floor, and you can book them online up to a week in advance. There are eight rooms in total. On the second floor we have the special collections, which include rare historical documents. You can only view those by appointment. Finally, the café is in the basement, open until eight in the evening. Any questions? No? Then let's begin.

reading.title: The Rise and Fall of Ancient Cities
reading.passage:
Throughout history, great cities have risen, flourished and then declined, sometimes vanishing almost entirely from the landscape. The reasons for their rise are often clear enough: a favourable location near water, fertile soil for agriculture, or a position on an important trade route. Once established, a successful city could attract people, wealth and skilled workers, growing steadily in size and influence over many generations.

The causes of decline, however, are frequently more complex and harder to identify. In some cases a single dramatic event, such as an earthquake or an invasion, brought a city to a sudden end. More often, decline was a gradual process resulting from several overlapping pressures. Trade routes might shift, leaving a once-busy market town isolated. Rivers could change course or dry up, depriving a settlement of the water on which it depended.

Environmental factors have attracted particular attention from modern researchers. Intensive farming and the clearing of forests sometimes exhausted the surrounding land, reducing the food supply available to a growing population. When harvests failed repeatedly, people had little choice but to move elsewhere, and the city's population shrank year by year.

Political instability could accelerate this process. Weak or divided leadership made it difficult to organise defence, maintain public buildings or respond effectively to crises. A city already weakened by economic or environmental problems was far less able to survive a period of poor government. In the end, the abandonment of a great city was rarely the result of one cause alone, but rather of many difficulties combining over time.

questions.q: Cities often grew because of a good location near water or trade routes.
The reasons for a city's decline are usually simpler than the reasons for its rise.

options: It made cities easier to defend
It made it harder to handle crises

questions.q: What is the main conclusion of the passage?
options: Cities fall for one clear reason

STUDENT: How long is the journey?
LECTURER: It's roughly an hour and a half, so we should arrive around ten. When we get there, you'll be divided into four groups, each led by one of the site supervisors. Now, a few practical points. Wear sturdy boots, because the ground can be muddy, and bring a hat and sunscreen, as there's very little shade. Lunch is not provided, so pack your own.
STUDENT: Can we take photographs?
LECTURER: Yes, but only in the marked areas. Please don't photograph the storage tent. And remember to bring your notebook, as you'll need to record measurements. We'll return to campus by half past four. Any questions can wait until the morning.

reading.title: The Age of Exploration
reading.passage:
During the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, European sailors undertook a series of long ocean voyages that gradually connected continents that had previously known little of one another. This period, often called the Age of Exploration, was driven by a mixture of motives. Some rulers hoped to find new sources of gold and spices; others were eager to spread their religion; and many ordinary sailors simply sought adventure and the chance of wealth.

These voyages would not have been possible without important advances in technology. New types of ship, capable of sailing against the wind, allowed crews to travel further and return more safely. Improved instruments helped navigators to estimate their position, and better maps recorded the coastlines that explorers discovered. Together, these developments transformed long-distance sea travel from a desperate gamble into a difficult but achievable enterprise.

The consequences of exploration were enormous and often deeply troubling. The arrival of Europeans in the Americas led to the exchange of plants, animals and diseases between continents that had been separated for thousands of years. While some of these exchanges, such as the spread of new food crops, eventually benefited millions, others were catastrophic. Diseases brought by Europeans devastated populations that had no resistance to them.

Historians continue to debate how this remarkable period should be understood. For some, it represents a triumph of human curiosity and courage. For others, it marks the beginning of centuries of conquest and exploitation. What is beyond dispute is that the voyages of this era reshaped the entire world, creating connections and conflicts whose effects are still felt today.

Better instruments helped sailors estimate where they were.

options: They all agree it was positive

listening.title: A Lecture on Early Navigation
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today I want to talk about how early sailors found their way across open water long before modern instruments existed. The first and oldest method was simply following the coastline. By keeping land in sight, sailors could recognize familiar landmarks and avoid getting lost. The obvious limitation, of course, was that this only worked close to shore.
Once ships ventured into open ocean, sailors relied heavily on the stars. The position of the North Star, in particular, gave a reliable indication of direction at night. During the day, the sun served a similar purpose, though it was harder to use precisely.
A major breakthrough came with the magnetic compass, which probably reached Europe from China around the twelfth century. For the first time, sailors could determine direction even when clouds hid the sky. Later, an instrument called the astrolabe allowed navigators to measure the height of the sun or a star above the horizon, which helped them estimate how far north or south they were. None of these methods was perfect, but together they made longer voyages possible.

reading.title: The Industrial Revolution and Everyday Life
reading.passage:
The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late eighteenth century, is often described in terms of machines, factories and economic growth. Yet for the millions of people who lived through it, the most powerful changes were felt in the rhythms of daily life. The way people worked, where they lived and how they spent their time were all transformed within a few generations.

Before industrialisation, most people lived in the countryside and worked on the land or in small workshops. Work followed the seasons and the hours of daylight, and families often laboured together at home. With the spread of factories, however, large numbers of people moved into rapidly growing towns in search of paid employment. There, work was governed not by the sun but by the clock, and the factory bell came to dictate the shape of the day.

Living conditions in the new industrial towns were frequently poor. Housing was built quickly and cheaply to accommodate the swelling population, and overcrowding was common. Clean water and proper sanitation were often lacking, and disease spread easily in such crowded districts. For many families, the move to the town brought regular wages but also new forms of hardship.

Over time, conditions slowly improved. Reformers campaigned for limits on working hours and for laws to protect children in factories. Local authorities gradually invested in water supplies, drains and public health. Although progress was uneven and frequently resisted, by the end of the nineteenth century the lives of ordinary working people in industrial towns had begun, in many respects, to improve.

questions.q: The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the late eighteenth century.
Before industrialisation, most people worked in large factories.
In factories, work was controlled by the clock rather than by daylight.
Housing in the new towns was usually spacious and well built.

Why did disease spread easily in industrial towns?
options: There was overcrowding and poor sanitation

questions.q: What does the final paragraph mainly describe?
options: Gradual improvements through reform and investment

listening.title: A Discussion About a History Project

TUTOR: So, Maya, how's your project on the Industrial Revolution coming along?
MAYA: Quite well, I think. I've decided to focus on living conditions in factory towns rather than the machines themselves.

MAYA: Mostly from old newspapers and a few local government reports. The library has digitised some records from around eighteen fifty.
TUTOR: Excellent. And how long is the essay meant to be?
MAYA: The limit is two thousand five hundred words, and it's due on the thirtieth of March.
TUTOR: Right. One piece of advice: don't just list facts. Try to argue a clear point. For example, you could compare conditions at the start and end of the century.
MAYA: That's helpful. Should I include images?
TUTOR: A couple of relevant ones are fine, but they don't count towards the word limit. Make sure you reference every source properly.
MAYA: I will. Thanks very much.

reading.title: Rethinking the Daily Step Target
reading.passage:
For more than two decades, the figure of ten thousand steps a day has been treated as a universal benchmark for good health. Yet the number did not originate in a laboratory. It can be traced to a Japanese marketing campaign in the 1960s, when a company sold a pedometer whose name loosely translated as "ten-thousand-step meter". The round, memorable figure stuck, and it gradually came to be repeated as though it were settled medical advice.

Recent research has begun to question whether the target is appropriate for everyone. A large study following older adults found that the sharpest reduction in early death occurred between roughly four thousand and seven thousand steps a day. Beyond that range, additional steps still brought benefits, but the gains levelled off rather than continuing to rise steeply. For many people, in other words, a more modest goal may deliver most of the advantage with far less discouragement.

Researchers also stress that intensity matters, not merely volume. A brisk ten-minute walk that raises the heart rate appears to do more for cardiovascular health than the same number of steps taken slowly throughout the day. This helps explain why two people with identical step counts can show very different fitness outcomes.

Public-health officials are cautious about abandoning the familiar slogan entirely. They argue that a clear, single number is easy to remember and motivates people who might otherwise do nothing. The challenge is to keep that simplicity while making clear that the precise figure is flexible, and that any increase in regular movement is worthwhile.

questions.q: The ten-thousand-step figure was first established through clinical research.
The name of the original pedometer referred to a number of steps.
The study of older adults found no health benefit beyond seven thousand steps.
The pedometer company later changed its product name after the research appeared.
According to the passage, what does recent research suggest about intensity?
options: It matters less than the total number of steps
It can affect fitness outcomes independently of step count
It only matters for older adults

questions.q: Why do public-health officials hesitate to drop the slogan?
options: A single number is easy to remember and motivating

questions.q: The original pedometer came from a Japanese ________ campaign in the 1960s.
In the older-adults study, the steepest drop in early death was between about four thousand and ________ thousand steps.

listening.title: Booking a Community Health Check
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Riverside Community Clinic, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a free health check, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. Can I take your full name?

CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Foster. F-O-S-T-E-R.

RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. And your date of birth?

CALLER: The twelfth of March, 1990.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. The check takes about thirty minutes and includes blood pressure and a cholesterol test. We have appointments on Tuesday afternoon or Thursday morning.

CALLER: Thursday morning suits me better.

RECEPTIONIST: I can offer nine fifteen.

CALLER: That's perfect.

RECEPTIONIST: One thing to note: please don't eat anything for eight hours before the cholesterol test. Water is fine.

CALLER: Right, I'll fast beforehand.

RECEPTIONIST: Great. The clinic is on Maple Street, just opposite the library. Bring a form of photo identification.

CALLER: Will do. Thank you very much.

questions.q: The caller's surname is spelled ________.
The health check lasts about ________ minutes.
Which appointment does the caller choose?
The appointment time is ________.
The caller must not eat for ________ hours before the cholesterol test.

speaking.q: Let's talk about exercise. How do you usually try to stay active during the week? Do you prefer exercising alone or with other people, and why?

writing.q:

Age group | 2010 | 2022

30-44 | 7,200 | 6,900
45-59 | 6,500 | 6,700
60+ | 4,900 | 5,600

reading.title: The Return of the Bicycle to City Streets
reading.passage:
When motor traffic first filled the world's cities, the bicycle was widely dismissed as a relic of a slower age. For much of the twentieth century, urban planning was shaped almost entirely around the car, and cycling was pushed to the margins. In recent years, however, a growing number of public-health experts have argued that the humble bicycle deserves a central place in the modern city.

The case rests partly on physical activity. Many adults struggle to find time for deliberate exercise, but cycling to work or school folds movement into the daily routine without demanding a separate hour at the gym. Studies in several European cities suggest that regular commuting cyclists have lower rates of obesity and heart disease than those who travel only by car.

There is also an environmental dimension. Bicycles produce no exhaust, take up little road space, and are far cheaper to maintain than vehicles. As cities grapple with both congestion and air pollution, planners increasingly see cycling networks as a way to address several problems at once.

Yet barriers remain. The single greatest obstacle, surveys repeatedly find, is fear of traffic. People who would happily cycle on a quiet path are often unwilling to share busy roads with lorries and cars. For this reason, the most successful schemes have invested heavily in protected lanes physically separated from motor traffic. Where such lanes appear, cycling rates tend to climb quickly, particularly among women, older riders, and families who had previously felt the roads were simply too dangerous.

questions.q: During the twentieth century, cities were mainly designed around the car.
Cycling requires people to set aside a separate hour for exercise.
European studies found commuting cyclists had higher rates of heart disease.
Electric bicycles have become the most popular type in these cities.
What does the passage identify as the biggest barrier to cycling?
What effect do protected lanes tend to have?
options: They raise cycling rates, especially among cautious groups

questions.q: Bicycles produce no ________ and take up little road space.
The most successful schemes invested heavily in ________ lanes separated from traffic.

listening.title: University Cycle Scheme Briefing
listening.script:
STAFF: Welcome, everyone, to this short briefing about our new campus cycle-hire scheme. Let me run through how it works.

First, registration. You sign up online using your student email, and there's a one-off fee of fifteen dollars for the whole year. Once you're registered, you'll receive a digital pass on your phone.

The bikes are kept at three docking stations: outside the main library, near the science building, and at the south gate. To unlock a bike, you simply scan the code on the handlebars with the app.

The first sixty minutes of every trip are free. After that, it's two dollars per hour, charged to your account at the end of the month.

A quick word on safety. Helmets are required by law here, and we lend them free of charge from the library desk. Please return the bike to any docking station when you're finished, and lock it properly, or the trip won't end.

If a bike has a fault, report it through the app so our team can collect it. Any questions, email the transport office. Thank you.

questions.q: Students register online using their student ________.
The annual registration fee is ________ dollars.
How many docking stations are there?
The first ________ minutes of every trip are free.
Where can students borrow a helmet free of charge?
A faulty bike should be reported through the ________.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you changed how you travel somewhere regularly. You should say:
- what change you made
- why you decided to make it
- how it affected your daily routine
and explain whether you would recommend the change to others.

writing.q:
Some people believe that city governments should spend more money building cycling lanes and less on roads for cars. Others argue that cars remain essential and should stay the priority. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

Critics dismissed cycling as old-fashioned.

reading.title: Why We Forget to Drink Water
reading.passage:
Hydration is one of the simplest health messages imaginable, yet it is surprisingly easy to get wrong. The body loses water continuously through breathing, sweating, and other ordinary processes, and even a mild shortfall can dull concentration and trigger headaches. Despite this, many people drift through the day in a state of low-level dehydration without ever feeling especially thirsty.

Part of the explanation lies in how the sense of thirst works. Thirst is a relatively slow alarm: by the time it registers strongly, the body is often already short of fluid. The signal also weakens with age, which is one reason older adults are particularly vulnerable. A further complication is that thirst can be mistaken for hunger, prompting people to reach for a snack when a glass of water would have served better.

The popular rule of eight glasses a day is, like many such figures, more convenient than precise. Individual needs vary with body size, climate, and activity. Someone exercising in hot weather may require far more, while a sedentary person in a cool office may need less. Importantly, a good share of daily fluid comes not from drinking at all but from food, especially fruit and vegetables with high water content.

For those who genuinely forget, researchers suggest simple anchors: a glass with breakfast, a bottle kept visible on the desk, or a sip after every visit to the kitchen. The aim is not to follow a rigid number but to build small habits that keep fluid topped up before thirst ever becomes urgent.

questions.q: Mild dehydration can affect concentration.
Thirst is described as a fast and reliable warning signal.
The sense of thirst can become stronger as people get older.
Drinking cold water is more effective than warm water for hydration.
According to the passage, the 'eight glasses a day' rule is best described as
Why might people eat a snack when they are actually short of fluid?
options: Thirst can be mistaken for hunger

questions.q: A good share of daily fluid comes from food, especially fruit and ________.
Researchers suggest keeping a bottle ________ on the desk as a reminder.

listening.title: Seminar: Hydration and Performance
listening.script:
TUTOR: So, in today's seminar we're looking at hydration and physical performance. Maya, you read the study on the cyclists, didn't you?

MAYA: Yes. The researchers tested twenty riders over a two-week period.

TUTOR: And what did they measure?

MAYA: Mainly endurance. They found that when riders lost about two percent of their body weight in fluid, their endurance dropped noticeably.

TUTOR: That's a useful figure. Tom, anything to add?

TOM: The interesting part for me was reaction time. Even mild dehydration slowed it, which has obvious implications beyond sport, for driving, say.

TUTOR: Good point. Now, a common myth is that thirst is a reliable guide. The paper challenges that, doesn't it, Maya?

MAYA: It does. The authors recommend drinking on a schedule during long exercise rather than waiting to feel thirsty.

TUTOR: Exactly. For your assignment, I'd like a five-hundred-word summary by Friday, focusing on the methods section. Email it to me, please.

MAYA: Will do.

questions.q: The study tested ________ riders.
The study lasted ________ weeks.
What was the main thing the study measured?
Endurance dropped when riders lost about ________ percent of body weight in fluid.
What do the authors recommend during long exercise?
The assignment summary must be ________ words long.

speaking.q: Let's discuss health habits more broadly. Why do you think people often know what is good for them but still fail to do it? What role should governments play in encouraging healthier behaviour?

writing.q:
The table below shows where adults in a survey said they got most of their daily fluid, comparing two countries. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Source | Country A (%) | Country B (%)
Plain water | 48 | 32
Tea or coffee | 22 | 38
Soft drinks | 14 | 9
Food (fruit/veg) | 16 | 21

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Hydration is a simple but easily neglected health message.
The body loses water continuously.
Even a mild shortfall can cause headaches.
Dehydration can dull concentration.
A shortfall can trigger headaches.
People drift through the day mildly dehydrated.
By the time thirst registers, you are already short of fluid.
Older adults are particularly vulnerable.
A further complication is mistaking thirst for hunger.
The rule is more convenient than precise.
A sedentary worker may need less fluid.
Use simple anchors like a glass with breakfast.
The aim is not to follow a rigid number.
Drink before thirst becomes urgent.

reading.title: The Quiet Cost of Noise
reading.passage:
When people list the threats to urban health, they tend to think of polluted air or contaminated water. Noise rarely makes the list, yet a growing body of evidence suggests that constant exposure to sound is far more damaging than its invisibility implies. Unlike a chemical spill, noise leaves no stain and is quickly forgotten once it stops, which may be precisely why it has been overlooked for so long.

The most obvious harm is to hearing, but researchers are increasingly concerned with subtler effects. Chronic exposure to traffic and aircraft noise has been linked to raised blood pressure, disturbed sleep, and higher levels of stress hormones. The body appears to treat persistent noise as a low-level threat, keeping itself in a state of mild alert even during sleep, when the mind is not consciously aware of the sound.

Children may be especially affected. Several studies of schools near busy roads or flight paths have reported that pupils there make slower progress in reading than comparable children in quieter areas. The leading explanation is that background noise interferes with the concentration and memory that learning depends upon.

Tackling the problem is awkward because noise is woven into modern life. Still, cities have options. Quieter road surfaces, restrictions on night flights, and the careful placing of barriers and green spaces can all reduce exposure. Some planners now treat tranquillity as a resource worth protecting, much as they protect clean air. The challenge, as with so many public-health issues, is persuading people to value something whose absence is felt far more keenly than its presence.

questions.q: Noise is frequently listed among the main threats to urban health.
The body may stay in a mild state of alert during sleep because of noise.
Some studies found slower reading progress among children near busy roads.
Noise barriers are more expensive to build than quieter road surfaces.
Why has noise been overlooked as a health threat, according to the passage?
options: It leaves no visible trace and is soon forgotten

questions.q: What is the leading explanation for children's slower reading?
options: Noise interferes with concentration and memory

questions.q: Chronic noise has been linked to raised blood pressure, disturbed sleep, and higher levels of stress ________.
Some planners now treat ________ as a resource worth protecting.

listening.title: Lecture: Measuring Urban Noise
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to introduce how we actually measure noise in cities, because the methods shape the conclusions we draw.

Noise is measured in units called decibels. A quiet library sits at around forty decibels, ordinary conversation at about sixty, and a busy main road can reach eighty or more. What's crucial is that the scale is not linear: every ten-decibel rise roughly doubles the perceived loudness.

Now, a single reading tells us little, because traffic noise rises and falls. So researchers record over a full twenty-four-hour period and calculate an average that gives extra weight to the night, when noise is more harmful to sleep.

The equipment itself is simple: a microphone, a recorder, and software. The real difficulty is placement. A sensor on a high balcony gives very different numbers from one at street level, so we follow a standard height of four meters.

For next week, please read chapter three and bring one question about the night-weighting method. We'll start there.

questions.q: Noise is measured in units called ________.
Ordinary conversation is about ________ decibels.
What happens with every ten-decibel rise?
Researchers record over a full ________-hour period.
Why is extra weight given to the night in the average?
options: Night noise is more harmful to sleep

questions.q: Sensors are placed at a standard height of ________ meters.

speaking.q: Let's talk about where you live. Is your neighbourhood quiet or noisy? What kinds of sounds do you notice most, and how do they affect how you feel?

writing.q:
Some people think that noise pollution should be treated as seriously as air pollution by city authorities. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: People worry about contaminated water.
Constant exposure to noise can be harmful.
Noise is dangerous despite its invisibility.
Noise has been overlooked for years.
Researchers study subtler effects of noise.
Chronic exposure raises blood pressure.
The body treats persistent noise as a threat.
The body stays in a mild state of alert.
Compared with comparable children in quiet areas.
Background noise interferes with concentration.
Tackling the problem is awkward.
Noise is woven into modern life.
Restrictions on night flights can help.
Planners protect tranquillity as a resource.
Its absence is felt keenly.

reading.title: Vaccines for Forgotten Diseases
reading.passage:
Modern medicine has produced vaccines against some of history's deadliest infections, yet a striking gap remains. A cluster of illnesses, often grouped under the unflattering label of neglected tropical diseases, still affects more than a billion people, mostly in poorer regions. For these conditions, effective vaccines are frequently absent, not because the science is impossible, but because the economics are unattractive.

Developing a vaccine is enormously expensive, and pharmaceutical companies, like any business, tend to invest where they expect a return. Diseases that mainly strike wealthy markets attract intense research; those confined to communities with little money to spend on medicine attract far less. The result is that some infections causing immense suffering have been studied only thinly, despite being well understood in principle.

This imbalance has prompted new ways of funding research. Public bodies and charitable foundations have stepped in to share the financial risk, effectively guaranteeing that a successful product will be bought even if the open market would not reward it. Such arrangements have already accelerated work on several previously stalled vaccines.

Progress, however, is uneven and fragile. A vaccine that exists is of little use if it cannot reach remote villages, survive without refrigeration, or be afforded by the health systems meant to deliver it. Researchers therefore increasingly design with these constraints in mind from the very start, favouring formulations that are cheap, stable in heat, and simple to administer. The lesson, many argue, is that solving the scientific puzzle is only the first step; the harder challenge lies in delivery.

questions.q: Neglected tropical diseases affect more than a billion people.
Vaccines for these diseases are missing mainly because the science is impossible.
Public bodies and charities have helped share the financial risk of research.
Most neglected tropical diseases were first identified in the last decade.
Why do some serious diseases attract little vaccine research?
options: The affected markets offer little financial return

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the harder challenge after developing a vaccine?
options: Delivering it to those who need it

questions.q: Researchers now favour formulations that are cheap, stable in ________, and simple to administer.
New funding effectively guarantees that a successful product will be ________ even if the market would not reward it.

listening.title: Discussion: Designing a Vaccine Trial
listening.script:
SUPERVISOR: Right, let's talk through your proposed vaccine trial. You're planning it in two regions, is that correct?

STUDENT: Yes. We'll recruit volunteers from both a city clinic and a rural one, to compare delivery conditions.

SUPERVISOR: Sensible. How many participants are you aiming for?

STUDENT: Eight hundred in total, split evenly between the sites.

SUPERVISOR: And the timeline?

STUDENT: We expect recruitment to take three months, then we follow each volunteer for a full year.

SUPERVISOR: Good. Now, the storage question is critical. The vaccine normally needs refrigeration, doesn't it?

STUDENT: It does, but we're testing a new heat-stable version, which is the whole point of the rural site.

SUPERVISOR: Excellent. One concern: make sure your consent forms are translated into the local language. That's often overlooked.

STUDENT: Noted. I'll arrange that with the community office.

SUPERVISOR: Then send me the revised protocol by the end of the month.

questions.q: Where will volunteers be recruited from?
options: A city clinic and a rural clinic

questions.q: The trial aims for ________ participants in total.
Recruitment is expected to take ________ months.
Each volunteer will be followed for a full ________.
What is special about the version being tested?
The supervisor reminds the student to translate the consent ________ into the local language.

speaking.q: Let's consider global health more broadly. Should wealthy countries be responsible for funding medical research that mainly benefits poorer nations? What might happen if such cooperation did not exist?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of new vaccines approved for four disease categories over two decades. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Disease category | 2001-2010 | 2011-2020
Respiratory infections | 6 | 11
Tropical diseases | 2 | 5
Childhood illnesses | 8 | 7
Other infections | 4 | 9

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: A striking gap remains in vaccine coverage.
A cluster of illnesses affects poorer regions.
They share the unflattering label of 'neglected'.
Neglected tropical diseases receive little funding.
The economics are unattractive to companies.
Firms invest where they expect a return.

These infections cause immense suffering.

The vaccine should be simple to administer.

reading.title: The Rise of Microcredentials
reading.passage:
Over the past decade, a new form of qualification has quietly entered the world of higher education. Known as microcredentials, these are short, focused certificates that confirm a learner has mastered a specific skill, such as data analysis or project management. Unlike a traditional three- or four-year degree, a microcredential can often be completed in a matter of weeks, and many are delivered entirely online.

Supporters argue that microcredentials respond to a genuine need. The job market changes rapidly, and workers frequently find that the skills they acquired at university have become outdated. Rather than returning for another full degree, an employee can simply take a short course to fill a particular gap. Employers, too, appear to value these certificates, partly because they signal that a candidate is willing to keep learning throughout their career.

However, critics raise several concerns. Because microcredentials are offered by a wide range of providers, from universities to private technology firms, their quality varies enormously. There is, as yet, no single authority that checks whether a given certificate genuinely reflects the skills it claims to certify. A learner may therefore invest time and money in a credential that carries little weight with future employers.

A further question concerns equality of access. Although online courses are often described as open to all, completing them still requires a reliable internet connection, a suitable device, and enough free time to study. For people working long hours in low-paid jobs, these conditions are not always easy to meet. Some researchers worry that microcredentials could widen, rather than narrow, the gap between advantaged and disadvantaged learners.

Despite these debates, the popularity of microcredentials continues to grow. Several national governments have begun to fund pilot schemes, and a handful of universities now allow students to combine a series of microcredentials into a recognised diploma. Whether this trend will reshape higher education or simply complement it remains an open question.

questions.q: A microcredential usually takes longer to complete than a traditional degree.
Employers tend to view microcredentials as a sign that a candidate keeps learning.
There is currently a single official body that checks the quality of all microcredentials.
Most workers prefer microcredentials to face-to-face training.
According to the passage, one concern about access is that online study requires
options: a reliable internet connection and free time

questions.q: What does the final paragraph suggest about the future of microcredentials?
options: Their long-term role is still uncertain

questions.q: Some researchers fear that microcredentials could ________ the gap between advantaged and disadvantaged learners.
A few universities now let students combine several microcredentials into a recognised ________.

listening.title: Enrolling at the Community Learning Centre
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, welcome to the Community Learning Centre. How can I help?

VISITOR: Hi, I'd like to sign up for one of your evening courses.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. Which course were you interested in?

VISITOR: The introduction to digital photography, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely choice. That class runs on Wednesday evenings, from seven until nine.

VISITOR: Perfect. How many weeks does it last?

RECEPTIONIST: It's a twelve-week course, starting on the fourteenth of September.

VISITOR: And the fee?

RECEPTIONIST: It's a hundred and forty pounds in total, but members get a ten per cent discount.

VISITOR: I'm not a member yet. Can I join today?

RECEPTIONIST: Absolutely. Membership is fifteen pounds a year. Could I take your name?

VISITOR: It's Daniel Hayes. That's H-A-Y-E-S.

RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. And a contact number?

VISITOR: It's 0-7-7-0-0, nine-double-two-four-one-six.

RECEPTIONIST: Great. One last thing: you'll need to bring your own camera, but the centre provides tripods and lighting.

VISITOR: That's really helpful. Thank you so much.

questions.q: Which course does the visitor want to join?
The class runs on Wednesday evenings from seven until ________.
The course lasts ________ weeks.
The total fee for the course is ________ pounds.
Annual membership costs ________ pounds.
What must the visitor bring to the class?

speaking.q: Let's talk about learning new skills. Have you ever taken a short course outside of school or university? What was it, and why did you choose it? Would you like to learn a new skill in the future?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of learners (in thousands) who completed online microcredential courses in one country over four years.

Category | 2019 | 2021 | 2023
Business | 40 | 65 | 90
Technology | 55 | 110 | 180
Health | 20 | 25 | 50

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: She listed her academic credentials on the application form.
It took him months to master the new software.
Many of his skills had become outdated after a decade.
The certificate signals a willingness to keep learning.
The quality of the courses varies enormously.
No single authority checks every certificate.
Learners invest both time and money in these courses.
The credential carries little weight with employers.
Equality of access remains a serious concern.
Advantaged learners often have more free time.
The government funded a pilot scheme last year.
The new courses may simply complement existing degrees.

reading.title: Learning in Two Languages
reading.passage:
Bilingual education, in which students are taught school subjects in two languages, has a long and varied history. In some regions it grew out of the practical need to educate children from immigrant families; in others it was introduced deliberately to preserve a minority language. Today, many schools across the world offer some form of dual-language instruction, and researchers continue to study its effects on learning.

One widely reported benefit concerns mental flexibility. Several studies suggest that children who regularly switch between two languages develop stronger skills in controlling their attention. Because a bilingual child must constantly choose the right word from two systems, the brain is given frequent practice at filtering out distractions. Some psychologists believe this advantage may even extend to other tasks that have nothing to do with language.

However, the picture is not entirely straightforward. Critics point out that bilingual children sometimes have a smaller vocabulary in each individual language, at least in the early years. They may also take slightly longer to retrieve common words. Most experts agree that these effects are temporary and that they fade as the child grows older, yet they remind us that the benefits of bilingualism are not automatic.

The success of a bilingual programme also depends heavily on how it is designed. Programmes that treat both languages as equally valuable tend to produce better results than those that present one language as merely a stepping stone to another. When a community's home language is respected within the school, children are more likely to stay motivated and to feel that their identity is valued.

Ultimately, bilingual education is less a single method than a broad family of approaches. Its outcomes vary according to the resources available, the training of teachers, and the attitudes of the wider society. What seems clear, however, is that growing up with two languages is far from the disadvantage it was once thought to be.

questions.q: Bilingual education has always been introduced for the same reason everywhere.
Some psychologists think the attention benefits of bilingualism may apply to non-language tasks.
Experts believe bilingual children's smaller vocabularies are usually permanent.
Bilingual schools cost more to run than single-language schools.
According to the passage, bilingual programmes work best when
options: one language is clearly more important
both languages are treated as equally valuable
lessons are taught only in the home language
the school ignores the community's culture

questions.q: Why might switching between languages help with attention?
options: It forces the brain to filter out distractions

questions.q: Children are more motivated when their ________ is valued by the school.
The author concludes that bilingual education is a broad family of ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Study Groups
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right everyone, today I want to talk briefly about study groups and how to get the most out of them. First, keep your group small. In my experience, four to five students works best. Larger groups tend to drift off topic.

Second, agree on a clear goal before each meeting. Maybe you want to review one chapter, or practise a set of past questions. Without a goal, the session can easily turn into a chat.

Third, rotate the role of leader. Each week a different member should prepare the agenda and keep everyone on track. This stops one person from doing all the work and helps everyone build confidence.

Fourth, set a fixed time limit. I'd suggest no more than ninety minutes. After that, concentration drops sharply and you start to waste time.

Finally, finish each session by summarising what you've learned and deciding what to focus on next time. A quick five-minute review at the end really does make the material stick. If you follow these five tips, you'll find that group study becomes far more productive than working alone.

questions.q: The ideal study group has four to ________ students.
What should a group agree on before each meeting?
The role of ________ should be rotated each week.
Each session should last no more than ________ minutes.
A quick ________-minute review at the end helps the material stick.
What is the main message of the talk?
options: Group study can be more productive if organised well

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you learned something with the help of another person. You should say:
- what you learned
- who helped you and how
- where and when this happened
and explain how you felt about learning in this way.

writing.q: Some people believe that children should learn a second language from a very young age, while others argue that it is better to wait until they have fully mastered their first language. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: She grew up in a bilingual household.
The school offers dual-language instruction.
The programme aims to preserve a minority language.
Bilingualism may improve mental flexibility.
The brain learns to filter out distractions.
He found it hard to ignore the distractions around him.
The picture is not entirely straightforward.
They may take longer to retrieve common words.
These effects are usually temporary.

Respected children tend to stay more motivated.
Its outcomes vary according to resources.

reading.title: The Forgotten Art of Note-Taking
reading.passage:
In an age of digital recordings and shared online slides, it is easy to assume that taking notes by hand has become unnecessary. After all, why scribble down a lecturer's words when the entire presentation can be downloaded later? Yet a growing body of research suggests that the simple act of writing notes may play a surprisingly important role in how we learn.

The central idea is that handwriting forces a kind of mental processing that typing does not. When students write by hand, they cannot usually keep up with every word, so they must listen carefully, decide what matters most, and put ideas into their own phrasing. This act of selecting and rephrasing appears to help the brain store information more deeply. Students who type, by contrast, often record speech almost word for word without truly thinking about it.

One influential experiment compared two groups of university students. Both watched the same lecture, but one group took notes by hand and the other used laptops. When tested a week later on the underlying concepts, the handwriting group performed noticeably better, even though the laptop users had recorded more words overall. The researchers concluded that fewer, more thoughtful notes can be more valuable than a complete transcript.

This does not mean that digital tools have no place. Typed notes are easier to search, share, and edit, and for some learners, especially those with certain disabilities, a keyboard may be essential. The lesson is not that one method is always superior, but that the way we take notes shapes what we remember.

Perhaps the most practical advice is to combine approaches. A learner might capture key ideas by hand during a lecture and later organise them digitally. What matters most, the evidence suggests, is not the tool itself but the effort of engaging actively with the material rather than copying it down passively.

questions.q: Research suggests handwriting notes can affect how well people learn.
Students who type usually record fewer words than those who write by hand.
In the experiment, the laptop group scored higher on the concept test.
The experiment was repeated in several different countries.
Why might handwriting help learning, according to the passage?
options: It forces students to select and rephrase ideas
It avoids the need to listen

questions.q: What does the writer say about digital note-taking tools?
options: They are always better than handwriting
They have advantages such as being easy to search

questions.q: The researchers concluded that fewer, more ________ notes can be more valuable than a full transcript.
What matters most is engaging actively rather than copying material ________.

listening.title: Library Orientation
listening.script:
LIBRARIAN: Welcome to your library orientation. I'll walk you through the basics quickly. The library is open from eight in the morning until eleven at night on weekdays. On weekends, we close a little earlier, at six.

Let's start with borrowing. As a student, you can take out up to fifteen items at a time. Most books can be kept for three weeks, but reference titles must stay in the building.

If you need a book that's already on loan, you can place a hold, and we'll email you when it's returned. There's no charge for that service.

Now, late returns. If you keep a book past its due date, the fine is twenty cents per day, up to a maximum of ten dollars per item. So please return things on time.

The second floor is our quiet study area, while group discussion is allowed on the ground floor only. Printing costs five cents a page for black and white. And don't forget, you'll need your student card to borrow anything or to use the printers. Any questions, just ask at the front desk.

You must wait at the desk

speaking.q: Let's discuss memory and learning more generally. Why do you think some people remember information more easily than others? Do you think modern technology has made our memories weaker? How might schools help students remember what they learn?

Method | 2015 | 2023
Handwriting only | 48 | 30
Typing only | 22 | 41
Both methods | 25 | 26

reading.title: The School Garden Movement
reading.passage:
In recent years, a quiet revolution has been taking root in school playgrounds. Where there were once only concrete and grass, many schools have begun to plant vegetable beds, fruit trees, and flower borders. The school garden movement, as it is sometimes called, aims to bring children into direct contact with the natural world and, in doing so, to teach a range of lessons that cannot easily be learned from a textbook.

The educational arguments are wide-ranging. Tending a garden requires patience, planning, and teamwork. Pupils must decide what to plant, learn when and how to water it, and wait, often for weeks, before seeing any results. Teachers report that these tasks can be especially valuable for restless children who struggle to sit still in a traditional classroom. Working outdoors gives them a sense of purpose and an opportunity to succeed in ways that written tests rarely allow.

There are nutritional benefits too. Studies in several countries have found that children who grow their own vegetables are more willing to eat them. A child who has watched a carrot develop from a seed is far more curious about how it tastes than one who simply finds it on a plate. Some schools have linked their gardens to cookery lessons, turning the harvest into simple, healthy meals.

Yet running a school garden is not without difficulties. Gardens need year-round care, including during the long summer holidays when the school is empty. Funding can also be a problem, since beds, tools, and seeds all cost money, and few schools have spare budgets. For this reason, many successful gardens depend heavily on volunteers, often parents or local residents who give their time freely.

Despite these obstacles, supporters argue that the rewards are well worth the effort. A garden, they say, is a living classroom in which science, mathematics, and even history can come alive. Above all, it offers children something increasingly rare: the chance to slow down and watch something grow.

questions.q: School gardens are intended to replace textbooks completely.
Teachers say gardening can be especially helpful for restless children.
Children who grow vegetables tend to be more willing to eat them.
School gardens have been shown to improve children's exam results.
According to the passage, one practical difficulty of school gardens is
options: gardens need care even during the holidays

questions.q: Why are many gardens dependent on volunteers?
options: Teachers are not allowed to garden

questions.q: Some schools have connected their gardens to ________ lessons, using the harvest for meals.
Supporters describe a garden as a living ________ where many subjects come alive.

listening.title: Planning a Field Trip
listening.script:
TEACHER: Okay class, let me give you the details for our trip to the Botanic Gardens next Friday. We'll meet at the school gate at half past eight, so please don't be late. The coach leaves at quarter to nine sharp.

The journey takes about an hour, so we should arrive at around ten. When we get there, a guide named Mrs Patel will meet us and take us on a tour of the tropical greenhouse first.

After the tour, you'll have a worksheet to complete in pairs. It focuses on how different plants survive in dry conditions, so look carefully and take notes.

We'll stop for lunch at twelve thirty in the picnic area. Please bring a packed lunch and, importantly, a refillable water bottle. There are taps on site, so you won't need to buy water.

In the afternoon, we'll visit the seed laboratory, where a scientist will show us how seeds are stored for the future. We expect to leave by three o'clock and be back at school by four. Don't forget a waterproof jacket, as rain is likely.

reading.title: Rethinking the School Timetable
reading.passage:
For more than a century, the school day in most countries has followed a familiar pattern: lessons begin in the early morning, often around half past eight, and run until the middle of the afternoon. This timetable was largely inherited from an industrial age, when families wanted their children's schedules to match the working day. In recent years, however, researchers have begun to question whether such an early start truly suits the way young people learn.

The strongest challenge comes from the study of sleep. During adolescence, the body's internal clock naturally shifts, making teenagers feel sleepy later at night and reluctant to wake early in the morning. This is not laziness but biology. As a result, many teenagers arrive at their first lesson before their brains are fully alert, struggling to concentrate on demanding subjects such as mathematics or science.

A number of schools, particularly in parts of the United States and Europe, have responded by pushing back their start times by an hour or so. Early reports from these schools are encouraging. Attendance has improved, students appear more attentive, and some institutions have even noted a modest rise in test scores. Teachers describe classrooms that feel calmer and more focused in the first lessons of the day.

Nevertheless, changing the timetable is far from simple. A later start often means a later finish, which can clash with sports clubs, part-time jobs, and family routines. Parents who leave for work early may struggle to organise childcare, and bus services may need to be rescheduled at considerable expense. For these reasons, some communities have resisted the change despite the apparent benefits.

What the debate reveals is that the school timetable is not a fixed law of nature but a human decision, shaped as much by habit and convenience as by evidence about learning. As our understanding of the teenage brain improves, it seems likely that more schools will at least consider whether the traditional early start still makes sense.

questions.q: The traditional school timetable was designed to fit modern research on sleep.
Teenagers' tendency to sleep later is described as a biological change rather than laziness.
Schools that delayed their start times reported some improvements in attendance.
Later start times have been made compulsory by national governments.
According to the passage, why do some teenagers struggle in early lessons?
options: Their brains are not yet fully alert

questions.q: Which is mentioned as a difficulty of starting school later?
options: It may clash with sports and jobs

questions.q: The writer argues the timetable is a human decision shaped by habit and ________.
During adolescence, the body's internal ________ naturally shifts.

listening.title: A Student Discusses Her Schedule
listening.script:
ADVISOR: Come in, Mia. You wanted to talk about your timetable for next term?

MIA: Yes, I'm finding my current schedule really tough. My first class starts at eight fifteen, and I live quite far away.

ADVISOR: I see. What time do you have to leave home?

MIA: Around seven, so I'm exhausted by the afternoon.

ADVISOR: Well, we do have a later option. Some courses now begin at nine thirty instead.

MIA: That would make a huge difference. Which subjects are available?

ADVISOR: For the morning group, you could take history and then chemistry. The later group offers economics and biology.

MIA: I'd prefer the later group. Biology is my favourite subject.

ADVISOR: Good choice. Just be aware that the later group finishes at half past four, rather than three.

MIA: That's fine. I'd rather start late and finish late.

ADVISOR: Excellent. I'll need to confirm your decision by Friday, the twentieth. Could you also fill in this form and return it to room two-one-four?

MIA: Of course. Thank you so much for your help.

reading.title: The Rise of the Skyscraper
reading.passage:
The skyscraper is often regarded as the defining building type of the modern city, yet its emergence depended on a handful of technical breakthroughs rather than on any single architect's vision. Before the late nineteenth century, the height of a building was limited by its walls. Because the lower storeys had to bear the entire weight of everything above them, walls at street level grew impossibly thick as buildings rose, swallowing valuable floor space and admitting little light.

The decisive change came with the development of the steel frame. Instead of relying on heavy masonry, engineers learned to construct a skeleton of steel beams and columns that carried the load. The external walls were now merely a skin, free of any structural duty. This innovation, first exploited in Chicago in the 1880s, allowed buildings to climb far higher while keeping their lower floors open and bright.

Height alone, however, would have been useless without a reliable way of reaching the upper levels. Few people were willing to climb ten flights of stairs each morning. The safety elevator, fitted with a device that stopped it falling if its cable snapped, made tall buildings genuinely practical. It is sometimes said that the elevator, as much as the steel frame, created the skyscraper.

The new towers were not welcomed by everyone. Critics complained that they cast neighbouring streets into permanent shadow and that their sheer scale overwhelmed the older city around them. Some cities responded by introducing regulations requiring upper storeys to be set back from the street, a rule that gave many early skyscrapers their distinctive stepped silhouette.

questions.q: The skyscraper was made possible mainly by the vision of one architect.
In tall masonry buildings, walls at street level had to be very thick.
With a steel frame, the external walls became merely a ________ with no structural duty.
Why does the writer mention the safety elevator?
options: it made tall buildings genuinely practical to use

questions.q: The steel frame was first exploited in ________ in the 1880s.
The architects who designed the first skyscrapers became very wealthy.
Some critics argued that skyscrapers left neighbouring streets in shadow.
Rules requiring upper storeys to be set back gave many towers a stepped ________.

listening.title: Enquiry: Joining a Local Art Class
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Riverside Community Arts, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like some information about your evening drawing classes.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We run a beginners' course on Tuesdays and a more advanced one on Thursdays. Both start at seven o'clock.

CALLER: I'm a complete beginner, so Tuesday sounds right. How long does each session last?

RECEPTIONIST: Two hours, finishing at nine. The course runs for ten weeks in total.

CALLER: And what's the cost?

RECEPTIONIST: It's a hundred and forty pounds for the whole course, which includes basic materials. You'll just need to bring a sketchbook.

CALLER: That's fine. Where exactly is the studio?

RECEPTIONIST: We're at fourteen Mill Lane, just behind the library. There's parking on the street after six.

CALLER: Great. Do I need to book in advance?

RECEPTIONIST: Yes, please. Classes are limited to twelve people, so they fill up quickly. You can reserve a place on our website or pay a deposit here.

CALLER: Perfect. Could I give you my name now?

RECEPTIONIST: Certainly. Let me take a few details.

reading.title: Colour in Design
reading.passage:
Colour is among the most powerful tools available to a designer, yet it is also one of the most easily misused. A well-chosen palette can make a product feel trustworthy, a website easy to navigate, or a room calm and welcoming. A poor one can quietly undermine all of these aims, often without the viewer being able to say exactly why.

Much of colour's power lies in association. Over time, particular colours have come to suggest particular ideas. Blue is widely linked with reliability and is therefore favoured by banks and technology firms; green suggests nature and health and is common in the branding of food and environmental groups. These associations are not fixed, however. They vary from culture to culture, and a colour considered cheerful in one country may carry quite different meanings in another.

Designers must also think about how colours behave together. A single colour seen in isolation looks different once it is placed beside another, because the eye judges each shade in relation to its neighbours. Two colours that work beautifully on their own can clash when combined, while a dull colour can appear surprisingly vivid against the right background.

Practical concerns matter too. Roughly one in twelve men has some form of colour-vision deficiency, which means that designs relying on colour alone to convey information may exclude a significant audience. For this reason, careful designers add a second cue, such as a symbol or a label, so that no message depends on colour by itself. Good colour design, then, is rarely about choosing pretty shades. It is about understanding how colour is seen, felt, and interpreted by real people.

questions.q: A poor colour choice can weaken a design even when viewers cannot explain why.
Blue is widely linked with reliability and is favoured by banks and ________ firms.
The meanings attached to colours are the same in every culture.
Why does a single colour look different when placed beside another?
options: the eye judges each shade in relation to its neighbours

questions.q: Roughly one in ________ men has some form of colour-vision deficiency.
Careful designers add a second cue so a message does not depend on colour alone.
Most companies now employ a specialist to test their colour schemes.

listening.title: Tour Briefing: A Design Museum Visit
listening.script:
GUIDE: Morning, everyone, and welcome to the City Design Museum. Before we go in, let me give you a quick overview of how the visit will work.

We'll start on the ground floor with the furniture gallery, which traces chair design over the past century. After that, we'll take the stairs up to the first floor for the poster collection, which I think is the highlight of the museum.

The visit lasts about ninety minutes in total, and there'll be a fifteen-minute break halfway through, when you can use the cafe or the shop. Please remember that photography is allowed everywhere except the poster gallery, where the lighting is kept low to protect the older prints.

If we get separated, don't worry. We'll all meet again at the main entrance at exactly half past eleven. There are lockers near the cloakroom if you'd like to leave bags, and they're free, though you'll need a one-pound coin that you get back afterwards.

One last thing: the museum is running a special exhibition on packaging design upstairs, and entry to that is included in your ticket today. Right, shall we begin?

Describe a colour that you like. You should say:
- what the colour is

reading.title: The Return of Traditional Crafts
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, traditional crafts seemed destined to disappear. As factories learned to produce furniture, pottery and textiles cheaply and in vast quantities, the handmade object came to look like an expensive luxury that few could justify. Skilled makers grew old, and younger people, drawn to better-paid work, rarely took their place. In many regions, techniques that had been passed down for generations were practised by only a handful of people.

In recent years, however, this decline has begun to reverse. A growing number of consumers, tired of mass-produced goods that all look the same, have started to seek out objects with a sense of history and individuality. A handmade bowl, slightly irregular and bearing the marks of its maker, offers something a machine cannot easily imitate. Buyers increasingly value not only the object itself but the story behind it.

The internet has played an unexpected part in this revival. Online marketplaces allow a potter in a remote village to reach customers on the other side of the world, removing the need for a shop in an expensive city centre. Social media, meanwhile, lets makers show their work in progress, building an audience that feels personally connected to the craft.

Supporters argue that the benefits go beyond commerce. Reviving a craft can keep a local identity alive, provide work in areas with few other opportunities, and pass valuable skills to a new generation. Critics caution that the trend can be fragile, depending heavily on fashion and on tourists who may not return. Even so, many once-dying crafts now look healthier than they have for decades.

questions.q: During the twentieth century, handmade objects came to seem like an expensive luxury.
Young people were eager to learn traditional crafts throughout the century.
Buyers increasingly value not only the object but the ________ behind it.
How has the internet helped the craft revival, according to the passage?
options: it lowered the cost of raw materials
it lets makers reach distant customers without a city shop
it replaced handmade goods with digital ones

questions.q: On social media, makers can show their work in ________, building an audience.
Reviving a craft can help keep a local identity alive.
Governments now offer grants to every craft maker who applies.

options: it can be fragile and depend on fashion and tourists
it is too expensive to start

listening.title: Radio Feature: A Village Pottery
listening.script:
PRESENTER: Today we're visiting a small pottery in the village of Ashcombe, where Maria Cole has been making ceramics for over twenty years. Maria, how did it all begin?

MARIA: Well, I trained as a graphic designer, but I felt something was missing. I took an evening class in pottery and was hooked almost immediately. Within three years I'd given up my office job and set up a studio here.

PRESENTER: And the village itself, why Ashcombe?

MARIA: Two reasons, really. The rent was affordable, which mattered when I was starting out, and there's a long tradition of pottery in this valley. The clay here is excellent.

PRESENTER: I understand you sell most of your work online now.

MARIA: That's right. About eighty per cent of my sales come through my website. The rest I sell at a craft fair in the city each autumn.

PRESENTER: Do you teach as well?

MARIA: I do. I run weekend workshops for beginners, usually six people at a time. They're always full, which tells me people are genuinely keen to learn.

PRESENTER: Wonderful. Maria Cole, thank you for showing us around.

reading.title: Designing for Daylight
reading.passage:
For most of human history, buildings were shaped by the sun. Without electric light, architects had no choice but to plan rooms around the path of daylight, placing windows where they would catch the morning or evening sun and keeping interiors shallow so that light could reach the back of every space. The arrival of cheap electric lighting in the twentieth century removed this constraint, and for several decades designers largely ignored the sun. Deep, artificially lit offices became normal, and natural light came to be treated as a pleasant extra rather than a necessity.

That attitude is now changing, for two reasons. The first is cost. Electric lighting and the air conditioning needed to remove the heat it generates consume a great deal of energy, and as energy prices have risen, the appeal of free daylight has grown accordingly. The second reason is human wellbeing. A substantial body of research suggests that people who work in daylit spaces feel more alert, sleep better and report higher satisfaction than those confined to windowless rooms.

Designing for daylight, however, is more subtle than simply adding windows. Too much direct sun can cause glare on screens and overheat a room, forcing occupants to close blinds and switch the lights on anyway. Skilled architects therefore control daylight rather than merely admit it, using shading devices, reflective surfaces and carefully angled glass to spread soft, even light through a space.

The most ambitious projects treat daylight as a material in its own right, as fundamental to the design as concrete or steel. In such buildings, light is not an afterthought but a shaping force, changing in quality through the day and giving each room a character that no electric bulb can quite reproduce.

questions.q: Before electric light, architects had to plan rooms around the path of daylight.
Interiors were kept ________ so that light could reach the back of every space.
After electric lighting arrived, designers immediately valued natural light more.
What are the two reasons given for the renewed interest in daylight?
Research suggests people in daylit spaces feel more alert and sleep better.
Skilled architects control daylight using shading devices, reflective surfaces and angled ________.
Buildings designed for daylight are usually cheaper to construct.
How do the most ambitious projects treat daylight?
options: as a material as fundamental as concrete or steel
as something to be blocked out

listening.title: Lecture: Light and Mood in Buildings
listening.script:
LECTURER: In today's session I want to look at how the light inside a building affects the way we feel and behave, something architects increasingly take seriously.

Let's begin with brightness. Studies have found that in well-lit classrooms, students concentrate for longer and make fewer errors than in dim ones. The effect is strongest when the light comes from windows rather than from overhead bulbs.

Direction matters too. Light from the side, falling across a room, tends to feel natural and comfortable. Light directly overhead, by contrast, can feel harsh and is often described as tiring after long exposure.

Then there's the colour of light. Cooler, bluer light tends to make people feel awake and is useful in places where alertness matters, such as offices. Warmer, more yellow light feels relaxing and suits homes and restaurants.

Finally, change is important. Natural light shifts throughout the day, and there's growing evidence that this variation helps regulate our body clock. A space lit by an unchanging electric glow, however bright, can leave occupants feeling slightly out of step with the natural rhythm of the day.

questions.q: In well-lit classrooms, students do what compared with dim ones?
options: concentrate for longer and make fewer errors

questions.q: The effect is strongest when light comes from ________ rather than overhead bulbs.
Light directly ________ can feel harsh and tiring after long exposure.
What kind of light suits offices, according to the lecturer?
Warmer, more ________ light feels relaxing and suits homes.
Natural light that changes through the day can help regulate the body clock.

speaking.q:
Describe a room or building that you find pleasant to be in. You should say:

reading.title: The Art of the Public Square
reading.passage:
Every great city has its public squares, the open spaces where people gather, rest and watch one another go by. Though they may seem to be simply gaps between buildings, the best squares are in fact carefully composed, and small differences in their design can determine whether a square teems with life or stands empty.

Scale is the first consideration. A square that is too large feels exposed and discouraging, offering little shelter and no sense of enclosure; people tend to hurry across it rather than linger. One that is too small, by contrast, can feel cramped once a crowd gathers. Designers have long sought a middle ground, an area generous enough to hold a crowd yet intimate enough to feel sociable.

Equally important is the edge. A square comes alive when its boundary is active, lined with cafes, shops and entrances that draw people in and give them reasons to pause. A square ringed by blank walls or busy roads, however handsome its centre, will rarely attract a crowd. The life of a square, planners often say, is created at its edges rather than in its middle.

Detail matters too. Places to sit, especially movable chairs that people can arrange to suit themselves, encourage visitors to stay. Shade in hot climates, shelter from wind in cold ones, and a little water or greenery all make a square more comfortable. None of these elements is dramatic on its own, yet together they decide a square's fate.

Perhaps the deepest lesson is that a successful public square cannot be designed for any single purpose. The squares people love are flexible, accommodating markets one day and concerts the next, busy commuters in the morning and unhurried families in the evening. Their value lies precisely in their openness to whatever a city's life may bring.

questions.q: Small differences in design can decide whether a square is lively or empty.
What problem does a square that is too large create?
options: it feels exposed, so people hurry across rather than linger

questions.q: A square comes alive when its ________ is active, lined with cafes and shops.
A square surrounded by blank walls will usually attract large crowds.
Movable ________ that people can arrange to suit themselves encourage visitors to stay.
The author states which city has the most successful public square in the world.
What does the writer suggest is the deepest lesson about public squares?
options: they should be as large as possible
they should be designed for a single clear purpose
they should be flexible and open to many uses
they should avoid water and greenery

questions.q: Squares that people love can host different activities at different times.

listening.title: Discussion: Redesigning the Town Square
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, you two have been studying the plan to redesign Market Square. Sam, what did you find?

SAM: Well, the main issue is that the square feels empty most of the day. There's a lot of open paving but almost nowhere to sit, so people just pass through.

TUTOR: And your suggestion?

SAM: Add seating, obviously, but also trees for shade. At the moment there's no shelter from the sun at all.

TUTOR: Priya, do you agree?

PRIYA: Mostly, yes. But I think the bigger problem is the edges. One whole side is taken up by a car park, which kills the atmosphere. If you replaced that with small shops or a cafe, the square would feel much livelier.

TUTOR: Interesting. What about events?

PRIYA: The council already runs a market there on Saturdays, and it's popular. I'd keep that, and maybe add an evening film screening in summer.

SAM: I'd also widen the entrance from the High Street. It's quite narrow now, so people don't realise the square is there.

TUTOR: Good. Those are exactly the sorts of details that make or break a public space. Let's write them up.

options: it feels empty with nowhere to sit

reading.title: The Rise of the Subscription Economy
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, businesses earned money mainly by selling products outright. A customer paid once, took the goods home, and the transaction was complete. Over the past two decades, however, a different approach has spread rapidly across many industries. Instead of buying a product, customers now pay a regular fee to use a service, an arrangement commonly known as the subscription model.

The shift began with software companies, which discovered that charging a monthly fee produced steadier income than selling a single licence. Music and film streaming services soon followed, replacing the sale of discs with unlimited online access for a fixed price. More recently, the idea has reached unexpected corners of the economy, from monthly boxes of razors to car ownership schemes in which drivers swap vehicles whenever they wish.

The appeal to companies is clear. A subscription generates predictable revenue, which makes financial planning easier and reassures investors. It also creates an ongoing relationship with the customer, allowing firms to gather data about how their services are used and to adjust their offerings accordingly. For customers, the model can reduce the large upfront cost of ownership and provide flexibility, since most subscriptions can be cancelled at any time.

Yet the approach has drawbacks. Consumers may sign up for several services and then forget to cancel them, quietly paying for products they no longer use. Critics argue that the convenience of automatic billing can encourage wasteful spending. There is also a question of value: a customer who subscribes for many years may eventually pay far more than the cost of simply buying the item. Whether the subscription economy continues to expand will depend on whether companies can keep persuading customers that constant access is worth a constant price.

questions.q: In the twentieth century, most businesses earned money by selling products in a single transaction.
Software companies were among the first to adopt the subscription model.
Subscription services always cost less in total than buying a product outright.
Most governments have introduced laws to control subscription billing.
According to the passage, why do subscriptions appeal to companies?
options: They eliminate the need for advertising
They require no contact with customers

questions.q: What drawback for consumers does the writer mention?
options: Services are difficult to cancel by law

Prices are fixed by the government
