listening.title: Cancelling a Gym Membership
listening.script:
STAFF: Good afternoon, Riverside Fitness, how can I help?

MEMBER: Hi, I'd like to cancel my monthly membership, please.

STAFF: I'm sorry to hear that. Could I take your membership number?

MEMBER: Yes, it's four-two-seven-one.

STAFF: Thank you. And your name?

MEMBER: Thomas Hale. That's H-A-L-E.

STAFF: Right, I can see your account. You're on the standard plan at thirty pounds a month. May I ask why you're leaving?

MEMBER: I'm moving to another city for work, so I won't be able to come in.

STAFF: I understand. Please note that we require thirty days' notice, so your final payment will be taken on the fifteenth of next month.

MEMBER: That's fine. Will I get a confirmation?

STAFF: Yes, we'll email you within two working days. Is your address still the one ending in gmail?

MEMBER: Yes, that's correct.

STAFF: Lovely. Your membership will end on the fifteenth, and you're welcome to rejoin any time.

MEMBER: Thank you very much.

reading.title: Why Small Businesses Matter to an Economy
reading.passage:
When people picture a country's economy, they often think first of large corporations with famous names and towering offices. Yet in most nations, the vast majority of firms are small businesses, employing only a handful of workers each. Although individually modest, together these enterprises form the backbone of economic life, providing jobs, services and innovation in almost every community.

One of the most important contributions of small businesses is employment. In many developed economies, firms with fewer than fifty staff account for around half of all private-sector jobs. Because such businesses are spread widely across regions, they help to distribute work beyond the major cities, supporting towns and rural areas that large corporations might otherwise overlook. When a local workshop or shop closes, the effect on a small community can be severe.

Small firms are also a powerful source of innovation. Without the heavy bureaucracy of large organisations, they can experiment quickly and respond to changing customer needs. Many products and services that are now widespread began as bold ideas in tiny companies. Some of today's giant technology firms, it is worth remembering, started in garages and spare rooms.

However, small businesses face genuine challenges. They often struggle to borrow money, since banks tend to view them as risky compared with established corporations. They may lack the resources to comply with complicated regulations, and a single bad year can threaten their survival. For these reasons, many governments offer support such as grants, tax relief or advice services. Whether such measures are effective is debated by economists, but few dispute that a healthy economy depends on the energy and resilience of its smallest firms.

questions.q: In most countries, small businesses outnumber large corporations.
Small firms are mainly concentrated in major cities.
Banks generally find small businesses riskier to lend to than large corporations.
Government grants have been proven to increase small-business survival rates.
Why can small firms innovate quickly, according to the passage?
options: They have less bureaucracy than large organisations
They face fewer regulations than corporations

questions.q: What does the writer say about the closure of a small local business?
options: It rarely affects the wider community
It can have a severe effect on a small community
It usually benefits larger firms nearby
It is always caused by poor management

questions.q: Together, small enterprises form the ________ of economic life.

listening.title: A Talk on Starting a Market Stall
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Good evening, everyone, and welcome to tonight's session on running your first market stall. I'm Karen, and I've managed a stall at the riverside market for nine years, so I'll share a few practical tips.

First, choosing the right market matters. Our riverside market runs every Saturday from eight in the morning until two in the afternoon, and it attracts about three thousand visitors a week. A weekly stall costs forty-five dollars, which is good value for a busy site.

Second, think carefully about what you sell. The best-selling items here are fresh food and handmade crafts. Avoid products that are easily found in supermarkets, because shoppers come to a market for something different.

Third, presentation is everything. A clean, well-lit table with clear prices will draw far more customers than a cluttered one. I'd also suggest accepting card payments, since fewer people carry cash these days.

Finally, talk to your neighbours. The other stallholders are not really your rivals; they are a friendly source of advice. Right, let's open the floor to questions.

reading.title: Understanding Inflation
reading.passage:
Inflation is one of the most discussed ideas in economics, yet it is often misunderstood. In simple terms, inflation describes a general rise in the prices of goods and services over time. When inflation is present, each unit of money buys slightly less than it did before, so the purchasing power of a currency gradually falls. A modest, steady rate of inflation is usually considered normal and even healthy in a growing economy.

There are several reasons why prices may rise. Sometimes demand grows faster than supply: if many people wish to buy a product that is in short supply, sellers can charge more. At other times, the costs of production increase, perhaps because raw materials or wages become more expensive, and businesses pass these higher costs on to customers. Both forces can push prices upward, often at the same time.

Governments and central banks watch inflation closely because it affects nearly everyone. If prices rise too quickly, savings lose value and households on fixed incomes find it harder to afford essentials. To control inflation, a central bank may raise interest rates, which makes borrowing more expensive and tends to cool down spending. This tool is powerful, but it must be used with care, since very high rates can slow the economy too much.

A small amount of inflation is generally manageable, but rapid or unpredictable inflation can be deeply damaging. In extreme cases, known as hyperinflation, prices may double within days, and money becomes almost worthless. Such episodes are rare, yet they show why maintaining stable prices is one of the central goals of economic policy.

questions.q: Inflation means that money loses some of its purchasing power over time.
A small, steady rate of inflation is regarded as abnormal in a growing economy.
Raising interest rates tends to encourage people to spend more.
Most countries have experienced hyperinflation at least once.
According to the passage, one cause of inflation is when:
Why must central banks use high interest rates carefully?
options: They have no effect on borrowing
They can slow the economy too much
They increase the value of savings instantly

questions.q: Inflation describes a general rise in the ________ of goods and services.
In extreme cases known as ________, prices may double within days.

listening.title: A Lecture on Household Budgeting
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today's short lecture is about managing a household budget, a skill that becomes especially valuable when prices are rising.

The first principle is simple: know exactly where your money goes. Studies suggest that the average household spends around thirty-five percent of its income on housing, whether that is rent or a mortgage. The next largest cost is usually food, at roughly fifteen percent.

The second principle is to separate needs from wants. Needs are essentials such as housing, food and transport. Wants are the extras, like dining out or the latest gadgets. When inflation rises, the wants are the first place to make savings.

Third, try to build an emergency fund. Most advisers recommend saving enough to cover three months of expenses. This protects you if you lose your job or face an unexpected bill.

Finally, review your budget regularly. I suggest checking it once a month, because your income and costs both change over time. A budget you wrote a year ago may no longer fit your situation. Thank you, and I'll take questions now.

reading.title: The Gig Economy and the Changing Workplace
reading.passage:
A generation ago, most workers expected to hold a single job with one employer, often for many years. Today, a growing number of people earn their living differently, taking on short, separate tasks for a variety of clients. This pattern of work is widely called the gig economy, a phrase borrowed from musicians who are paid for individual performances rather than a fixed salary.

The gig economy has been driven largely by technology. Online platforms now connect people who need a service with those willing to provide it, whether the task is delivering food, designing a website or driving a passenger across the city. A worker can accept a job with a few taps on a phone, complete it, and be paid almost immediately. For many, the chief attraction is freedom: they can choose when to work and how much to take on.

Yet this flexibility comes at a price. Gig workers are usually classed as self-employed, which means they receive none of the protections that traditional employees enjoy, such as paid holidays, sick pay or a guaranteed wage. Their income can vary sharply from week to week, and a quiet period can leave them in financial difficulty. Critics argue that platforms have shifted risk onto the individual while keeping much of the reward.

Governments around the world are now wrestling with how to respond. Some have introduced rules requiring platforms to provide minimum benefits, while others fear that heavy regulation might destroy the very flexibility that workers value. The debate is far from settled. What is clear is that the boundary between employment and self-employment, once sharp, is becoming increasingly blurred.

questions.q: The phrase 'gig economy' originally comes from the world of music.
Gig workers usually receive the same protections as traditional employees.
Technology has played a major role in the growth of the gig economy.
Most gig workers would prefer to return to traditional full-time jobs.
What does the writer say is the main attraction of gig work?
options: Freedom to choose when to work

questions.q: What concern do critics raise about gig platforms?
options: They have shifted risk onto the individual

questions.q: Gig workers are usually classed as ________.

listening.script:
MAYA: Hey, Daniel. You said you started doing some food delivery work. How's it going?

DANIEL: Yeah, it's all right. I use one of those apps. The best thing is I can work whenever I have free time between lectures.

MAYA: That sounds flexible. How much do you make?

DANIEL: It varies a lot. On a good evening I earn about twelve pounds an hour, but on a quiet day it can drop to seven.

MAYA: Hmm, that's a big difference. Do you get any holiday pay or anything?

DANIEL: No, nothing like that. I'm counted as self-employed, so there's no sick pay either. If I'm ill, I just don't earn.

MAYA: That worries me a bit. I've been thinking of working at the campus library instead. It only pays ten pounds an hour, but the hours are fixed and I'd get proper breaks.

DANIEL: That's probably more reliable, to be honest. The freedom is nice, but the uncertainty can be stressful.

MAYA: Right. I think I'll apply to the library this week.

reading.title: The Power of Brands
reading.passage:
Walk down any high street and you will be surrounded by brands. A brand is far more than a logo or a name; it is the set of impressions and feelings that a company hopes to plant in the minds of its customers. A strong brand can persuade shoppers to choose one product over a nearly identical rival, and even to pay considerably more for it. For this reason, businesses invest enormous sums in building and protecting their brands.

The value of a brand lies largely in trust. When customers recognise a familiar name, they feel reassured that the product will meet a certain standard. This sense of reliability reduces the effort of choosing, since shoppers do not have to research every purchase from scratch. Over many years, a trusted brand can become one of a company's most valuable assets, sometimes worth more than its factories or equipment.

Brands also communicate identity. The clothes people wear, the cars they drive and the coffee they carry can all send signals about who they are or wish to be. Clever marketing encourages customers to see a purchase not merely as a product but as a statement about themselves. This emotional connection helps explain why people will queue for hours to buy the latest model of a favoured device.

However, a powerful brand can be fragile. A single scandal, a faulty product or a careless comment can damage a reputation that took decades to build. In the age of social media, bad news travels faster than ever, and companies can find their carefully crafted image undermined within hours. Maintaining a brand, therefore, is not a single achievement but a constant and demanding task.

questions.q: A brand consists only of a company's logo and name.
A trusted brand can sometimes be worth more than a company's physical equipment.
Social media has made it easier for bad news about a brand to spread.
Most companies spend more on branding than on manufacturing.
According to the passage, the value of a brand lies largely in:
Why do brands help reduce the effort of shopping?
options: Customers feel reassured about a familiar standard
They remove the need for advertising

questions.q: Recognising a familiar name reduces the ________ of choosing a product.
A single scandal or faulty product can damage a ________ that took decades to build.

MANAGER: Right, thanks for coming. Let's go over the plan for our new coffee brand. First, the budget. We've been given a total of fifty thousand dollars for the launch.

LEO: That's reasonable. How should we split it?

MANAGER: I suggest spending half on social media, since that's where most of our customers are. The rest can go on in-store samples and packaging.

LEO: Makes sense. What about the launch date?

MANAGER: We're aiming for the third of October, just before the cooler weather, when coffee sales usually climb.

PRIYA: Good timing. And the name? Are we keeping 'Morning Hill'?

MANAGER: Yes, the test group liked it. Eighty percent said it sounded warm and trustworthy.

PRIYA: Excellent. One concern: our main rival is launching a similar product the same month.

MANAGER: I know. That's why our message must stress quality and local sourcing, which they can't match. Let's meet again next Tuesday to review the first designs.

reading.title: The Hunt for Exoplanets
reading.passage:
For most of human history, the planets we knew were limited to the handful orbiting our own Sun. The idea that other stars might host worlds of their own was an ancient speculation, but it remained unproven until the final decade of the twentieth century. The first confirmed detection of a planet circling a Sun-like star came in 1995, and it triggered a revolution in astronomy that continues today. By the early 2020s, astronomers had catalogued more than five thousand of these distant bodies, known as exoplanets.

Detecting an exoplanet is extraordinarily difficult because such worlds are tiny and dim compared with the stars they orbit. Rather than observing the planets directly, astronomers usually rely on indirect methods. The most productive of these is the transit technique. When a planet passes in front of its host star from our point of view, it blocks a minuscule fraction of the star's light, causing a tiny, regular dip in brightness. By measuring how much the light fades and how often the dimming repeats, scientists can estimate the planet's size and the length of its year.

A second widely used approach is the radial-velocity method. A planet does not simply revolve around a stationary star; instead, both bodies orbit a shared centre of mass. As a result, the star wobbles slightly, and this motion shifts the colour of its light in a measurable way. The size of the wobble reveals the planet's approximate mass. Combining the two methods gives astronomers both the mass and the diameter of a world, from which its density and likely composition can be inferred.

The ultimate aim of much exoplanet research is to identify worlds that might support life. Particular attention is paid to planets lying within the so-called habitable zone, the band of orbits where temperatures could allow liquid water to exist on a planet's surface. Finding such a planet is only the beginning, however. Confirming whether it actually possesses water, an atmosphere, or any sign of biological activity will require a new generation of enormous telescopes still under construction.

questions.q: The existence of planets around other stars was confirmed before the twentieth century.
Astronomers usually photograph exoplanets directly rather than using indirect methods.
The transit method can reveal how long a planet takes to orbit its star.
The radial-velocity method is more accurate than the transit method.
What does a star's wobble allow scientists to estimate?
The habitable zone is defined by the possibility of:
By the early 2020s, astronomers had catalogued more than ________ thousand exoplanets.
Combining the transit and radial-velocity methods allows scientists to calculate a planet's ________ and likely composition.

listening.title: Joining the University Astronomy Club
listening.script:
OFFICER: Good afternoon. Are you here to sign up for the Astronomy Club?

STUDENT: Yes, I saw your poster in the science building. How much does it cost to join?

OFFICER: Membership is fifteen pounds for the whole year, which covers all our regular meetings.

STUDENT: That sounds reasonable. When do you usually meet?

OFFICER: We meet every Thursday evening at seven o'clock, in Room 12 of the Physics block.

STUDENT: And do you ever use a real telescope?

OFFICER: We do. The club owns a large reflecting telescope, and twice a term we drive out to the countryside for an observing night, away from the city lights.

STUDENT: That sounds wonderful. Do I need any equipment of my own?

OFFICER: Not at all for the first year. Later, some members buy a pair of binoculars, but it's optional. Just bring a warm coat, because it gets very cold standing outside.

STUDENT: Good advice. Is there anything happening soon?

OFFICER: Yes, our next event is a talk on Saturn's rings, given by Doctor Hughes on the fifth of November.

STUDENT: Perfect. Where do I pay my membership?

OFFICER: Just fill in this form and bring it to the office on Monday.

When can the student hand in the membership form?

speaking.q: Let's talk about the night sky. Do you ever look at the stars? When you were a child, did you learn anything about the planets or the Moon? Would you like to visit a planetarium or an observatory? Why or why not?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of confirmed exoplanets discovered by four detection methods in two years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Detection method | 2010 | 2020
Transit | 90 | 2,400
Radial velocity | 410 | 820
Direct imaging | 25 | 60
Microlensing | 10 | 130

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The idea was pure speculation until the first exoplanet was confirmed.
The discovery triggered a revolution in modern astronomy.
Scientists have catalogued thousands of distant worlds.
The planet blocks only a minuscule fraction of the star's light.
During a transit, a planet passes in front of its star.
Astronomers noticed a regular dip in the star's brightness.
The star's wobble reveals the mass of an unseen planet.
From its density, scientists can infer the planet's composition.
The composition of a planet affects its likely habitability.
Few exoplanets fall within the habitable zone.
The method gives only an approximate mass.
Both bodies revolve around a shared centre of mass.

reading.title: Mapping the Surface of Mars
reading.passage:
Mars has fascinated observers for centuries, but only in recent decades have scientists been able to map its surface in remarkable detail. Early astronomers, peering through small telescopes, sketched dark patches and bright regions, and some convinced themselves they could see straight lines they called canals. These supposed channels later proved to be an optical illusion, a product of the limits of human vision rather than genuine engineering by Martians.

The modern understanding of Mars rests on data gathered by spacecraft. Orbiters circling the planet carry cameras and instruments capable of measuring height, temperature and mineral content. From these measurements, researchers have produced detailed maps showing that Mars is a world of extremes. It is home to Olympus Mons, the tallest known volcano in the solar system, which rises far higher than any mountain on Earth. The planet also contains an enormous canyon system, Valles Marineris, stretching for thousands of kilometres across its surface.

Perhaps the most significant discovery has been evidence that water once flowed across the Martian landscape. Orbital images reveal dried-up riverbeds, ancient lake basins and mineral deposits that typically form in the presence of water. This suggests that billions of years ago Mars may have been warmer and wetter, with conditions far more welcoming to life than the cold, dry desert we observe today.

Mapping the surface is not merely an academic exercise. Detailed maps are essential for choosing safe and scientifically promising landing sites for robotic rovers, and eventually for any human mission. Planners must avoid steep slopes, large boulders and regions covered in fine dust that could trap a vehicle. By combining maps of terrain with data on the chemistry of the rocks, mission designers can target the places most likely to preserve clues about the planet's watery past, and possibly traces of life that may once have existed there.

questions.q: The lines that early astronomers called canals turned out not to be real structures.
Olympus Mons is the tallest known volcano in the solar system.
Valles Marineris was formed by flowing water.
Mars today is warmer and wetter than it was billions of years ago.
Modern knowledge of the Martian surface mainly comes from:
Why are detailed maps important for missions?
options: to estimate the length of a Martian year

questions.q: Mineral deposits and dried-up riverbeds suggest that ________ once flowed on Mars.
Mission planners must avoid steep slopes, large boulders and regions covered in fine ________.

listening.title: Booking a Planetarium Show
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, City Planetarium. How can I help you?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book tickets for the evening show this Friday.

STAFF: Certainly. Which show were you interested in? We have one on the Moon at six, and one on the rings of Saturn at eight.

CALLER: The Saturn one, please. How long does it last?

STAFF: It runs for forty minutes, followed by a short question session.

CALLER: Great. I'd like four tickets, two adults and two children.

STAFF: Adult tickets are twelve dollars each, and children are seven. So that comes to thirty-eight dollars in total.

CALLER: Perfect. Is there parking nearby?

STAFF: Yes, there's a free car park behind the building, but it fills up quickly, so I'd recommend arriving by half past seven.

CALLER: Will do. And can we buy refreshments inside?

STAFF: There's a small cafe in the foyer that sells drinks and snacks until the show begins. Can I take a name for the booking?

CALLER: Yes, it's under Patterson, spelled P-A-T-T-E-R-S-O-N.

STAFF: Lovely. Your tickets will be waiting at the desk.

questions.q: Which show does the caller choose?
The show lasts for ________ minutes.

The staff recommends arriving by half past ________.
What is available in the foyer?

speaking.q:
Describe a place you would like to visit in space if it were possible. You should say:
- where this place is

and explain why this place appeals to you in particular.

writing.q:
Some people believe that governments should spend large amounts of money exploring planets such as Mars. Others argue that this money would be better spent solving problems here on Earth. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Mars has fascinated observers for centuries.

Certain rocks may preserve clues about the past.

reading.title: The Lives of Stars
reading.passage:
Stars may appear permanent and unchanging when we glance up at the night sky, yet each one is born, lives and eventually dies over an immense span of time. The process begins inside vast clouds of gas and dust called nebulae. Under the pull of gravity, regions of such a cloud slowly collapse and grow denser. As the material falls inward, it heats up, and when the centre becomes hot enough, nuclear reactions ignite. At this moment a true star is born, releasing energy by fusing hydrogen into helium.

The length of a star's life depends largely on its mass. It might seem that larger stars, with more fuel available, would survive the longest. In fact the opposite is true. Massive stars burn through their hydrogen at a furious rate and may last only a few million years, while small, cool stars consume their fuel so economically that they can shine steadily for many billions of years, far longer than the current age of the universe.

When a star finally exhausts its central supply of hydrogen, dramatic changes occur. A star of moderate size, similar to our Sun, swells into a red giant, sheds its outer layers and leaves behind a small, dense core known as a white dwarf, which slowly cools over time. The most massive stars meet a far more violent end, collapsing suddenly and exploding as a supernova, briefly outshining an entire galaxy.

These explosions are not merely spectacular events; they are essential to the chemistry of the universe. The heavier elements that make up the rocks beneath our feet and the iron in our blood were forged inside ancient stars and scattered across space when those stars died. In a very real sense, then, living things are constructed from the recycled remains of earlier generations of stars, a fact that links every human body to the distant cosmos.

questions.q: Stars are formed inside clouds of gas and dust called nebulae.
Massive stars live longer than small, cool stars.
A star similar to the Sun will eventually explode as a supernova.
White dwarfs are brighter than red giants.
What chiefly determines how long a star lives?
According to the passage, supernova explosions are important because they:
A newborn star releases energy by fusing hydrogen into ________.
A Sun-like star leaves behind a dense core called a white ________.

listening.title: A Lecture on the Solar Cycle
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning, everyone. Today I want to talk about the Sun's activity, and in particular the eleven-year solar cycle. You might think of the Sun as a constant, steady source of light, but in fact its surface changes over time in a fairly regular pattern.

The most obvious sign of this activity is sunspots, which are darker, cooler patches on the Sun's surface. At the start of a cycle there are very few sunspots. Their number then rises to a peak, which we call solar maximum, before falling again to a quiet period known as solar minimum. The whole pattern repeats roughly every eleven years.

Why does this matter to us here on Earth? During solar maximum the Sun releases more bursts of energy and particles. These can disturb our communications, interfere with satellites, and even cause power failures in extreme cases. On a more pleasant note, they also create brighter and more frequent displays of the northern lights, which can sometimes be seen much further south than usual. Understanding the cycle helps engineers protect important equipment, so accurate forecasting is now a serious field of research.

questions.q: The solar cycle lasts roughly ________ years.
The darker, cooler patches on the Sun are called ________.
The peak of activity is known as solar ________.
What can solar activity do during solar maximum?
According to the lecturer, the northern lights during solar maximum are:
Accurate ________ of the cycle helps engineers protect equipment.

speaking.q: Let's discuss science and society. Why do you think some people find astronomy fascinating while others are not interested at all? Should governments fund scientific research that has no immediate practical benefit? How might discoveries about distant stars change the way people see their own lives?

writing.q:
The table below shows the average number of sunspots recorded per year over three decades and the number of related power disruptions reported in one country. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Decade | Avg. sunspots per year | Power disruptions
1990s | 90 | 12
2000s | 70 | 8
2010s | 55 | 5

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Stars change over an immense span of time.
Regions of the cloud slowly collapse under gravity.
The core grows denser as material falls inward.
Nuclear reactions ignite at the star's centre.
Stars fuse hydrogen into helium.
Massive stars burn fuel at a furious rate.
Small stars use their fuel economically.

reading.title: The Problem of Space Debris
reading.passage:
Since the launch of the first artificial satellite in 1957, the region of space just above our planet has grown increasingly crowded. Thousands of working satellites now circle the Earth, providing services such as navigation, weather forecasting and global communication. Yet alongside these useful machines orbits a far larger population of useless objects, known collectively as space debris. This debris consists of dead satellites, spent rocket stages, and countless fragments produced when such objects collide or break apart.

The danger posed by debris arises from the extraordinary speeds involved. Objects in low orbit travel at around eight kilometres per second, far faster than any bullet. At such velocities, even a fragment the size of a marble carries enough energy to disable or destroy a functioning spacecraft. A single collision can shatter a satellite into thousands of new pieces, each of which becomes a fresh hazard. Scientists worry about a chain reaction in which one collision triggers others, gradually filling certain orbits with so much debris that they become unusable.

Tracking this material is a considerable challenge. Ground-based radar and telescopes can follow larger objects, and operators of valuable satellites are sometimes warned to shift their craft out of harm's way. However, the smaller fragments, which are too numerous and too tiny to monitor individually, remain effectively invisible, and these account for the majority of the threat.

Various solutions have been proposed to tackle the growing problem. Some engineers are designing nets, harpoons or robotic arms capable of capturing large pieces of debris and dragging them down to burn up in the atmosphere. Others argue that prevention is more practical than removal, and call for stricter rules requiring new satellites to be moved into a safe disposal orbit, or guided back to Earth, once their working life is over. Whichever approach is adopted, most experts agree that ignoring the issue is no longer an option if space is to remain usable for future generations.

questions.q: Space debris includes fragments created when objects collide or break apart.
A small fragment can carry enough energy to destroy a working spacecraft.
Small fragments are easier to track than large objects.
Capturing debris with nets is cheaper than guiding satellites back to Earth.
What makes space debris so dangerous?
The feared chain reaction would result in:
Objects in low orbit travel at around ________ kilometres per second.
Some engineers propose using nets, harpoons or robotic ________ to capture debris.

listening.title: Two Students Plan a Project on Satellites
listening.script:
MAYA: So, Tom, we need to decide what our group project on satellites will cover. Any ideas?

TOM: Well, I was thinking we could focus on how satellites help with weather forecasting. It's really practical.

MAYA: That's good, but quite a few groups are doing weather. What about space debris? It's a serious issue and not many people understand it.

TOM: I like that. We could explain how the debris forms and why it's dangerous.

MAYA: Exactly. We'll need some good visuals, though. Could you make a diagram showing the different orbits?

TOM: Sure. And maybe you could prepare the section on possible solutions, like the nets and robotic arms.

MAYA: Happy to. When is the project due, again?

TOM: It has to be handed in on the twentieth of March, so we've got about three weeks.

MAYA: Plenty of time if we start now. Shall we meet in the library on Tuesday to put it together?

TOM: Tuesday works for me. Let's aim for two o'clock by the computers on the first floor.

MAYA: Perfect. I'll bring my laptop and the research I've already done.

questions.q: What topic do the students finally choose?
Tom will make a diagram showing the different ________.
Maya will prepare the section on possible ________.
The project must be handed in on the ________ of March.
Where will they meet on Tuesday?
They agree to meet at ________ o'clock.

speaking.q: Let's talk about technology in daily life. Do you use any services that rely on satellites, such as maps or weather apps? How important is the internet to your everyday routine? Do you think people depend on technology too much these days?

writing.q:
As more countries and companies launch satellites, the amount of debris in space is increasing. Some people think individual companies should be responsible for cleaning up their own debris, while others believe this should be the job of international organisations. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The first artificial satellite was launched in 1957.
The region above Earth has grown crowded.
Space debris threatens working satellites.

reading.title: Living and Working on the Space Station
reading.passage:
High above the Earth, a large laboratory has been circling our planet continuously for more than two decades. The orbiting station provides a unique environment in which astronauts from many nations live and carry out scientific research. Because it travels at great speed, it completes a full orbit roughly every ninety minutes, which means its crew experiences sixteen sunrises and sunsets during a single day. Such an unusual rhythm makes ordinary tasks like sleeping surprisingly complicated.

The most striking feature of life aboard the station is weightlessness. Objects, and indeed people, float freely unless they are fixed in place, so everything from tools to food packets must be secured with straps or fasteners. Astronauts sleep in small compartments, zipped into bags attached to a wall, to stop themselves from drifting around while unconscious. Eating, washing and exercising all require careful adaptation to an environment in which nothing stays put.

Weightlessness is not merely an inconvenience; over time it has serious effects on the human body. Without the constant pull of gravity, muscles weaken and bones gradually lose density, much as they do in elderly people on Earth, only far more quickly. To counter this, crew members follow a strict exercise routine, working out for around two hours each day on specially designed machines that imitate the resistance of lifting weights.

Despite these difficulties, the station offers irreplaceable scientific opportunities. Experiments conducted in weightlessness reveal how flames, fluids and living cells behave when freed from gravity, producing insights that would be impossible to obtain in any laboratory on the ground. The station also serves as a testing ground for the technologies and human endurance that longer journeys, perhaps one day to Mars, will demand. In this way it is far more than a remarkable feat of engineering; it is a stepping stone towards humanity's wider exploration of space.

questions.q: The station completes one orbit of the Earth approximately every ninety minutes.
Astronauts must secure objects so they do not float away.
Bones lose density faster in space than they do on Earth.
The exercise machines on the station were invented by the astronauts themselves.
How many sunrises do the crew see in a single day?
Why do astronauts exercise for around two hours daily?
options: to counter muscle and bone loss

questions.q: Astronauts sleep zipped into bags attached to a ________.
The station is described as a stepping ________ towards wider exploration of space.

listening.title: Talk: Preparing Food for Astronauts
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today I'll explain how food is prepared for astronauts living on the space station. It might surprise you to learn just how much thought goes into every meal.

The first challenge is weight. Launching anything into space is enormously expensive, so food must be as light as possible. For this reason, many meals are dried out, a process that removes the water. The astronaut simply adds hot water before eating to make the meal ready again.

The second challenge is mess. In weightlessness, crumbs and droplets float everywhere and can damage equipment or get into someone's eye. That's why bread is usually replaced with tortillas, which produce far fewer crumbs. Salt and pepper are supplied as liquids, so they don't drift away.

Finally, there's the question of morale. Eating the same dull meals for months can lower a crew's spirits, so menus are carefully varied, and astronauts can even request some of their favourite dishes from home. A good meal, it turns out, is not a luxury in space but an important part of keeping the crew happy and healthy.

questions.q: Why must space food be as light as possible?
Many meals are ________ to remove the water.
Bread is replaced with ________ because they make fewer crumbs.
How are salt and pepper supplied?
Varying the menu helps keep up the crew's ________.
What is the speaker's main point about meals in space?
options: They help keep the crew happy and healthy

speaking.q: Let's consider human exploration. What qualities do you think a person needs to live and work in space for a long time? Do you believe humans will one day live permanently on other planets? Some people say exploration is part of human nature. To what extent do you agree?

writing.q:
The table below shows how astronauts on a space station spend an average working day, in hours, compared with a typical office worker on Earth. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Activity | Astronaut (hrs) | Office worker (hrs)
Work/research | 9 | 8
Exercise | 2 | 0.5
Sleep | 8 | 7
Meals | 2 | 1.5
Leisure | 3 | 7

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The station has orbited continuously for decades.
It completes one orbit every ninety minutes.
The unusual rhythm makes sleeping difficult.
The most striking feature is weightlessness.
Objects float freely in weightlessness.
Tools must be secured with straps.
Astronauts may drift around while asleep.

The station is a remarkable feat of engineering.

reading.title: The Hidden Life of Soil Fungi
reading.passage:
Beneath almost every forest floor lies a vast and largely invisible network of fungal threads known as mycelium. These threads, each far thinner than a human hair, spread through the soil in dense mats that can extend for hundreds of metres. For a long time scientists treated fungi as little more than decomposers, organisms that simply broke down dead leaves and wood. In recent decades, however, research has revealed that many soil fungi form intimate partnerships with living plants, and these partnerships are far more important than anyone once imagined.

The most common of these relationships involves mycorrhizal fungi, which attach themselves to the roots of trees and other plants. The fungus penetrates the root and, in exchange for sugars produced by the plant during photosynthesis, supplies the plant with water and mineral nutrients such as phosphorus and nitrogen. Because the fungal threads reach into tiny pores in the soil that roots cannot access, they greatly increase the surface area available for absorption. Studies estimate that a single tree may obtain up to forty per cent of its nitrogen through such fungal partners.

Perhaps the most surprising discovery is that these fungal networks can link many separate plants together. A seedling growing in deep shade, unable to make enough sugar for itself, may receive carbon transferred through the fungal threads from a larger, sunlit neighbour. Some researchers have described this arrangement as a kind of underground market, in which resources flow towards wherever they are most needed. Critics caution that the benefits are not always mutual, and that fungi may sometimes act in their own interest rather than as generous helpers.

Understanding these networks has practical consequences. When forests are cleared or soil is heavily disturbed by machinery, the delicate fungal threads are broken, and young trees planted afterwards often struggle to establish themselves. Some restoration projects now deliberately add fungal material to the soil before planting, hoping to rebuild the connections that took centuries to form.

questions.q: Scientists once believed that fungi were mainly responsible for breaking down dead material.
Mycorrhizal fungi give plants sugars in return for water and minerals.
Fungal threads can reach soil spaces that plant roots are too large to enter.
Forests with fungal networks recover from fire faster than those without.
What does the passage say about the transfer of carbon between plants?
options: It only occurs between trees of the same species
A shaded seedling may receive carbon from a sunlit plant
It always harms the larger plant
It happens only in restored forests

questions.q: Why do critics question the 'underground market' idea?
options: The fungi may act for their own benefit
Plants do not produce enough sugar
The networks are too small to matter

questions.q: A single tree may obtain up to ________ per cent of its nitrogen through fungal partners.
Some restoration projects add ________ material to the soil before planting trees.

listening.title: Booking a Nature Reserve Tour
listening.script:
STAFF: Good morning, Greenfield Wetlands Reserve, how can I help?
VISITOR: Hi, I'd like to book a guided walk for this Saturday, please.
STAFF: Of course. We have a morning tour at nine o'clock and an afternoon one at two. Which suits you?
VISITOR: The morning one, please. How long does it last?
STAFF: It runs for about ninety minutes, and our guide focuses mainly on the water birds.
VISITOR: Lovely. How much is a ticket?
STAFF: It's twelve pounds for adults, and children under twelve go free.
VISITOR: Great, I'll bring my son, he's ten. So that's just one adult ticket.
STAFF: Perfect. Can I take your name?
VISITOR: Yes, it's Patel, P-A-T-E-L.
STAFF: Thank you. One thing to remember, the paths can be muddy, so please wear waterproof boots. And bring binoculars if you have them, though we do lend out a few.
VISITOR: Good to know. Where exactly do we meet?
STAFF: At the visitor centre by the main car park. Try to arrive ten minutes early.
VISITOR: Will do. Thanks very much.

An adult ticket costs ________ pounds.
Why does the visitor pay for only one ticket?
options: The second ticket is sold out

questions.q: Visitors are advised to wear waterproof ________.
The group meets at the ________ by the main car park.

speaking.q: Let's talk about nature. Do you enjoy spending time outdoors? What kinds of natural places do you like to visit, and is there a park or natural area near where you live?

writing.q:
The table below shows the estimated number of a particular type of soil fungus (in millions per gram of soil) found in four different land types in one region. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Land type | Fungi (millions per gram)
Undisturbed forest | 9.4
Managed woodland | 6.1
Grassland | 3.8
Ploughed farmland | 1.2

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The mycelium spread silently through the damp soil.
Fungi act as decomposers, breaking down fallen leaves.
The two species form an intimate partnership.
The fungal threads penetrate the plant's roots.
The roots increase the absorption of water.
Carbon is transferred between the linked plants.
The benefit is not always mutual.
Heavy machinery disturbs the fragile soil.
The delicate threads break easily.
Young trees struggle to establish themselves.
The restoration project rebuilt the woodland.
They deliberately added fungi to the soil.

reading.title: How Birds Find Their Way
reading.passage:
Each autumn, billions of birds set off on journeys that can stretch across continents and oceans. A small songbird weighing less than a coin may fly thousands of kilometres from northern Europe to central Africa, often returning to the very same hedge the following spring. How these animals navigate with such precision has puzzled scientists for generations, and only recently has a clearer picture begun to emerge.

Birds appear to rely on several different cues at once. During the day, many species use the position of the sun, adjusting for the way it moves across the sky. At night, others orient themselves by the pattern of the stars, much as early human sailors once did. Young birds raised under an artificial planetarium sky have been shown to learn the rotation of the stars around a fixed point, which helps them identify north.

The most remarkable ability, however, is the apparent capacity of some birds to sense the Earth's magnetic field. Experiments suggest that certain proteins in the eye respond to magnetism, allowing a bird to literally see the direction of the field as a faint visual pattern. This magnetic sense seems to work even on cloudy nights when neither sun nor stars are visible, providing a reliable backup system.

Navigation is not purely instinctive, though. Older, experienced birds tend to follow more efficient routes than those flying for the first time, suggesting that memory and learning play a part. Landmarks such as coastlines, rivers and mountain ranges may guide birds along familiar corridors. When researchers displaced experienced adults far from their usual path, the birds were able to correct their course and reach the correct destination, while inexperienced young birds simply continued in their original direction.

Understanding these mechanisms matters for conservation. As cities grow brighter and taller, artificial light and glass buildings disorient migrating birds, causing millions of deaths each year. Knowing how birds read the sky may help planners design safer routes through an increasingly crowded landscape.

questions.q: Some small songbirds return to the exact same location year after year.
Birds rely on only one method to find their way.
Young birds can learn star patterns under an artificial sky.
The magnetic sense in birds works better than the use of stars.
What did the displacement experiment reveal?
options: Experienced adults could correct their course
Young birds reached the destination first
Birds refused to fly when moved

questions.q: According to the passage, the magnetic sense is useful because it
options: works when the sun and stars are hidden

questions.q: Certain ________ in the eye are thought to respond to magnetism.
Artificial light and ________ buildings cause millions of bird deaths each year.

listening.title: A Lecture on Bird Migration Research
listening.script: LECTURER: Right, today we're looking at how researchers actually track migrating birds. In the past, the main method was simple metal rings placed on a bird's leg, each printed with a unique number. If the bird was caught again somewhere else, you'd know it had travelled. The problem, of course, is that very few ringed birds are ever found a second time, perhaps one in a hundred. So the data was patchy. These days we use lightweight electronic tags. The smallest, called geolocators, weigh less than half a gram and record light levels, which let us estimate the bird's position from sunrise and sunset times. The drawback is that you must recapture the bird to download the data. More advanced satellite tags transmit a position directly, but they remain too heavy for the smallest species. For our field project next month, you'll be fitting geolocators on a population of swallows near the coast. Please remember three rules: handle each bird for no more than five minutes, record the exact time of capture, and never tag a bird that weighs under twenty grams. We'll meet at the field station at six in the morning, so an early night is recommended.

questions.q: What was the main weakness of metal leg rings?
options: Few birds were ever found again

questions.q: The smallest electronic tags, called geolocators, weigh less than ________ a gram.
Why are satellite tags not used on the smallest birds?
Students must handle each bird for no more than ________ minutes.
Students must never tag a bird weighing under ________ grams.
The group will meet at the field station at ________ in the morning.

speaking.q:
Describe an animal that you find interesting. You should say:
- what the animal is
- where it lives and what it looks like
- what it does that interests you
and explain why you find this animal so interesting.

writing.q:

vocab.example: Birds navigate using the stars and the sun.

reading.title: The Return of the Beaver
reading.passage:
For more than four hundred years, the wild beaver was absent from much of Britain, hunted to extinction for its fur, its meat and a substance from its glands once used in medicine. By the seventeenth century the animal had vanished entirely from the country. In recent years, however, beavers have begun to reappear, partly through deliberate releases and partly because some escaped from private collections. Their return has sparked a lively debate about whether they belong in a modern, heavily farmed landscape.

Supporters point to the remarkable effect beavers have on their surroundings. By felling small trees and building dams across streams, they create ponds and wetlands where there were once only narrow channels. These new habitats slow the flow of water, which can reduce the risk of flooding further downstream. The pools also filter out soil and pollutants, leaving the water cleaner, and they provide homes for fish, insects, amphibians and water birds. For this reason ecologists often describe the beaver as an 'engineer' that shapes the environment for the benefit of many other creatures.

Not everyone welcomes the newcomers, however. Some farmers complain that beaver dams flood valuable fields and block drainage ditches, while their burrows can weaken riverbanks. Foresters worry about the loss of young trees. In several areas, conflicts have arisen over who should pay for any damage and who has the authority to remove a troublesome animal.

Managing these tensions requires careful planning. In places where beavers cause genuine problems, wildlife officers can install special pipes that allow water to pass through a dam without the animal noticing, keeping the pond at a fixed level. Trees of particular value can be protected with wire mesh. Studies carried out over the past decade suggest that, where such measures are applied sensibly, the overall benefits to wildlife and water quality clearly outweigh the costs. Even so, the question of how humans and beavers can share a crowded landscape is far from fully resolved.

questions.q: Beavers disappeared from Britain partly because they were hunted for their fur.
All of the returning beavers were released on purpose by scientists.
Beaver ponds can help reduce flooding in areas downstream.
Beavers have now spread to every region of Britain.
Why do ecologists call the beaver an 'engineer'?
options: It reshapes the environment for other species
It repairs damaged rivers for money
It removes pollution from the air

questions.q: What is one method used to manage problem beavers?
Beavers create ponds and wetlands by felling trees and building ________.
Valuable trees can be protected from beavers using wire ________.

listening.title: Radio Interview About a Beaver Project
listening.script:
HOST: Today I'm joined by Dr Lena Forsythe, who leads a beaver reintroduction project in the west of the country. Lena, welcome. How many beavers did you start with?
GUEST: Thank you. We began with just two pairs, so four animals in total, released back in 2018.
HOST: And how is the population now?
GUEST: It's grown steadily. We now count around thirty individuals living along the river valley.
HOST: That's impressive. What has surprised you most?
GUEST: Honestly, the speed at which the landscape changed. Within two years the beavers had built five large dams, and the number of frog species in the area doubled.
HOST: Have there been any complaints from local people?
GUEST: A few, mainly from one farm where a field flooded. We solved it by lowering the water level with a drainage pipe, and the farmer is happy now.
HOST: What's the next step?
GUEST: We're running free guided walks every Sunday so the public can see the dams for themselves. People can book through our website, and we ask for a small donation of five pounds towards monitoring costs.
HOST: Wonderful. Thank you, Lena.

Year | Estimated beavers | Number of dams

2022 | 21 | 9
2024 | 30 | 14

reading.title: The Secret Senses of Plants
reading.passage:
We tend to think of plants as passive organisms, rooted in one spot and unable to respond to the world around them. Yet a growing body of research shows that plants are far more aware of their surroundings than their stillness suggests. Although they have no brain or nervous system, plants can detect light, touch, chemicals and even sound, and they adjust their behaviour accordingly.

The most familiar of these abilities is the response to light. A young shoot will bend towards a window, growing in the direction of the brightest source. This happens because light triggers the movement of a growth hormone to the shaded side of the stem, causing that side to lengthen faster and the plant to lean. Plants can also measure the length of the night, which allows many species to flower at exactly the right time of year.

More surprising is the way plants react to touch and damage. When a caterpillar begins chewing on a leaf, the plant may release chemicals that make its tissues harder to digest, and some species even emit airborne signals that attract the predators of the attacking insect. Remarkably, neighbouring plants can detect these warning signals and begin preparing their own defences before they are attacked themselves.

The claim that plants can sense sound is more controversial. A few studies report that the roots of certain plants grow towards the sound of running water, and that some flowers produce sweeter nectar when they detect the wing-beats of a passing bee. Critics argue that these results are difficult to repeat and may have simpler explanations. Nonetheless, the experiments have encouraged scientists to take plant perception seriously rather than dismissing it.

Whatever the final verdict on plant hearing, the wider lesson is clear. Plants are not the simple, unfeeling things they once appeared to be. They gather information, communicate with one another and make decisions about where to grow and how to defend themselves - all without a single nerve cell. Understanding these abilities could one day help farmers grow stronger crops with fewer chemicals.

questions.q: Plants can respond to their environment despite lacking a nervous system.
A shoot bends towards light because the lit side of the stem grows faster.
Some plants release chemicals that attract the enemies of attacking insects.
Plants that sense sound produce more seeds than those that do not.
How do neighbouring plants benefit from warning signals?
options: They grow taller than the attacked plant
They prepare their defences in advance

questions.q: Why is the claim about plants sensing sound described as controversial?
options: The results are hard to repeat

questions.q: Light triggers the movement of a growth ________ to the shaded side of the stem.

listening.title: Two Students Plan a Plant Experiment
listening.script:
MAYA: So, for our biology project on plant senses, what should we actually test?
LIAM: I was thinking we could see whether bean seedlings really do bend towards light.
MAYA: Nice. We'd need a controlled setup though. How many plants?
LIAM: Let's grow twelve seedlings, six in normal light and six with light coming from only one side.
MAYA: Okay. And we measure the angle of the stem every day, right?
LIAM: Exactly. We record it at the same time each morning, say eight o'clock, for two weeks.
MAYA: That works. What about the soil and water - we have to keep those the same.
LIAM: Right, same compost for all of them, and we give each pot fifty millilitres of water daily.
MAYA: Good. Where do we keep them?
LIAM: The lab has a shelf by the window, but Professor Adams said we should use the side room instead, because the temperature there is more stable.
MAYA: Makes sense. Oh, and we need to take photos for the report.
LIAM: I'll bring my camera. Let's start planting on Monday.
MAYA: Perfect. I'll write up the method section tonight.

reading.title: Life in the Deep Ocean
reading.passage:
Below a depth of about a thousand metres, the ocean enters a world of permanent darkness. No sunlight reaches these depths, the temperature hovers just above freezing, and the weight of the water above creates pressures that would instantly crush an unprotected human body. For a long time scientists assumed that almost nothing could survive in such conditions. The deep sea was imagined as a cold, empty desert. Modern exploration has overturned that view entirely.

Far from being lifeless, the deep ocean teems with strange and beautifully adapted creatures. Many produce their own light through a chemical reaction inside their bodies, a phenomenon known as bioluminescence. This living light serves several purposes: it can lure prey towards a hidden mouth, startle an approaching predator, or signal to a potential mate in the surrounding blackness. Some fish even carry glowing lures that dangle in front of their jaws like a fishing rod.

With no plants able to photosynthesise in the dark, most deep-sea life ultimately depends on a slow rain of dead matter drifting down from the sunlit waters above. Yet there are remarkable exceptions. Around cracks in the sea floor, superheated water rich in minerals gushes out from beneath the Earth's crust. Here, communities of bacteria convert chemicals into energy, supporting clusters of tube worms, clams and pale crabs that thrive in total darkness without relying on the sun at all.

Studying these environments is extraordinarily difficult and expensive. Submarines and remote-controlled vehicles must be built to withstand crushing pressure, and a single dive may cost a large sum and yield only a few hours of observation. Even so, each expedition tends to discover species never seen before, suggesting that the great majority of deep-sea life remains entirely unknown to science. Some researchers warn that mining companies, attracted by the valuable metals on the ocean floor, may begin to damage these fragile habitats before we have even learned what lives there.

questions.q: Scientists once believed very little could live in the deep ocean.
Bioluminescence is used only to attract mates.
Some deep-sea communities survive without depending on sunlight.
Mining companies have already destroyed several deep-sea habitats.
What is most deep-sea life ultimately dependent on?
options: Dead matter drifting down from above

questions.q: How do communities near sea-floor cracks obtain energy?
Light produced by living creatures through a chemical reaction is called ________.
Each new expedition tends to discover ________ never seen before.

listening.title: Talk on Deep-Sea Exploration Equipment
listening.script: SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone, to this short talk on the tools we use to explore the deep ocean. The first thing to understand is pressure. For every ten metres you descend, the pressure increases by about one atmosphere, so at three thousand metres the pressure is roughly three hundred times what we feel at the surface. That's why our equipment must be incredibly strong. Our main vehicle is a remotely operated vehicle, or ROV, connected to the ship by a long cable that carries power and sends video back in real time. It's fitted with two robotic arms for collecting samples and bright lights, because, as you know, there is no natural light down there. A typical dive lasts around eight hours, and we can reach a maximum depth of four thousand metres. One limitation is the cable itself, which restricts how far the ROV can travel from the ship. For deeper or wider searches we sometimes use a free-swimming robot that follows a programmed route, but it cannot be controlled directly. After the talk, you're welcome to visit the lab on the second floor, where one of these vehicles is on display.

reading.title: How Children Acquire a Second Language
reading.passage:
The ease with which young children pick up additional languages has long fascinated researchers. A child who moves to a new country at the age of five may, within a year or two, speak the local language with a fluency and accent indistinguishable from those of native speakers. An adult making the same move, by contrast, often struggles for years and rarely loses a foreign accent entirely. This striking difference has led scientists to ask whether there is a special window of time during which language learning comes most naturally.

The idea is usually known as the critical period hypothesis. According to this view, the human brain is especially well prepared for language during early childhood, and this readiness gradually fades as a person matures. Supporters point to the fact that almost all children master their first language without formal instruction, simply by being surrounded by it. They argue that the same biological advantage extends to second languages learned during these early years.

Not everyone accepts this explanation, however. Some researchers suggest that the apparent advantage enjoyed by children has less to do with biology than with circumstance. Children are typically immersed in the new language for many hours each day, at school and at play, and they feel few of the anxieties that can hold adults back. Freed from the fear of making mistakes, they experiment constantly, and it is this relaxed, intensive practice, rather than any fixed deadline in the brain, that produces such rapid progress.

Whatever the true explanation, the practical lesson seems clear. Exposure matters enormously. Studies comparing learners in classrooms with those living among native speakers consistently find that the second group advances more quickly, regardless of age. For adults hoping to acquire a new language, the message is encouraging rather than discouraging: while perfect pronunciation may remain out of reach, genuine fluency is far from impossible.

questions.q: A child who relocates abroad at five may come to sound like a native speaker within a couple of years.
Adults who move to a new country usually lose their foreign accent completely.
The critical period hypothesis claims that the brain's readiness for language declines as a person grows older.
Most governments now fund language immersion programmes for adult immigrants.
What alternative explanation do some researchers offer for children's success?
options: Their circumstances and lack of anxiety

questions.q: According to the final paragraph, learners living among native speakers tend to
options: progress more slowly than classroom learners
advance more quickly regardless of age

questions.q: Supporters note that children master their first language without formal ________.
The text concludes that genuine ________ is far from impossible for adult learners.

listening.title: Enrolling in a Language Course
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, City Language Centre, how can I help?
STUDENT: Hi, I'd like to sign up for a Spanish evening class.
RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We run beginner and intermediate levels. Which suits you?
STUDENT: Intermediate, I think. I studied a little at school.
RECEPTIONIST: Perfect. The intermediate group meets on Thursdays from seven until nine.
STUDENT: That works for me. How much is the course?
RECEPTIONIST: It's ninety pounds for a term of ten weeks, and that includes the workbook.
STUDENT: Great. And where exactly do the classes take place?
RECEPTIONIST: In Room 14, on the second floor of the main building.
STUDENT: Could I get the teacher's name?
RECEPTIONIST: Certainly. Your tutor will be Ms Alvarez. She's a native speaker from Madrid.
STUDENT: Lovely. Do I need to bring anything to the first session?
RECEPTIONIST: Just a notebook and a pen. Everything else is provided.
STUDENT: Thank you. How do I confirm my place?
RECEPTIONIST: Simply pay online by Friday and you'll receive a confirmation email.

Language | 2020 | 2023

reading.title: The Hidden Power of Body Language
reading.passage:
When we think of communication, we usually picture words. Yet a great deal of what passes between people is never spoken at all. Posture, gesture, facial expression and eye contact form a silent channel that runs alongside speech, often carrying meaning that words cannot. Researchers who study this nonverbal channel argue that it shapes our impressions of others far more powerfully than we tend to realise.

Consider a simple handshake or the way a person enters a room. Within seconds, observers form judgements about confidence, warmth and trustworthiness, drawing almost entirely on signals that the speaker may not even be aware of sending. A slight lean forward suggests interest; crossed arms may hint at discomfort; a steady gaze can convey honesty. These cues are processed rapidly and, for the most part, unconsciously.

Much of this nonverbal behaviour appears to be shared across cultures. Smiles of genuine happiness, expressions of fear and gestures of disgust seem to be recognised by people everywhere, regardless of language or upbringing. This has led some scientists to conclude that certain expressions are not learned but inherited, part of our common biological heritage as a species.

Other signals, however, vary widely from one society to another. The amount of personal space people prefer, the acceptability of touching during conversation, and the meaning of particular hand gestures can differ dramatically between cultures. A gesture considered friendly in one country may cause offence in another. For travellers and businesspeople alike, misreading these silent signals can lead to awkwardness or even serious misunderstanding.

Understanding body language, then, is not simply a matter of decoding a universal code. It requires sensitivity to context and culture. Those who learn to read and use nonverbal cues skilfully often find that their relationships, both personal and professional, become noticeably smoother.

questions.q: Nonverbal signals can communicate things that words are unable to express.
People always remain fully aware of the body language they are producing.
Certain facial expressions appear to be recognised by people in all cultures.
Training in body language is now compulsory in most business schools.
Why do some scientists believe certain expressions are inherited?
options: They are taught in schools worldwide
They are recognised regardless of language or upbringing

questions.q: What does the passage say about hand gestures?
options: They can cause offence in another culture

questions.q: A slight lean forward suggests ________ in a conversation.
Reading nonverbal cues skilfully can make ________ smoother.

listening.title: A Lecture on Communication Skills
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today we're looking at how we communicate without words. Now, studies suggest that in a face-to-face exchange, a large share of the emotional message comes not from the words themselves but from tone and body language. One often-quoted figure is that only about seven per cent of emotional meaning is carried by the actual words. The rest comes from voice and physical signals. Let me give you three practical tips. First, maintain eye contact, but don't stare; aim for around sixty per cent of the time. Second, watch your posture. Standing or sitting upright signals confidence and keeps your voice clear. Third, mirror the other person gently. When you subtly match their pace and gestures, they tend to feel more at ease. Now, a word of caution. These rules are not universal. In some cultures, prolonged eye contact is seen as rude rather than honest. So always adjust to your audience. For your assignment, observe one conversation this week and note three nonverbal signals you spot. Bring your notes to next Tuesday's seminar.

For eye contact, aim for around ________ per cent of the time.
What does upright posture signal, according to the lecturer?
What is the third tip the lecturer gives?
In some cultures, prolonged eye contact is seen as ________.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you understood someone without them speaking. You should say:
- who the person was
- what the situation was
- how you understood what they meant
and explain how you felt about the experience.

vocab.example: Good posture conveys confidence.
He made a welcoming gesture with his hand.

reading.title: The Rise and Fall of Endangered Languages
reading.passage:
Of the roughly seven thousand languages spoken today, linguists estimate that nearly half may disappear before the end of this century. Languages die when their last fluent speakers pass away without having handed the language on to a younger generation. Each loss is more than a statistic, for a language carries within it a particular way of seeing the world, along with stories, songs and knowledge that often exist nowhere else.

The forces driving this decline are not new, but they have accelerated sharply. As people move from villages to cities in search of work, they increasingly adopt the dominant national language, which offers wider opportunities. Education systems, broadcasting and the internet tend to reinforce a handful of major languages, leaving smaller ones with fewer and fewer domains in which they are useful. Within a generation or two, parents may decide that their children are better served by the dominant tongue and stop passing on their own.

The consequences reach beyond sentiment. When a language vanishes, so does a vast store of practical knowledge: the names of local plants, traditional methods of healing, and detailed understanding of the surrounding environment. Researchers studying biodiversity have found that regions rich in languages are often rich in species too, and that indigenous vocabularies sometimes record ecological details that scientists have yet to document.

Efforts to reverse the trend are underway in many places. Communities have set up immersion schools where lessons are taught entirely in the threatened language, and digital tools now allow speakers to record dictionaries and stories before they are lost. Revival is difficult and rarely complete, but a number of languages once thought to be dying have begun, against the odds, to recover. Their survival depends, above all, on whether young people choose to speak them.

questions.q: Linguists believe close to half of today's languages may vanish within this century.
A language dies only when it is officially banned by a government.
Regions with many languages are often also rich in biological species.
The United Nations has set a target date for saving all endangered languages.
Why do people often adopt the dominant national language?
What does the passage say revival ultimately depends on?
options: Whether young people choose to speak the language

questions.q: Communities have set up ________ schools where lessons are taught only in the threatened language.
Indigenous vocabularies sometimes record ________ details unknown to scientists.

listening.title: A Radio Feature on a Language Revival
listening.script: PRESENTER: Welcome back. Today we're hearing about an unusual success story from a small island community. For decades, the local language, Marun, was spoken only by a handful of elderly residents. By the year 2000, fewer than forty fluent speakers remained. Then a group of teachers decided to act. They opened a nursery where children would hear Marun all day, every day. The idea was simple: surround the youngest learners with the language before they started formal schooling. Funding was tight at first, with the project relying entirely on donations. But the results were striking. Within fifteen years, the number of confident young speakers had risen to over three hundred. The community also produced a printed dictionary containing some twelve thousand words, many of which had never been written down before. Today, Marun is even taught at the island's secondary school. The organisers say the key lesson is that revival must start with the very young. Adults can help, but it is children who carry a language into the future.

What did the teachers open first?
At first the project relied entirely on ________.
Within fifteen years, young speakers numbered over ________.
The dictionary contained about ________ words.
What does the speaker say is the key lesson?
options: Revival must start with the very young

speaking.q: Let's discuss languages in society. Why do you think some languages disappear while others spread around the world? Should governments spend money to protect minority languages, or should resources go elsewhere?

reading.title: The Surprising History of Punctuation
reading.passage:
The marks that organise our writing today, the commas, full stops and question marks we scatter across the page almost without thinking, have a longer and stranger history than most readers imagine. For much of the ancient world, writing contained no spaces between words and no punctuation at all. A reader faced an unbroken stream of letters and had to work out, often by reading aloud, where one word ended and the next began. Reading was therefore a slow and demanding skill, practised by relatively few.

The first systems of punctuation are usually credited to scholars working in the ancient library of Alexandria. To help readers know when to pause, they introduced a series of dots placed at different heights, each indicating a break of a particular length. These early marks were intended less as rules of grammar than as guides to performance, telling a reader aloud when to breathe and when to stop.

For centuries afterwards, punctuation remained inconsistent. Different scribes used different symbols, and there was no agreement on what each one meant. It was only with the spread of printing in the fifteenth century that conventions began to settle. Printers, who produced many identical copies of a text, had a strong interest in standardisation, and gradually a shared set of marks emerged across Europe.

Punctuation continues to evolve even now. The rise of digital messaging has produced new conventions, from the playful use of multiple exclamation marks to the deliberate omission of full stops, which some younger writers feel can make a message seem cold or abrupt. Far from being fixed rules handed down from antiquity, punctuation marks are living tools, constantly adjusted to suit the way people actually read and write.

questions.q: Ancient texts often contained no spaces between words.
Early punctuation was designed mainly as a set of grammar rules.
Printing encouraged the standardisation of punctuation.
The question mark was the first punctuation symbol ever invented.
Why was reading a difficult skill in the ancient world?
options: Texts had no spaces or punctuation

questions.q: What did the scholars of Alexandria introduce?
Printers had a strong interest in ________.
Some younger writers feel that omitting full stops can make a message seem ________.

listening.title: A Student Seminar on Writing Systems
listening.script: TUTOR: Okay, let's get started. Today's seminar is on the history of writing systems, and I want each of you to take notes for the quiz. So, the earliest known writing appeared in Mesopotamia around five thousand years ago. It began not as poetry or letters, but as a way of keeping records, mostly lists of goods and trade. The symbols were pressed into wet clay using a reed, and we call this script cuneiform. Now, here's something interesting. Writing was invented independently in at least three different parts of the world. Besides Mesopotamia, it arose in China and in Central America. For your assignment, I'd like a short essay comparing any two of these systems. It should be about eight hundred words, and it's due on the fifteenth of next month. Please upload it to the course website rather than emailing it to me. And one more thing: the library has a special exhibition on ancient scripts this week, so do try to visit before it closes on Friday.

questions.q: The earliest known writing appeared around ________ thousand years ago.
What was the earliest writing mainly used for?
The early script pressed into clay is called ________.
The essay should be about ________ words.
The essay is due on the ________ of next month.
How should students submit the essay?

speaking.q: Let's talk about writing. Do you prefer writing by hand or typing on a device? When was the last time you wrote a letter or card by hand, and how did it feel?

writing.q:
Some people think that the increasing use of digital messaging is damaging young people's ability to write properly. Others believe it simply reflects a natural change in language. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Punctuation guides the reader through a sentence.
We scatter commas across the page.
Ancient texts were an unbroken stream of letters.
Reading was a demanding skill.
Punctuation is credited to Alexandrian scholars.
Each dot indicated a break of a certain length.
Early punctuation was inconsistent.
Different scribes used different symbols.
