Punctuation continues to evolve.
The omission of full stops can seem cold.
A message can seem abrupt without punctuation.

reading.title: Can Machines Really Translate?
reading.passage:
For decades, the dream of a machine that could translate one language into another instantly seemed firmly out of reach. Early attempts, dating back to the middle of the twentieth century, relied on enormous dictionaries and rigid grammatical rules. The results were often comical, producing sentences that were technically composed of the right words yet made little sense. Translation, it turned out, was far more than a matter of swapping one word for another.

The breakthrough came not from better dictionaries but from a change in approach. Instead of teaching computers grammatical rules, engineers began feeding them vast quantities of texts that had already been translated by humans. By analysing millions of such examples, the software learned to recognise patterns, predicting how a phrase in one language was most likely to appear in another. This statistical method, later refined by powerful learning systems, transformed the quality of machine translation almost overnight.

Today, automatic translation tools handle billions of requests every day, helping travellers order meals, students read foreign articles and businesses communicate across borders. For straightforward, factual texts, the output can be remarkably accurate. Yet serious limitations remain. Machines still struggle with humour, irony and cultural references, and they can be misled by words that carry several meanings. A mistranslated joke is harmless enough, but errors in legal or medical documents can have grave consequences.

Most experts therefore see these tools not as a replacement for human translators but as an aid to them. A machine can produce a rough draft in seconds, which a skilled human can then refine. Far from making human language skills obsolete, the technology may make them more valuable, since judging whether a translation truly captures meaning remains, for now, a distinctly human task.

questions.q: Early machine translation relied on large dictionaries and fixed grammar rules.
The major breakthrough came from building even larger dictionaries.
Modern translation tools can be confused by words with several meanings.
Most universities now require translation students to study computer programming.
What new approach transformed machine translation?
How do most experts view translation tools?
options: As a complete replacement for humans
As an aid to human translators
As a danger to be banned

questions.q: Errors in legal or ________ documents can have grave consequences.
A machine can produce a rough ________ in seconds for a human to refine.

listening.title: An Interview with a Translator
listening.script:
HOST: I'm joined today by Daniel, a professional translator. Daniel, do machine tools worry you?
DANIEL: Not really. They've actually changed my job for the better. I now use software to produce a first draft, then I spend my time polishing it.
HOST: So they save you time?
DANIEL: Enormously. A task that once took a full day might now take three hours. But the machine alone is never enough.
HOST: Why is that?
DANIEL: Because it doesn't understand context. Last week it translated a marketing slogan literally, and the result was almost insulting in the target language. A human caught it instantly.
HOST: What advice would you give someone wanting to enter the field?
DANIEL: Master at least two languages deeply, of course, but also learn the subject area you translate in. I specialise in medical texts, and that knowledge is what clients pay for.
HOST: Fascinating. And where can listeners find your work?
DANIEL: I run a blog at translatewell dot net, with new articles every Monday.

questions.q: How does Daniel feel about machine translation tools?
options: They have changed his job for the better

questions.q: A task that once took a full day might now take about ________ hours.
What problem does Daniel give as an example?
Daniel specialises in ________ texts.
He advises learners to master at least ________ languages deeply.
Daniel posts new blog articles every ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss technology and language. As translation software improves, do you think people will still bother to learn foreign languages? What might be gained or lost if everyone relied on machines to communicate across languages?

writing.q:
The chart below shows the percentage of people in one country who used machine translation tools for four purposes in 2015 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Purpose | 2015 | 2023
Travel | 30% | 62%
Study | 25% | 48%
Work | 18% | 55%
Reading news | 12% | 40%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The app translates text instantly.
Early systems used rigid grammar rules.
The early results were often comical.
The breakthrough came from a new approach.
A statistical method improved translation.
A human can refine the rough draft.
The output can be remarkably accurate.
Machines struggle to detect irony.

reading.title: The Return of the Heritage Wheat
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the goal of plant breeders was clear: to produce wheat that yielded as much grain as possible. The varieties that emerged were short, sturdy and remarkably productive, and they helped to feed a rapidly growing world. Yet in pursuing yield above all else, breeders narrowed the range of wheat grown on farms until a handful of modern types dominated fields across entire continents.

In recent years a small but determined group of farmers and bakers has begun to look backwards, reviving older varieties known as heritage or landrace wheats. These plants are typically taller and produce less grain per field, which is why they fell out of favour. Their supporters argue, however, that they offer qualities the modern varieties lack: deeper flavour, a more complex texture and, some claim, greater tolerance of poor soils and unpredictable weather.

The evidence for these claims is mixed. Carefully controlled trials suggest that heritage wheats can indeed survive drought and disease better than their modern counterparts in certain conditions, partly because their long roots reach water deep underground. On the other hand, their lower yields make them an uncertain choice for farmers who must cover their costs, and the flour they produce behaves differently in baking, which can frustrate those used to standard ingredients.

Advocates insist that the value of heritage wheat lies not in replacing modern crops but in widening the genetic pool from which future varieties might be bred. As the climate grows less predictable, they argue, a diverse store of traits could prove invaluable. Critics counter that nostalgia is doing much of the persuading, and that romantic talk of forgotten grains should not distract from the practical task of feeding billions. What both sides accept is that the narrowing of the world's crops has gone further than is wise, and that some reversal of that trend is overdue.

questions.q: Twentieth-century plant breeders prioritised the quantity of grain produced over other qualities.
Heritage wheat varieties generally produce more grain per field than modern ones.
Heritage wheat is now grown more widely than modern wheat in Europe.
According to the passage, why can some heritage wheats cope with drought?
options: Their long roots reach deep water

questions.q: What do advocates say is the main value of heritage wheat?
options: It will replace all modern crops
It widens the genetic pool for future breeding

questions.q: Critics argue that ________ is doing much of the persuading in favour of heritage grains.
Both sides agree that the narrowing of the world's crops has gone further than is ________.
The flour from heritage wheat behaves the same way in baking as standard flour.

listening.title: Joining a Community Vegetable Garden
listening.script:
ORGANISER: Hello, Riverside Community Garden, this is Priya speaking.
CALLER: Hi, I saw your poster and I'd like to join the garden. How does it work?
ORGANISER: Lovely. Each member rents a small plot and grows whatever they like. The membership fee is forty pounds for the whole year.
CALLER: That sounds reasonable. What does the fee cover?
ORGANISER: It covers your plot, plus shared tools and the cost of water. We also run free workshops once a month.
CALLER: Brilliant. When can I come and look round?
ORGANISER: We hold an open session every Saturday morning, from ten until twelve.
CALLER: Perfect. Should I bring anything?
ORGANISER: Just sturdy shoes and gloves if you have them. We provide everything else.
CALLER: And how big is each plot?
ORGANISER: They're about three metres by four. Enough for plenty of vegetables.
CALLER: Great. My name's Tom Barnes, that's B-A-R-N-E-S.
ORGANISER: Thank you, Tom. I'll add you to the list and see you on Saturday.

speaking.q: Let's talk about food and cooking. Do you enjoy cooking at home, or do you prefer eating out? What kinds of food did you eat most often as a child? Have your eating habits changed in recent years, and why?

Crop | 2010 | 2022
Modern wheat | 420 | 360
Heritage wheat | 5 | 28
Barley | 180 | 150

Bakers are working to revive forgotten wheat varieties.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Cheap Meat
reading.passage:
Meat has never been cheaper, at least in much of the wealthy world. A chicken that would once have fed a family for a week can now be bought for the price of a coffee, and supermarkets compete to drive prices lower still. To shoppers, this looks like simple progress. Yet a growing number of economists argue that the true cost of cheap meat is not reflected on the label, but is paid elsewhere, often by people who never see the bill.

The falling price of meat is the result of intensive farming, in which large numbers of animals are raised quickly in confined spaces and fed carefully formulated diets. This system is extraordinarily efficient at converting grain into protein. It also produces large quantities of waste, depends heavily on antibiotics, and generates greenhouse gases that contribute to climate change. None of these effects appears in the supermarket price, and so consumers have little reason to take them into account.

Economists call such hidden burdens externalities: costs created by an activity but borne by third parties. When a factory pollutes a river, the people downstream pay through dirtier water, not the factory itself. In the case of cheap meat, the externalities include the medical risk posed by overused antibiotics, the environmental damage of intensive production, and the future costs of a warming planet.

Some governments have considered taxes designed to reflect these hidden costs, nudging prices upward so that they tell a more honest story. Such proposals are deeply unpopular, since few voters welcome paying more for a weekly staple, and critics warn that the heaviest burden would fall on poorer households. Supporters reply that the present system simply postpones the bill, handing it to future generations along with a damaged environment. The debate, they note, is not really about whether meat is cheap, but about who ultimately pays for it.

questions.q: The supermarket price of meat reflects all the costs of producing it.
What does the passage say intensive farming is especially good at?
Economists use the term ________ for costs created by an activity but paid by third parties.
Proposed taxes on meat have proved popular with most voters.
Cheap meat is more common in cities than in the countryside.
According to critics, who would be hit hardest by a meat tax?
Supporters of the tax argue that the current system delays the cost rather than removing it.
The example given of an externality is a factory that pollutes a ________.

listening.title: A Farm Shop Order
listening.script:
STAFF: Morning, Greenfields Farm Shop, how can I help you?
CUSTOMER: Hi, I'd like to place an order for delivery on Friday, please.
STAFF: No worries. What would you like?
CUSTOMER: Could I get a dozen free-range eggs, two kilos of potatoes, and a loaf of the sourdough bread?
STAFF: Sure. The eggs are four dollars fifty, the potatoes are six dollars, and the sourdough is seven dollars.
CUSTOMER: Great. Oh, and do you still have the local honey?
STAFF: We do, but only the small jars are left. They're nine dollars each.
CUSTOMER: I'll take one of those as well.
STAFF: Lovely. So that's a delivery for Friday. We deliver between two and five in the afternoon.
CUSTOMER: Perfect, someone will be home.
STAFF: And there's a flat delivery charge of five dollars within town.
CUSTOMER: That's fine. Can I pay by card on arrival?
STAFF: Of course. Could I take a name for the order?
CUSTOMER: Yes, it's under Mary Coleman.
STAFF: Thanks, Mary. See you Friday.

questions.q: The customer orders a dozen, that is ________, free-range eggs.
The sourdough loaf costs $________.
Which size of honey jar is available?
The honey costs $________ per jar.
Delivery is between two and ________ in the afternoon.
The flat delivery charge within town is $________.

speaking.q:
Describe a meal that you particularly enjoyed. You should say:
- what the meal was and where you ate it
- who you were with
- why the food was special to you
and explain how you felt during and after the meal.

writing.q:
Some people believe that governments should tax unhealthy or environmentally harmful foods, such as cheap meat, to encourage people to make better choices. Others argue that food prices should be kept as low as possible so that everyone can afford to eat. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Intensive farming raises many animals in small spaces.
The animals are kept in confined spaces.
The system converts grain into protein efficiently.
Pollution is a classic example of an externality.
The hidden burden falls on future generations.
Third parties bear the cost of the pollution.
A tax could nudge prices gently upward.
Meat is a weekly staple for many families.
The system merely postpones the real bill.
The question is who ultimately pays.
The animals are fed carefully formulated diets.
The price should reflect the true cost.
A warming planet brings future costs.
Higher prices would tell a more honest story.

reading.title: Saving Seeds for an Uncertain Future
reading.passage:
Deep inside a mountain on a remote Arctic island lies one of the most ambitious insurance policies ever devised. The Global Seed Vault, completed in 2008, was built to store duplicate samples of seeds held in collections around the world. Should a national seed bank be destroyed by war, fire or natural disaster, the vault offers a backup from which lost varieties might one day be recovered. It is, in effect, a library of the world's crops.

The reasoning behind the project is straightforward. Modern agriculture relies on a surprisingly small number of plant varieties, and the older, more diverse types that once grew on farms are vanishing fast. Each variety carries a unique combination of genetic traits, some of which may turn out to be vital. A grain that resists a particular disease, or tolerates salty soil, could become priceless if conditions change. Once such a variety is lost, however, it cannot be recreated.

The vault's frozen tunnels keep seeds at well below freezing, slowing the natural process by which they lose the ability to sprout. Even without electricity, the surrounding permafrost would keep the chambers cold for a considerable time, a feature chosen deliberately to protect against power failures. Seeds remain the property of the institutions that deposited them, and only those depositors may withdraw their own samples.

The vault has already proved its worth. When a seed bank in the Middle East was forced to abandon its collection during a conflict, researchers were able to request duplicates from the Arctic store and re-establish their work elsewhere. Yet experts caution that a single building, however secure, is not a complete solution. The real protection, they argue, lies in keeping diverse crops alive in the fields and gardens of ordinary farmers, where they continue to adapt to a changing world rather than waiting, frozen, for a disaster that may never come.

questions.q: The Global Seed Vault stores the only existing copies of the seeds it holds.
Why does the passage say modern agriculture is vulnerable?
options: It relies on a small number of varieties

questions.q: Even without electricity, the surrounding ________ would keep the chambers cold.
Anyone may withdraw seeds from the vault regardless of who deposited them.
The vault is the largest seed bank in the world.
What real-world event showed the vault's value?
options: a seed bank abandoned during a conflict

questions.q: Experts believe the vault alone is a complete solution to crop loss.
Experts argue the real protection lies in keeping diverse crops alive in the ________ and gardens of ordinary farmers.

listening.title: A Lecture on Crop Diversity
listening.script: LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today we're looking at why crop diversity matters and how scientists try to protect it. Let's start with a striking figure. Although humans have eaten thousands of plant species over our history, today just three crops, rice, wheat and maize, provide around sixty per cent of the calories we get from plants. That narrow dependence is risky. If a single disease attacked one of these crops on a large scale, the consequences could be severe. To guard against this, researchers maintain what are called seed banks, collections that store seeds in cool, dry conditions. The largest backup of these, the Global Seed Vault, holds well over one million samples. But storing seeds is only part of the answer. Seeds cannot stay frozen forever, so every few years a portion must be grown out, allowed to produce fresh seed, and returned to storage. This process, known as regeneration, is slow and expensive, which is why funding remains a constant worry. For your seminar next week, please read chapter four and come prepared to discuss whether seed banks or living farms offer better long-term security.

questions.q: Three crops, rice, wheat and ________, provide most of our plant calories.
Those three crops supply around ________ per cent of plant calories.
What is the main risk of relying on so few crops?
options: a disease could have severe consequences

questions.q: The Global Seed Vault holds well over ________ samples.
Growing seeds out and returning fresh seed to storage is called ________.
For the seminar, students must read chapter ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss food security on a wider scale. Why do you think it is important for a country to grow a variety of crops rather than relying on just a few? What role should governments play in protecting the food supply? Do you think future generations will face greater difficulties in producing enough food, and why?

writing.q:
The chart below shows the share of total plant-based calories supplied by four crops worldwide in 1970 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Crop | 1970 | 2020
Rice | 23% | 21%
Wheat | 20% | 19%
Maize | 14% | 20%
Other crops | 43% | 40%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: It was one of the most ambitious projects ever attempted.
The vault stores duplicates of seed collections.
Lost varieties might one day be recovered.
Older crop types are vanishing fast.
Each variety carries useful genetic traits.
A grain that tolerates salty soil is valuable.
Old seeds gradually lose the ability to sprout.
The site was chosen deliberately for its cold.

The bank was forced to abandon its collection.
Researchers re-established their work elsewhere.
Regeneration of seeds is slow and costly.
A narrow crop base makes farming vulnerable.

reading.title: The Promise and Problem of Vertical Farms
reading.passage:
In a converted warehouse on the edge of a city, rows of leafy greens grow in stacked trays that rise from floor to ceiling. There is no soil and no sunlight; instead, the plants sit in nutrient-rich water and bathe in the pink-purple glow of energy-efficient lamps. This is vertical farming, a method that promises to grow food close to where people live, using a fraction of the land and water that traditional agriculture requires.

The appeal is easy to understand. Because the crops grow indoors, they are unaffected by drought, frost or pests, and can be harvested all year round. Stacking trays upwards means a small building can produce as much salad as a far larger field. Growing food within the city also cuts the distance it must travel, reducing both transport costs and the loss of freshness that comes with long journeys. For dense urban areas with little farmland nearby, the idea seems almost ideal.

Yet the economics remain stubborn. The lamps that replace the sun consume enormous amounts of electricity, and unless that power is cheap and clean, the environmental savings can shrink or even disappear. The buildings, pumps and climate-control systems are expensive to install and to run, which is why vertical farms have so far concentrated on high-value crops such as herbs and salad leaves rather than staples like wheat or rice. Few customers would pay a premium for vertically grown grain.

Supporters argue that costs will fall as the technology matures, much as they did for solar panels, and that vertical farms should be seen as a useful supplement rather than a replacement for conventional fields. Sceptics reply that the method may never escape its dependence on costly energy, and that grand claims about feeding cities from skyscrapers belong, for now, more to marketing than to reality. The truth, as so often, probably lies somewhere in between.

questions.q: Vertical farms grow plants without soil or natural sunlight.
Why can vertical farms harvest crops all year round?
options: The crops grow indoors, away from weather and pests
They are located in warm countries

questions.q: The lamps that replace the sun consume enormous amounts of ________.
Vertical farms have mainly focused on staple crops such as wheat and rice.
Vertical farms create more jobs than traditional farms.
What do supporters compare the falling costs to?
Supporters see vertical farms as a useful ________ rather than a replacement for conventional fields.
The writer concludes that the truth probably lies between the two views.

listening.title: A Visit to a City Vertical Farm
listening.script: GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to UrbanGrow, the city's first vertical farm. I'm Carla, and I'll be showing you around today. Before we go in, a few quick facts. This building used to be a parking garage, and we now grow leafy greens on twelve levels of shelving. We don't use any soil at all. Instead, the roots sit in water mixed with nutrients, a method called hydroponics. Now, you might wonder about the strange light. The plants grow under LED lamps, and we keep them on for about sixteen hours a day. Each level produces a fresh batch of lettuce roughly every three weeks, which is much faster than an outdoor farm. Compared with a traditional field, we use about ninety-five per cent less water, because we recycle almost all of it. The main downside, I'll be honest, is the electricity bill, which is our biggest single cost. After the tour, you'll be able to taste some of our salad in the cafe, and there's a small shop where you can buy herbs to take home. Any questions before we head inside?

questions.q: The building used to be a parking ________.
The greens grow on ________ levels of shelving.
What is the soil-free growing method called?
The LED lamps stay on for about ________ hours a day.
Compared with a field, the farm uses about ________ per cent less water.
What does the guide say is the farm's biggest single cost?

speaking.q:
Describe a place where food is grown or produced that you have visited or would like to visit. You should say:
- what kind of place it is
- where it is and what is grown there
- what you did or would do there
and explain why this place interests you.

writing.q:
As cities grow, some people believe that growing food indoors in 'vertical farms' is the best way to feed urban populations. Others think money should instead be spent on improving traditional farms in the countryside. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Plants grow in stacked trays from floor to ceiling.
The roots sit in nutrient-rich water.
It uses a fraction of the land farms require.
The crops can be harvested all year round.
Few would pay a premium for vertical grain.
The economics of the method remain stubborn.
The lamps consume large amounts of power.
It works best as a supplement to traditional farms.
Costs may fall as the technology matures.
Sceptics doubt the grand claims.

reading.title: Why Food Goes to Waste
reading.passage:
It is one of the strangest contradictions of the modern world that, even as millions go hungry, a vast share of the food that is grown is never eaten. Estimates vary, but studies repeatedly suggest that roughly a third of all food produced for human consumption is lost or wasted somewhere between the farm and the plate. Understanding where this loss occurs is the first step towards reducing it.

In poorer countries, most waste happens early, soon after harvest. Without reliable refrigeration, good roads or proper storage, crops rot before they reach the market. A farmer may grow an excellent crop only to watch much of it spoil in the heat or be ruined by pests during the long wait for transport. Here the problem is not carelessness but a lack of basic infrastructure.

In wealthier countries, by contrast, the bulk of waste occurs at the very end of the chain, in shops and homes. Supermarkets reject produce that fails to meet strict standards of appearance, and shoppers, encouraged by special offers, buy more than they can use. Confusion over date labels makes matters worse: many people throw away food that is still perfectly safe simply because a 'best before' date has passed, mistaking it for a warning about safety rather than a guide to quality.

Reducing waste therefore demands different solutions in different places. In developing regions, investment in storage and transport could save enormous quantities of food. In richer nations, the answers lie more in behaviour and policy: clearer labelling, the relaxation of cosmetic standards for fruit and vegetables, and a cultural shift away from treating food as something cheap and disposable. None of these changes is dramatic on its own, yet together they could close much of the gap between what we grow and what we actually eat.

questions.q: Studies suggest about a ________ of all food produced is lost or wasted.
In poorer countries, most food waste happens soon after harvest.
According to the passage, what is the main cause of waste in poorer countries?
In wealthy countries, most waste occurs early, at the farm.
Why do many shoppers throw away safe food?
Most supermarkets have already removed all appearance standards for produce.
In developing regions, investment in storage and ________ could save huge quantities of food.
The writer believes a single dramatic change would solve the problem.

listening.title: A Talk on Reducing Household Food Waste
listening.script: SPEAKER: Thanks for coming along this evening. Tonight I want to share a few simple ways that any household can cut down on the food it throws away. Let me begin with a number that surprises most people: the average family throws away around seventy pounds' worth of edible food every single month. That's money straight in the bin. So where do we start? First, plan your meals before you shop and write a list. People who shop with a list waste far less than those who don't. Second, learn what date labels really mean. A 'use by' date is about safety, so do take that seriously. But a 'best before' date is only about quality, and food is usually fine after it. Third, store things properly. Keeping bread in the freezer, for example, can make it last for weeks instead of days. And finally, get comfortable with leftovers. A bit of imagination can turn last night's dinner into today's lunch. If you'd like more tips, there are free leaflets at the back, and our next workshop is on the third of October.

questions.q: The average family wastes around £________ of edible food a month.
What is the first piece of advice given?
options: plan meals and write a list

questions.q: According to the speaker, a 'use by' date is about ________.
Keeping bread in the ________ can make it last for weeks.
Free ________ are available at the back of the room.
The next workshop is on the third of ________.

speaking.q: Let's talk more generally about food waste and consumption. Why do you think people in wealthier societies waste so much food? What could shops and governments do to reduce the amount of food that is thrown away? Do you think attitudes towards wasting food are changing among younger people?

writing.q:
The table below shows where food is lost or wasted along the supply chain in two types of country, as a percentage of total food waste. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Stage | Lower-income countries | Higher-income countries
Farm and harvest | 40% | 12%
Storage and transport | 35% | 10%
Shops | 10% | 25%
Homes | 15% | 53%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: It is a strange contradiction of modern life.
A third of food grown for consumption is wasted.
Crops rot before they reach the market.
Without refrigeration, food spoils quickly.
The problem is a lack of basic infrastructure.
Supermarkets reject misshapen produce.
Cosmetic standards reject ugly vegetables.
Food is often treated as cheap and disposable.
The relaxation of strict standards could help.
Much of the crop spoils in the heat.
Families throw away edible food every month.
Imagination can turn leftovers into lunch.
The bulk of waste occurs in homes.

reading.title: The Quiet Revival of Night Trains
reading.passage:
For decades, the sleeper train seemed destined to disappear from the map of European travel. Cheap flights and high-speed rail had made overnight journeys appear slow and old-fashioned, and many operators quietly withdrew their carriages during the early 2000s. Yet over the past few years, the night train has staged an unexpected comeback, driven less by nostalgia than by a growing unease about the environmental cost of flying.

The leading force behind this revival has been a state-owned Austrian operator, which acquired routes that other companies had abandoned. Rather than treating the sleeper as a relic, the company invested in modernised cabins with private pods, quiet compartments and improved dining. By 2022 its network stretched from Vienna to cities as distant as Amsterdam, Rome and Hamburg, and passenger numbers had risen sharply. Other national railways, watching this success, began to plan new connections of their own.

The appeal is partly practical. A traveller who boards in the evening and arrives the next morning effectively saves the cost of a hotel night and avoids the tedious procedures of airports. For business passengers, the time spent sleeping is time that would otherwise be lost. Environmental campaigners point out that a night train can produce a small fraction of the carbon emissions generated by an equivalent flight, a difference that increasingly influences both individual choices and government policy.

Obstacles remain, however. Sleeper carriages are expensive to build and maintain, and they earn less per square metre than a packed daytime train. Cross-border services are complicated by different electrical systems and safety rules, which can make a single journey surprisingly difficult to coordinate. Some analysts argue that without public subsidy the romantic image of the night train will never translate into reliable profit. Even so, for a generation increasingly reluctant to fly, the gentle rhythm of the rails at midnight has acquired a fresh and powerful charm.

questions.q: Many European sleeper services were discontinued in the early 2000s.
The Austrian operator designed entirely new types of trains rather than buying old routes.
The Austrian operator's network reached Amsterdam by 2022.
The Austrian operator earns higher profits than any airline on the same routes.
According to the passage, what mainly drives the revival of night trains?
options: Concern about the environmental cost of flying

questions.q: Which is mentioned as a practical advantage for travellers?
options: Saving the cost of a hotel night

questions.q: Cross-border services are made difficult by different electrical systems and ________ rules.
Some analysts believe that without public ________ the night train will not become reliably profitable.

listening.title: Booking a Sleeper Ticket
listening.script:
AGENT: Good afternoon, City Rail bookings, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a sleeper from Vienna to Amsterdam, please.

AGENT: Certainly. Which date were you thinking of?

CALLER: The fourteenth of March, if there's space.

AGENT: Let me check. Yes, we have availability. Would you prefer a shared couchette or a private cabin?

CALLER: A private cabin, please. How much is that?

AGENT: The private cabin is one hundred and forty euros, including breakfast.

CALLER: That's fine. What time does the train leave?

AGENT: It departs at nine fifteen in the evening and arrives the next morning at seven forty.

CALLER: Lovely. Is there a luggage limit?

AGENT: Two large bags per passenger, and please keep valuables with you. Could I take your surname?

CALLER: It's Bennett, B-E-N-N-E-T-T.

AGENT: Thank you, Mr Bennett. I'll email your ticket. Please arrive at least twenty minutes before departure, as the platform closes early.

CALLER: Understood. Thanks very much for your help.

questions.q: The caller wants to travel on the ________ of March.
What type of accommodation does the caller choose?

Route | 2018 | 2023

reading.title: Why Cities Spread Outwards
reading.passage:
Urban sprawl, the steady spreading of a city into the surrounding countryside, has shaped the way millions of people live and travel. Although the pattern is often blamed on careless planning, its causes are more varied. Cheap land at the edge of a city has always tempted developers, while the spread of the private car made it possible for families to live far from where they worked. Once roads were built, more houses followed, and the city's boundary crept ever further outwards.

For many residents, sprawl offered clear benefits. Land on the outskirts was affordable, allowing ordinary families to own larger homes with gardens that would have been impossible nearer the centre. Quieter streets and access to open space appealed especially to those raising children. For decades, moving outwards was widely seen as a sign of success, a reward for hard work.

Yet the costs of this expansion gradually became harder to ignore. As distances grew, people became almost entirely dependent on cars, and the time lost to commuting increased year by year. Spreading the population thinly made public transport expensive to run, since buses had to travel long routes to reach scattered passengers. Farmland and natural habitats were swallowed up, and the energy needed to heat and supply distant homes added to a city's overall footprint.

In response, many planners now favour a denser, more compact model of growth. They encourage building upwards rather than outwards, mixing homes, shops and offices in the same districts so that daily needs can be met on foot. Critics warn that compact living can feel cramped and that house prices in central areas may rise beyond the reach of ordinary buyers. Supporters reply that well-designed density, with parks and good transport, can deliver the convenience of city life without the endless sprawl that earlier generations took for granted.

questions.q: The passage suggests that urban sprawl has only one cause.
Land on the outskirts of cities was generally cheaper than land in the centre.
Public transport became more expensive to operate as the population spread out.
Most planners today still recommend continued outward expansion.
Compact developments produce more waste than sprawling suburbs.
Which factor is said to have made living far from work possible?
What do critics of compact living warn about?
Planners now encourage building ________ rather than outwards.

listening.title: A Geography Tutorial on Suburbs
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, today we're looking at how suburbs developed in the last century. Can anyone tell me what came first, the roads or the houses?

STUDENT: I'd guess the roads?

TUTOR: Exactly. Once a new road was built, developers quickly filled the land beside it with housing. We call this corridor growth. Now, jot this down: the biggest single driver of suburban spread was the private car.

STUDENT: So before cars, cities were more compact?

TUTOR: Much more. People had to live within walking distance of work, or near a tram line. The average commute in 1900 was about thirty minutes on foot. By the year 2000, in many cities, it had stretched to over an hour by car.

STUDENT: That's a huge change.

TUTOR: It is. And here's the cost: spreading people thinly makes buses expensive to run. For your essay, focus on three impacts. One, car dependency. Two, loss of farmland. Three, the rising cost of public services. Submit it by Friday, no later than five o'clock.

STUDENT: Got it. Thanks.

questions.q: According to the tutor, what was built first in suburban areas?
The tutor calls building beside a new road ________ growth.
The biggest single driver of suburban spread was the private ________.
In 1900 the average commute was about ________ minutes on foot.

Describe a neighbourhood you have lived in or visited that you liked. You should say:
- where it was

- what you could do there
and explain why you liked it.

Write at least 250 words.

Cheap land tempted many developers.

The daily commute grew longer each year.
Buses struggled to reach scattered passengers.

reading.title: The Hidden History of Human Migration
reading.passage:
Human beings have always been on the move. Long before written records, our ancestors crossed continents in search of food, milder climates and safety from rivals. For most of history, however, the story of these journeys could only be guessed at, pieced together from scattered bones and stone tools. In recent decades, the study of ancient DNA has transformed our understanding, allowing scientists to trace population movements that left no written trace at all.

By extracting genetic material from ancient skeletons, researchers can now compare populations separated by thousands of years. The results have often been surprising. In Europe, for example, DNA evidence suggests that the arrival of farming around eight thousand years ago was not simply a case of local people learning new skills. Instead, farmers themselves migrated from the Near East, gradually mixing with the hunter-gatherers already living there. A later wave of herders from the eastern steppe brought further genetic and cultural change.

These findings challenge older assumptions. Historians once tended to imagine that ideas, rather than people, spread across the ancient world. The genetic record shows that large-scale movements of people were far more common than previously believed. It also reveals that almost every modern population is a blend of many earlier groups, undermining any notion of a pure or unmixed ancestry.

The technique is not without limits. Ancient DNA survives best in cool, dry conditions, so the record from hot regions remains thin. Samples can be contaminated, and a single skeleton cannot represent an entire community. Researchers must therefore combine genetics with archaeology, linguistics and climate data to build a reliable picture. Even so, the field has grown remarkably quickly, and each new study adds detail to a map of human movement that was, until recently, almost entirely blank.

questions.q: Before the study of ancient DNA, early human migrations could only be guessed at.
DNA evidence shows that farming in Europe spread only through local people learning new skills.
Herders from the eastern steppe arrived in Europe before the first farmers.
Most modern populations are blends of several earlier groups.
Ancient DNA is now used more often than archaeology in all migration studies.
Where did the first European farmers migrate from, according to DNA evidence?
What is mentioned as a limitation of ancient DNA research?
options: It is too cheap to be reliable
DNA survives poorly in hot regions

questions.q: Researchers combine genetics with archaeology, linguistics and ________ data.

listening.title: A Lecture on Ancient DNA
listening.script:
LECTURER: Welcome back. Today I want to give you an overview of how ancient DNA is changing the study of migration. First, a key point: DNA can be extracted even from skeletons that are thousands of years old, provided they've been kept in cool, dry conditions.

Now, the most famous early success was in Europe. Researchers found that farming didn't just spread as an idea. Real people, farmers from the Near East, physically moved in and mixed with local hunter-gatherers. That happened roughly eight thousand years ago.

There's a second wave you should remember. Herders from the eastern steppe arrived later, bringing new genes and new languages. Together, these movements explain much of Europe's modern genetic makeup.

A word of caution, though. A single skeleton tells us about one individual, not a whole population. And samples are easily contaminated, so labs follow very strict procedures. For next week, please read chapter four and bring two questions to the seminar. The seminar starts at ten thirty, not eleven, so don't be late.

A later wave of ________ arrived from the eastern steppe.
What caution does the lecturer give about samples?
options: They are too small to find

questions.q: Next week's seminar starts at ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss migration in a broader sense. Why do you think people decide to move to other countries? What are the main benefits and challenges that migration brings to a society?

writing.q:
The table below shows the estimated source regions of migrants arriving in one country (as a percentage of total arrivals) in 2000 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Source region | 2000 | 2020
Europe | 45% | 22%
Asia | 30% | 41%
Africa | 15% | 28%
Americas | 10% | 9%

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Our ancestors crossed continents in search of food.
Scientists can trace ancient population movements.
They extract genetic material from skeletons.

Farmers migrated from the Near East.
The findings challenge older assumptions.
The data undermines the idea of pure ancestry.

reading.title: Mapping a Changing World
reading.passage:
A map seems like a simple, neutral record of the world, yet every map is the result of countless decisions. Because the Earth is a sphere and a map is flat, no representation can be entirely accurate. Mapmakers must choose a projection, a mathematical method of stretching the globe onto a flat surface, and each projection distorts something. Some preserve the true shape of countries while exaggerating their size; others keep areas correct but bend their outlines. The famous projection used in many classrooms, for instance, makes regions near the poles appear far larger than they really are.

These distortions are not merely technical. For generations, schoolchildren grew up with maps that made wealthy northern nations look enormous and equatorial regions look small. Critics argue that such images quietly shaped perceptions of importance and power. In response, designers have promoted alternative projections that show land areas in their true proportion, even if the shapes look unfamiliar.

Digital technology has changed mapping in equally profound ways. Satellite imagery and global positioning systems allow maps to be updated almost instantly, and ordinary users can now contribute corrections from their phones. Vast collaborative projects, built by volunteers, have mapped remote villages and footpaths that no commercial company ever bothered to record. In disaster zones, such rapidly drawn maps have helped rescue teams reach people in need.

Yet new problems have emerged. When a handful of companies control the most widely used digital maps, they also decide what is shown and what is left out. A road, a border or even an entire settlement may appear or vanish depending on commercial or political pressures. As maps become living, constantly updated tools rather than fixed documents, the old question returns in a new form: not simply how to draw the world accurately, but who has the power to decide what the world looks like.

questions.q: It is impossible to represent the spherical Earth on a flat map without some distortion.
The projection common in classrooms makes polar regions look smaller than they are.
Some critics believe traditional maps influenced ideas about which regions were important.
Volunteer mapping projects have recorded places ignored by commercial firms.
Most governments now require schools to use area-accurate projections.
What must mapmakers choose when turning the globe into a flat map?
What new concern does the passage raise about digital maps?
options: They are too slow to update
A few companies control what is shown

questions.q: Rapidly drawn maps have helped rescue teams in ________ zones.

listening.title: A Volunteer Mapping Workshop
listening.script:
LEADER: Thanks for coming to the mapping workshop. Today you'll learn to add missing roads and buildings to our open map. First, has everyone created an account?

VOLUNTEER: I have, but I'm not sure where to start.

LEADER: No problem. We'll begin with a small village in the north. The satellite image is from last year, so some new buildings may already be visible. Your job is to trace them carefully.

VOLUNTEER: How accurate do we need to be?

LEADER: Aim for roughly two metres. It doesn't have to be perfect, but rescue teams may rely on it, so don't guess. If you're unsure, leave a note for a reviewer.

VOLUNTEER: And what about footpaths?

LEADER: Good question. Add them if you can see them clearly. Footpaths matter enormously in rural areas. Right, the session runs until four o'clock, and we'll have a short break at two thirty. Please save your work every ten minutes. Any other questions? No? Then let's get mapping.

The satellite image is from ________ year.
Volunteers should aim for an accuracy of about ________ metres.
What should a volunteer do if they are unsure?
options: Leave a note for a reviewer

questions.q: The session runs until ________ o'clock.
Volunteers should save their work every ________ minutes.

speaking.q: Let's talk about maps and directions. Do you usually use paper maps or apps on your phone when you travel? Are you good at finding your way in a new place? Why or why not?

Write at least 250 words.

reading.title: The Return of the Wild Coast
reading.passage:
Along stretches of the European coastline, a quiet experiment in geography is underway. For more than a century, engineers fought to hold the sea back, building walls and barriers to protect farmland reclaimed from marsh and estuary. Now, in a striking reversal, some governments are deliberately allowing the water to return. This approach, known as managed realignment, accepts that defending every metre of coast is neither affordable nor wise, and that in places the wisest course is to retreat.

The reasoning is partly financial. Sea defences are enormously expensive to maintain, and as sea levels rise, the cost of holding firm grows steadily higher. By moving defences inland and letting low-lying fields flood, authorities can create salt marshes that absorb the energy of waves and storm surges. These marshes act as a natural buffer, protecting communities further inland far more cheaply than concrete ever could.

The environmental gains have been considerable. Restored marshes quickly attract wading birds, fish and rare plants, reviving habitats that intensive farming had erased. They also store large amounts of carbon in their waterlogged soils, offering a modest contribution to the fight against climate change. Visitors, too, have begun to arrive, drawn by the spectacle of wildlife returning to land that had been drained generations earlier.

Not everyone welcomes the change. Farmers whose families worked the reclaimed land for generations may feel that their heritage is being surrendered to the tide. Compensation can ease the financial loss but rarely the sense of attachment to a familiar landscape. Local debates can be heated, and projects sometimes stall for years. Supporters insist, nonetheless, that working with natural processes rather than against them represents the only realistic strategy for coastlines that the rising sea will, in time, reclaim regardless.

questions.q: For over a hundred years, engineers tried to keep the sea away from reclaimed land.
Managed realignment claims that every part of the coast can be defended affordably.
Salt marshes can help reduce the energy of waves and storm surges.
Restored marshes store carbon in their soils.
Tourists now spend more money in marsh areas than farmers once earned from the land.
What is one financial reason given for managed realignment?
options: Sea defences are very costly to maintain

questions.q: Why might some farmers oppose these projects?
options: They feel their heritage is being surrendered

questions.q: Restored salt marshes act as a natural ________ that protects inland communities.

listening.title: A Coastal Management Briefing
listening.script:
OFFICER: Good morning, everyone, and thanks for joining this briefing on our coastal plan. I'll keep it short. As many of you know, the old sea wall near the estuary is failing, and repairing it would cost around four million dollars.

RESIDENT: That's a lot of money. What's the alternative?

OFFICER: The alternative is managed realignment. In simple terms, we move the defence line back and allow the lowest fields to flood. Over time, this creates a salt marsh that absorbs wave energy and protects the village behind it.

RESIDENT: But what about the farmers on that land?

OFFICER: A fair point. Affected farmers will receive compensation, and we'll hold three public meetings before any final decision. The first one is on the ninth of October.

RESIDENT: And the wildlife?

OFFICER: That's the upside. We expect wading birds and fish to return within a few years. If you'd like more detail, leaflets are available at the back, and you can email the project team. Thank you.

What does managed realignment involve?
Letting the fields flood creates a salt ________ over time.

The first public meeting is on the ________ of October.
What wildlife does the officer expect to return?

speaking.q:
Describe a natural place you have visited that impressed you. You should say:
- where it is
- when you went there
- what you did there
and explain why it impressed you.

writing.q:
The table below shows the area of restored coastal salt marsh (in hectares) created in one country over four years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Year | Hectares restored
2020 | 120
2021 | 180
2022 | 150
2023 | 310

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Farmland was reclaimed from the marsh.
The sea wall stands near the estuary.
In a striking reversal, water is allowed to return.
Managed realignment moves defences inland.
Sometimes the wisest course is to retreat.
Marshes act as a natural buffer against storms.

reading.title: The Rise of the Podcast
reading.passage:
Twenty years ago, few people had heard the word "podcast". Today, hundreds of millions of listeners around the world download or stream spoken-word programmes on subjects ranging from history and science to comedy and crime. The format, which combines the intimacy of radio with the convenience of on-demand audio, has become one of the most rapidly growing branches of the media industry.

The appeal of podcasts lies partly in their flexibility. Unlike traditional radio, which broadcasts at fixed times, a podcast can be paused, replayed and consumed whenever the listener chooses. Many people fit episodes into otherwise empty moments, listening while commuting, exercising or doing housework. This portability has helped audio reclaim attention from screens, which dominate so much of modern leisure.

For producers, the barriers to entry are remarkably low. A microphone, a quiet room and some basic editing software are enough to launch a show, and there is no need to negotiate with a broadcaster for a slot. This openness has allowed niche topics to flourish, giving a platform to voices that mainstream stations might once have ignored. A programme about an obscure hobby may attract only a few thousand listeners, yet that audience can be loyal and engaged.

Nevertheless, the very ease of production has created a crowded marketplace. Millions of podcasts now compete for limited listening time, and most struggle to find an audience at all. Industry analysts note that a small number of well-funded shows, often backed by established broadcasters or technology companies, attract the bulk of listeners and advertising revenue. Independent creators, by contrast, frequently earn little or nothing, and many abandon their projects within a year.

Whether the boom can continue is uncertain. Some commentators warn that listeners are reaching saturation point and that growth will inevitably slow. Others argue that as recording tools improve and new advertising models emerge, the medium still has considerable room to expand. What seems clear is that podcasting has secured a permanent place in the contemporary media landscape.

questions.q: The word "podcast" was widely used twenty years ago.
According to the passage, one major advantage of podcasts over traditional radio is that they
options: can be listened to at any time the listener chooses
are produced by professional broadcasters only
are broadcast at fixed daily times

questions.q: The portability of podcasts has helped audio reclaim attention from ________, which dominate modern leisure.
Launching a podcast requires expensive professional equipment.
Most independent podcast creators earn a comfortable living from their shows.
Industry analysts observe that most listeners and advertising revenue go to
options: a small number of well-funded shows
shows that abandon their projects quickly

questions.q: Podcast listening is more popular in cities than in rural areas.
Commentators all agree that the podcast boom will soon end.

listening.title: Enquiry: Joining a Community Radio Station
listening.script:
VOLUNTEER: Good morning, Riverside Community Radio, how can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I saw a notice saying you're looking for new volunteers, and I'd love to get involved.

VOLUNTEER: Wonderful. Have you done any broadcasting before?

CALLER: Not really. I helped with a student newsletter at college, but nothing on the radio.

VOLUNTEER: That's absolutely fine. Most of our volunteers start with no experience. We run a free training course every month. The next one is on the fourteenth of March.

CALLER: Great. How long does the course last?

VOLUNTEER: It's three sessions, each two hours, on a Saturday morning. After that, you'd shadow an experienced presenter for a few weeks before going solo.

CALLER: And what kind of shows do you make?

VOLUNTEER: All sorts — local news, music, interviews with people in the area. We're especially keen to find someone for an arts programme at the moment.

CALLER: That sounds perfect, actually. I'm really into theatre.

VOLUNTEER: Brilliant. Could I take your name and email, and I'll send you the application form?

CALLER: Of course. It's Daniel Pryce — that's P-R-Y-C-E.

VOLUNTEER: Lovely, thank you, Daniel. Look out for an email from us this week.

Weekly podcast listeners (% of age group)
Age group | 2015 | 2023
18-29 | 18% | 47%

vocab.example: The presenter's quiet voice created a sense of intimacy with listeners.
On-demand audio lets users listen whenever they like.

reading.title: Fact-Checking in the Digital Age
reading.passage:
When false information spreads online, the damage can be done within minutes. A misleading claim shared by thousands of users can reach a global audience long before anyone questions whether it is true. In response, news organisations and independent groups have built a new profession around verifying claims: fact-checking.

The practice itself is not new. For decades, serious magazines employed staff to confirm names, dates and figures before an article went to print. What has changed is the scale and speed of the problem. Social media platforms allow anyone to publish instantly to a vast network, and false stories often travel faster than accurate ones, partly because they are designed to provoke strong emotions such as fear or outrage.

Modern fact-checkers use a range of techniques. They trace images back to their original source to see whether a photograph has been taken out of context. They examine official records, consult experts and compare a claim against reliable data. When a statement is found to be false, the fact-checker publishes a detailed explanation, showing readers not only the verdict but the evidence behind it. This transparency is intended to build trust and to teach the public how to evaluate information independently.

Yet fact-checking faces serious limitations. Verifying a single claim properly can take hours, while a false post can be created in seconds. By the time a correction appears, the original may already have been seen by millions, and research suggests that corrections rarely reach as many people as the falsehoods they address. There is also the difficulty that some users distrust fact-checkers themselves, dismissing their conclusions as biased.

Many experts therefore argue that fact-checking, while valuable, cannot solve the problem alone. They call for better education in media literacy, so that ordinary readers learn to pause, question sources and resist the urge to share before they have checked. In this view, the most reliable defence against misinformation is not a team of professionals but a more sceptical and thoughtful public.

questions.q: Fact-checking is a completely new practice that did not exist before the internet.
According to the passage, false stories often spread faster than accurate ones because they
options: are designed to provoke strong emotions

questions.q: Fact-checkers often trace ________ back to their original source to check for missing context.
Fact-checkers publish the evidence behind their conclusions in order to build trust.
Corrections usually reach more people than the false claims they address.
One limitation of fact-checking mentioned in the passage is that
options: it is illegal in many countries
verifying a claim takes far longer than creating a false post
it can only be done by computers

listening.title: Library Tour: The Newspaper Archive
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the city library's newspaper archive. This is one of the largest collections in the region, with material going back to the year 1841. Let me explain how it all works.

In the past, every newspaper was kept as a physical copy, but paper becomes fragile over time. So during the 1990s the library began transferring older issues onto microfilm — small rolls of photographic film that you read using these special machines along the wall. More recently, we've been digitising the collection, which means scanning each page so you can search it on a computer.

The great advantage of the digital archive is searching. Instead of scrolling through months of issues by hand, you simply type in a word — a name, a place, an event — and the system finds every article that mentions it. This has been a huge help to local historians.

There are just a few rules. You can read anything here for free, but to print pages there's a charge of twenty pence per sheet. And please don't bring food or drink into the reading room, as spills can ruin the equipment. If you'd like to use the digital terminals, just ask at the desk and we'll set you up with a guest account.

reading.title: The Decline of the Local Newspaper
reading.passage:
For more than a century, the local newspaper was a fixture of community life. It reported council meetings, school sports days and the small dramas of the neighbourhood, knitting together people who might otherwise have known little of one another. In many towns, however, that institution is now disappearing, and the consequences are only beginning to be understood.

The decline has been driven largely by economics. Local papers traditionally relied on advertising — from estate agents, car dealers and small shops — to fund their journalism. As advertisers shifted their budgets to online platforms, which could target customers far more precisely, that income collapsed. Readers, meanwhile, grew used to finding news free on the internet and were reluctant to pay for a printed edition. Caught between falling sales and vanishing advertising, many titles closed, while others survived only by cutting their staff to a handful of reporters.

The loss matters more than it might first appear. National outlets cover parliaments and wars, but they rarely send a reporter to a planning meeting in a small town. When local papers vanish, a great deal of public business goes unexamined. Studies have found that in areas without a local paper, fewer residents stand for council elections and turnout at the polls tends to fall. Without journalists to scrutinise them, local officials face less pressure to act responsibly.

Some communities have begun to experiment with new models. A handful of towns have launched non-profit news websites funded by donations and small grants, run partly by volunteers. Others have formed partnerships with universities, where journalism students cover local affairs as part of their training. These efforts are promising but fragile, and they reach only a fraction of the places that have lost their papers.

Whether such experiments can replace what has been lost remains an open question. What is certain is that the disappearance of the local newspaper has removed something difficult to measure but genuinely valuable: a shared account of a community's own life.

questions.q: Local newspapers once reported on small community events such as school sports days.

options: a lack of interesting local stories

options: fewer residents stand for council elections

listening.title: Talk: How a TV News Bulletin Is Made
listening.script:
PRESENTER: G'day, and thanks for coming in. Today I'll walk you through how we put together the evening news bulletin you see at six o'clock.

The day actually starts at half past eight in the morning, with a meeting we call the conference. The whole team gathers, and reporters pitch the stories they think we should cover. The editor then decides which stories make the cut and how much time each one gets.

Once that's settled, reporters head out with a camera operator to film interviews and gather footage. When they come back, they write a script and work with an editor to cut the pictures together. A typical news report is surprisingly short — usually about ninety seconds long.

Meanwhile, in the gallery, the production team prepares the running order — that's the list showing exactly when each item will appear. Things change right up to the last minute, because breaking news can push other stories aside.

The most stressful part is going live. When the presenter is on air, there's no second chance, so timing has to be perfect. If a report runs over, someone has to cut something quickly. It looks calm on screen, but behind the scenes it can be quite frantic.

reading.title: Streaming and the Reshaping of Television
reading.passage:
A generation ago, watching television meant sitting down at a fixed hour to catch a programme as it was broadcast. If you missed an episode, you waited for a repeat or simply went without. Streaming services have swept away that arrangement, allowing viewers to watch almost anything, at any time, on almost any device. In doing so, they have reshaped not only how people watch but what gets made.

The most obvious change is the freedom of choice. A single platform may offer tens of thousands of hours of content, from old films to brand-new dramas, available instantly at the touch of a button. Viewers are no longer at the mercy of a broadcaster's schedule, and the practice of releasing a whole season at once has encouraged "binge-watching", in which people consume many episodes in a single sitting.

Less visible, but arguably more significant, is the effect on production. Because streaming companies know precisely what their subscribers watch, pause and abandon, they can use this data to guide decisions about which programmes to commission. A drama that performs well with a particular audience may be renewed, while one that fails to hold viewers is quietly dropped. Some critics worry that this data-driven approach produces cautious, formulaic shows designed to keep people watching rather than to take creative risks.

There are also concerns about fragmentation. In the past, a popular programme could be a shared national experience, discussed by millions the next morning. Today, with dozens of services each guarding its own exclusive titles, audiences are scattered across competing platforms. To watch everything, a household might need several subscriptions, an expense that adds up quickly and that some viewers resent.

Despite these tensions, few expect a return to the old model. Streaming has given audiences a level of convenience and choice that traditional broadcasting could never match. The challenge now is to ensure that, amid the abundance, the medium continues to support the bold and original storytelling that made television worth watching in the first place.

options: audiences are scattered across competing platforms

listening.title: Lecture: How Recommendation Systems Choose What You See
listening.script:
LECTURER: Okay, let's talk about something you all use every day without thinking — recommendation systems. These are the algorithms that decide which video, song, or article appears in front of you next.

At their core, most of these systems work by spotting patterns. Imagine the platform notices that people who watched film A also tended to enjoy film B. So when you watch A, it suggests B. This approach is often called collaborative filtering, because it relies on the combined behavior of many users.

Now, there's a real benefit here. With millions of items available, you simply couldn't search through them all yourself, so the system saves you time by narrowing the choices. The downside is what researchers call a filter bubble. If you're only ever shown things similar to what you already like, you may stop encountering anything new or challenging.

There's a second risk too. Because these systems are usually designed to maximize the time you spend on the platform, they may favor content that's attention-grabbing over content that's accurate or balanced. That's one reason sensational videos sometimes spread so widely.

So, as you design or simply use these tools, it's worth asking: is the system serving my interests, or the platform's?

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of "Free" Online Services
reading.passage:
Many of the digital services we use every day appear to cost nothing. We send messages, search for information, watch videos and share photographs without ever reaching for a wallet. Yet these services are run by some of the most valuable companies in the world. The puzzle dissolves once we understand a simple principle of the modern information economy: when a service is free, the user is often not the customer but the product.

What these companies sell is attention, and the means of selling it is advertising. To make their advertising effective, they gather vast quantities of data about their users — which pages they visit, how long they linger, what they buy and even where they go. From this raw material, the company builds a detailed profile, which advertisers can then use to target their messages with remarkable precision. The more a service knows about a person, the more valuable that person's attention becomes.

For users, the bargain has clear attractions. Sophisticated tools that would once have been costly are now available to anyone with an internet connection, at no obvious charge. Many people consider this a fair exchange, reasoning that a few advertisements are a small price to pay for such convenience.

Critics, however, point to costs that are easy to overlook. The constant collection of personal data raises serious questions about privacy, particularly when information is shared with third parties or leaked through security breaches. There is also concern that services designed to capture attention are engineered to be habit-forming, encouraging users to spend far longer online than they intend. The very features that make a platform free may also make it difficult to put down.

Growing awareness of these costs has prompted calls for change. Some governments have introduced regulations giving users greater control over their data, while a small number of companies now offer paid services that promise to collect no information at all. Whether such alternatives can succeed on a large scale is unclear, but the comfortable assumption that "free" means without cost is, at last, being questioned.

questions.q: What principle does the passage use to explain why many digital services are free?
options: the companies are run as charities
when a service is free, the user is often the product
advertising has been banned by law

options: are too expensive for most users

questions.q: Some companies now offer paid services that promise to collect no data.

listening.title: Seminar: Protecting Your Privacy Online
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, in today's session I want to give you some practical advice on protecting your privacy online. None of this is complicated, but it does require a little attention.

Let's start with passwords. The single most useful thing you can do is use a different password for every account. I know that sounds like a lot to remember, which is why I'd recommend a password manager — a piece of software that stores them securely for you.

Next, think carefully about what you share. Once something is posted publicly, you've effectively lost control of it; even if you delete it later, copies may already exist. So pause before you post.

Third, check your privacy settings. Most platforms collect far more data than you might expect, but they also allow you to limit this if you dig into the menus. Take ten minutes to review them.

Finally, be cautious with free public wi-fi, in cafés or airports. These networks are often unsecured, which means others may be able to see what you're doing. If you must use one, avoid logging into anything sensitive, like online banking.

Do those four things, and you'll already be far safer than most people online.

reading.title: The Origins of the Jury System
reading.passage:
The jury, a panel of ordinary citizens who decide questions of fact in a court of law, is often regarded as one of the cornerstones of common-law justice. Yet its origins are far from the noble image many people hold today. Early forms of collective judgement existed in several ancient societies, but the modern jury is usually traced to medieval England, where it gradually replaced older and far harsher methods of determining guilt.

Before juries became common, disputes were sometimes settled by ordeal. An accused person might be forced to carry a piece of hot iron or plunge a hand into boiling water; if the resulting wounds healed cleanly, the individual was judged innocent. Such practices rested on the belief that divine power would protect the truthful. In 1215, the Church withdrew its support for trial by ordeal, and courts were obliged to find an alternative. The jury, drawn from local men who were thought to know the parties involved, offered a practical solution.

Early jurors were not the impartial strangers we expect today. On the contrary, they were chosen precisely because they had personal knowledge of the dispute and the people concerned. Only over several centuries did the expectation shift, so that jurors came to be valued for their neutrality rather than their familiarity with the case. By the eighteenth century, the principle that a jury should weigh evidence presented in court, rather than rely on private knowledge, had become firmly established.

Critics have long questioned whether twelve untrained citizens can reliably assess complex evidence, particularly in cases involving financial fraud or scientific testimony. Supporters reply that the jury protects defendants from the power of the state and keeps the law connected to community values. Whatever its flaws, the institution has proved remarkably durable, surviving repeated calls for reform across many centuries.

options: their personal acquaintance with the accused

reading.title: Whistleblowers and the Public Interest
reading.passage:
A whistleblower is a person who exposes wrongdoing within an organisation, often at considerable personal risk. Such individuals may reveal fraud, safety violations, or the misuse of public funds. While many people admire whistleblowers as defenders of the public interest, others view them as disloyal employees who damage the institutions that employ them. This tension lies at the heart of the ethical and legal debates surrounding their actions.

The central justification for whistleblowing is that some information is too important to remain hidden. When a company conceals a dangerous product, or a government department covers up incompetence, ordinary procedures for raising concerns may fail. In these circumstances, supporters argue, an employee has a moral duty to alert the public, even if doing so breaks internal rules. The benefit to society, they claim, outweighs the breach of loyalty to the employer.

Critics raise several objections. They point out that whistleblowers sometimes act on incomplete information, causing unnecessary alarm or unfairly damaging reputations. There is also a risk that disgruntled workers may use the label of whistleblowing to disguise personal grievances. For these reasons, many legal systems now require that concerns first be reported through official channels, and that public disclosure be a last resort.

To address these concerns, numerous countries have introduced legislation designed to protect genuine whistleblowers from dismissal or harassment. Such laws typically offer protection only when the disclosure is made in good faith and concerns a matter of genuine public importance. In practice, however, many whistleblowers still face severe consequences, including the loss of their careers. Critics of existing legislation argue that the protections look strong on paper but are difficult to enforce, leaving those who speak out dangerously exposed.

According to the passage, legal protection for whistleblowers usually applies when
options: the disclosure is made in good faith on a matter of public importance

listening.title: A Workplace Ethics Induction

TRAINER: Welcome, everyone, to the staff ethics induction. I'll keep this fairly brief. The aim today is to make sure you all understand how to report a concern if you ever see something that doesn't seem right at work.

First, every new employee is asked to read the code of conduct during their first week. You'll find it on the staff portal under the heading Policies. Please sign the form once you've read it.

Now, if you witness something you believe is wrong, the first step is to speak to your line manager. If you feel unable to do that, perhaps because the manager is involved, you can contact the confidential ethics line. The number is on the back of your staff card.

All reports are treated seriously, and we promise that no one who raises a genuine concern will be punished for doing so. That protection is central to our policy.

Finally, we run a refresher session every twelve months, so this won't be the last you hear from me. Are there any questions before we finish?

reading.title: Restorative Justice in Practice
reading.passage:
Traditional criminal justice tends to focus on punishment: an offender breaks the law, is judged guilty, and receives a penalty intended to deter future crime. Restorative justice offers a strikingly different approach. Rather than asking only what rule was broken and what punishment is deserved, it asks who was harmed and how that harm might be repaired. Increasingly, courts and communities around the world are experimenting with this alternative model.

At the centre of restorative justice is the idea of bringing together the offender and the victim, usually with a trained facilitator present. In a carefully managed meeting, victims can describe the impact of the crime, ask questions, and express how they feel. Offenders, in turn, are encouraged to take responsibility for what they did and to consider its consequences. The process may end with an agreement, such as an apology, a payment, or some form of community service.

Supporters argue that this approach offers benefits that conventional courts cannot. Victims often report a greater sense of closure, having been heard rather than reduced to a name on a file. Offenders, confronted directly with the human cost of their actions, may be less likely to reoffend. Some studies suggest that reoffending rates fall when restorative methods are used, although the evidence is not consistent across all types of crime.

The approach is not without critics. Some argue that it is unsuitable for the most serious offences, where face-to-face contact could distress or even endanger the victim. Others worry that offenders might simply pretend to feel remorse in order to avoid harsher penalties. There are also practical limits, since the process depends on the willingness of both parties to take part. For these reasons, restorative justice is generally seen as a complement to the traditional system rather than a replacement for it.

questions.q: Restorative justice focuses mainly on choosing the correct punishment for an offender.

Some critics believe offenders may fake remorse to receive lighter penalties.
According to the passage, victims who take part in restorative justice often report
Restorative justice is generally regarded as
options: a complement to the traditional system
