listening.title: A Lecture on Sentencing Options
listening.script:
LECTURER: Today I want to give you a quick overview of the main sentencing options available to courts, so you can see how flexible the system actually is.

The most severe option, of course, is imprisonment. But courts use it far less often than the public tends to assume. For many minor offences, a fine is imposed instead, and the amount usually depends on the seriousness of the crime and the offender's income.

A second common option is the community order. Here the offender must complete a set number of hours of unpaid work, perhaps cleaning public spaces or helping a local charity. This keeps the person in employment while still providing a meaningful penalty.

Thirdly, courts can issue a suspended sentence. This means a prison term is set but not activated, provided the offender commits no further crime within a fixed period, often two years.

Finally, for younger offenders, restorative approaches are increasingly favoured, since the goal is to prevent a lifelong pattern of offending. Next week, we'll look more closely at how judges decide between these options.

questions.q: According to the lecturer, courts use imprisonment
options: more often than the public thinks
less often than the public assumes

reading.title: The Ethics of Animal Research
reading.passage:
Few areas of scientific practice provoke as much ethical disagreement as the use of animals in research. For more than a century, experiments on animals have contributed to medical advances, from vaccines to surgical techniques. Yet the practice raises a difficult question: can the suffering of animals ever be justified by the benefits gained for human beings? The debate involves not only scientists but also philosophers, lawmakers, and the wider public.

Those who defend animal research argue that it has been essential to progress. Many treatments now taken for granted, they point out, were developed and tested on animals before being given to people. They also stress that strict regulations govern modern laboratories, requiring researchers to minimise pain and to use the smallest possible number of animals. Without such research, they contend, the development of new medicines would be far slower and more dangerous.

Opponents counter that animals are capable of suffering and therefore deserve moral consideration in their own right. From this perspective, inflicting pain on a sentient creature for human benefit is difficult to defend, however valuable the outcome. Some critics also question the scientific value of the research itself, arguing that results obtained from animals do not always apply reliably to humans.

In response to these concerns, scientists have increasingly adopted the principle known as the "three Rs": replacement, reduction, and refinement. Replacement encourages the use of alternatives, such as computer models or cell cultures, wherever possible. Reduction aims to limit the number of animals used, and refinement seeks to lessen any suffering involved. While these principles have not ended the controversy, they reflect a growing recognition that the moral status of animals cannot simply be ignored.

questions.q: Animal experiments have played a role in medical advances for over a hundred years.
Defenders of animal research claim that modern laboratories are subject to strict regulations.
Opponents argue that results from animals always apply reliably to humans.
The number of laboratory animals used worldwide has fallen every year since 2000.
The "three Rs" have completely ended the debate about animal research.
The principle of "replacement" encourages researchers to
options: use alternatives such as computer models

questions.q: Opponents of animal research believe that
options: animals deserve moral consideration in their own right

listening.title: A Debate Society Meeting
listening.script:
CHAIR: Okay, let's get started. Tonight's motion is that this house would ban all cosmetic testing on animals. Maya, you're speaking first for the motion.

MAYA: Thanks. My argument is simple. Testing lipstick or shampoo on a rabbit causes real pain for a trivial benefit. We already have reliable alternatives, like tests on artificial skin, so there's no excuse to continue.

CHAIR: Thank you. And against the motion, we have Tom.

TOM: I actually agree that cosmetic testing is hard to defend. But I'd warn against a total ban. A sudden ban could push the testing to countries with weaker rules, where animals suffer more, not less. I'd prefer a phased reduction with strict oversight.

CHAIR: Interesting point. Maya, a quick response?

MAYA: A phased approach just delays the inevitable. If something is wrong, we should stop it now, and offer companies support to switch methods.

CHAIR: Right. We'll open it to the floor in a moment, but first let's take a vote on whether anyone has changed their mind. Please raise your hand if Tom's argument has shifted your view.

reading.title: Intellectual Property in the Digital Age
reading.passage:
Intellectual property law protects creations of the mind, including books, music, inventions, and brand names. It grants creators certain exclusive rights, allowing them to control how their work is used and, importantly, to earn a living from it. The underlying idea is that society benefits when talented people are rewarded for their efforts and therefore encouraged to produce more. Yet the rise of digital technology has placed this long-established system under unprecedented strain.

In the past, copying a book or a record was costly and time-consuming, which naturally limited the scale of infringement. The digital revolution changed everything. A film, a song, or a piece of software can now be duplicated perfectly and shared with millions of people in seconds, often at no cost. As a result, creators and companies have struggled to prevent the unauthorised distribution of their work, and large industries have seen their traditional business models disrupted.

The response has been varied. Some rights holders have pursued legal action against individuals who share files illegally, a strategy that has often proved expensive and unpopular. Others have adapted by offering convenient, affordable services, reasoning that consumers will pay a reasonable price rather than risk breaking the law. The success of music and video streaming platforms suggests that this second approach can work, though it has not eliminated piracy entirely.

Meanwhile, a deeper debate continues about how far intellectual property rights should extend. Critics argue that overly strict rules can stifle creativity, since new works often build upon existing ones. They warn that if every reference or adaptation requires permission and payment, innovation may slow rather than flourish. Striking the right balance between rewarding creators and allowing the free flow of ideas remains one of the central challenges of modern law.

questions.q: Intellectual property law allows creators to control how their work is used.

Suing individual file-sharers has often been costly and unpopular.
Streaming platforms have completely eliminated piracy.
The author believes intellectual property law will be abolished within ten years.
According to the passage, the digital revolution made it possible to
options: copy works slowly and at high cost
duplicate works perfectly and share them instantly

listening.title: A Seminar on Copyright for Students
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, in this short seminar I want to clear up some common misunderstandings about copyright, because they cause students real trouble every year.

The first myth is that anything you find online is free to use. That's simply not true. Most images, articles, and videos are protected the moment they are created, whether or not they carry a copyright symbol.

The second myth is that copying a small amount is always acceptable. In fact, there's no fixed percentage that's automatically safe. What matters is whether your use is fair, and how much of the original work you take in relation to your purpose.

Third, many students assume that citing the source means they can copy as much as they like. Citation prevents the charge of plagiarism, but it does not by itself give you permission to reproduce protected material.

My advice is straightforward. When in doubt, look for material released under an open licence, which clearly states what you may do. And if you still aren't sure, just ask a librarian. They deal with these questions all the time and are always happy to help.

reading.title: The Rise of Community Solar
reading.passage:
Not everyone who wants to use solar power can install panels on their own roof. Tenants, people living in apartment blocks and households whose roofs face the wrong direction have traditionally been excluded from the solar revolution. Community solar schemes were designed to remove these barriers. Under such a scheme, a single large array is built on a suitable site, and dozens or even hundreds of local households buy or lease a share of the panels. The electricity the array generates is fed into the grid, and each participant receives a credit on their monthly bill in proportion to their share.

The appeal of this model lies in its flexibility. Participants do not need to pay the high upfront cost of a private installation, nor do they have to worry about maintenance, which is handled by the operator. If a household moves to a new home within the same region, it can usually transfer its subscription. Because the array is built at scale, the cost of generating each unit of electricity tends to be lower than it would be for a small rooftop system. Supporters argue that this makes clean energy accessible to lower-income families who would otherwise be priced out.

However, community solar is not without its critics. Some point out that the financial savings for individual subscribers are modest, often amounting to only a small percentage of a typical bill. Others note that the schemes depend heavily on government incentives, and that when these incentives are withdrawn, projects can stall. There is also the question of land use: large arrays require open space, and in densely populated areas suitable sites are scarce. Despite these concerns, the number of community solar projects has grown steadily, and several regional governments now treat the model as a central part of their plans to widen access to renewable power.

questions.q: Community solar schemes were created mainly to help people who cannot install panels on their own property.
Participants in a community solar scheme are responsible for maintaining the array themselves.
A household that moves house within the same region can often keep its subscription.

options: It is built at a large scale

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to join your community solar scheme, but I rent my flat, so I can't put panels on the roof.

CALLER: How much does a share cost to start?

reading.title: Storing Wind Power Underground
reading.passage:
Wind power has become one of the cheapest sources of electricity in many parts of the world, but it has a stubborn weakness: the wind does not always blow when demand is highest. On a gusty night, turbines may produce far more power than the grid can use, while on a still summer evening they may produce almost none. To make wind a reliable backbone of the energy system, engineers must find ways to store surplus electricity and release it later. One promising approach is known as compressed air energy storage, or CAES.

The principle is straightforward. When turbines generate more electricity than is needed, the surplus is used to run powerful pumps that force air into a sealed underground cavern, often a disused salt mine. The air is squeezed to many times normal atmospheric pressure. Later, when demand rises, the compressed air is released, heated, and used to drive a turbine that generates electricity. In effect, the cavern acts like a giant battery, but one made of rock and air rather than chemicals.

CAES has several advantages. The underground caverns can hold enormous quantities of energy, far more than a typical chemical battery, and they can do so for days at a time with little loss. The geological formations required are also relatively common in some regions, which keeps costs down. Yet the technology is not perfect. Compressing air generates heat that is often wasted, which lowers the overall efficiency of the system. Newer designs aim to capture and reuse this heat, and early trials suggest that doing so can raise efficiency substantially. If these improvements prove durable at full scale, underground storage could help wind power deliver electricity around the clock, smoothing out the natural swings of the weather.

questions.q: Wind turbines sometimes generate more electricity than the grid can immediately use.
In a CAES system, air is stored in sealed underground caverns.
Chemical batteries can usually store more energy than CAES caverns.
CAES caverns are most commonly built beneath large cities.
What is described as a weakness of CAES?
options: Compressing air produces heat that is often wasted
The system cannot store energy overnight

questions.q: Why does CAES tend to be affordable in some regions?
options: Suitable geological formations are relatively common there

questions.q: The surplus electricity is used to run pumps that force air into a sealed underground ________.

listening.title: A Tour of a Storage Site
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone, to the Blackrock energy storage facility. I'm Dana, and I'll show you how we store wind power underground.

VISITOR: So this used to be a working mine?

GUIDE: That's right. It was a salt mine until nineteen ninety-eight. Now the cavern below us stores compressed air.

VISITOR: How deep is the cavern?

GUIDE: It sits about six hundred metres below the surface. The salt walls are sealed naturally, so no air escapes.

VISITOR: And where does the electricity come from?

GUIDE: From the wind farm on the ridge. When the turbines produce extra power at night, we use it to pump air down here.

VISITOR: How long can you store the energy?

GUIDE: Up to three days with very little loss. When the grid needs power, we release the air to drive a turbine.

VISITOR: Is it efficient?

GUIDE: The older method wasted heat, but our new system reuses it, pushing efficiency up to around seventy per cent.

VISITOR: That's impressive.

GUIDE: Indeed. Now, please put on a helmet before we go closer to the shaft.

options: A wind farm on the ridge

reading.title: Greening the Concrete Industry
reading.passage:
Concrete is the most widely used building material on Earth, second only to water among substances consumed by humanity. It is cheap, strong and astonishingly versatile. Yet its production carries a heavy environmental price. The key ingredient of concrete is cement, and making cement releases vast quantities of carbon dioxide. The chemical reaction that turns limestone into cement gives off the gas directly, and the furnaces that heat the mixture, often fuelled by coal, add still more. Together, cement production is responsible for roughly eight per cent of global carbon emissions, a figure larger than the entire aviation industry.

Reducing this footprint is difficult precisely because concrete is so useful. The world is not going to stop building roads, bridges and homes. Instead, researchers are pursuing several parallel strategies. One is to replace a portion of the cement with industrial by-products such as fly ash from power stations or slag from steel mills. These materials behave in a similar way to cement but generate far fewer emissions. Another approach is to redesign the furnaces so that they can run on cleaner fuels or capture the carbon dioxide they emit before it escapes into the atmosphere.

A more radical idea is to change the chemistry of cement altogether. Some start-up companies are developing novel formulas that absorb carbon dioxide as they harden, effectively locking the gas inside the finished structure. If such products can match the strength and durability of conventional concrete, they could transform the industry. For now, however, these alternatives remain expensive and are produced only in small quantities. Builders are also cautious, since a material that fails after a few years could be catastrophic. Changing an industry as vast and conservative as construction will take decades, but the environmental stakes ensure that the search for greener concrete will continue.

questions.q: Concrete is the most consumed substance by humans after water.
Cement production releases more carbon dioxide than the aviation industry.
Fly ash and slag produce more emissions than ordinary cement.
Most countries have passed laws banning the use of coal in cement furnaces.
Why are builders cautious about new types of cement?
options: A material that fails could be disastrous

questions.q: What does the most radical idea involve?
options: Cement that absorbs carbon dioxide as it hardens

questions.q: Cement production is responsible for roughly ________ per cent of global carbon emissions.
Part of the cement can be replaced with industrial ________ such as fly ash.

listening.title: Lecture: Cutting Cement Emissions
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we'll look at how the cement industry is trying to cut its emissions. As you know, cement is responsible for about eight per cent of global carbon dioxide. That's a striking figure.

The first strategy is substitution. We replace some cement with fly ash, a waste product from coal power stations. This can lower emissions by up to thirty per cent without weakening the concrete.

The second strategy is carbon capture. Modern plants fit equipment to the furnace chimney that traps the gas before it escapes. The captured carbon can then be stored underground or even reused.

The third and most experimental strategy is new chemistry. Several companies have created cements that actually absorb carbon dioxide as they set. One firm in Canada reports that its product locks away around twenty kilograms of gas per tonne of concrete.

The main barrier, of course, is cost. These greener products are still two to three times more expensive than ordinary cement. But as carbon taxes rise, that gap is expected to narrow over the next decade. For your essay, focus on one strategy and evaluate it critically.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Fast Fashion
reading.passage:
The clothing on the shelves of a typical high-street shop is cheaper today, in real terms, than it has been for decades. Shoppers can buy a new outfit for the price of a sandwich, wear it a handful of times, and discard it without a second thought. This model, known as fast fashion, depends on producing enormous volumes of garments quickly and selling them at low prices. Its environmental consequences, however, are anything but cheap.

The production of textiles consumes staggering amounts of water and energy. Growing the cotton for a single shirt can require thousands of litres of water, and dyeing fabric releases chemicals that pollute rivers in many manufacturing regions. Most clothing is now made from synthetic fibres derived from oil, which shed tiny plastic particles every time they are washed. These particles flow into the sea, where they enter the food chain. Meanwhile, the rapid turnover of styles means that millions of tonnes of clothing are thrown away each year, much of it ending up in landfill or being burned.

Efforts to make the industry more sustainable are gathering pace, though progress is uneven. Some brands now use recycled fibres or organic cotton, and a growing number offer repair services to extend the life of their products. Second-hand and rental platforms have also become fashionable, particularly among younger consumers who are more conscious of waste. Critics warn, however, that such measures can amount to little more than marketing unless they are matched by a genuine reduction in the overall quantity of clothing produced. The deeper challenge, they argue, is cultural: as long as shoppers expect endless novelty at rock-bottom prices, the pressures driving fast fashion will remain. Changing those expectations may prove harder than changing any factory.

questions.q: Clothing is now cheaper in real terms than it was decades ago.
Synthetic fibres release plastic particles when washed.

options: They may be mainly marketing unless total production falls

options: A cultural expectation of cheap novelty

PRIYA: Hi! It's simple. People bring clean clothes they no longer wear, and they take home something new to them, all for free. We just ask that each item is in good condition.

HOST: How many people usually come?

HOST: That's a lot. Why do you think these events are growing?

PRIYA: People are tired of throwing clothes away. The average household here bins around thirty kilograms of textiles a year. A swap keeps those clothes in use.

HOST: Do you do anything with the leftovers?

HOST: And how often do you hold these?

PRIYA: Once a month, always on the first Saturday. The next one is on July the fifth.

HOST: Wonderful. Thank you, Priya, and good luck with the rest of the day.

reading.title: Tidal Power: Energy from the Moon
reading.passage:
While the sun and the wind capture most of the attention in discussions of renewable energy, a quieter source flows twice a day along the world's coastlines. The tides, driven by the gravitational pull of the moon, move staggering volumes of water with remarkable regularity. Unlike sunshine, which disappears at night, or wind, which can fall still for days, the tides are almost perfectly predictable. Engineers can calculate the times and heights of tides years in advance, which makes tidal power uniquely reliable among renewable sources.

There are two main ways to harness this energy. The first is the tidal barrage, a kind of dam built across a bay or estuary. As the tide rises, water flows through gates and is held back; when the tide falls, the trapped water is released through turbines, generating electricity. The second method uses underwater turbines, which resemble wind turbines but are placed on the seabed in fast-flowing channels. These spin as the tidal current passes over them and have a smaller impact on the surrounding landscape.

Despite its reliability, tidal power has been slow to spread. The main obstacle is cost: building structures that can survive decades of pounding by salt water and storms is extremely expensive. Barrages, in particular, can disturb the delicate ecosystems of the estuaries where they are built, drawing opposition from conservation groups. Suitable locations are also limited, since strong tides occur only in certain coastal areas. Nevertheless, a handful of countries with favourable coastlines have invested heavily in the technology, and improvements in materials are gradually bringing costs down. If these trends continue, the gentle but relentless rhythm of the tides may yet supply a meaningful share of the world's clean electricity.

questions.q: The tides are more predictable than sunshine or wind.
Underwater turbines are placed on the seabed in fast-flowing channels.

listening.title: Seminar: Assessing a Tidal Project
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, let's review the proposed tidal project in Marsh Bay. Sam, can you summarise the key figures?

SAM: Sure. The barrage would stretch across the estuary for about two kilometres. It's expected to supply electricity to roughly forty thousand homes.

TUTOR: A solid output. What about the cost?

SAM: That's the difficult part. The construction budget stands at around nine hundred million pounds, and it would take seven years to build.

TUTOR: And the environmental objections?

SAM: Conservation groups are worried about the wading birds that feed on the mudflats. The barrage could change the tidal patterns the birds depend on.

TUTOR: A genuine concern. Is there a compromise?

SAM: The engineers suggest underwater turbines instead of a full barrage. They generate slightly less power but cause far less disruption to wildlife.

TUTOR: Good. For next week, I'd like each of you to write a one-page recommendation. Decide whether the barrage or the turbines represent the better option, and justify your choice with evidence.

SAM: Will do.

reading.title: The Lost Workshops of Ancient Glassmakers
reading.passage:
For centuries, glass was among the most prized materials of the ancient Mediterranean, yet the people who actually produced it left surprisingly few traces. Unlike pottery, whose broken fragments survive almost everywhere, the furnaces and tools of glassmakers were often dismantled, reused, or simply lost. As a result, archaeologists have long struggled to locate the workshops where raw glass was first manufactured rather than merely shaped into finished objects.

A recent survey along a stretch of coast in the eastern Mediterranean has begun to change this picture. Working with magnetometers, which detect faint variations in the magnetic field caused by intense heat, researchers identified a cluster of buried structures that they believe were primary glass furnaces. When a trial trench was opened, the team uncovered thick slabs of unworked glass, each weighing several hundred kilograms. These slabs, they argue, were broken into chunks and shipped to secondary workshops elsewhere, where craftsmen reheated and blew them into vessels.

The discovery is significant because it suggests a two-stage industry on a far larger scale than previously assumed. Raw glass appears to have been produced at a handful of specialised sites, then distributed across a wide trading network. Chemical analysis of the slabs revealed unusually high levels of a particular mineral, pointing to a local source of sand that was especially suited to glassmaking. This may explain why the region became a centre of production while other coastal areas, despite having abundant sand, did not.

Not everyone is convinced that the structures were furnaces at all. A few specialists caution that the magnetic signatures could equally indicate kilns used for firing tiles. The excavators acknowledge this uncertainty but note that the sheer quantity of glass waste recovered nearby strongly favours their interpretation. Further trenches are planned for the coming season, and the team hopes that a more complete furnace, with its walls still standing, may eventually be found.

questions.q: Pottery fragments are generally easier to find than the remains of glass workshops.
Magnetometers work by detecting changes in the magnetic field linked to high temperatures.
The glass slabs found in the trench were already shaped into finished vessels.
The researchers have determined the exact number of craftsmen employed at the secondary workshops.
According to the passage, why may this region have become a glassmaking centre?
options: Its sand contained a mineral well suited to glass production
It was closer to major cities than other coastal areas
Its furnaces could reach higher temperatures
It had a longer history of pottery making

questions.q: What objection do some specialists raise about the structures?
options: They may be too small to be furnaces
The magnetic signatures might come from tile kilns instead
They could date from a much later period
The glass nearby may have been imported

questions.q: Each slab of unworked glass weighed several hundred ________.
The team plans to open further ________ in the coming season.

listening.title: Booking a Museum Tour
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, City Archaeology Museum, how can I help?

VISITOR: Hi, I'd like to book a guided tour for next week, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. We run tours on Tuesdays and Thursdays. Which day suits you?

VISITOR: Thursday would be ideal.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. And how many people in your group?

VISITOR: There will be six of us, all adults.

RECEPTIONIST: Right, six adults. The standard tour costs fifteen pounds per person, but for groups of five or more we offer a ten per cent discount.

VISITOR: That's helpful. What time does it start?

RECEPTIONIST: The morning tour begins at half past ten and lasts about ninety minutes. It focuses on our new glassmaking gallery.

VISITOR: Perfect. Do we need to bring anything?

RECEPTIONIST: Just comfortable shoes, as there's a fair amount of walking. Photography is allowed, but please don't use flash near the fragile exhibits.

VISITOR: Understood. Can I pay on the day?

RECEPTIONIST: You can, but I'd recommend paying now to secure your place. Could I take a name for the booking?

VISITOR: Yes, it's under Harper.

RECEPTIONIST: Thank you, Mr Harper. You're all booked for Thursday.

reading.title: Reading the Tree Rings of the Past
reading.passage:
When archaeologists want to know how old a wooden beam or a buried post is, they increasingly turn to a technique called dendrochronology, or tree-ring dating. The method rests on a simple observation: in most climates, a tree adds one new ring of growth every year. In a wet year the ring is wide; in a dry year it is narrow. Over decades, these varying widths form a distinctive pattern, almost like a barcode, that is shared by all the trees growing in the same region at the same time.

By matching the pattern in a piece of ancient timber against a master sequence built from living trees and older samples, researchers can sometimes pinpoint the exact year in which a tree was felled. This precision is remarkable. Radiocarbon dating, by contrast, usually provides a range of several decades rather than a single year. For sites where wood is well preserved, dendrochronology can therefore transform a vague estimate into a firm date.

The technique has its limits, however. It works only with species that produce clear annual rings, and only in regions where a long master sequence has already been assembled. In tropical zones, where growth is continuous throughout the year, the rings may be indistinct or absent altogether. Moreover, the wood being tested must contain enough rings to be matched confidently; a short fragment with only a dozen rings is rarely sufficient.

Despite these constraints, dendrochronology has reshaped the study of the ancient world. It has helped date entire settlements, exposed forgeries of supposedly old furniture, and even revealed periods of drought that may have driven communities to abandon their homes. Perhaps most strikingly, by overlapping samples of ever-increasing age, scientists have extended some master sequences back more than ten thousand years, creating a continuous calendar against which other dating methods can be checked.

questions.q: A tree usually produces one growth ring each year in most climates.
Narrow rings tend to form during particularly wet years.
Radiocarbon dating typically gives a more precise year than dendrochronology.
Dendrochronology is the cheapest dating method available to archaeologists.
Why is dendrochronology often unreliable in tropical regions?
options: Growth is continuous, so rings may be unclear
There are too few tree species

questions.q: What is one application of the technique mentioned in the passage?
options: Measuring the height of ancient trees

questions.q: The varying ring widths form a pattern compared to a ________.
Some master sequences now extend back more than ________ thousand years.

listening.title: A Student Lab Induction
listening.script:
SUPERVISOR: Welcome to the dating laboratory. Before you start handling the wood samples, I'll run through a few rules.

STUDENT: Great, thanks. I've never worked in a lab like this before.

SUPERVISOR: No worries. First, always sign in at the front desk and collect a visitor badge. You'll need to wear it at all times.

STUDENT: Got it. Do I need gloves for the samples?

SUPERVISOR: Yes, absolutely. The cotton gloves are in the second drawer on your left. Never touch the timber with bare hands, because oils from your skin can contaminate it.

STUDENT: Understood. And where do I record my measurements?

SUPERVISOR: Each sample has a code. You log the ring widths in the shared spreadsheet under that code. Please double-check the number before you save.

STUDENT: How many samples will I be working on today?

SUPERVISOR: Just three to begin with. Once you're confident, we'll give you more. The microscope you'll use is the one nearest the window.

STUDENT: One last thing, when does the lab close?

SUPERVISOR: It shuts at six o'clock sharp, so please tidy your station by quarter to six.

options: Skin oils can contaminate the timber

reading.title: The Underwater City
reading.passage:
Beneath the calm waters of a sheltered bay lie the remains of a harbour town that flourished some two thousand years ago. Once a busy port handling grain, wine and pottery, the settlement gradually slipped below the waves. For generations, local fishermen reported glimpsing walls and columns on the seabed, but it was only when a team of marine archaeologists mapped the bay with sonar that the full extent of the drowned city became clear.

The cause of the submergence was not a single dramatic flood but a slow, relentless process. The coastline in this area sits above an unstable section of the earth's crust, and over the centuries the land has steadily sunk relative to the sea. A series of earthquakes accelerated the decline, dropping whole districts beneath the waterline within a few decades. What had been streets and squares became, in time, a silent landscape of toppled masonry colonised by fish and weed.

Excavating underwater poses challenges quite unlike those on land. Divers can work for only limited periods, sediment clouds the water at the slightest disturbance, and every artefact must be carefully stabilised before it is raised, since objects that have lain in salt water for millennia may crumble once exposed to air. To address this, the team built a temporary tank on a nearby barge where finds could be kept wet and gradually treated.

The rewards, however, have been considerable. Because the town was abandoned quickly and then sealed by mud, it preserves details rarely found at sites on dry land. Wooden mooring posts still stand upright; storage jars remain in the cellars where they were stored; even the ruts worn by cart wheels are visible in the paving. The excavators describe the site as a snapshot of daily life, frozen at the moment the sea claimed it. Plans are now under way to create an underwater trail so that divers can visit the ruins without damaging them.

questions.q: The drowned city was first fully mapped using sonar equipment.
The town sank because of one sudden, catastrophic flood.
Artefacts from the site may break apart when first exposed to air.
The underwater trail will charge divers an entrance fee.
What accelerated the sinking of the town?
options: A rise in global sea levels
The collapse of the harbour wall

questions.q: Why does the site preserve unusual detail?
options: It was protected by a city wall
It was quickly abandoned and sealed by mud
It was never visited by looters

questions.q: Finds were kept in a temporary ________ on a nearby barge.

listening.title: A Diving Safety Briefing
listening.script:
INSTRUCTOR: Right everyone, gather round for the safety briefing before we head out to the wreck site.

DIVER: How deep is the dive today?

INSTRUCTOR: The main ruins sit at about eighteen metres, so this is well within recreational limits. Even so, we'll dive in pairs, and you must stay with your buddy at all times.

DIVER: What's the maximum time we can spend down there?

INSTRUCTOR: Forty minutes on the bottom, then we begin our ascent. Watch your air gauge, and signal me when you reach one hundred bar.

DIVER: Are we allowed to touch the artefacts?

INSTRUCTOR: Absolutely not. This is a protected site. You can look and photograph, but nothing is to be moved or lifted. Disturbing the sediment also ruins visibility for everyone.

DIVER: Where do we meet if we get separated?

INSTRUCTOR: Surface slowly, then return to the boat. The boat's name is Sea Otter, and it'll be flying a blue and white flag.

DIVER: Got it. What's the water temperature?

INSTRUCTOR: Around sixteen degrees, so your wetsuits will be fine. Any other questions? No? Then let's gear up.

reading.title: What Ancient Diets Reveal
reading.passage:
It is easy to imagine that the diet of ancient peoples can be reconstructed simply from the bones of animals and the seeds left behind at their settlements. Such remains certainly help, but they tell only part of the story. Bones from a rubbish pit may reflect a single feast rather than everyday eating, and delicate plant foods often vanish without trace. To build a fuller picture, scientists have turned to the bodies of the people themselves, and in particular to the chemical traces locked inside their teeth and bones.

The principle is straightforward. As a person grows, the food they eat leaves a chemical fingerprint in their tissues. Different foods contain slightly different proportions of certain elements, and these proportions are preserved long after death. By measuring them, researchers can estimate whether an individual relied mainly on land plants, on meat, or on resources drawn from rivers and the sea. Teeth are especially valuable because, unlike bone, they stop changing once they have formed in childhood, offering a record of early diet that the rest of the skeleton cannot provide.

The results have sometimes overturned long-held assumptions. At one inland cemetery, scholars had supposed that the community lived chiefly on cereals and herded animals. The chemical evidence revealed instead that fish from a nearby river had been a major part of the diet, hinting at trade or seasonal movement that had left no other archaeological sign. Elsewhere, differences between the diets of richer and poorer graves have offered a glimpse of social inequality that grave goods alone could not show.

The approach is not without difficulty. Contamination from the surrounding soil can distort the readings, and interpreting the chemistry requires careful comparison with local plants and animals. Yet when combined with the more traditional evidence of bones and seeds, the chemical study of human remains is steadily transforming our understanding of how ancient communities fed themselves, and of how closely diet was tied to status, geography and the changing seasons.

questions.q: Animal bones from a rubbish pit always reflect everyday eating habits.
Teeth preserve a record of a person's diet during childhood.
Chemical analysis can suggest whether someone ate mainly plants, meat or aquatic foods.
Most ancient communities preferred fish to cereals.
Why are teeth particularly useful to researchers?
options: They survive longer than any other body part
They stop changing after forming in childhood
They contain more elements than bone
They are easier to find than bones

questions.q: What did the chemical evidence reveal at the inland cemetery?
options: The people ate almost no meat
Fish was an important part of the diet
Richer people ate less than poorer ones

questions.q: Food leaves a chemical ________ in a person's tissues.
Readings can be distorted by ________ from the surrounding soil.

listening.title: A Lecture on Ancient Food Storage
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon. Today we're looking at how ancient communities stored their food, because storage was just as important as growing or hunting it.

Let's start with grain. The most common method was the storage pit, dug into the ground and lined with clay or basketry. Once filled, the pit was sealed to keep out air and moisture. Surprisingly, the grain at the very edge would sprout and form a crust, and this crust actually protected the grain inside.

Next, consider raised granaries. These were small wooden buildings set on stone supports, which kept the floor about half a meter above the ground. The gap stopped rodents and damp from reaching the harvest.

For liquids like oil and wine, large clay jars were used. They were often buried up to the neck in the cool earth of a cellar, which kept the contents at a steady temperature.

Finally, salting and drying preserved meat and fish for the winter months. These simple techniques meant a household could survive a poor harvest, and they form a recurring theme in the archaeology of almost every ancient society. Next week we'll examine cooking methods.

questions.q: What was the most common method of storing grain?
Storage pits were lined with clay or ________.
Raised granaries kept the floor about half a ________ above the ground.
Why were raised granaries built on supports?
options: To make them easier to move
To keep out rodents and damp

questions.q: Clay jars for liquids were often buried in the cool earth of a ________.
What does the lecturer say these storage techniques allowed?

speaking.q: Let's talk about food and meals. What kind of food did you eat most often when you were a child? Do you prefer to cook at home or eat out? Has the food people eat in your country changed much over time?

writing.q:
Some people think that studying the diets and daily lives of ancient peoples has little value in the modern world. Others believe that understanding the past helps us make better decisions today. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Scientists try to reconstruct ancient diets.

These proportions are preserved after death.

reading.title: The Writing That Took a Century to Read
reading.passage:
When a forgotten script is unearthed, archaeologists face one of their most tantalising challenges: how to read words that no living person can understand. The decipherment of an unknown writing system is rarely the work of a single brilliant moment. More often it is a slow accumulation of clues, false starts and patient comparison, sometimes stretching across several generations of scholars.

Consider the case of a script discovered on clay tablets at a ruined palace early in the twentieth century. For decades the symbols resisted every attempt to interpret them. They were clearly not a known alphabet, and there was no convenient bilingual inscription, no equivalent of a stone bearing the same text in a familiar language, to provide a starting point. Some researchers suspected the language was related to others spoken in the region, while others insisted it was entirely unique.

The breakthrough came from an unexpected direction. Rather than guessing at meanings, one investigator focused purely on patterns: which symbols appeared at the beginnings of words, which at the ends, and which seemed to change according to grammar. By treating the script as a puzzle of structure rather than a mystery of meaning, she was able to show that certain signs behaved like grammatical endings. Once the underlying language was tentatively identified, individual words slowly fell into place.

Even so, decipherment is seldom complete. Many ancient scripts remain only partly understood, and a single new tablet can force scholars to revise conclusions once thought secure. Some systems, despite enormous effort, have never been cracked at all, either because too few examples survive or because the language they record has left no surviving relatives. What the successful cases share is not luck but method: a willingness to set aside assumptions, to count and compare with great care, and to accept that reading the silent voices of the past may demand the labour of a lifetime, or even longer.

questions.q: Deciphering an unknown script is usually achieved by one sudden insight.
A bilingual inscription was available to help read the palace tablets.
The key investigator concentrated first on the structure of the symbols rather than their meaning.
The investigator who made the breakthrough received a major award for her work.
Why have some ancient scripts never been deciphered?
options: The clay tablets were destroyed in wars
Too few examples survive or the language has no relatives
The symbols were too simple to analyse

questions.q: According to the passage, what do successful decipherments have in common?
options: The good fortune of the researchers

questions.q: The mysterious script was found on clay ________ at a ruined palace.
A single new tablet can force scholars to ________ earlier conclusions.

listening.title: Planning a Field Project
listening.script:
PROFESSOR: So, before we submit the funding application, let's go over the plan for the summer field project.

STUDENT: Sure. Where exactly will we be working this year?

PROFESSOR: At the hillside site near the old quarry. We've been granted permission to dig two new areas.

STUDENT: How long is the season?

PROFESSOR: Six weeks in total, beginning on the first of July. The first week will be spent setting up the camp and the recording system.

STUDENT: And what's the main aim?

PROFESSOR: Our priority is to recover any inscribed material. Last year we found a fragment of an unknown script, so we're hoping for more tablets.

STUDENT: That's exciting. How many students will join us?

PROFESSOR: Twelve undergraduates plus three specialists, including a conservator. Accommodation is in the village guesthouse.

STUDENT: What about equipment?

PROFESSOR: Most of it is stored on site, but we'll need to hire a generator and order new sieves. Could you handle the equipment list?

STUDENT: Of course. When's the application deadline?

PROFESSOR: The fifteenth of March, so we've got just under three weeks. Let's aim to finish a draft by Friday.

Site | Tablets found | Deciphered (%)

reading.title: The Hidden Economics of Orchestras
reading.passage:
Symphony orchestras occupy an unusual position in the modern economy. Unlike most industries, they cannot easily increase their productivity. A string quartet written in 1800 still requires four musicians today, playing for roughly the same length of time as audiences heard two centuries ago. Economists describe this phenomenon as the "cost disease", a term coined in the 1960s to explain why the expenses of live performance tend to rise faster than those in manufacturing, where machines steadily replace human effort.

This structural problem means that ticket sales alone almost never cover an orchestra's costs. A typical professional ensemble in Europe recovers only between thirty and forty per cent of its budget from the box office. The remainder must come from a mixture of public subsidy, corporate sponsorship and private donations. In several countries, government support has declined over the past two decades, forcing orchestras to compete more aggressively for the attention of wealthy patrons and charitable foundations.

Some ensembles have responded with imaginative strategies. A few have shortened concerts to attract office workers during lunch breaks, while others have experimented with relaxed performances where applause between movements is welcomed rather than discouraged. Streaming platforms have opened a further source of revenue, although the sums earned per listener remain modest. Critics argue that such innovations risk diluting the artistic seriousness that gives orchestras their value.

Despite these pressures, attendance in many cities has remained surprisingly stable. Surveys suggest that audiences value the irreplaceable quality of a live, shared experience, something no recording can fully reproduce. Whether this loyalty will be enough to sustain orchestras through the coming decades, however, depends largely on decisions made far beyond the concert hall, in the offices of governments and benefactors who continue to decide what culture deserves financial protection.

questions.q: The term "cost disease" was first used in the nineteenth century.
Most professional European orchestras earn the majority of their income from ticket sales.
Government funding for orchestras has increased in recent years across Europe.
Streaming generates large amounts of money for each individual listener.
The author personally believes relaxed performances damage musical quality.
According to the passage, why is it difficult for orchestras to raise productivity?
options: Musicians refuse to learn new pieces
The amount of human labour required cannot easily be reduced

questions.q: What does the passage suggest audiences most value about orchestras?
A typical European orchestra recovers only between thirty and ________ per cent of its budget from the box office.

listening.title: Booking a Rehearsal Room
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good afternoon, Northgate Music Centre, how can I help?
CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a rehearsal room for my band, please.
RECEPTIONIST: Of course. Which day were you thinking of?
CALLER: Next Tuesday, if possible, in the evening.
RECEPTIONIST: We have Studio Three free from six until nine. The hourly rate is fourteen pounds.
CALLER: That sounds good. We'd want all three hours.
RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. Does the room need a drum kit?
CALLER: Yes, please, and we'll bring our own guitars.
RECEPTIONIST: No problem. There's also an amplifier included at no extra charge. Could I take a name?
CALLER: It's Daniel Forster, that's F-O-R-S-T-E-R.
RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. And a contact number?
CALLER: It's oh-seven-seven-double-five, three-one-nine-eight-two-six.
RECEPTIONIST: Got it. Please arrive ten minutes early to collect the key from the front desk. Payment is taken on the night, card only.
CALLER: Perfect, thanks very much.

reading.title: Why Songs Get Stuck in Our Heads
reading.passage:
Almost everyone has experienced the curious sensation of a tune repeating itself involuntarily in the mind, sometimes for hours at a time. Researchers call these fragments "earworms", and over the past fifteen years they have become a serious subject of psychological study. Far from being a trivial annoyance, earworms offer scientists a window into how the brain stores and retrieves musical memory.

Studies suggest that the songs most likely to lodge in our heads share certain musical features. They tend to have a fast, predictable rhythm and a melodic shape that rises and falls in a way that is easy to follow yet contains an unexpected twist. This combination of familiarity and surprise appears to keep the brain engaged, prompting it to rehearse the fragment repeatedly. Crucially, it is rarely the whole song that returns, but a short section, often the chorus.

Researchers have also examined when earworms strike. They are most common during moments of low mental demand, such as walking, showering or performing routine chores. When the mind is idle, it seems to fill the silence with recently heard music. Stress and tiredness can intensify the effect, as can hearing only part of a song and being interrupted before it finishes, a tendency psychologists link to the brain's dislike of incomplete tasks.

Getting rid of an earworm is harder than producing one. Distraction sometimes works, particularly engaging in a moderately difficult activity that occupies the verbal parts of the brain. Some people report success by deliberately listening to the offending song all the way through, allowing the brain to register it as complete. Others chew gum, a technique one experiment found genuinely reduced the frequency of involuntary musical thoughts, though researchers remain uncertain exactly why such a simple action should help at all.

questions.q: Scientists have studied earworms intensively for over fifty years.
Earworms usually involve only a short part of a song rather than the whole piece.
Earworms occur most often when people are concentrating hard on a difficult task.
Chewing gum has been shown to completely cure earworms in all cases.
Classical music produces earworms more often than pop music does.
What combination of qualities makes a song likely to become an earworm?
options: Familiarity together with an unexpected twist

questions.q: Why might an unfinished song be especially likely to repeat in the mind?
options: The song was played too loudly

questions.q: Engaging in a moderately difficult ________ can help remove an earworm by occupying the verbal brain.

listening.title: Student Music Society Enquiry
listening.script:
STUDENT: Hi, I saw a poster about the university music society. Could you tell me a bit about it?
ORGANISER: Sure! We meet every Wednesday at half past six in the Arts Building, Room Twelve.
STUDENT: Great. Do I need to be able to play an instrument?
ORGANISER: Not at all. We have singers, players and people who just help with sound and lighting.
STUDENT: That's reassuring. How much does it cost to join?
ORGANISER: Membership is twenty dollars for the whole year, and that includes free entry to our concerts.
STUDENT: And how many concerts do you put on?
ORGANISER: Usually three a year, plus a big showcase in the summer term.
STUDENT: Sounds fun. Is there a rehearsal I could come and watch first?
ORGANISER: Definitely. Just turn up next Wednesday. Bring a friend if you like. Oh, and we'll be recording an album in November, so it's an exciting time to join.
STUDENT: Perfect, I'll be there.

reading.title: The Revival of the Vinyl Record
reading.passage:
When digital downloads swept the music industry in the early 2000s, few observers expected the humble vinyl record to survive, let alone flourish. Yet over the past decade sales of vinyl have climbed steadily, year after year, until they now form a significant slice of physical music revenue in several markets. This unexpected comeback has puzzled analysts and delighted a generation of collectors who were not even born when records first fell out of fashion.

Several explanations have been offered. For some buyers, the appeal lies in sound. Enthusiasts argue that the warm, slightly imperfect tone of analogue recording feels richer than the clinical precision of digital files, although blind listening tests have produced mixed and often contradictory results. For many others, however, the attraction has little to do with audio quality. A vinyl record is a physical object that can be held, displayed and given as a gift, in contrast to the invisible streams that dominate everyday listening.

The artwork plays a surprisingly large part. Large square sleeves give designers space that a tiny phone screen cannot, turning each album into a small piece of visual art. Younger buyers in particular describe the pleasure of building a collection that reveals their taste to visitors, a form of self-expression that a hidden digital library cannot provide. For artists, meanwhile, vinyl has become a valuable source of income at a time when streaming pays very little per play.

The revival is not without difficulties. Pressing plants, many of which closed decades ago, have struggled to keep pace with demand, leading to long delays. Production is also more expensive and less environmentally friendly than digital distribution. Whether the trend represents a lasting shift in how people value music, or merely a passing wave of nostalgia, remains a question that even the most confident industry forecasters are reluctant to answer.

questions.q: Most observers in the early 2000s predicted that vinyl would thrive.
Blind listening tests have clearly proven that vinyl sounds better than digital files.
For many buyers, the physical nature of a record is part of its appeal.
Vinyl pressing plants have easily met the rising demand.
Vinyl records cost more to produce in Europe than in Asia.
Why does the passage say album artwork matters to vinyl buyers?
options: It is cheaper to print than digital art
Large sleeves allow more impressive visual design

questions.q: What benefit does vinyl offer artists according to the passage?
options: Greater income per copy than streaming pays per play

questions.q: Industry forecasters are ________ to say whether the revival is lasting or just nostalgia.

listening.title: A Lecture on Concert Hall Acoustics
listening.script: LECTURER: Good morning. Today we'll look at why some concert halls sound far better than others. The key concept is reverberation, the time it takes for sound to fade away after a note stops. For orchestral music, the ideal reverberation time is around two seconds. Too short, and the music feels dry and lifeless; too long, and notes blur together into mush. The shape of the hall matters enormously. The famous shoebox design, a long rectangular room, reflects sound evenly and is still preferred by many musicians. Materials also count. Hard surfaces such as plaster reflect sound, while soft seats and curtains absorb it, so designers must balance the two. Interestingly, a full audience absorbs much more sound than an empty hall, which is why rehearsals in an empty room can sound strangely echoey. Modern halls sometimes use movable panels to adjust the acoustics for different kinds of performance. Next week we'll examine how digital modelling lets architects predict a hall's sound before a single brick is laid.

questions.q: The time it takes for sound to fade away is called ________.
The ideal reverberation time for orchestral music is around ________ seconds.
What is the shoebox hall design?
What effect do soft seats and curtains have on sound?
Why can an empty hall sound echoey during rehearsals?
Modern halls sometimes use movable ________ to adjust the acoustics.

speaking.q: Let's discuss music and technology more broadly. How has the way people buy and listen to music changed in the last twenty years? Do you think these changes have been mostly positive or negative for musicians, and what might music listening look like in the future?

writing.q:
The table below shows global sales of recorded music by format (in millions of units) in three selected years. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Format | 2005 | 2014 | 2023
CDs | 950 | 320 | 90
Digital downloads | 60 | 410 | 110
Streaming subscriptions | 0 | 180 | 720
Vinyl records | 5 | 12 | 48

Vinyl now forms part of the label's revenue.

Some prefer the warmth of analogue sound.
Critics call digital audio too clinical.
The studies gave contradictory results.
Streaming now dominates everyday listening.
A collection can be a form of self-expression.
Factories could not keep pace with demand.
The trend is driven partly by nostalgia.
Forecasters are reluctant to predict the outcome.
The humble cassette is also returning.
Vinyl has made a surprising comeback.
Digital distribution is cheap and instant.

reading.title: The Science of Stage Fright
reading.passage:
Performance anxiety, commonly known as stage fright, affects musicians at every level, from nervous beginners to celebrated soloists who have played to thousands of audiences. The condition is rooted in the body's ancient response to perceived threat. When a performer steps in front of an audience, the brain may interpret the situation as dangerous, releasing hormones that prepare the body to fight or flee. The heart races, the hands tremble and the mouth goes dry, all of which can sabotage the precise control that musical performance demands.

Researchers stress that a moderate level of arousal is not only normal but beneficial. A degree of nervous energy sharpens concentration and lends performances an edge of excitement that completely relaxed playing often lacks. Problems arise only when the response becomes overwhelming, tipping over into panic that disrupts memory and coordination. Studies indicate that the most damaging factor is not the physical symptoms themselves but the performer's fearful interpretation of them.

This insight has shaped modern treatment. Rather than trying to eliminate nerves entirely, therapists increasingly teach musicians to reframe their physical sensations, viewing a pounding heart as a sign of readiness rather than impending disaster. Controlled breathing, careful preparation and gradual exposure to performing in front of small groups all help to keep anxiety within manageable limits. Some performers also use mental rehearsal, vividly imagining a successful concert in advance.

More controversially, a number of professional musicians take medication that slows the heart rate and steadies the hands. Critics worry that such drugs may dull the emotional intensity that makes a performance compelling, and that relying on them prevents musicians from developing genuine psychological resilience. Supporters reply that, used occasionally, these aids simply allow talented players to demonstrate skills that crippling anxiety would otherwise hide. The debate reflects a deeper question about whether performance is meant to be flawless or, above all, human.

questions.q: Stage fright affects only inexperienced musicians.
Researchers believe a moderate amount of nervousness can improve a performance.
Studies suggest the physical symptoms themselves are more harmful than how performers interpret them.
Modern therapy usually aims to remove nerves completely.
Medication for stage fright is more commonly used by string players than by singers.
According to the passage, what does the fight-or-flight response cause in performers?
options: Physical symptoms such as a racing heart

questions.q: How do modern therapists suggest performers should view a pounding heart?
Some performers use mental ________, vividly imagining a successful concert beforehand.

listening.title: Two Students Plan a Performance Project
listening.script:
MAYA: So, for the group project, we need to put on a short performance and write a report on it. Where should we start?
LEO: I think we should pick the theme first. How about music from different decades?
MAYA: I like that. We could do one song each from the sixties, eighties and the present day.
LEO: Perfect. That's three pieces, which fits the ten-minute limit nicely.
MAYA: Should we book the small hall or the studio?
LEO: The studio's better for us; it seats about forty people, which is plenty.
MAYA: Agreed. Now, who's doing what? I'm happy to handle the singing.
LEO: Great, and I'll play guitar. We'll need someone for the lighting, though.
MAYA: I'll ask Priya. She did the lighting for the drama club last term.
LEO: Good idea. Let's aim to have a first rehearsal by Friday.
MAYA: Friday works. I'll email the tutor today to confirm the studio.

His hands trembled before the recital.

Anxiety can disrupt memory.
Therapists teach players to reframe their nerves.

vocab.meaning_ko: 감당할 수 있는, 다룰 수 있는

reading.title: How Film Scores Shape Our Emotions
reading.passage:
Few cinema-goers leave a film consciously praising its musical score, yet the music has quietly guided almost every emotion they felt. A well-crafted soundtrack can transform an ordinary scene into something tense, joyful or heartbreaking, often without the audience noticing how the effect was achieved. Composers who write for film therefore occupy a curious role: their finest work is frequently the work least likely to be remembered, precisely because it serves the story rather than drawing attention to itself.

The techniques involved are surprisingly systematic. Rising musical lines tend to build tension, while a sudden silence can be more startling than any loud chord. Composers exploit cultural associations that audiences have absorbed over a lifetime: minor keys are widely heard as sad, fast rhythms as urgent, and deep, rumbling tones as threatening. Many film composers also assign a recurring melody, known as a leitmotif, to a particular character or idea, so that the music itself can remind viewers of a person even when that person is absent from the screen.

Timing is everything. A musical cue arriving a fraction of a second too early or too late can break the spell, which is why composers work closely with editors, adjusting their music frame by frame to match the action. This painstaking synchronisation explains why scores are usually composed only after filming is complete, when the pace of each scene is finally fixed.

The psychological power of film music has not gone unexamined. Experiments in which the same footage is shown with different soundtracks reveal that viewers can interpret identical images as comic, romantic or menacing depending entirely on what they hear. Such findings raise intriguing ethical questions about the extent to which an audience's emotional responses are being deliberately engineered. For most viewers, however, the manipulation is welcome, part of the very magic that draws them back to the cinema again and again.

questions.q: Audiences often consciously notice and praise a film's musical score.
A sudden silence can have a stronger effect than a loud chord.
Film scores are usually written before filming begins.
The same footage can produce different emotional reactions depending on the music.
Most modern film composers were originally trained as classical pianists.
What is a leitmotif, according to the passage?
options: A recurring melody linked to a character or idea
A silent moment in a film

questions.q: Why is precise timing so important for film music?
options: A cue arriving slightly early or late can break the effect

questions.q: Composers usually write scores only after filming is complete, when the ________ of each scene is fixed.

listening.title: Radio Talk on a Local Music Festival
listening.script: PRESENTER: And now for what's on this weekend. The Riverside Music Festival returns for its eighth year, and it's bigger than ever. The festival runs from Friday evening right through to Sunday night, across four outdoor stages. Headlining on Saturday is the folk band Marram Grass, who topped the charts last spring. A weekend pass costs forty-five dollars, but if you book before Thursday you'll save ten dollars. Children under twelve go free. Organisers are encouraging visitors to use public transport, as parking near the site is very limited. There'll be a shuttle bus from the central station every fifteen minutes. Food stalls will offer everything from wood-fired pizza to vegan curries, and this year there's a dedicated area for local craft makers. Don't forget to bring a raincoat, because the forecast is, shall we say, typically changeable. For the full line-up and to buy tickets, visit the festival website. That's all from me; enjoy your weekend.

How can visitors save ten dollars?

questions.q: The shuttle bus runs from the central station every ________ minutes.

speaking.q: Let's discuss music and emotion. Why do you think music has such a strong effect on people's feelings? Should film and advertising be allowed to use music deliberately to influence how audiences feel, and are there any situations where this might be unfair or manipulative?

The table below shows the number of visitors (in thousands) attending four sections of a music festival over three days. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Section | Friday | Saturday | Sunday

Composers exploit cultural associations.

A recurring tune marks the hero's entrance.
The musical cue must arrive on time.
The synchronisation requires painstaking work.

reading.title: The Return of the Tram
reading.passage:
For much of the twentieth century, the tram was regarded as a relic of a slower age. In city after city, the rails that had once carried streetcars through busy centres were torn up and paved over, dismissed as obstacles to the motor car. By the 1960s many planners assumed the tram had gone for good, replaced by buses that could be rerouted at will and by private vehicles that promised freedom and convenience.

Yet over the past three decades the tram has staged a remarkable comeback. Cities from Manchester to Melbourne have invested heavily in new networks, often branded as light rail to distance them from their old-fashioned predecessors. The reasons are partly practical. A single tram can carry far more passengers than a bus, and because it runs on a fixed, segregated track, it is largely immune to the congestion that clogs ordinary roads. Its electric motor produces no exhaust at street level, a significant advantage in centres struggling with air quality.

There is also a subtler argument, one that planners are increasingly willing to make openly. Unlike a bus route, which can be quietly withdrawn, a tram line is a visible, permanent commitment. Property developers and residents alike treat the presence of rails as a guarantee that an area will remain well connected, and investment tends to follow. Studies in several European cities suggest that land values rise measurably along new tram corridors, although researchers caution that the tram may simply be attracted to districts already poised for growth.

Critics, however, point to the enormous upfront cost of construction, which can run to many millions per kilometre. Laying track, diverting utilities and rebuilding streets is far more expensive than painting a bus lane, and the disruption during construction can be considerable. Supporters reply that the comparison is misleading: a tram system, once built, lasts for generations and its operating costs per passenger are comparatively low. Whether a given city should choose rails or rubber tyres, then, depends less on nostalgia than on a sober assessment of demand, geography and long-term ambition.

questions.q: In the 1960s, many planners expected the tram to disappear permanently.
New tram networks are often branded as ________ to separate them from older systems.
Why is a tram less affected by congestion than a bus?
options: it travels at a higher speed
it runs on a fixed, segregated track

questions.q: Researchers are certain that tram lines are the direct cause of rising land values.
Trams in Melbourne are more profitable than those in Manchester.
According to supporters, why is comparing tram and bus costs misleading?
options: buses are actually more expensive to buy
a tram lasts for generations with low operating costs
trams need no maintenance once built

questions.q: A tram line is described as a visible, permanent ________ that encourages investment.
The writer argues that the choice between tram and bus should rest on nostalgia.

listening.title: Enquiry: Joining a City Cycle-Hire Scheme
listening.script:
ASSISTANT: Good afternoon, Riverside Cycle Hire. How can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to register for the bike-sharing scheme. What do I need to do?

ASSISTANT: Of course. First, can I take your full name?

CALLER: It's Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.

ASSISTANT: Lovely. And there are three membership options. The pay-as-you-go is one pound twenty per ride. The monthly pass is fifteen pounds, and the annual pass works out cheaper at ninety pounds.

CALLER: I cycle most days, so the annual one sounds best.

ASSISTANT: Good choice. With the annual pass the first thirty minutes of every journey are free. After that it's twenty pence per minute. To collect a bike, you tap your member card at the docking station on Bridge Street.

CALLER: And if a bike has a flat tyre or a fault?

ASSISTANT: Just press the red button on the docking point to report it, and never leave a faulty bike at the roadside. One more thing: helmets aren't provided, so we strongly recommend bringing your own.

CALLER: Understood. How soon can I start?

ASSISTANT: Your card will be activated within twenty-four hours of payment.

Which option does the caller choose?

speaking.q: Let's talk about how you travel. How do you usually get around your city or town? Do you prefer public transport or travelling by car, and why?

Transport | 2010 | 2020
Bus | 420 | 380
Underground | 510 | 640
Tram | 90 | 250

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

The tram avoids the congestion on ordinary roads.
It runs on a segregated track.

Light rail distances itself from its predecessors.

reading.title: The Fifteen-Minute City
reading.passage:
In recent years a deceptively simple idea has captured the imagination of urban planners around the world: the fifteen-minute city. The concept, popularised by a French academic, holds that residents should be able to reach most of their daily needs — shops, schools, workplaces, parks and clinics — within a quarter of an hour on foot or by bicycle. Rather than designing cities around the car, with vast distances between home and everything else, the approach seeks to weave essential services back into every neighbourhood.

The appeal is easy to understand. A city of short journeys promises cleaner air, since fewer people need to drive, and livelier streets, since more activity takes place locally. It also offers a measure of resilience: when a sudden crisis confines people to their immediate area, a well-stocked neighbourhood is far less disruptive than a sprawling one. Advocates argue that proximity, not speed, should be the true measure of a successful city.

Translating the idea into reality, however, has proved harder than its elegant slogan suggests. Many neighbourhoods, particularly those built in the second half of the twentieth century, were deliberately zoned to separate housing from commerce and industry. Retrofitting them with local shops and services requires changes to planning law, patient investment and, often, the cooperation of landowners with little incentive to alter what already earns them money.

There is also a risk that the model benefits some residents far more than others. Wealthier districts, already supplied with cafes and clinics, may find the transition straightforward, while poorer areas lack the commercial demand to sustain new amenities. Without careful intervention, critics warn, the fifteen-minute city could deepen the very inequalities it claims to ease. Supporters counter that this is an argument for thoughtful policy, not for abandoning the goal. The question is not whether shorter journeys are desirable, but whether cities have the political will to redistribute opportunity street by street.

questions.q: The fifteen-minute city aims to let residents reach daily needs within a ________ of an hour.
The concept was made popular by a French academic.
What do advocates say should be the true measure of a successful city?
Neighbourhoods built after about 1950 often separated housing from commerce.
Most landowners are eager to convert their buildings into local shops.
The French academic later moved to another country to advise its government.
What concern do critics raise about the model?
A well-stocked neighbourhood offers a measure of ________ during a crisis.

listening.title: Campus Tour: Getting Around the New Campus
listening.script:
GUIDE: Welcome, everyone. I'll quickly explain how to get around our new campus, because it's larger than the old site. The good news is that almost everything is within a ten-minute walk.

Let's start at the main gate, where you're standing now. Straight ahead is the library, which is open until eleven at night during term. To your left is the science block, and just behind it you'll find the bike racks — there's space for two hundred bicycles, and they're covered, so your bike stays dry.

Now, if walking isn't for you, there's a free shuttle bus that loops the campus every twelve minutes. It stops at four points: the gate, the library, the sports centre and the halls of residence. Look for the bright orange signs.

A word of warning: cars aren't allowed in the central area between eight in the morning and six in the evening, so don't plan to drive to lectures. There is a car park, but it's a fifteen-minute walk from the centre, near the east entrance.

Finally, download our campus app — it shows the shuttle in real time and flags any path closures. Any questions before we set off?

questions.q: How far is almost everything on the new campus?
During term, the library is open until ________ at night.
The covered bike racks have space for ________ bicycles.
The free shuttle bus loops the campus every ________ minutes.
Cars are allowed in the central area at all times of day.
The campus ________ shows the shuttle in real time.

speaking.q:
Describe a neighbourhood you would like to live in. You should say:
- where it is and what it looks like
- what facilities and services it has
- how easy it is to get around
and explain why you would like to live there.

writing.q: Some people believe that cities should be redesigned so that residents can reach all their daily needs on foot or by bicycle, even if this restricts the use of cars. Others argue that such restrictions are unrealistic and unfair. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.
