reading.title: Designing Streets for People
reading.passage:
For most of the past century, the design of urban streets was guided by a single overriding goal: to move motor vehicles as quickly and smoothly as possible. Engineers widened roads, removed obstacles and timed traffic lights to keep cars flowing. Pedestrians, by contrast, were corralled onto narrow pavements, channelled towards distant crossings and, in some cases, banished underground or onto bridges so that they would not interrupt the traffic below. The street, once a shared social space, became a corridor for machines.

A growing number of designers now regard this legacy as a mistake. They argue that streets are not merely conduits for movement but places where people meet, trade and linger. The Dutch concept of the woonerf, or living yard, pioneered this rethinking. In such schemes, the strict separation of cars and people is deliberately blurred: kerbs are lowered, signs are removed and the carriageway is paved like a courtyard. Drivers, uncertain of their priority, slow down and proceed with caution. Counter-intuitively, removing the apparatus of safety can make streets safer.

Evidence from a number of trials supports the claim. When a busy junction in one English town stripped out its traffic signals and railings, vehicle speeds fell and the number of collisions dropped, even as the volume of traffic remained much the same. Drivers, it seems, rely on eye contact and judgement when the rules are ambiguous, and behave more carefully as a result. Critics, however, caution that such schemes can unsettle vulnerable pedestrians, including the elderly and people with impaired sight, who may struggle without the certainty of a marked crossing.

The broader lesson is that street design is never neutral. Every kerb, sign and signal sends a message about who the street is for. Reclaiming streets for people does not mean banishing the car altogether, but it does mean treating walking, cycling and simply being present as legitimate uses of public space, rather than as inconveniences to be managed.

questions.q: For most of the past century, street design prioritised the movement of motor vehicles.
The Dutch concept that pioneered the rethinking is called the ________.
Why do drivers slow down in a woonerf scheme?
options: the speed limit is strictly enforced
they are uncertain of their priority
the roads are too narrow for cars

questions.q: In the English trial, the volume of traffic fell sharply after the changes.
What concern do critics raise about removing signals and railings?
options: it makes journeys slower for everyone
it costs too much to install

questions.q: The English town that ran the trial later removed all traffic signals across the whole town.
The writer believes reclaiming streets means banning cars completely.
The writer argues that street design is never ________.

listening.title: Lecture: Why Pedestrians Choose the Routes They Do
listening.script:
LECTURER: This morning I want to examine a question that sounds trivial but isn't: why do people walk the way they do? Understanding pedestrian behaviour is essential if we want to design cities that encourage walking.

The first principle is that people seek the shortest path. You'll have seen the evidence yourself — those worn lines across a lawn where walkers have ignored the paved route. Planners call these desire lines, and ignoring them is a common and costly mistake.

The second factor is comfort. Pedestrians avoid steep gradients, busy crossings and exposed, windswept spaces. A slightly longer route that feels pleasant will often beat a direct one that feels hostile. So shade, shelter and greenery matter enormously.

Third, there's the question of perceived safety. People walk more readily where there are other people, well-lit streets and active shopfronts. An empty underpass, however direct, is often avoided after dark.

The practical conclusion is straightforward. If we want people to walk, we shouldn't simply tell them to. We should remove the obstacles, follow the desire lines, and make the walking experience genuinely attractive. Behaviour follows design, not instruction.

questions.q: The first principle is that people seek the ________ path.
Worn lines across a lawn are called ________ by planners.
According to the lecturer, what often beats a direct but hostile route?
options: a longer route that feels pleasant
a route with more traffic lights

questions.q: People walk more readily where streets are well-lit and shopfronts are ________.
An empty underpass is often avoided after dark even though it is direct.
The lecturer concludes that behaviour follows design, not ________.

speaking.q: Let's discuss public spaces in cities. Some people say that streets should be designed mainly for pedestrians rather than for cars. To what extent do you agree, and how do you think the way a street is designed affects the way people use it?

writing.q:
The table below shows the average number of road traffic collisions per year on streets in one city, before and after they were redesigned to slow traffic.

Street type | Before redesign | After redesign
Main roads | 48 | 41
Residential streets | 36 | 14
Shopping streets | 27 | 12
School zones | 19 | 5

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: A single overriding goal guided design.
Pedestrians were corralled onto narrow pavements.
Walkers were channelled towards distant crossings.
Pedestrians were banished underground.
Streets are not merely conduits for movement.
Streets are places where people linger.
The separation of cars and people is blurred.
Counter-intuitively, fewer signs made streets safer.
Drivers behave carefully when rules are ambiguous.
Such schemes can unsettle vulnerable people.
People with impaired sight may struggle.
Walking is a legitimate use of public space.
Pedestrians avoid steep gradients.

reading.title: The Promise and Peril of the Driverless Car
reading.passage:
Few technologies have been promised so confidently, and delivered so cautiously, as the autonomous vehicle. For more than a decade, engineers have assured the public that cars capable of driving themselves were only a few years away. The vision is undeniably attractive. A fleet of self-driving vehicles, summoned by an app and shared rather than owned, could in principle slash the number of cars on the road, free up the vast urban acreage devoted to parking, and spare us the tedium and danger of driving ourselves.

The safety argument is especially compelling. The overwhelming majority of road accidents are caused by human error — by distraction, fatigue or poor judgement. A machine that never tires, never looks at its phone and reacts in milliseconds ought, in theory, to be far safer than any human driver. Manufacturers point to this potential as the moral case for pressing ahead.

Reality, however, has proved stubborn. Teaching a vehicle to handle a motorway in clear weather is one thing; teaching it to cope with a child darting between parked cars, a cyclist signalling ambiguously or a police officer waving traffic through a malfunctioning junction is quite another. These rare, messy situations — what engineers call edge cases — are precisely where human intuition still outperforms the machine. Each one must be anticipated and programmed for, and the list is effectively endless.

There are thornier questions, too. When an accident is unavoidable, how should a car be programmed to behave, and who bears the blame when it errs — the owner, the manufacturer or the software? Sceptics also warn of an unintended consequence: if travelling by car becomes effortless, people may simply travel more, clogging roads that planners had hoped to empty. The driverless car, in other words, may solve some problems only by creating others. Its arrival, when it comes, is likely to reshape cities in ways that are difficult to predict and harder still to reverse.

questions.q: What is the main safety argument for autonomous vehicles?
options: they travel more slowly than human drivers
most accidents are caused by human error

questions.q: Rare, difficult situations are called ________ by engineers.
Driving on a motorway in clear weather is harder for a machine than handling a darting child.
The writer says the list of edge cases is effectively endless.
Most countries have already passed laws deciding who is to blame when a driverless car crashes.
What unintended consequence do sceptics warn about?
options: people may travel more, clogging the roads

questions.q: Shared self-driving vehicles could free up urban land currently used for ________.
The writer is confident that driverless cars will solve city problems without creating new ones.

listening.title: Discussion: Planning a Group Trip Without a Car
listening.script:
TOM: So, we need to get the whole group to the conference in Greenfield without renting a car. Any ideas?

MAYA: The direct train is the easiest. It leaves Central Station at nine fifteen and gets in around eleven.

TOM: That's nearly two hours. Isn't there something faster?

MAYA: There's an express coach, but it only runs on weekdays, and the conference is on a Saturday.

TOM: Right, so the coach is out. What about cost? The train tickets aren't cheap.

MAYA: If we book as a group of ten or more, we get thirty percent off. So it's about twenty-one dollars each instead of thirty.

TOM: That's worth it. And from the station to the venue?

MAYA: It's a fifteen-minute walk, or there's a free shuttle the conference runs from the station entrance.

TOM: Let's tell people about the shuttle — not everyone will want to walk with luggage. One last thing: should we book today?

MAYA: Definitely. The group discount only applies if we reserve at least a week in advance, and we're cutting it close.

questions.q: The direct train leaves Central Station at ________.
Why can't the group take the express coach?
A group of ten or more gets ________ percent off.
With the discount, each ticket costs about ________ dollars.
How does the group decide to travel from the station to the venue?
The group discount applies only if they reserve at least a ________ in advance.

speaking.q: Let's talk about technology and travel. Do you use any apps or technology to help you travel? Do you think self-driving cars would be useful in your country, and why?

writing.q: Some people believe that self-driving cars will make cities safer and more efficient. Others worry that they will create new problems, such as more traffic and unclear responsibility for accidents. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Write at least 250 words.

Cars can be summoned by an app.
It spares us the tedium of driving.
The safety argument is compelling.
Accidents are often caused by fatigue.
Reality has proved stubborn.

reading.title: Cooling the Concrete City
reading.passage:
Anyone who has crossed a city on a hot summer afternoon knows that towns and cities are warmer than the countryside that surrounds them. This phenomenon, known as the urban heat island, can raise night-time temperatures in a dense city centre by several degrees compared with nearby fields. As heatwaves grow more frequent and severe, this once-academic curiosity has become a pressing matter of public health.

The causes are well understood. Concrete, asphalt and brick absorb the sun's energy during the day and release it slowly at night, so cities never fully cool down. Dark surfaces, particularly tarmac roads and flat roofs, soak up far more heat than the pale, vegetated landscapes they replaced. Meanwhile, the very engines of urban life — cars, air conditioners, factories — pump out waste heat of their own, and the tall, closely packed buildings trap it, restricting the breeze that might otherwise carry it away.

Fortunately, the same understanding points towards remedies. The most celebrated is simply to plant trees. A mature tree shades the ground beneath it and cools the surrounding air through the evaporation of water from its leaves, a process that can lower local temperatures noticeably on a hot day. Parks act as reservoirs of cool air that drift into nearby streets after dark. Lighter-coloured, reflective roofs and pavements bounce sunlight back into the sky rather than absorbing it, and some cities now require new flat roofs to be painted white or planted with vegetation.

None of these measures is a complete solution on its own, and some carry hidden costs: thirsty trees, for instance, can strain water supplies in already dry regions. Yet the broader principle is sound. By weaving greenery and reflective surfaces back into the urban fabric, planners can take the edge off the worst heat without abandoning the density that makes cities efficient. The challenge, as ever, is to act before the next heatwave rather than after it.

questions.q: The phenomenon by which cities are warmer than the countryside is called the urban ________.
Concrete and asphalt release the heat they absorb very quickly at night.
How does a mature tree help cool the air?
options: by absorbing waste heat from cars
by shading the ground and evaporating water from its leaves
by reflecting sunlight back into the sky
by trapping the breeze between buildings

questions.q: Some cities now require new flat roofs to be white or planted.
Planting trees can sometimes strain water supplies in dry regions.
White roofs have been shown to reduce a building's electricity bills more than green roofs.
What is the writer's overall view of the remedies described?
options: each one alone fully solves the problem
together they can ease the worst heat without losing density
they should replace dense cities entirely

questions.q: Parks act as reservoirs of cool air that drift into nearby ________ after dark.

listening.title: Lecture: Green Roofs in the Modern City
listening.script:
LECTURER: In today's session we'll look at green roofs — roofs that are partly or wholly covered with growing plants — and why so many cities are now encouraging them.

Let's start with the benefits, of which there are several. The most obvious is temperature. A layer of soil and vegetation insulates the building beneath, keeping it cooler in summer and warmer in winter, which cuts energy bills considerably.

The second benefit concerns water. A green roof can absorb a large share of the rain that falls on it — often around half — releasing it slowly instead of sending it rushing into the drains. That eases the pressure on the city's drainage during a storm.

Third, there's biodiversity. Even a small roof garden can provide a habitat for insects and birds in a place where green space is scarce.

Now, the drawbacks. Green roofs are heavier than ordinary ones, so the building must be strong enough to bear the weight. They also cost more to install, though the savings on energy usually repay that over time. Maintenance is modest but not zero — the plants need occasional care, especially in the first year. We'll examine a case study from Berlin after the break.

questions.q: What is described as the most obvious benefit of a green roof?
A green roof can absorb around ________ of the rain that falls on it.
Green roofs can provide a ________ for insects and birds.
Green roofs are lighter than ordinary roofs.
Maintenance of a green roof is needed especially in the first year.
The lecturer will examine a case study from ________ after the break.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when the weather affected how you travelled or what you did in your city. You should say:
- what the weather was like
- where you were going or what you planned to do
- how the weather changed your plans
and explain how you felt about it.

writing.q:
The table below shows the average summer afternoon temperature (in degrees Celsius) recorded in four areas of one city, and the percentage of each area covered by trees and parks.

Area | Avg temperature (°C) | Green cover (%)
City centre | 34 | 8
Inner suburbs | 31 | 22
Outer suburbs | 29 | 35
Riverside park | 27 | 60

Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: The urban heat island is a well-known phenomenon.
It has become a pressing matter of public health.
Concrete absorbs the sun's energy.
Pale, vegetated landscapes were replaced.
Trees cool the air through evaporation.
Parks act as reservoirs of cool air.
Reflective roofs bounce sunlight away.
The same understanding points to remedies.
Thirsty trees can strain water supplies.
Weaving greenery into the urban fabric.
Soil and vegetation insulate the building.
Roof gardens support biodiversity.

reading.title: The Return of the Monsoon
reading.passage:
Across much of South Asia, the seasonal monsoon is far more than a weather event; it is the foundation of an entire way of life. For roughly four months each year, moisture-laden winds sweep inland from the Indian Ocean, delivering the heavy rains on which hundreds of millions of farmers depend. A timely monsoon can mean abundant harvests of rice and wheat, while a delayed or weak one can push rural communities toward hardship and debt.

The mechanics of the monsoon are deceptively simple. During the hottest months, the land warms faster than the surrounding sea. The heated air over the continent rises, creating an area of low pressure that draws in cooler, wetter air from the ocean. As this damp air is forced upward over hills and mountains, it cools, and the moisture it carries condenses into rain. When the land cools again in autumn, the pattern reverses, and the rains retreat.

Forecasting the monsoon has long challenged scientists. Although meteorologists can now predict its general arrival within a week or two, the precise distribution of rainfall remains stubbornly uncertain. A region may receive double its usual rainfall while a neighbouring district stays dry. Some researchers argue that warming oceans are making these patterns increasingly erratic, with rains arriving in shorter, more violent bursts rather than steady showers.

Governments have responded by investing in reservoirs, early-warning systems, and drought-resistant crops. Yet many experts insist that the most effective protection lies in supporting small farmers directly, through insurance schemes and flexible credit. Whatever the strategy, the monsoon remains a powerful reminder that human prosperity is still tightly bound to the rhythms of the atmosphere.

questions.q: The monsoon affects the livelihoods of hundreds of millions of farmers.
During the hottest months, the sea warms more quickly than the land.
Scientists can now predict exactly where rain will fall during the monsoon.
The government insurance schemes mentioned have reduced farmer debt in the past decade.
What creates the low-pressure area that draws in ocean air?
options: Heated air rising over the land

questions.q: Some researchers believe warming oceans are causing rain to arrive in
Damp air cools and its moisture ________ into rain as it is forced upward.
Many experts say the best protection lies in supporting small ________ directly.

listening.title: Booking a Weather Station Tour
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Hillcrest Weather Station, how can I help?

VISITOR: Hi, I'd like to book a guided tour for a school group, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. How many students are coming?

VISITOR: There'll be twenty-eight students and three teachers.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. Our standard tour lasts about ninety minutes. It starts with the instrument garden, where we show the rain gauges and thermometers, and then moves to the forecasting room.

VISITOR: That sounds perfect. What's the cost?

RECEPTIONIST: For school groups it's four pounds per student, and teachers go free.

VISITOR: Great. Could we come on the fifteenth of July?

RECEPTIONIST: Let me check. Yes, the morning slot at ten o'clock is available. I'd recommend you arrive about fifteen minutes early.

VISITOR: Will the children need anything special?

RECEPTIONIST: Just sensible shoes, as part of the tour is outdoors. And if it rains, we provide umbrellas.

VISITOR: Wonderful. Could I give you my name to confirm? It's Davies, D-A-V-I-E-S.

RECEPTIONIST: Perfect, Ms Davies. You're all booked for the fifteenth.

questions.q: The group consists of ________ students.
The standard tour lasts about ________ minutes.
Where does the tour begin?
The cost is ________ pounds per student.
The tour is booked for the ________ of July.
What should the children bring?

speaking.q: Let's talk about weather. What kind of weather do you enjoy most, and why? Do you prefer hot or cold seasons? How does the weather affect your mood or daily plans?

The table below shows the average monthly rainfall (in millimetres) in three cities over a typical year. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Average monthly rainfall (mm):
Month | Mumbai | London | Cairo
January | 2 | 55 | 5
April | 1 | 44 | 1
July | 840 | 45 | 0
October | 60 | 68 | 1

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Moisture-laden winds blew in from the sea before the storm.
The early rains led to an abundant harvest that year.

The puzzle looked deceptively simple but took hours.

reading.title: Cities That Make Their Own Heat
reading.passage:
On a still summer evening, the centre of a large city can feel several degrees warmer than the open countryside surrounding it. This phenomenon, known as the urban heat island effect, has been recognised for more than a century, yet it is only now attracting serious attention as global temperatures climb and city populations swell.

The causes are varied but interconnected. Dark surfaces such as asphalt roads and tar roofs absorb solar radiation during the day and release it slowly after sunset. Tall buildings trap warm air in narrow streets and block cooling breezes. Meanwhile, the energy used by vehicles, factories, and air conditioners adds further heat directly to the atmosphere. The result is a dome of warmth that lingers long after rural areas have cooled.

The consequences are not merely uncomfortable. Elevated night-time temperatures prevent residents from recovering between hot days, raising the risk of heat-related illness, particularly among the elderly and the very young. Higher temperatures also increase the demand for air conditioning, which in turn generates more heat and consumes more electricity, creating a troubling cycle.

Fortunately, the heat island effect can be softened. Planting trees along streets provides shade and cools the air through evaporation. Painting roofs in pale, reflective colours can lower indoor temperatures dramatically. Some cities have introduced green roofs covered in vegetation, while others have widened parks and restored rivers to bring cooler air into dense districts.

Urban planners increasingly view these measures not as luxuries but as essential adaptations. As climate scientists warn of more frequent heatwaves, the way cities are designed may prove as important to public health as any medical intervention.

questions.q: The urban heat island effect was only discovered in recent years.
Dark surfaces release absorbed heat slowly after sunset.
Air conditioning use both consumes electricity and adds heat to the air.
Green roofs are more effective than reflective roofs at cooling buildings.
Why are elevated night-time temperatures dangerous?
options: They prevent residents from recovering between hot days

questions.q: How do street trees help cool the air?
options: By providing shade and cooling through evaporation

questions.q: Tall buildings can trap warm air and block cooling ________.
Urban planners now see cooling measures as essential ________ rather than luxuries.

listening.title: A Lecture on City Climate
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today I want to look at why our cities are heating up faster than the land around them. Now, the single biggest factor is surface material. Concrete and asphalt soak up sunlight and store it as heat. A dark road can be up to twenty degrees hotter than the air above it on a sunny day.

The second factor is the loss of vegetation. When we replace fields and forests with buildings, we lose the natural cooling that plants provide through evaporation. A single mature tree can have the cooling power of several air conditioners.

Third, there's waste heat. Every car engine, every factory, every air conditioner pumps heat straight into the streets.

So what can we do? The cheapest solution is paint. Coating roofs white can cut indoor temperatures by around five degrees. Planting trees is slower but far more powerful in the long run. And finally, designing wider streets allows the heat to escape at night. Right, let's look at some city case studies.

questions.q: What does the lecturer call the single biggest factor?
A dark road can be up to ________ degrees hotter than the air above it.
Plants provide natural cooling through ________.
What is described as the cheapest solution?
Coating roofs white can cut indoor temperatures by around ________ degrees.
What allows heat to escape at night?

speaking.q:
Describe a time when extreme weather affected your plans. You should say:
- what the weather was like
- what you had originally planned to do
- how you changed your plans
and explain how you felt about the situation.

writing.q:
Some people believe that governments should focus on reducing the energy used in cities to fight climate change, while others think individual citizens are responsible for making changes. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: The heat island is a well-studied phenomenon.
City populations continue to swell each year.
The causes are varied but interconnected.
Dark roofs absorb solar radiation all day.
Asphalt absorbs solar radiation during the day.
A dome of warmth lingers over the city at night.

reading.title: Reading the Rings of Ancient Trees
reading.passage:
Long before thermometers and rain gauges existed, the world's climate was quietly recording itself in the trunks of trees. Each year, a living tree adds a new layer of wood beneath its bark, forming a ring. In a warm, wet season the ring grows wide; in a cold or dry year it remains narrow. By studying these patterns, scientists in a field called dendrochronology can reconstruct centuries of past weather with remarkable precision.

The technique relies on matching the ring patterns of many trees. A single tree may live only a few hundred years, but by overlapping the records of younger trees with those of older, sometimes long-dead, specimens, researchers can build a continuous timeline stretching back thousands of years. Wood preserved in old buildings, bogs, and riverbeds extends the record even further.

Tree rings reveal more than temperature and rainfall. A scar within the wood may mark the year of a forest fire, while a sudden burst of narrow rings can indicate a volcanic eruption that dimmed the sky and chilled the climate. Some rings even capture the chemical traces of distant events, allowing scientists to date eruptions that occurred long before written history.

This natural archive has practical value today. By comparing modern climate data with the long record stored in trees, researchers can judge whether recent warming falls within the range of natural variation or represents something genuinely unusual. The humble tree ring, it turns out, offers one of the clearest windows we have into the climate of the deep past.

questions.q: A tree ring grows wider during a warm, wet season.
Dendrochronology can only use trees that are still alive.
Wood found in old buildings can help extend the climate record.
Most volcanic eruptions in history have been dated using tree rings.
What does a sudden burst of narrow rings suggest?
Why is the tree-ring record valuable today?
options: It helps judge whether recent warming is unusual

questions.q: The field that studies tree rings is called ________.
A ________ within the wood may mark the year of a forest fire.

listening.title: A Conversation About a Research Trip
listening.script:
STUDENT A: Hey, are you joining the dendrochronology field trip next month?

STUDENT B: I think so. Where exactly are we going?

STUDENT A: To the Caldwell Forest, up in the mountains. We leave on the twelfth and come back three days later.

STUDENT B: Nice. What do we actually do there?

STUDENT A: We take core samples from old pine trees using a special drill. It's hollow, so it removes a thin cylinder of wood without harming the tree.

STUDENT B: Clever. Do we need to bring anything?

STUDENT A: Warm clothes, definitely. It can drop to about minus five at night. Oh, and waterproof boots.

STUDENT B: Got it. How many samples do we collect?

STUDENT A: The professor wants at least forty per group, so we'll be busy.

STUDENT B: And where do we stay?

STUDENT A: There's a research cabin near the river. It's basic, but it has heating.

STUDENT B: Sounds great. I'll sign up tomorrow.

questions.q: The group leaves on the ________ of the month.
How long is the trip?
Samples are taken using a special hollow ________.
Temperatures can drop to about minus ________ at night.
Each group must collect at least ________ samples.
Where will the students stay?
options: A research cabin near the river

speaking.q: Let's discuss the natural world. Why do you think it is important to study the climate of the distant past? How might knowledge of past climates influence the decisions societies make today? Some argue that scientific research is too expensive; how would you respond?

writing.q:
The table below shows the number of research samples collected by four university teams during a five-year forest study. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Number of tree-core samples collected:
Year | Team A | Team B | Team C | Team D
Year 1 | 120 | 90 | 60 | 40
Year 2 | 150 | 110 | 80 | 55
Year 3 | 200 | 130 | 95 | 70
Year 4 | 210 | 160 | 100 | 90
Year 5 | 230 | 180 | 140 | 120

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Scientists can reconstruct past climates from tree rings.
The dates can be fixed with remarkable precision.
Researchers overlap records from many trees.
An ancient specimen extended the timeline.
They built a continuous timeline of climate.
Wood preserved in bogs lasts for centuries.
A scar in the wood marks an old fire.
A volcanic eruption chilled the climate that year.
Ash dimmed the sky for months.
Trees form a natural archive of the climate.

The humble tree ring is a powerful tool.

reading.title: The Mystery of the Jet Stream
reading.passage:
High above our heads, far beyond the reach of clouds, ribbons of fast-moving air circle the planet. These powerful currents, known as jet streams, race from west to east at speeds that can exceed two hundred kilometres per hour. Though invisible from the ground, they exert an enormous influence over the weather experienced by billions of people, steering storms, separating warm air from cold, and shaping the seasons.

Jet streams form along the boundaries between air masses of different temperatures. The greater the temperature contrast, the faster the stream flows. In winter, when the difference between polar and tropical air is at its sharpest, the jet stream strengthens and shifts toward the equator, dragging cold weather with it. In summer, as the contrast eases, it weakens and retreats toward the poles.

For decades the jet stream was viewed as a relatively stable and predictable feature. Recently, however, some scientists have proposed that it is becoming wavier and slower. They suggest that as the Arctic warms faster than the rest of the planet, the temperature contrast that drives the stream is shrinking. A sluggish, meandering jet stream, they argue, can become stuck in place, locking regions into long spells of the same weather, whether that means weeks of rain or stubborn drought.

The theory remains contested. Critics point out that the atmosphere is enormously complex and that natural variability could explain the observed changes. What is not disputed is the jet stream's importance. Whether calm or chaotic, these high-altitude rivers of air will continue to govern much of the weather that shapes daily life across the northern hemisphere.

questions.q: Jet streams flow from west to east.
A larger temperature contrast makes the jet stream flow more slowly.
In summer the jet stream moves toward the poles.
Most climate scientists now agree the jet stream is becoming wavier.
According to some scientists, why might the jet stream be slowing?
options: The Arctic is warming faster than the rest of the planet

questions.q: What can a sluggish, meandering jet stream cause?
options: Regions stuck in long spells of the same weather

questions.q: Jet streams can exceed two hundred ________ per hour.
Jet streams form along the boundaries between air ________ of different temperatures.

listening.title: A Radio Weather Briefing
listening.script:
PRESENTER: And now over to our meteorologist, Sarah, for the week ahead. Sarah, what can we expect?

SARAH: Thanks, Tom. Well, it's all about the jet stream this week. At the moment it's sitting well to the north of us, which means we're staying in mild, settled air. Daytime temperatures will reach around eighteen degrees, which is unusually warm for October.

PRESENTER: That sounds pleasant. Will it last?

SARAH: Not quite. By Thursday the jet stream dips southward, dragging cooler air down from the north. Expect temperatures to fall to about eleven degrees, with showers arriving on Friday.

PRESENTER: Any warnings for the weekend?

SARAH: Yes, there's a chance of strong winds on Saturday, gusting up to sixty kilometres per hour along the coast. Inland areas should stay calmer. I'd advise anyone planning a coastal walk to check the forecast again on Saturday morning.

PRESENTER: Good advice. Thanks, Sarah.

SARAH: My pleasure, Tom.

questions.q: Where is the jet stream sitting at the moment?
Daytime temperatures will reach around ________ degrees.
What happens by Thursday?
Temperatures are expected to fall to about ________ degrees.
Winds may gust up to ________ kilometres per hour on the coast.
What does Sarah advise coastal walkers to do?
options: Check the forecast again on Saturday morning

speaking.q: Let's talk about your daily routine and the weather. Do you check the weather forecast often? How reliable do you find weather forecasts where you live? Has a forecast ever caused you to change your plans?

writing.q:
Modern technology now allows scientists to forecast the weather more accurately than ever before. Some people think this benefits society greatly, while others believe people rely on forecasts too much. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Ribbons of fast air circle the planet.
Jet streams exert a huge influence on weather.
The stream steers storms across the ocean.
Jet streams form along the boundary of air masses.
A sharp temperature contrast speeds the stream.
A sluggish jet stream gets stuck in place.

These high-altitude rivers of air move quickly.

reading.title: When the Sea Remembers the Storm
reading.passage:
Coastal communities have always lived with the threat of storms, but in recent decades the danger has shifted in character. Rather than facing rarer, single catastrophes, many low-lying towns now confront a slow accumulation of smaller floods, driven by a gradually rising sea. The ocean, scientists say, has begun to remember every storm, holding onto water that once drained away.

The rise itself stems from two main processes. As the planet warms, vast quantities of ice locked in glaciers and ice sheets melt and flow into the sea. At the same time, seawater expands as it heats, occupying more space even without any new water added. Together these forces have lifted the global average sea level by around twenty centimetres over the past century, with the pace quickening in recent years.

For coastal cities, even a modest rise can be transformative. A higher baseline means that ordinary high tides, once harmless, now spill over defences during storms. So-called nuisance flooding, in which streets fill with water on otherwise calm days, has become routine in some ports. The cost of repeated minor damage can rival that of a single dramatic disaster.

Responses vary widely. Wealthier cities have built sea walls, raised roads, and installed enormous pumps. Others have chosen retreat, relocating homes and infrastructure to higher ground. A growing number are experimenting with nature itself, restoring marshes and oyster reefs that absorb wave energy. There is no universal solution, but one principle is becoming clear: ignoring the rising sea is the costliest option of all.

questions.q: Many low-lying towns now face frequent smaller floods rather than only rare large catastrophes.
Seawater contracts as it heats.
Global average sea level rose by about twenty centimetres over the past century.
Sea walls have proven more effective than restored marshes at stopping floods.
What is 'nuisance flooding'?
options: Streets filling with water on otherwise calm days

questions.q: Which response involves moving homes to higher ground?
Sea level rise stems partly from melting glaciers and ice ________.
Restored marshes and oyster reefs can absorb wave ________.

listening.title: A Talk on Coastal Defences
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Thank you all for coming this evening. I'm here to talk about how our town can prepare for rising seas. Now, you might think the answer is simply to build a bigger wall. But the truth is more interesting.

Let me start with the numbers. Over the last fifty years, the sea here has risen by about fifteen centimetres. That may not sound like much, but it has tripled the number of flood days each year.

There are three options on the table. The first is a concrete sea wall, which would cost around twelve million dollars and last about forty years. The second is restoring the natural wetlands to the south, which is cheaper and supports wildlife, but takes longer to work. The third option is a system of movable barriers that close only during storms.

The council will vote next month, on the eighth. I'd encourage every resident to attend the meeting and share your views. Right, are there any questions?

questions.q: The sea here has risen by about ________ centimetres over fifty years.
What effect has the rise had on flood days?
A concrete sea wall would cost around ________ million dollars.
What advantage does restoring wetlands have?
options: It is cheaper and supports wildlife

questions.q: The council will vote on the ________ of next month.
What does the third option involve?
options: Movable barriers that close during storms

speaking.q: Let's consider coastal change. Whose responsibility should it be to protect communities from rising seas: national governments, local councils, or residents themselves? Do you think people should be encouraged to move away from high-risk areas? How might societies balance the cost of protection against other needs?

writing.q:
The table below shows the estimated cost and expected lifespan of four flood-defence options considered by a coastal town. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Flood-defence options:
Option | Cost (million $) | Lifespan (years) | Jobs created
Concrete sea wall | 12 | 40 | 30
Movable barriers | 18 | 50 | 45
Restored wetlands | 6 | 60 | 20
Raised roads | 9 | 35 | 25

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: A slow accumulation of small floods harms the town.
Water that once drained away now lingers.
The rise stems from two main processes.
Seawater expands as it heats.
A higher baseline means tides spill over walls.
Even a modest rise can be transformative.
The rise has a transformative effect on the city.

Marshes absorb wave energy.

reading.title: The Science of the Marathon Wall
reading.passage:
Long-distance runners often describe a sudden, overwhelming sense of exhaustion that strikes after about thirty kilometres of a marathon. This phenomenon, popularly known as "hitting the wall", is more than a psychological hurdle. It has a measurable physiological basis rooted in the way the human body stores and burns fuel.

During exercise, muscles draw on two main energy sources: carbohydrates, stored as glycogen, and fats. Glycogen is the preferred fuel because it can be converted into energy quickly and efficiently. However, the body can store only a limited quantity, typically enough for around ninety minutes to two hours of sustained effort. Once these reserves are depleted, the body is forced to rely more heavily on fat, which releases energy far more slowly. The result is a dramatic drop in pace and a feeling that the legs have turned to lead.

Sports scientists have developed several strategies to delay this collapse. The most familiar is carbohydrate loading, in which runners increase their intake of pasta, rice and bread in the days before a race to maximise glycogen stores. During the event itself, athletes consume energy gels and sports drinks to top up their supplies. Crucially, experienced runners also pace themselves carefully in the early stages, resisting the temptation to set off too fast and burn through their glycogen prematurely.

Training itself can reshape the body's metabolism. Regular endurance work teaches the muscles to use fat more readily, sparing precious glycogen for later in the race. Some coaches even advocate occasional "fasted" runs, performed before breakfast, to encourage this adaptation. While such methods cannot eliminate the wall entirely, they can push it back far enough that a well-prepared runner may never encounter it at all.

questions.q: The sensation of "hitting the wall" has only a psychological explanation.
Glycogen provides energy more rapidly than fat does.
The body can store enough glycogen for an entire marathon.
Carbohydrate loading was invented by a specific named scientist.
According to the passage, why do experienced runners avoid starting too quickly?
options: To avoid using up their glycogen too early
To warm up their muscles slowly
To stay with the leading group

questions.q: What is the purpose of "fasted" runs?
options: To lose weight before a race
To train the muscles to burn fat more readily

questions.q: During a race, athletes consume energy gels and sports ________ to replenish their supplies.
Glycogen reserves typically last around ________ minutes to two hours of sustained effort.

listening.title: Joining the University Running Club
listening.script:
STUDENT: Hi, I'd like to sign up for the running club. Is there a membership fee?

STAFF: Welcome! Yes, the annual fee is fifteen pounds, which covers all group sessions and a club T-shirt.

STUDENT: Great. When do you meet?

STAFF: We have three sessions a week. The main group run is on Tuesday evenings at half past six, starting from the sports centre. There's also a Saturday morning session at nine.

STUDENT: And what about beginners? I haven't run seriously before.

STAFF: No problem at all. The Thursday session is specifically for beginners. It's a gentle five-kilometre route through the park, and a qualified coach leads it.

STUDENT: Perfect. Should I bring anything special?

STAFF: Just comfortable running shoes and a water bottle. We provide high-visibility vests for the darker evenings. Oh, and please fill in the health form before your first run.

STUDENT: Where do I find that?

STAFF: It's on our website, under the Membership tab. Bring the completed form to reception.

Which session is intended for beginners?

speaking.q: Let's talk about exercise. Do you do any sport or physical activity regularly? Why or why not? How did you feel about sport when you were at school?

Sport | 2010 | 2020
Running | 1,200 | 2,400
Swimming | 1,800 | 1,500
Cycling | 600 | 1,700

vocab.example: After the long climb, his energy reserves were completely depleted.
The marathon requires a sustained effort over several hours.
Fatigue has a clear physiological cause.
Endurance training can reshape the body's metabolism.
The body's glycogen reserves are limited.
Some coaches advocate fasted runs in the morning.
This adaptation helps muscles burn fat efficiently.
Running too fast can deplete glycogen prematurely.
The body converts glycogen into energy quickly.
A premature sprint can ruin a race.

Loading carbohydrates helps maximise glycogen stores.

reading.title: Altitude Training and the Limits of Oxygen
reading.passage:
For decades, elite endurance athletes have travelled to mountainous regions to train at high altitude, convinced that thin air will sharpen their competitive edge. The logic seems straightforward: at altitude, the air contains less oxygen, forcing the body to adapt by producing more red blood cells. When the athlete then returns to sea level, these extra cells deliver oxygen more efficiently to working muscles, in theory boosting performance.

The biological mechanism behind this effect is now well understood. A drop in available oxygen triggers the kidneys to release a hormone called erythropoietin, or EPO, which stimulates the bone marrow to manufacture additional red blood cells. The process unfolds gradually, and most experts agree that an athlete must spend at least three weeks at altitude before meaningful changes occur. The benefits, once gained, fade within a few weeks of returning to lower ground.

Yet the picture is more complicated than early enthusiasts assumed. Training hard at altitude is difficult precisely because of the reduced oxygen; athletes often cannot maintain the intensity they would achieve at sea level, and their fitness can actually decline. To resolve this paradox, scientists developed the "live high, train low" approach. Athletes sleep and rest at altitude to stimulate red blood cell production, but descend to lower elevations for their hardest workouts, preserving training quality.

Not everyone responds to altitude in the same way. Studies have revealed that some individuals, dubbed "non-responders", show little or no increase in red blood cells regardless of how long they stay. The reasons remain unclear, though genetics are thought to play a part. As a result, coaches increasingly tailor altitude programmes to the individual, monitoring blood markers closely rather than assuming that the mountains will work their magic on everyone alike.

questions.q: At high altitude, the air contains less oxygen than at sea level.
EPO is released by the kidneys in response to reduced oxygen.
The benefits of altitude training last permanently after returning to sea level.
The first athlete to use altitude training won an Olympic gold medal.
What problem does the "live high, train low" method solve?
options: The difficulty of training intensely at altitude

questions.q: What are "non-responders"?
options: People whose blood does not change much at altitude
Runners who train only at sea level

questions.q: An athlete must usually spend at least ________ weeks at altitude for meaningful changes.
The hormone EPO stimulates the bone ________ to produce more red blood cells.

listening.title: A Lecture on Sports Recovery
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good morning. Today we're looking at recovery, which many athletes underestimate. Recovery is not simply resting; it's an active part of training. Let me outline four key elements.

First, sleep. Most research suggests that endurance athletes need between eight and nine hours per night. During deep sleep, the body releases growth hormone, which repairs muscle tissue.

Second, nutrition. The thirty minutes after exercise is often called the recovery window. Consuming protein and carbohydrate during this period speeds up muscle repair significantly.

Third, hydration. Even a two per cent loss of body water can reduce performance, so drinking regularly throughout the day matters more than people think.

Finally, what we call active recovery: gentle movement such as walking or light cycling on rest days. This increases blood flow and helps clear waste products from the muscles.

In next week's session, we'll examine how technology, including wearable trackers, can measure recovery more precisely.

questions.q: According to the lecturer, recovery is best described as:
Endurance athletes need between eight and ________ hours of sleep per night.
During deep sleep, the body releases growth ________, which repairs muscle.
The thirty minutes after exercise is called the recovery ________.
What can even a two per cent loss of body water do?
Examples of active recovery include walking or light ________.

speaking.q:
Describe a time when you had to push yourself physically. You should say:
- what the activity was
- when and where it happened
- why it was challenging
and explain how you felt afterwards.

writing.q:
Some people believe that professional athletes are paid far too much money compared with people in more essential jobs, such as nurses and teachers. To what extent do you agree or disagree? Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Elite athletes train for many hours each day.
Altitude training may give runners a competitive edge.
EPO stimulates the production of red blood cells.
The adaptation unfolds gradually over weeks.
The paradox is that altitude both helps and hinders training.
Training low preserves workout quality.
Coaches tailor programmes to each athlete.
The biological mechanism is well understood.
Bone marrow manufactures red blood cells.

reading.title: The Rise of Data in Football Tactics
reading.passage:
A generation ago, football managers relied chiefly on instinct and experience when selecting players and planning tactics. Today, the sport is awash with data. Every match generates millions of data points, tracking the position of every player and the ball many times each second. This flood of information has quietly transformed the way professional clubs operate, on the pitch and behind the scenes.

The most visible application of data is in recruitment. Clubs once depended on scouts who travelled to watch matches in person, forming subjective judgements about a player's potential. While scouts remain valuable, their opinions are now supplemented by detailed statistics measuring everything from passing accuracy to the distance a player covers in a single game. Smaller clubs with limited budgets have used such analysis to identify undervalued talent overlooked by wealthier rivals, occasionally allowing them to compete far above their financial weight.

Data also shapes tactics during a match. Analysts sitting high in the stands feed information to the coaching staff, highlighting patterns invisible to the naked eye. A team might discover, for instance, that an opponent is vulnerable to attacks down a particular flank, or that a key player tires noticeably in the final twenty minutes. Armed with such insights, managers can adjust their approach in real time.

Nevertheless, many within the game caution against an over-reliance on numbers. Football, they argue, contains qualities that resist measurement: the leadership a captain provides, the morale of a dressing room, the unpredictable spark of individual brilliance. The most successful clubs, observers increasingly suggest, are those that blend rigorous analysis with the human judgement of experienced coaches, treating data as a powerful tool rather than an infallible oracle.

questions.q: In the past, managers relied mainly on instinct and experience.
Human scouts have been completely replaced by data analysis.
Smaller clubs have used data to find undervalued players.
Data analysis has reduced ticket prices for fans.
What can analysts in the stands help coaches identify during a match?
options: Patterns invisible to the naked eye

questions.q: What does the passage say about qualities such as leadership and morale?
Every match generates millions of data ________ about players and the ball.
The most successful clubs blend analysis with the human ________ of coaches.

listening.title: Booking a Sports Facility
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good afternoon, Riverside Sports Centre. How can I help?

CALLER: Hi, I'd like to book a five-a-side football pitch for next Saturday.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. What time were you thinking?

CALLER: Around seven in the evening, if possible.

RECEPTIONIST: Let me check. We have a slot free from seven until eight. The indoor pitch is forty pounds an hour, and the outdoor one is thirty.

CALLER: We'll take the outdoor pitch, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. Can I take a name for the booking?

CALLER: It's under Daniel Foster. That's F-O-S-T-E-R.

RECEPTIONIST: Thank you. One more thing: do you need to hire a ball and bibs? It's an extra five pounds.

CALLER: Yes, please, that would be helpful.

RECEPTIONIST: Perfect. So that's the outdoor pitch, Saturday at seven, plus equipment. Please arrive ten minutes early to collect a key from reception.

Which pitch does the caller choose?

speaking.q: Let's discuss technology in sport. How has technology changed the way professional sports are played and watched? Do you think relying on data and statistics takes away from the excitement of sport? Should there be limits on the use of technology in competition?

The chart below shows how three football clubs spent their annual budget (in millions) in one year. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Category | Club A | Club B | Club C

reading.title: Why Some Athletes Crack Under Pressure
reading.passage:
Every sport produces its share of dramatic collapses: the golfer who misses a simple putt to lose a championship, the footballer who fires a penalty over the bar in a crucial shoot-out. Such failures are commonly described as "choking", and they have fascinated psychologists for decades. Why do highly skilled performers, capable of executing the same action flawlessly thousands of times in practice, suddenly falter when the stakes are highest?

Research points to two distinct explanations, which may operate separately or together. The first concerns attention. Under intense pressure, an athlete may begin to think too consciously about a movement that has become automatic through years of training. By monitoring each step of a swing or stride, the performer disrupts the smooth, instinctive execution that practice has built. Paradoxically, trying harder makes things worse.

The second explanation involves distraction. Rather than focusing too much on the task, the anxious athlete focuses on the wrong things entirely: the size of the crowd, the consequences of failure, the watching cameras. Mental resources that should be devoted to the action are consumed by worry, leaving fewer available for the performance itself.

Encouragingly, choking is not an unchangeable trait. Sports psychologists have devised practical techniques to reduce its grip. Athletes are taught to develop pre-performance routines, fixed sequences of actions that occupy the conscious mind and shield the automatic skill beneath. Others practise under deliberately stressful conditions, so that competition pressure feels familiar rather than overwhelming. Perhaps most importantly, performers learn to interpret the physical signs of nerves, such as a racing heart, not as threats but as signals that the body is ready. With training, the same pressure that once caused collapse can become a source of focus and strength.

questions.q: "Choking" refers to athletes failing when the pressure is greatest.
Thinking consciously about an automatic movement can disrupt it.
Choking is a fixed trait that cannot be changed.
Female athletes choke more often than male athletes.
According to the passage, the "distraction" explanation says that anxious athletes focus on:
options: The wrong things, such as the crowd

questions.q: What is the purpose of a pre-performance routine?
options: To occupy the conscious mind and protect automatic skill
To slow down the heart rate completely

questions.q: Researchers point to two main explanations: attention and ________.
Athletes can learn to read signs of nerves, such as a racing ________, as positive signals.

listening.title: A Talk on Sports Psychology
listening.script:
SPEAKER: Welcome, everyone. Today I want to share three mental tools that help athletes perform under pressure.

The first is visualization. Before a competition, athletes picture themselves performing perfectly, step by step. Studies show this mental rehearsal activates many of the same brain regions used during the real movement.

The second tool is self-talk. The words we say to ourselves matter enormously. Replacing "don't miss" with "smooth and steady" can change how the body responds. Negative phrasing tends to make athletes tense up.

The third is the pre-performance routine. A tennis player who always bounces the ball three times before serving is using a routine to settle the mind and block out distractions.

In our workshop after the break, you'll design your own routine. Please bring a notebook, and think beforehand about one situation where nerves usually affect your game.

questions.q: What does visualization involve?
Mental rehearsal activates many of the same brain ________ used during real movement.
According to the speaker, negative self-talk tends to make athletes:
The tennis player bounces the ball ________ times before serving.
The third mental tool is the pre-performance ________.
For the workshop, participants should bring a ________.

speaking.q: Let's talk about pressure and stress. Do you usually feel nervous before something important, such as an exam or a presentation? How do you try to calm yourself down? Do you think a little pressure can be helpful?

writing.q:
Some people think that children should be encouraged to take part in competitive sports from a young age, while others believe this puts too much pressure on them. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience.

Write at least 250 words.

vocab.example: Even great players falter under pressure.
She performed the routine flawlessly.
The stakes were highest in the final.
Overthinking disrupts smooth movement.
Years of practice make the action instinctive.
Paradoxically, trying harder can hurt performance.
An anxious athlete focuses on the wrong things.
Psychologists have devised useful techniques.
A routine shields the automatic skill.
They train under deliberately stressful conditions.
Athletes learn to interpret nerves positively.
The pressure can feel overwhelming.
Mental rehearsal improves performance.
A routine helps settle the mind.

reading.title: The Hidden Cost of Overtraining
reading.passage:
In the pursuit of excellence, athletes are often praised for their dedication to training. Yet there is a point at which hard work ceases to bring improvement and instead begins to cause harm. This condition, known as overtraining syndrome, is one of the most poorly understood threats to athletic performance, and it can derail a promising career if left unrecognised.

The central principle of training is straightforward: stress the body, then allow it to recover and grow stronger. Problems arise when the balance tips too far towards stress. If an athlete trains intensely without adequate rest, the body never fully repairs itself. Fatigue accumulates, and over weeks or months the athlete enters a state of chronic exhaustion from which simple rest provides little relief.

The symptoms can be deceptively varied. Performance plateaus or declines despite continued effort, which often tempts the athlete to train even harder, deepening the problem. Sleep becomes disturbed, the immune system weakens, leaving the athlete prone to frequent colds, and motivation drains away. Mood disturbances, including irritability and depression, are common. Because none of these signs points unmistakably to overtraining, the syndrome is frequently misdiagnosed or dismissed.

Prevention is far easier than cure. Coaches now build periods of lighter training, known as recovery weeks, into their programmes, and they monitor athletes for early warning signs such as a raised resting heart rate. Once overtraining syndrome has taken hold, however, recovery can be slow, sometimes requiring months of reduced activity. The lesson, gradually being absorbed across the sporting world, is that rest is not the enemy of progress but an essential part of it.

questions.q: Overtraining syndrome can damage an athlete's career if not recognised.
Simple rest quickly cures chronic overtraining exhaustion.
A declining performance may tempt athletes to train even harder.
Overtraining syndrome affects endurance athletes more than team-sport athletes.
Why is overtraining syndrome often misdiagnosed?
options: It only appears in older athletes
Its symptoms do not point clearly to one cause
It has no symptoms at all

questions.q: What is the main message of the final paragraph?
options: Rest is an essential part of progress

questions.q: Coaches build periods of lighter training called recovery ________ into their programmes.
An early warning sign of overtraining is a raised resting ________ rate.

listening.title: Designing a Training Programme: A Seminar
listening.script:
TUTOR: Right, let's think about how to structure a sensible training week for an amateur runner. The key principle is balance, so we'll divide the week into different types of session.

Monday should be a complete rest day. Recovery, as we discussed last week, is when the body actually adapts. On Tuesday, schedule an interval session: short bursts of fast running with rests in between, which builds speed.

Wednesday is an easy recovery run, no faster than a conversational pace. Thursday, include some strength work in the gym, focusing on the legs and core.

Friday is rest again. Then on Saturday, the most important session of the week: the long run, gradually increasing the distance by no more than ten per cent each week to avoid injury.

Sunday can be a gentle activity like swimming or cycling. Next week, we'll look at how to adjust this plan as a race approaches.

questions.q: Monday should be a complete ________ day.
What does the Tuesday interval session build?
The Wednesday run should be no faster than a ________ pace.
Thursday's gym work focuses on the legs and ________.
Which is described as the most important session of the week?
The long run should increase by no more than ________ per cent each week.

speaking.q: Let's discuss attitudes towards rest and work. In your country, do people tend to value hard work more than rest and relaxation? Why do you think some people find it difficult to take breaks? How might society's attitude to rest change in the future?

writing.q:
The table below shows the weekly training hours of an athlete across four phases of preparation for a competition. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.

Activity | Base phase | Build phase | Peak phase | Taper phase
Endurance | 8 | 10 | 12 | 4
Speed work | 2 | 4 | 6 | 3
Strength | 4 | 4 | 2 | 1
Rest days | 2 | 1 | 1 | 3

Write at least 150 words.

vocab.example: Her dedication to training is impressive.
Injury can derail a promising career.
Fatigue can accumulate over weeks.
He suffered from chronic exhaustion.
Performance may plateau despite effort.
The symptoms are deceptively varied.
Overtrained athletes are prone to colds.
Irritability is a common symptom.
The syndrome is often misdiagnosed.
Athletes need adequate rest.
The lesson is gradually being absorbed.
In the pursuit of excellence, athletes train hard.

Strong core muscles improve running form.

reading.title: The Quiet Rise of the Four-Day Week
reading.passage:
For most of the twentieth century, the standard working week of five days and roughly forty hours was treated as a fixed feature of economic life. In recent years, however, a growing number of companies across Europe, Japan and New Zealand have begun to question whether so many hours are genuinely necessary. The most widely discussed alternative is the four-day week, in which employees work fewer days but receive the same pay, on the condition that they maintain or even improve their output.

Supporters argue that the model rewards efficiency rather than mere presence. When staff know they have one less day to complete their tasks, they tend to cut unproductive meetings, limit interruptions and concentrate their attention more sharply. A large trial conducted in Britain in 2022, involving roughly seventy organisations, reported that the overwhelming majority chose to continue with the shorter week after the experiment ended. Managers noted that levels of stress fell noticeably, while the number of staff resigning declined.

Critics, however, caution against treating the four-day week as a universal solution. In sectors such as healthcare, hospitality and retail, where services must be delivered continuously, simply removing a day of labour is rarely practical without hiring additional workers. Some economists also warn that the productivity gains reported in early trials may partly reflect the enthusiasm of firms that volunteered to take part, rather than a benefit that every workplace could expect.

Despite these reservations, interest in flexible scheduling shows no sign of fading. Younger employees in particular increasingly regard time as more valuable than incremental rises in salary. Whether the four-day week becomes the new norm or remains a privilege of certain industries, it has already reshaped the conversation about what a fair and sustainable working life should look like.

questions.q: The five-day working week was legally required throughout the twentieth century.
In a four-day week arrangement, workers are expected to keep their output at the same level or higher.
Most organisations in the 2022 British trial returned to a five-day week after it ended.
According to supporters, why might shorter weeks improve efficiency?
options: Staff are paid more for each hour
Staff reduce wasteful meetings and distractions

questions.q: Why do critics say healthcare and retail are difficult cases?
options: Salaries in these sectors are too low

questions.q: Some economists suggest early productivity gains may reflect the ________ of firms that volunteered.
Younger employees increasingly value time more than small increases in ________.
The four-day week has changed how people discuss what a fair working life means.

listening.title: Booking a Career Coaching Session
listening.script:
RECEPTIONIST: Good morning, Fairview Career Centre, how can I help?

CALLER: Hello, I'd like to book a one-to-one coaching session, please.

RECEPTIONIST: Of course. Could I take your name first?

CALLER: Yes, it's Daniel Pryce. That's P-R-Y-C-E.

RECEPTIONIST: Thank you, Daniel. And what sort of coaching are you after?

CALLER: Mainly help with changing careers. I currently work in retail but I'd like to move into project management.

RECEPTIONIST: Right. Our standard session lasts ninety minutes and costs forty-five pounds. We also have a shorter review session for thirty pounds.

CALLER: The full session sounds better. When are you free?

RECEPTIONIST: We have a slot on Thursday the twelfth at two o'clock, or Friday morning at ten.

CALLER: Thursday afternoon works well.

RECEPTIONIST: Lovely. Please bring an up-to-date copy of your CV, and think about three roles you'd like to discuss. The coach is called Maria Lawson.

CALLER: Perfect, thank you.

reading.title: Will Machines Take the White-Collar Jobs?
reading.passage:
For decades, automation was associated mainly with factories, where mechanical arms gradually replaced human hands on assembly lines. Office workers, by contrast, often assumed that their roles were safe, since the tasks of analysing information, drafting documents and advising clients seemed to require distinctly human judgement. The recent emergence of powerful software capable of summarising reports, writing routine emails and even producing computer code has unsettled that comfortable assumption.

Economists who study the labour market draw an important distinction between routine and non-routine work. Routine tasks follow predictable rules and are therefore relatively easy to automate, whether they are performed by a clerk or by an algorithm. Non-routine tasks, which depend on creativity, empathy or complex negotiation, are far harder for machines to imitate. The unsettling feature of the latest technology is that it has begun to encroach on activities once thought to belong firmly in the second category.

Yet history offers some reassurance. When cash machines were introduced, many predicted the disappearance of bank tellers; instead, banks opened more branches and tellers shifted towards advising customers. The lesson, some argue, is that technology tends to transform jobs rather than abolish them outright. Workers whose routine duties are taken over by software may find their time freed for tasks that machines cannot yet handle well.

The genuine danger, according to many specialists, is not mass unemployment but a growing gap between those who can work alongside intelligent systems and those who cannot. Governments and employers, they suggest, will need to invest heavily in retraining so that the benefits of automation are widely shared. The future of office work, in this view, depends less on the capabilities of the machines themselves than on the choices societies make in response to them.

questions.q: Office workers traditionally believed their jobs were less vulnerable to automation than factory jobs.
Routine tasks are described as harder to automate than non-routine tasks.
What makes the latest software unsettling, according to the passage?
options: It is cheaper than older machines
It threatens tasks once seen as non-routine

questions.q: What happened after cash machines were introduced?
options: All bank tellers lost their jobs

questions.q: The author states that automation has already caused mass unemployment in offices.
Non-routine tasks rely on creativity, empathy or complex ________.
Specialists warn of a growing ________ between workers who can use intelligent systems and those who cannot.
The author believes the future of office work depends mainly on societies' choices.

listening.title: A Lecture on Reskilling the Workforce
listening.script:
LECTURER: Good afternoon, everyone. Today I want to look at how workers can adapt as technology changes their industries. We use the word reskilling to describe learning entirely new abilities, and upskilling to mean improving the ones you already have.

Let's start with some numbers. A recent survey of employers found that around sixty per cent expected their staff to need significant retraining within five years. Yet only about a third of companies currently offer formal training programmes. That gap is the heart of today's problem.

There are three main barriers. The first is cost: good courses are expensive, and small firms often cannot afford them. The second is time, because employees struggle to study while working full-time. The third, and perhaps the most overlooked, is motivation. People rarely commit to learning unless they can see a clear benefit.

So what works? Evidence suggests that short, practical courses delivered in the workplace are far more effective than long academic ones. Mentoring also helps, as experienced colleagues guide newcomers. Finally, governments can support the process by offering tax incentives to firms that train their staff. I'll hand out a reading list at the end covering each of these solutions in detail.

How many companies currently offer formal training?
Which barrier does the lecturer call the most overlooked?

speaking.q: Describe a skill you would like to learn for your future career. You should say: what the skill is; why you want to learn it; how you would go about learning it; and explain how this skill might help you in the world of work.

reading.title: The Promise and Peril of the Gig Economy
reading.passage:
Over the past fifteen years, a new way of organising labour has spread rapidly across the world's cities. In the so-called gig economy, workers are not employed in the traditional sense but are instead hired for short, individual tasks, typically arranged through a smartphone application. A driver may complete dozens of separate journeys in a single day, while a designer might take on a string of brief commissions from clients she never meets in person. For its admirers, this arrangement represents the ultimate form of flexibility.

The attractions are real. Workers can decide when, where and how much they wish to work, fitting their hours around family responsibilities, studies or other commitments. Companies, meanwhile, gain access to a vast pool of labour without the long-term obligations that come with permanent contracts. In economies where steady jobs are scarce, the gig model has provided a vital source of income for millions who might otherwise struggle to find any work at all.

Nevertheless, the same flexibility that appeals to some leaves others dangerously exposed. Because gig workers are usually classified as self-employed, they rarely enjoy the protections afforded to ordinary employees, such as paid holidays, sick leave or a guaranteed minimum wage. Their earnings can fluctuate sharply from week to week, and the algorithms that allocate tasks are often opaque, leaving workers uncertain about why they receive more assignments on some days than on others.

Governments have responded in markedly different ways. Some have introduced laws compelling platforms to treat their workers as employees, granting them basic entitlements. Others have preferred a lighter touch, fearing that heavy regulation might drive the companies away and reduce the opportunities available. What is clear is that the gig economy is no passing fashion. As digital platforms continue to multiply, societies will be forced to decide how to reconcile the genuine convenience they offer with the security that workers have long depended upon.

questions.q: Gig economy tasks are usually arranged through a smartphone application.
Gig workers typically receive the same legal protections as permanent employees.
What advantage do companies gain from the gig model?
options: Access to labour without long-term obligations

questions.q: Why are gig workers often uncertain about their assignments?
The passage states that gig workers earn more on average than permanent employees.

The author concludes that the gig economy is likely to disappear soon.

listening.title: Debating the Future of Remote Work
listening.script:
HOST: Welcome back. Today we're asking whether remote work is here to stay. I'm joined by economist Rachel Ortiz and HR manager Tom Becker. Rachel, your view?

RACHEL: Thanks. I think remote work is permanent for many roles. Our research shows employees save on average ninety minutes a day by not commuting, and most say they're more productive at home.

HOST: Tom, do you agree?

TOM: Partly. Productivity often does rise, but I worry about collaboration. Younger staff in particular learn a great deal from being around experienced colleagues, and that's harder to replicate online.

RACHEL: That's fair. The compromise most firms are reaching is hybrid work, usually three days in the office and two at home.

TOM: Exactly. In our company, attendance on Mondays and Fridays dropped sharply, so we now require everyone in on Wednesdays for team meetings.

HOST: And what about costs?

RACHEL: Companies are cutting office space, sometimes by up to forty per cent, which saves significant money on rent.

TOM: True, though they then spend more on better video technology and on occasional travel to bring teams together.

HOST: Fascinating. We'll take a short break and return with your questions.

questions.q: What is Rachel's overall view of remote work?
options: It is permanent for many roles

questions.q: Employees save on average ________ minutes a day by not commuting.

The compromise many firms reach is called ________ work.

speaking.q: Let's discuss the changing nature of work. Some say that flexible, short-term jobs give people freedom, while others believe permanent jobs offer more security. How do you think the balance between flexibility and security will change in the future, and what responsibilities do governments have in protecting workers?

writing.q: In many countries, an increasing number of people work in temporary or short-term jobs rather than holding permanent positions. Do the advantages of this trend outweigh the disadvantages? Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.
